2020-03-21

Building An Antidote to a Plague

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • In crisis, resist the impulse to hoard and instead look for ways to give generously—material resources, skills, encouragement, and time—because God's economy runs on generosity, not scarcity.
  • When overwhelmed and unable to find words, trust that the Spirit intercedes with groanings too deep for words (Romans 8:26); not knowing how to pray is not a barrier to God hearing you.
  • Use seasons of forced separation not merely for physical safety but as an opportunity to draw closer to God and reconnect with people through whatever means are available—calls, messages, intentional encouragement.
One-Line Action
Identify one person in your circle who is struggling this week and reach out with a specific act of generosity or encouragement rather than retreating into self-preservation.
Memorable Quote
The pandemic is not as bad as the panic. That is spreading faster than even the disease.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi Rosenberg delivers Beth Zion's first online Shabbat service during the COVID-19 pandemic, drawing from the Torah portion Vayakhel (He Assembled) and noting the irony that the portion about assembling together arrives while everyone must physically separate. He traces a pattern of God delivering His people from plagues across scripture—from Purim's plague of hatred through Haman, to the plagues of Egypt before Passover—observing that this pandemic falls between those two commemorations on the Jewish calendar. From Exodus 35-36, he highlights how the Israelites gave so generously toward the tabernacle that Moses had to tell them to stop, contrasting this with modern panic-hoarding of toilet paper and supplies.

He pivots to 2 Corinthians 10 to argue that the real battle is spiritual, not worldly, and that panic is spreading faster than the disease itself. Drawing from Philippians 4, he calls the congregation to contentment and prayer rather than worry, and from Romans 8 he builds to his climax: nothing—not death, life, hardship, or coronavirus—can separate believers from the love of God through Messiah Yeshua. He closes by reframing the physical act of handwashing as symbolic of spiritual purification in preparation for Passover, urging the congregation to bring their gifts and skills to serve others rather than retreat into bunkers.

Hook / Wordplay

The portion is called Vayakhel—'He Assembled'—yet we are disassembling, separating from one another to avoid the plague. The pandemic is not as bad as the panic spreading faster than the disease itself.

2020-03-28

Called To Be Separated

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When guilt surfaces, bring it into the open before God rather than hiding it; confession and restitution are the biblical pattern, not denial or self-punishment.
  • Treat dry or uncertain seasons as preparation that makes the coming refreshment meaningful—rivers in the desert follow the thirst.
  • Replace rumination on worst-case outcomes with deliberate 'think on these things' discipline: redirect attention to what is true, honorable, and excellent.
One-Line Action
This week, identify one unresolved guilt or grievance you have been hiding, confess it honestly, and take a concrete step toward restitution or reconciliation.
Memorable Quote
When you know me better, you'll trust me more.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi Rosenberg opens the Vayikra Torah portion by connecting the completion of the tabernacle to God's desire to dwell among His people, not merely to receive sacrifices. He walks through Leviticus 1–6 to show that even inadvertent sin carries guilt—for the priest, the community, and the individual—yet God always provides a sacrificial path to atonement and restitution. He then pivots to Hebrews 10, arguing that the sacrificial system was a shadow pointing to Yeshua's once-for-all offering, which accomplishes what repeated animal sacrifice could not.

He draws a three-fold wordplay on separation: sin separates us from God, the current pandemic separates us from each other, but God's intent is to separate us to Himself and make us holy. He emphasizes that guilt is a crushing weight God wants to remove, quoting Jeremiah 31 on Torah written in the heart. He closes with Romans 8, urging listeners not to shrink back but to trust God through the present storm, confident that it is the storm—not them—that is bound to blow away.

Hook / Wordplay

Three kinds of separation: sin separates us from God, quarantine separates us from each other, but God separates us to Himself—set apart, not shut out.

Primary Theme

2020-04-04

Removing All the Chametz

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Before trying to correct others, examine and remove the chametz in my own life first — the log-and-speck principle applied to spiritual housecleaning before Passover.
  • Don't become 'inoculated' against Yeshua by settling for surface-level faith; pursue deep, intimate relationship that actually transforms rather than a comfortable partial engagement.
  • In seasons of isolation or upheaval, redirect energy from blame and excuse-making toward letting God renew my mind and restore my trust through His Word and presence.
One-Line Action
Identify one specific area of spiritual compromise (your personal chametz) and bring it before God in honest repentance this week before Passover.
Memorable Quote
You don't want to be vaccinated against Him. You want Him to be the vaccination that brings about transformation.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi Rosenberg delivers a Shabbat HaGadol message connecting the physical removal of chametz before Passover to the spiritual imperative of purging sin from our lives. He traces the theme through the Torah portion's command to keep the altar fire burning continually, then moves to Malachi 3 where God rebukes the Levitical priests for abandoning their calling and promises to come as a refiner's fire to purify rather than destroy them. He links this to Yeshua's cleansing of the Temple in Mark 11, noting the religious leaders asked 'by what authority' rather than calling him crazy, because they recognized the Malachi prophecy being fulfilled.

Drawing from Romans 12, he urges listeners not to conform to worldly standards but to be transformed by renewed minds, comparing sin to a virus that slowly takes over. He offers a striking metaphor: the world has been 'inoculated' against Yeshua by taking just a little bit of him, building immunity to his true transformative power, when instead Yeshua should be the vaccine against sin. He closes by connecting Jeremiah 29:11 to the COVID quarantine experience, framing it as an exile God promises to reverse.

Hook / Wordplay

Sin is like a virus — a little leaven leavens the whole lump. But the world has also been vaccinated against Yeshua by taking just enough of him to build immunity to who he really is. You don't want to be vaccinated against him; you want him to be the vaccination that transforms you.

2020-04-11

Can We Live Again?

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When circumstances feel one day past any possible recovery, that may be precisely the moment God is setting up a demonstration of His power — resist writing off situations as 'too far gone.'
  • Cycles of straying and return are normal in Scripture; what matters is taking words of repentance back to God (Hosea 14:2) rather than constructing golden-calf substitutes out of our own ability.
  • Reframe Gethsemane: Yeshua's prayer was not reluctance but radical submission — 'If I die here before fulfilling the mission, Your will be done' — a model for surrendering even our plans for serving God back to God.
One-Line Action
Identify one area of your life you have written off as dead and bring it back to God in honest prayer this Passover week.
Memorable Quote
It's our tradition at Beth Zion to begin our service with the sounding of the shofar, the Ramshorn, calling our hearts to the attention of God's presence as we come together to worship him.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi Rosenberg teaches on Shabbat Hal HaMoed, the special Shabbat during Passover week, whose Haftarah reading is Ezekiel 37 — the valley of dry bones. He establishes that resurrection is inherently a Jewish theme woven into the Passover narrative: Israel emerged from Egypt as good as dead, slaves with no future, and God raised them to nationhood. He connects Exodus 33-34, where Moses intercedes for Israel after the golden calf, showing that even after grievous sin God promises His presence will go with them and will do awesome wonders.

He draws a parallel to Ezekiel 37, where God promises breath and life to scattered, dry-boned Israel — fulfilled literally in 1948 and spiritually for all who return to God. He then moves to John 11 and Yeshua's deliberate four-day delay before raising Elazar, one day beyond what was considered possible, to demonstrate that he is 'the resurrection and the life.' Rabbi Rosenberg offers a distinctive reading of Gethsemane: Yeshua was not trying to escape the execution stake but was experiencing a physiological cup of death in the garden itself, fearing he might die before reaching the cross, yet yielded to the Father's will. He closes by urging listeners that no matter how dry or dead their situation appears — economically, spiritually, relationally — God's resurrection power is available through trust in Yeshua.

Hook / Wordplay

Four days — one day past the point anyone believed resurrection was possible — yet that is exactly the moment Yeshua chose to show up.

2020-04-08

BZ Passover Online Seder

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • The Passover Seder is designed to be experienced 'as if we ourselves were there,' and that participatory identification applies equally to the sacrifice of the lamb — when we take the Afikomen, we identify personally with redemption, not just remember it historically.
  • Seasons of forced isolation and hardship are wake-up calls to refocus on what matters; Deuteronomy 30 teaches that no matter how far we stray, the moment we turn back God immediately begins restoration.
  • Yeshua's 'hard sayings' in John 6 about eating His flesh were not shocking new theology but a Passover Seder illustration delivered a year early — context unlocks meaning.
One-Line Action
Use this Passover season to identify one area of personal 'exile' and take Deuteronomy 30's invitation to return.
Memorable Quote
Normally we have our congregational sator at our house restaurant.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi Jonathan led Beth Zion's first online Passover Seder from his home with Marlene during COVID-19 quarantine, walking through the Passover Trilogy Messianic Haggadah. He drew sustained parallels between the Israelites sheltering under the blood of the lamb in Egypt and the present-day quarantine, framing both as moments requiring trust and obedience. The Seder moved through all traditional elements — Karpas, Maror, the four cups, the four questions, Dayenu, and Hallel — while highlighting Messianic connections at each stage.

Central to the teaching was the Afikomen: its breaking, wrapping, hiding, and return were presented as a type of Messiah's affliction, burial, and resurrection, with the Greek etymology 'I came' underscored as evidence of Messianic significance embedded in the tradition itself. He explored how Yeshua's 'hard sayings' about being the living bread in John 6 only make sense within the Passover Seder context, and how Judas departed after the bitter herbs but before the cup of redemption and Afikomen.

The teaching concluded with Rabbi Shlomo Riskin's story — 'The Mashiach is not waiting for us; He is waiting for us' — and the image of Messiah knocking at the door from Revelation, tying the traditional Jewish expectation of a Messianic banquet to the marriage supper of the Lamb. Throughout, the rabbi emphasized that calamity is a wake-up call to return to God, citing Deuteronomy 30 as the Torah's love chapter.

Hook / Wordplay

The Afikomen is not Hebrew but Greek — meaning 'I came' — a Messianic confession hiding in plain sight inside every traditional Jewish Seder.

2020-04-18

Making Every Day Count

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • During seasons of forced stillness or disruption, intentionally study one messianic prophecy or teaching of Yeshua each day rather than passively waiting for circumstances to change.
  • Examine whether my spiritual 'fire' is self-generated ambition (strange fire) or Spirit-empowered conviction—test it by whether it produces humility and service rather than self-promotion.
  • Treat relational proximity (family, community in lockdown) as an assignment from God to model faith through daily living, not just through formal teaching moments.
One-Line Action
Each day of this season, open one passage Yeshua taught or fulfilled and ask Him to make your heart burn with understanding.
Memorable Quote
Like Jeremiah who said it was, he wanted to stop speaking and he says, but it was like a fire shut up in my bones and I had to proclaim it.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi Rosenberg frames the Omer counting period between Passover and Shavuot as a call to make every day spiritually purposeful rather than merely ritualistic. He draws from the Road to Emmaus narrative in Luke 24, showing how the two disciples were grieving and disoriented after the crucifixion, unable to recognize the risen Yeshua walking beside them. He contrasts the 'strange fire' of Nadav and Avihu—who presumed upon God's presence—with the holy fire of Shavuot, where the Spirit empowered Peter to preach with authority.

The rabbi urges the congregation to use the current COVID-19 lockdown season the way the early disciples used the forty days between resurrection and ascension: immersing in Scripture, revisiting messianic prophecies, and letting Yeshua open the Tanakh to them personally. He emphasizes that circumstances—however devastating—are not the final word, because God is unchangeable and longs for relationship. The sermon closes with a challenge to move beyond a surface-level faith into a transforming daily walk where hearts 'burn within us' as the disciples experienced on the Emmaus road.

Hook / Wordplay

Making every day count / counting the days — the Omer count becomes a mandate to make each day spiritually purposeful, not just numerically sequential.

2019-05-04

Part One of Jeannie's Story

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • The obligation to share difficult history is not gloating but a responsibility to future generations—silence lets hatred's consequences be forgotten.
  • Esther's instinct to keep the family together at all costs, even pursuing children into a war zone, was the single decision that saved everyone; family unity in crisis is not sentimental but strategic.
  • When someone is labeled the 'scapegoat' or black sheep, withhold final judgment—the one blamed may turn out to be the instrument of redemption, as with Ezra-el and as with Yeshua.
One-Line Action
Share a family story of resilience with someone younger this week, because testimony that dies in silence fails its purpose.
Memorable Quote
And my mother said, it was meant one for each of my children and called out by the name of worship of Salah.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

This Yom HaShoah service centers on a first-person Holocaust testimony from Jeannie Parnas-Wexler, introduced by her son Bruce, who sets the historical context of the Hitler-Stalin Pact and Operation Barbarossa. Jeannie recounts how her mother Esther's fierce determination to keep the family together—triggered when two children fled with a disapproved suitor—led to the entire Parnas family's escape from Skala, eastern Poland, just hours before it became impossible. She describes a harrowing three-and-a-half-month flight eastward toward Russia, punctuated by repeated miraculous survivals: bombings that left their wagon untouched while others perished, a bridge destroyed moments after they crossed, and unexploded bombs encircling the hut where her children slept.

The family endured years of refugee hardship in the Soviet Union—starvation, disease, and separation—before eventually crossing back to the American-occupied zone after the war. Upon returning to their hometown, they learned every Jew who stayed had been killed.

Rabbi Greg then draws a theological thread from the week's Torah portion, Acharei Mot, connecting the Azazel scapegoat to Yeshua and noting how the one blamed often becomes the redeemer. He urges the congregation to recognize that people are not the enemy but that a spiritual adversary works behind hatred, and that God's presence never leaves even in the darkest moments.

Hook / Wordplay

The 'scapegoat' Ezra-el—blamed by the family for pulling them into danger—turned out to be the one who saved them all, mirroring the Torah's Azazel and the Messiah: the rejected one becomes the redeemer.

2020-04-25

Quarantine & The Unclean

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Use the enforced stillness of quarantine as a deliberate spiritual practice—withdraw to pray like Yeshua did, rather than just enduring isolation passively.
  • Audit my own speech for lashon hara: accusation, blame, and rumor-spreading are spiritually contagious and can dehumanize others just as propaganda did historically.
  • When others are suffering or outcast, follow Yeshua's model of reaching out and touching rather than distancing—show compassion even when it costs social comfort.
One-Line Action
Each day during this quarantine season, examine one way your words may be spreading uncleanness and replace accusation with encouragement.
Memorable Quote
The worst disease we have is our tongue. The worst disease we have is using words to bring destruction to other people, to undermine character, to have character assassination, to undermine their integrity.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi Rosenberg draws a direct parallel between the COVID-19 quarantine and the biblical quarantine laws for tzaraat in Leviticus 13-14, noting that the concept of isolating the sick originated in Scripture. He explains that the rabbis associated tzaraat not only with physical disease but with lashon hara (evil tongue), citing Miriam and Elisha's servant as examples of speech-caused affliction. He contrasts Yeshua's radical compassion in touching and healing the leper in Luke 5 with the social distancing norms of that era.

Drawing from multiple Gospel accounts—the woman with the hemorrhage, Yeshua's teaching on what truly defiles a person, and Zacchaeus—he argues that the worst disease is the tongue and that true uncleanness comes from the heart, not external contact. He warns against fear-imposed and snitch-imposed quarantines, drawing a sobering connection to the Holocaust's isolation of Jews through propaganda and dehumanization. He closes with an extended illustration from the film Scent of a Woman about integrity under pressure, urging the congregation to use this season of physical quarantine for spiritual cleansing and to guard their speech and character rather than succumbing to accusation and blame.

Hook / Wordplay

We worry about washing our hands and wearing masks, but the worst contagion we carry is our tongue—and there's no sanitizer for lashon hara.

2020-05-02

After the Death, Can We Live Again?

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Identify what I turn to instead of God when anxious or uncomfortable—social media, approval-seeking, comfort habits—and name it as idolatry that erodes clear thinking, then consciously redirect to prayer.
  • Practice loving difficult people not by approving their actions but by praying for them and refusing to nurse anger, following Yeshua's teaching that harboring resentment is the seed of the sin itself.
  • When failure or crisis feels like death, remember Deuteronomy 30's pattern: the moment you turn your heart back to the Lord, He immediately begins restoring your fortunes—don't stay in the mourning phase.
One-Line Action
This week, identify one 'broken cistern' you run to instead of God when stressed, and each time the urge arises, pause and pray instead.
Memorable Quote
Sin is our choice. We think we can handle it.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi Rosenberg teaches the double Torah portion Acharei Mot-Kedoshim, framing the sermon around the question: after experiencing death—spiritual, national, or circumstantial—can we live again? He surveys Leviticus 16–20's catalogue of abominations not as mere condemnation but as God showing a better way, emphasizing that the land vomited out nations impartially for these sins, including Israel if they followed suit. He connects idolatry to anything we place above God, arguing that such substitution erodes our capacity to think clearly and process life rightly.

Yeshua's Sermon on the Mount teachings are presented as the heart-level fulfillment of Torah's commands during what would have been the Omer counting period after His resurrection. Drawing from Ephesians, he contrasts being dead in disobedience with being raised to life through Messiah's power.

He warns from 2 Peter that returning to old entanglements after knowing the Lord makes one's condition worse, yet stresses that God's restorative provision remains available. The sermon culminates in Amos 9's promise to rebuild David's fallen booth and Deuteronomy 30's pattern of exile-repentance-restoration, tying Israel's modern rebirth on Yom HaAtzmaut to prophetic fulfillment and offering the same resurrection hope to individuals navigating the COVID-19 crisis.

Hook / Wordplay

The portion is called 'Acharei Mot'—after the death. Rabbi Rosenberg turns that title into the sermon's recurring refrain: after every kind of death—sin, exile, pandemic, personal failure—God's answer is always resurrection and restoration.

2020-05-09

The Appointed Time for Second Chances

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When I feel spiritually distant or in 'cruise control,' I should recognize that as being on the 'distant road' of Numbers 9—and actively return rather than passively drifting, because God has already made provision for my return.
  • Resisting the adversary is not about willpower or commanding the enemy away—it is about drawing near to God so completely that there is no vacancy left for anything else.
  • Traditions and religious routines, while valuable, can themselves become a form of idolatry if they replace genuine connection with God—I need to check whether my observance is producing relationship or just ritual.
One-Line Action
Identify one area of spiritual 'cruise control' in your life this week and intentionally draw near to God in that specific place.
Memorable Quote
And of course when we say Hashem it refers to the name and refers to the covenantal name of God.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi Rosenberg teaches from the Torah portion Emor (Leviticus 21-23) and connects it to Pesach Sheni, the Second Passover from Numbers 9, which fell on the preceding Thursday. He explains that Pesach Sheni is unique among biblical holidays as the only one offering a second chance for those who missed the appointed time due to ritual impurity or distance. He frames spiritual disconnection from God as a type of death and being on a 'distant road' as a metaphor for wandering from one's essential purpose.

Moving to Galatians 4, he shows how Paul identifies an 'appointed time' when God sent Messiah to redeem those enslaved to both legalism and pagan idolatry. He connects Yeshua's post-resurrection Torah teaching on the road to Emmaus with the current Omer-counting season, emphasizing that Yeshua opened the disciples' minds to see Himself throughout the Hebrew Scriptures.

He draws a parallel between the plague that ceased on Lag B'Omer in Rabbi Akiva's time and the current coronavirus pandemic, urging prayer for deliverance. He concludes with Romans 8, teaching that the Torah of the Spirit in union with Messiah sets believers free from sin and death, and that drawing near to God is itself the way to resist the adversary.

Hook / Wordplay

Disconnection from God is a type of death, and being on a distant road means we've wandered from where we're supposed to be—but Pesach Sheni proves God always builds in a second chance.

2020-05-16

Regulations That Remove All Plagues

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • God's instructions are not restrictive legalism but life-giving revelations; approach Torah study and biblical commands as gifts that strengthen spiritual immunity rather than burdens to endure.
  • Creation itself groans under human disobedience (Romans 8:19-22) -- faithful living is not just personal piety but has ecological and communal consequences; stewardship of the land and obedience to God are inseparable.
  • In seasons of crisis, be a 'first responder' in God's kingdom by bringing hope and peace rather than joining the fear; the harvest is ripe precisely when the world is most afraid.
One-Line Action
This week, approach one obligation or command you have been treating as a burden and reframe it as a divine provision meant to strengthen and bless you.
Memorable Quote
Even when we don't do what God wants us to do, we are still the ones that God wants.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi Rosenberg teaches from the double portion Behar-Bechukotai, contrasting the blessings God promises for obedience with the consequences of disobedience in Leviticus 26. He develops an environmental theology showing the land itself has a covenant relationship with God, observing Shabbat and groaning under human sin, connecting this to Romans 8 where creation waits for the sons of God to be revealed. He reframes God's regulations not as legalism but as divine revelations that produce blessing, healing, and fruitfulness.

Drawing on Jeremiah 17, he contrasts trust in human solutions with trust in God, applying this to the COVID-19 pandemic context where people look to science and government as ultimate answers. He challenges the tradition of mourning during the Omer count, arguing it should be a season of joyful anticipation as originally intended in Torah.

Using the morning/mourning wordplay, he calls believers to shift from weeping to restoration. He closes with Romans 8:38-39 and 1 Corinthians 15:54-57, declaring that nothing can separate believers from God's love and that death is swallowed up in victory through Yeshua.

Hook / Wordplay

Reagan's 'It's morning in America' (M-O-R-N-I-N-G, dawn of a new day) versus today's 'mourning in America' (M-O-U-R-N-I-N-G, weeping) -- this season should not be mourning but restoration and anticipation of God's harvest.

Primary Theme
Obedience and Holiness

2020-05-23

B'Midbar: Being Courted By God

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When I find myself in a 'wilderness' season—dry, stripped of comforts—reframe it as God clearing away distractions so He can speak directly to my heart, not as punishment but as courtship.
  • Stop expecting worldly substitutes (career validation, social approval, material security) to deliver what only God's covenant love can provide; they are 'empty clouds that promise freedom but bring corruption.'
  • Practice the Deuteronomy 30 / 2 Chronicles 7:14 pattern concretely: humble myself, pray, seek His face, and turn—then trust that restoration follows immediately, just as Jerusalem's restoration proved God's faithfulness after two thousand years.
One-Line Action
Identify one 'lover' (distraction or false source of security) you've been pursuing and consciously redirect that energy toward seeking God's presence this week.
Memorable Quote
I was noticing in the news a lot of information about masks.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi Rosenberg develops the metaphor of God courting His people, drawing primarily from Hosea 2. He walks through the passage where God describes Israel's unfaithfulness—pursuing other lovers and forgetting Him—yet rather than condemning her, God says He will woo her and bring her into the wilderness to speak to her heart. The wilderness, a desolate place stripped of distractions, becomes the setting where God's courtship begins.

He connects this to the Ahor Valley being transformed from a place of sin into a gateway of hope. The sermon ties in the celebration of Yom Yerushalayim as evidence that God keeps His promises across millennia. Rabbi Rosenberg emphasizes that Yeshua's sacrificial death was the ultimate act of this courtship love—choosing forgiveness over condemnation even while being executed.

He draws a parallel between the betrothal language of Hosea ('I will betroth you to me forever in righteousness, justice, grace, and compassion') and the marriage covenant God desires with each person. The message concludes with an invitation to stop pursuing empty substitutes and return to God, who stands ready to restore anyone from any failure through genuine repentance.

Hook / Wordplay

God doesn't just want to date you—He wants to betroth you forever. But first He brings you to the wilderness, because that's where the music begins to play.

2020-05-30

Shavuot: When God Moves Suddenly

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When everything seems to change suddenly for the worse, remember that God also moves suddenly for restoration — position yourself through prayer and humility rather than panic.
  • Pursue justice without stereotyping or favoritism in any direction; Deuteronomy 16's 'justice, only justice' applies to how I evaluate every group and situation.
  • Shavuot is an invitation to renew covenant intimacy with God — treat it as a personal recommitment season, not just a historical commemoration.
One-Line Action
This week, identify one area of spiritual compromise or complacency and bring it before God in honest prayer, trusting His promise to hear and restore.
Memorable Quote
Some fires destroy, God's fire illuminates and purges and cleanses us.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi Rosenberg delivers a Shavuot message set against the backdrop of the COVID-19 pandemic and the killing of George Floyd, drawing a sharp contrast between man-made fire that destroys and God's fire that purifies and illuminates. He walks through the Sinai narrative showing that God spoke the Ten Commandments aloud before Moses ever ascended the mountain, making Israel's subsequent golden calf violation all the more grievous. He then builds a detailed textual case that the Acts 2 outpouring of the Spirit occurred in the Temple courts during the Shacharit morning service, not in the upper room, citing the Ezekiel 1 haftarah traditionally read on Shavuot and its striking parallels to Acts 2's wind, fire, and voices.

He highlights the symmetry of 3,000 dying at the golden calf and 3,000 being saved through Peter's first sermon. Drawing on Deuteronomy 16's demand for justice without favoritism and 2 Chronicles 7:14's conditional promise of national healing, he argues that restoration begins not with political action but with God's people humbling themselves. He closes with the Book of Ruth as a picture of sudden divine provision and the Jesus Movement of the late 1960s as proof that God still moves suddenly in desperate times.

Hook / Wordplay

Some fires destroy, God's fire illuminates — man-made flames rage in the streets while heaven's flame fell on the mountain and in the Temple to purify, not to consume.

Primary Theme
Justice and Mercy

Confidence
High
2020-06-06

What's Shining in Your Face?

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When I find myself consumed by blame or accusation, I should stop and ask which 'image maker' I'm emulating—the accuser or the One who brings light.
  • Carrying Yeshua's dying in my body means accepting His sacrificial posture rather than demanding my rights; resurrection power flows from that surrender.
  • The 2 Chronicles 7:14 pattern is personal before it's national: humble myself, pray, seek God's face, turn from my own darkness—then expect healing.
One-Line Action
This week, catch yourself each time you complain or accuse—pause, seek God's face instead, and ask how you can bring light rather than more darkness.
Memorable Quote
If all we do is complain and all we do is accuse, we are no different than the accuser of the brethren and the liar from the beginning.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi Rosenberg opens by noting the eerie parallels between current events (pandemic, riots, civil unrest) and the plagues of Exodus, but affirms that God will bring deliverance just as He did at Passover. He anchors the message in the Aaronic benediction from Numbers 6, asking what is truly shining on our faces—the glow of fires and broken glass, or God's countenance. Drawing from 2 Corinthians 4, he teaches that God's glory shines in the face of Messiah and that believers carry this treasure in clay jars so the power is clearly from God.

He connects Isaiah 14's account of the adversary's fall through pride to the human tendency to exalt ourselves and blame others. From Isaiah 40 he contrasts human frailty (grass that withers) with God's eternal word and His desire to comfort and restore. Using 2 Corinthians 10, he argues that spiritual weapons—not worldly ones—demolish strongholds and that full obedience to God precedes any authority to address disobedience.

He warns that constant complaining and accusing mirrors the adversary, not God. He closes with Psalm 27's declaration of trust and 2 Chronicles 7:14's call for God's people to humble themselves, pray, seek His face, and turn from wickedness so He will heal the land.

Hook / Wordplay

You can't turn off darkness—you turn on the light. The question isn't who's to blame for the darkness, but whether you're letting God's face shine on yours.

2020-06-13

Setting Up Or Tearing Down

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When I catch myself complaining, recognize it as a spiritual virus that distorts reality — the Israelites remembered Egypt's free fish but forgot they were slaves
  • Anger closes off the hippocampus and sound reason; before responding to conflict, submit to God first so the adversary flees (James 4)
  • Practice Isaiah 58's true fast: address real needs (hungry, homeless, oppressed) rather than performative gestures that assuage guilt without producing change
One-Line Action
Replace complaint and accusation with the Isaiah 58 fast — tangibly serving the oppressed and hungry — so your light breaks forth like noonday.
Memorable Quote
When anger levels rise, sound reason disappears.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi Rosenberg uses the Torah portion Beha'alotcha to contrast God's pattern of setting up — lighting the menorah, consecrating the Levites, guiding Israel by cloud and fire — with Israel's repeated pattern of tearing down through complaining, quarreling, and accusing. He traces how the mixed multitude's grumbling infected even Moses, and how Miriam and Aaron's criticism of Moses (ostensibly about his Ethiopian wife, but really sibling rivalry over prophetic authority) brought severe consequences. He then pivots to the contemporary moment of racial unrest following George Floyd's murder, arguing that anger distorts reason and that violence and destruction cannot produce the justice people seek, quoting Martin Luther King Jr.

on the immorality of violence. Drawing on Zechariah 3-4, he shows how God cleanses even the defiled high priest Joshua and promises restoration 'not by might nor by power, but by my Spirit.' He grounds his call to action in Isaiah 58's true fast — releasing the oppressed, feeding the hungry, clothing the naked — as the path that causes light to break forth. He concludes that tearing down monuments and history erases the lessons needed to prevent repetition, and that God's design is always to rebuild, restore, and reconcile through humility, repentance, and genuine love for one another.

Hook / Wordplay

God is always setting up — lighting lamps, consecrating servants, guiding by cloud. But we keep tearing down what He builds, through complaint, accusation, and anger.

2020-06-20

Are You Part of a Fringe Group?

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When I see myself as a 'grasshopper' facing impossible circumstances, I'm measuring by my own inadequacy rather than God's track record — shift the lens to what He has already done.
  • The tzitzit principle applies spiritually: build deliberate reminders into daily life that interrupt the drift toward compromise before it gains momentum.
  • Praying for those who wrong me is not excusing their behavior but refusing to let bitterness become my own chain — unforgiveness is a prison cell of its own.
One-Line Action
Identify one area where fear of inadequacy is blocking you and deliberately recall God's past faithfulness there.
Memorable Quote
Unforgiveness is a prison cell of its own. Forgiveness doesn't let the other person off the hook.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi Rosenberg draws a wordplay connection between rebellious 'fringe groups' and the biblical tzitzit (fringes) commanded in Numbers 15. He traces how the Israelite spies returned with a negative report, saw themselves as grasshoppers, and let fear overtake faith, leading to 40 years of wandering. He contrasts this with the woman in Luke 8 who, despite 12 years of suffering, reached out in trust to touch Yeshua's tzitzit and was healed instantly.

The sermon addresses the social unrest of the moment, drawing parallels between Israel's complaints escalating to violence and modern protests that move from grievance to destruction. He emphasizes that God's formula is redemption not revenge, restoration not segregation, and that loving enemies and praying for persecutors is the path Yeshua taught.

The tzitzit represent God's holiness interwoven in every aspect of life, a reminder that left to ourselves we go astray. He closes with Yeshua's invitation in Matthew 11:28-30 to come and find rest, urging listeners to be part of God's 'fringe group' rather than factions driven by fear and anger.

Hook / Wordplay

'Fringe group' as both the rebellious factions pulling away from God and the tzitzit fringes that pull us back toward Him — the question is which fringe group you belong to.

2020-06-27

For People or For Power

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When I see injustice or conflict, my first response should be intercession—falling on my face before God—rather than joining word battles or controversies that accomplish nothing useful.
  • Guard against the subtle shift from wanting to serve to wanting control; ambition can cause me to lose what I already have by grasping for what I think I want.
  • Be a voice of encouragement rather than joining the bandwagon of criticism and complaint—stand between the dead and the living like Aaron to stop the plague.
One-Line Action
This week, refuse to engage in word battles or controversies and instead intercede in prayer for someone you disagree with.
Memorable Quote
You can have what you want, but you may not want what you get.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi Rosenberg examines the rebellion of Korah in Numbers 16 as a timeless case study in how the quest for power disguises itself as advocacy for the people. Korah and 250 respected leaders—men of reputation and standing—challenged Moses and Aaron's authority under the pretense that 'the entire community is holy,' yet their real aim was to seize power for themselves. The rabbi highlights how Datan and Aviram even called Egypt 'a land flowing with milk and honey,' showing how power-lust distorts perception and memory.

He draws a parallel to Paul's experience in 2 Timothy, where Paul suffered for the sake of bringing deliverance rather than seeking authority. The sermon warns against word battles, stupid controversies, and movements that claim to serve people but ultimately devour them, citing Proverbs 30's description of insatiable appetites for power. Rabbi Rosenberg stresses that Moses consistently fell on his face to intercede for the very people attacking him, modeling the response God desires.

He calls the congregation to pursue change from the inside out rather than through force, legislation, or thought-policing, and to stand like Aaron between the dead and the living to stop the plague of hatred. The sermon closes with Churchill's 'never give in' quote reframed as spiritual perseverance, urging believers not to be ashamed of the good news but to let God transform hearts rather than trying to seize control themselves.

Hook / Wordplay

For the people or for the power? If it's for the power, it's never really for the people—except for an elite group that holds power until they devour themselves.

2020-07-04

Our Responsibility to Choose

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Before reacting in anger or frustration, pause and bring the situation to God first — Moses' story shows that even righteous anger without God's direction leads to consequences that cannot be undone.
  • Take full ownership of mistakes without excuse-making or deflection; David's refusal to offer God something that cost him nothing is the model for genuine repentance.
  • Guard against humor or carelessness at others' expense, especially in vulnerable moments — the rabbi's personal confession shows how a single impulsive remark can wound someone deeply and permanently alter a relationship.
One-Line Action
This week, before making any significant decision or responding in a heated moment, stop and ask God for His direction first.
Memorable Quote
My God burnt offerings that cost me nothing.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi Rosenberg uses Independence Day as a launching point to explore the weight of our choices before God. He examines Moses striking the rock in anger rather than speaking to it as commanded, showing how even a faithful leader's momentary lapse brought severe consequences. He draws a parallel with King David's unauthorized census, highlighting David's willingness to take full responsibility and refuse to offer God something that cost him nothing.

The sermon traces how Balaam's compromise for personal gain led others into sin, reinforcing that yielding to temptation is always our choice. Rabbi Rosenberg connects the Declaration of Independence's opening assertion of rights with its closing pledge of lives, fortunes, and sacred honor, arguing that freedom without responsibility is incomplete. He shares a personal confession about making a hurtful joke at a friend's expense during prayer, illustrating how impulsive words can cause lasting damage.

The sermon calls believers to remain in union with Yeshua as the true vine, making decisions through God's direction rather than emotion or circumstance. He closes with Joshua's challenge to choose whom you will serve, urging the congregation to take responsibility for their choices and trust God to direct their path.

Hook / Wordplay

He opens with a satirical multiple-choice test where every answer is the same, illustrating how the world offers an illusion of choice while demanding conformity — then pivots to the real freedom and weight of genuine choosing before God.

2020-07-11

Making Every Day Count During the Omer

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • During seasons of grief or difficulty, actively guard against letting pain become a barrier to recognizing God's presence — like the Emmaus disciples whose downcast faces kept them from seeing Yeshua right beside them.
  • Use the Omer counting period (or any set-apart season) not as rote ritual but as intentional daily study of Messianic prophecy and Yeshua's teachings, letting each day build spiritual depth toward harvest readiness.
  • Move beyond a 'sinner's prayer' starting point into ongoing transformative relationship with Messiah through consistent time in the Word and prayer, becoming equipped to share the message rather than fixating on personal lack.
One-Line Action
Choose one Messianic prophecy or teaching of Yeshua to study each day this week, asking the Ruach to open your understanding as He did on the Emmaus road.
Memorable Quote
And you shall love the Lord your God with all your heart, with all your soul and with all your might.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi Rosenberg teaches from Parashat Shemini and Luke 24 during the ninth day of counting the Omer. He connects the consecration of Aaron and his sons with the disciples' post-crucifixion grief, showing how both groups struggled to perceive God's glory through their pain. The Leviticus passage reveals how proper sacrifice brought God's glory to appear, while Nadav and Avihu's unauthorized fire showed the danger of going through religious motions without genuine consecration.

Turning to Luke 24, he parallels the disciples' grief-stricken inability to recognize Yeshua on the Emmaus road with our own tendency to let anguish blind us to God's presence. Yeshua opened the Tanakh to the two disciples, showing Messianic prophecy throughout Moses and the Prophets, and their hearts burned within them.

Rabbi Rosenberg emphasizes that the Omer period is not mere ritual counting but an intentional season to study Yeshua's teachings, internalize Messianic prophecy, and prepare for spiritual harvest. He urges the congregation to move beyond ritualistic observance and develop genuine relationship with Messiah, allowing God to open Scripture and equip them as laborers for the harvest.

Hook / Wordplay

Making every day count — not just counting the days of the Omer, but making each day count by drawing near to Messiah and letting Him open the Scriptures to us.

2020-07-11

What Are The Odds?

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When discouragement tempts me to go silent about faith, remember Jeremiah 20:9—God's word is a fire in the bones that cannot be suppressed.
  • Stop rehearsing bad news on repeat; redirect that energy toward declaring good news and practical hope to the people around me.
  • Hold possessions and outcomes loosely but hold God closely—clinging to things guarantees they slip away, while clinging to Him adds everything else.
One-Line Action
This week, replace one daily cycle of rehearsing bad news with a deliberate act of speaking encouragement or hope to someone who needs it.
Memorable Quote
Then it seemed as though a fire was burning in my heart, imprisoned in my bones. I wear myself out trying to hold it in, but I just can't do it.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi opens with the Torah portion Pinchas, where God grants Phineas a covenant of peace after his zealous act stops the plague. He then pivots to the Haftarah in Jeremiah 1, where God tells Jeremiah he was known and set apart before birth. The sermon's central frame is probability: the odds of being born are one in 400 quatrillion, yet God ordained each person's existence, so nothing is random.

Jeremiah wanted to quit prophesying because no one listened and everyone mocked him, yet the word of God burned like fire in his bones and he could not stay silent. Rabbi draws parallels to the current cultural moment of 2020, arguing that every judgment is a wake-up call, not a reason to retreat. He cites Paul's shipwreck, Peter's miraculous release from prison, and the Emmaus Road encounter to show that God consistently defies the odds.

He stresses that believers carry a message of repentance and redemption that the world's ideologies lack, and must declare it with love rather than argument. He closes with Psalm 139's invitation to let God search our hearts, insisting that with God on our side, the outcome is never a gamble but always a sure thing.

Hook / Wordplay

The odds of being born are one in 400 quatrillion—you already beat the lottery just by existing, so why doubt the God who placed those odds in your favor?

2020-07-19

Mattot/Massei: Stages In Our Journey

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Every stage of life — even uncomfortable or transitional ones — is recorded and meaningful in God's plan, not wasted time.
  • Worship and devotion are not dependent on a building or familiar setting; God's presence meets you wherever you gather.
  • The Shema's call to love God in every posture (sitting, walking, lying down, rising up) is a framework for integrating faith into every life stage.
One-Line Action
Reflect on your current life stage and thank God for what He is doing in it, even if it feels like wilderness.
Memorable Quote
God's always there. So it's all just great.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi introduces the double Torah portion Matot-Massei, meaning tribes and stages. The service was held in a new outdoor setting during the COVID-19 period, emphasizing that God is present regardless of location or circumstance. The portion recounts the stages of Israel's journey through the wilderness, and the rabbi draws parallels to the stages believers go through in their own spiritual lives.

The Shema is recited and connected to loving God through every season and stage. Worship centered on themes of God's faithfulness, the coming of Messiah Yeshua, and the enduring love of God demonstrated through sacrifice. The gathering reflected a community navigating change together while anchoring in consistent devotion and the centrality of Yeshua.

Hook / Wordplay

Matot-Massei — tribes and stages: every stop in the wilderness was recorded because every stage of your journey matters to God.

2020-07-26

Devarim: Words & Reason

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When God's timing closes a door, acting in my own stubbornness won't reopen it—I need to wait for His command rather than forcing outcomes.
  • Ritual correctness without relational intimacy with God is worthless; I should examine whether my spiritual practices are producing genuine closeness or just checking boxes.
  • Fruitfulness is a byproduct of staying connected to Messiah, not a goal to chase independently—prioritize the relationship and the fruit follows.
One-Line Action
This week, identify one spiritual practice you do by rote and intentionally turn it into a moment of genuine conversation with God.
Memorable Quote
Though your sins are like scarlet, they shall be white as snow.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi introduces the Book of Devarim (Deuteronomy) as distinct from the earlier Torah books because these are Moses' own words, spoken with intimacy and passion as he nears the end of his life—comparable to John's Gospel among the four Gospels. He highlights the irony that an eleven-day journey took forty years because of Israel's lack of faith and their rebellion when they tried to act on their own timing rather than God's. Drawing from Isaiah 1, he contrasts empty ritual observance with God's invitation to reason together and be cleansed, showing that God's purpose is always restoration, not destruction.

He connects this to Hebrews 3-4, where entering God's rest is not mere relaxation but an arresting of the soul through trust and obedience. Through John 15, he explains that fruitfulness comes from remaining united with Messiah—the vine—and that asking in relationship with God is not a wish list but an overflow of intimacy. He closes by urging the congregation to let God's pruning produce healthy growth, to be light in darkness, and to use the coming seven weeks of Isaiah as a season of return and deepened relationship with God.

Hook / Wordplay

The 'rest of the story' is not just what comes next—it is the rest God wants us to enter when we walk in obedience and intimacy with Him.

2020-08-02

Shabbat Nachamu (Comfort & Consolation)

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Preparations are part of the calling itself; the mundane disciplines of daily faithfulness (sitting, walking, lying down, rising up) are not obstacles to purpose but the very training ground for it.
  • Love with less than all may not be love at all—partial devotion to God and neighbor falls short of what the Shema demands; examine where I'm holding back.
  • When I see foundational moral markers being violated around me, the response is not to attack others but to be a 'living letter'—let my actions deliver hope before my words do.
One-Line Action
This week, choose one 'mundane' daily routine and intentionally turn it into an act of devotion—pray while walking, teach while eating, worship while rising.
Memorable Quote
Love with less than all may not be love at all.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi opens by explaining Shabbat Nachamu—the Sabbath of Comfort that follows Tisha B'Av, the saddest day on the Jewish calendar commemorating the destruction of both Temples and the spies' evil report. He frames Isaiah 40:1 ('Comfort, comfort my people') as God's response to the consequences of drifting away from Him, not merely to life's hardships. He draws a parallel between the voice crying in the wilderness to prepare the way and Yeshua's own 40-day wilderness trial, noting that Yeshua fulfilled in 40 days what Israel failed to do in 40 years by answering every temptation with Scripture from this week's Torah portion.

The Ten Commandments are presented not as the totality of the law but as a litmus test—when these are being violated, it signals foundational cracks in a society. The Shema is explained as an encapsulation of the Commandments and a mandate for active transmission of faith to the next generation through daily living. Rabbi emphasizes that calling without preparation is meaningless, using the Karate Kid analogy of 'wax on, wax off' to illustrate how mundane disciplines become the very skills needed for real challenges.

He connects James 2:14 to argue that faith unaccompanied by action is dead, and that God comforts us not to make us comfortable but to equip us to comfort others, citing 2 Corinthians. He closes with a call to repentance and return, urging the congregation to be living letters and voices in the wilderness who bring hope to a world in agony.

Hook / Wordplay

God comforts us not to make us comfortable—He comforts us so we can comfort others with the comfort we received from Him.

2020-08-09

Ekev: Love Letter of the Torah

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Faith cannot be inherited secondhand—I must model it in action, not just send my children to learn it, because by the third generation firsthand experience becomes empty hearsay
  • When fear about circumstances (health, economy, uncertainty) rises, the strategy is not a plan but drawing near to God's presence, which is the actual power source
  • The mezuzah principle: acknowledge God at every entry and exit point of daily life—not as ritual but as moment-by-moment conscious dependence
One-Line Action
This week, practice moment-by-moment acknowledgment of God in every transition—leaving the house, starting work, lying down—turning the mezuzah principle from ritual into lived dependence.
Memorable Quote
And he says, can a woman forget her nursing child?
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi frames the book of Deuteronomy not as a legal code but as a love letter from God to His people, beginning with the promise that obedience to His rulings triggers covenant faithfulness. He explores how God repeatedly warns Israel against fear, materialism, and self-reliance, emphasizing that God gives the power to make wealth rather than wealth being self-generated. The mezuzah is unpacked beyond a decorative box—the doorpost symbolizes the gateway to life, echoing the Passover blood on the doorposts, and represents acknowledging God in every coming and going.

Rabbi addresses how faith erodes across generations, using the bronze serpent illustration to show that by the third generation, firsthand experience becomes meaningless hearsay unless parents model faith in word and action. He connects Yeshua's wilderness temptation to the Deuteronomy passage about living by every word from God's mouth, showing the adversary's strategy was tempting Yeshua to trust in Himself rather than the Father.

Psalm 91 is applied to current fears—coronavirus, power outages—arguing that God's presence is the true power source, not circumstances. He closes with John 14, tying Yeshua's words about being the way, truth, and life back to the intimacy God has always sought, urging the congregation to draw near moment by moment rather than treating faith as a distant inheritance.

Hook / Wordplay

Deuteronomy begins with Devarim—words—and God's Word became flesh; the mezuzah is not just a box on your door, it is the gateway to life itself.

2020-08-16

Re'eh - Seeing The Clear Choice

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When I find myself repeating accusation or negativity about others, I am following the adversary's image — I should check whether my words reflect God's image or the accuser's.
  • My life actions, not my verbal intentions, demonstrate what I have actually chosen; I need to audit the gap between what I say I believe and how I actually live.
  • Instead of pointing out what is wrong in the people around me, I should actively encourage the seeds of God's work I see in them, directing their focus toward growth rather than failure.
One-Line Action
This week, catch yourself mid-accusation or mid-complaint and consciously redirect toward encouragement — let your actions match the blessing you claim to have chosen.
Memorable Quote
Your life demonstrates what your choice has been.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The rabbi teaches from Deuteronomy 11:26, where God sets before Israel a blessing and a curse, framing this not as coercion but as a gift of choice with real consequences. He explains that entering the Promised Land required removing idolatrous practices, not to destroy culture, but to protect Israel from spiritual stumbling blocks that had corrupted previous inhabitants. He draws a line from the Garden of Eden, where the adversary introduced a counterfeit image — being like God without God — to the present day, warning that when we traffic in accusation and negativity, we follow the accuser's image rather than God's.

He stresses that life demonstrates what our choices have been: Israel verbalized obedience but acted in rebellion, proving that words without corresponding action amount to choosing the curse. Using the art contest illustration of a mother bird feeding her young in a violent storm, he shows that true peace exists in the center of God's will regardless of external turmoil. He connects Isaiah 54-55 to show God's promise of peace for storm-ravaged lives, and Isaiah 55's call to seek the Lord while He may be found, because God's higher thoughts include free forgiveness for those who return.

He ties this to Yeshua and the Samaritan woman at the well, showing how Messiah offered living water while addressing the curses in her life, and to Paul's teaching in 1 Corinthians 5 about addressing compromise within the believing community rather than judging outsiders. He concludes that seeing the clear choice is first visualized, then verbalized, then acted upon, and encourages the congregation to point out seeds of God's work in each other rather than failures.

Hook / Wordplay

"See" (Re'eh) — God does not merely present options; He actively coaches us toward the blessing. Seeing the clear choice moves from visualization to verbalization to action.

2020-08-23

Shoftim: Undistorted Justice

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When I hear an accusation or negative report, pause and ask: where is the investigation, where are the witnesses? Don't form a verdict from headlines—Torah demands due process before judgment.
  • Audit my conversations: am I spending more time repeating bad news or bringing good news? Beautiful feet carry hope, not regurgitated negativity.
  • In personal conflicts, follow the Matthew 18 procedure—go privately first, then with witnesses, then to the community—rather than telling others and distorting someone's reputation.
One-Line Action
Before repeating any accusation this week, stop and ask yourself whether you have investigated it with the rigor Torah demands.
Memorable Quote
Blessed be His name, whose glorious kingdom is forever.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi Jan teaches from Parashat Shoftim that the core rule of law in Torah is fairness without favoritism, not merely law imposing order but God's standard of undistorted justice. He walks through the Torah's requirement for investigation, witnesses, and proper trial procedure, then reframes the woman caught in adultery: Yeshua was not dismissing Torah but exposing the lack of credible witnesses, since witnesses must cast the first stone and none could carry their testimony through. He shows how Moses himself received no favoritism when barred from the land, establishing that God's justice applies equally to all.

The sermon pivots to Isaiah 51-53, connecting the call to awake and clothe yourself with strength to the suffering servant passage, arguing that Yeshua's unjust trial—where witnesses could not agree—fulfilled the messianic prophecy within the very context of Torah's justice standards. He challenges the congregation to stop merely repeating bad news and instead bring good news, loosing chains and setting captives free. He closes with Matthew 18's process for conflict resolution and forgiveness, urging believers to model undistorted justice so the world can see genuine unity.

Hook / Wordplay

The rule of law is not law ruling everything—it is one rule above all: fairness without favoritism. Two scales are forbidden; one standard must apply to king and commoner alike.

2020-08-29

Ki Tetze: What to GET Out of Marriage

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Treat your closest relationships as ongoing courtships rather than completed transactions—cold embers result from taking love for granted, whether with a spouse or with God.
  • When you feel spiritually lost or distant, remember the Torah's lost-and-found principle: Messiah has been safeguarding what you think you've permanently lost, and reconciliation is always available the moment you turn back.
  • Adopt the 'tzitzit pause' before acting on impulse—the fringes were a physical checkpoint to stop and remember God's ways before yielding to destructive patterns, and you can build analogous checkpoints into your own decision-making.
One-Line Action
Actively court the relationships you already have—with God and with your household—rather than assuming the commitment sustains itself.
Memorable Quote
In this case, what we lost and think we could never find again, Messiah is holding for us all this time.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The Torah portion Ki Tetze contains roughly 70 commandments establishing social boundaries for community life, with particular attention to protecting women, children, and family structure. Rabbi Greg frames these laws not as a checklist but as God's blueprint for healthy relational and communal life. He highlights the concept of the 'get' (Jewish bill of divorcement) and traces the rabbinic debate between Hillel and Shammai on permissible grounds for divorce, showing that Yeshua sided with Shammai's stricter interpretation in Matthew 19.

The sermon draws a sustained parallel between human marriage and God's covenantal relationship with His people, using Ephesians 5 to show that a husband's sacrificial role mirrors Messiah laying down his life for his bride. Isaiah 54 portrays God as a husband calling back His abandoned wife with everlasting compassion, while Hosea declares God's permanent betrothal to His people. The rabbi creatively connects the Torah's lost-and-found laws to spiritual restoration: what we have lost spiritually, Messiah has been safeguarding for us, ready to return the moment we seek reconciliation.

He warns that relationships grow cold like embers without continued pursuit and courtship, both with a spouse and with God. The sermon concludes by framing the season leading to the High Holidays as God's invitation to return from spiritual exile and experience the intimacy He has always intended.

Hook / Wordplay

"Getting the get to get"—triple wordplay on the Hebrew 'get' (divorce document), what you get out of marriage, and getting the divorce out of marriage entirely by understanding God's covenantal design.

2020-09-06

Ki Tavo: Messianic Privilege

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Don't blame God for feeling distant—examine whether you moved away from Him, like the wife who slid to the passenger window and forgot the husband never moved.
  • Messianic privilege is not a trophy but a mandate: use your standing with God to bring life and hope to people in darkness rather than judging them.
  • When confusion and anger swirl, default to James 1: be quick to listen, slow to speak, slow to anger—a person's anger does not accomplish God's righteousness.
One-Line Action
Draw near to God daily and channel your spiritual privilege into bringing hope to others rather than policing their failures.
Memorable Quote
From Joel 2 to Kushefar Bitsyong Kuruatsura, blow a shofar in Zion, proclaim a solemn assembly.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Teaching from the Torah portion Ki Tavo, the rabbi introduces the concept of Messianic Privilege, contrasting it with contemporary cultural debates about privilege. He explains that God's privilege is not exclusionary or self-serving but is open to whosoever will come. Walking through the blessings and curses of Deuteronomy 26–29, he shows that obedience places us in the blessing column while self-directed living leads to the curses—insanity, blindness, confusion, and national collapse.

He uses the vivid car analogy of a wife who moved away from her husband and then blamed him for the distance, illustrating how we distance ourselves from God and then feel abandoned. Drawing on Isaiah 60, he shows that even after exile and darkness, God calls His people to arise and shine because restoration is available. He connects Yeshua as the one with ultimate privilege who emptied Himself to open the door for all.

Closing with James 1, he urges the congregation to be quick to listen, slow to speak, and doers of the word—not just hearers who look in a mirror and forget what they saw. The central charge is that Messianic privilege carries the responsibility to expand God's kingdom, not to judge others or hoard blessings.

Hook / Wordplay

Reframes modern "privilege" discourse (white privilege, economic privilege) into "Messianic Privilege"—God's privilege is uniquely non-exclusive: "for the exclusive only—whosoever will."

2020-09-13

Nitzavim: How to Stand Beyond Today

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Focus on inner transformation first—you cannot remove the speck from another's eye while a log remains in your own; this season of teshuvah begins with honest self-examination before God.
  • Treat each day as a fresh covenant moment rather than coasting on yesterday's spiritual momentum; daily time in the Word and prayer is the avenue through which God gains full access to our lives.
  • Be a restorer of breaches rather than a blamer—when you see broken cities and broken people, ask 'How can I bring healing?' instead of building evidence against them.
One-Line Action
Stand before God fresh each morning in His Word and prayer so that today's covenant faithfulness becomes the pattern for every tomorrow.
Memorable Quote
Even if you're scattered to the end of the sky, I will come and get you. That's what Yeshua did.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi Jan teaches from Parashat Nitzavim, focusing on Moses' charge 'today you are standing before the Lord' and asking how Israel—and believers today—can stand beyond just one moment of commitment. He observes that even as the people made covenant, the sinful nature was already working to undermine it, and warns against the 'root of bitterness' that defiles many, connecting Deuteronomy 29 to Hebrews 12:15. He calls Deuteronomy 30 'the love chapter of the Torah' because God promises to gather His people even from the farthest ends of the sky, framing Yeshua's coming as the ultimate fulfillment of that rescue mission.

He links the Torah portion to Yeshua reading Isaiah 61 in the synagogue at Luke 4, noting the calendar alignment places that reading on this very portion week. Applying the message to contemporary social unrest, he urges listeners not to blame people but to address the sin and bitterness driving division, grounding restoration in 2 Chronicles 7:14. He closes with Romans 10:4, declaring the goal of Torah is Messiah who offers righteousness to everyone who trusts, and calls believers to be restorers of the breach rather than assigners of blame.

Hook / Wordplay

'Today you are standing'—but today is the only day we actually have. The question is not whether you stood yesterday but whether you will stand again today, and let tomorrow's today be fully engaged in Him.

2020-09-19

Rosh Hashanah: Dealing With Impossible Circumstances

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When facing situations that feel impossible, remember that God already has the resolution in play behind the curtain—our job is to yield to Him rather than figure it all out ourselves
  • Resist the pull of blame culture and political polarization; spending all energy identifying who is at fault sidelines us from our actual calling to bring good news and transformation
  • The very difficulties that seem most unbearable often become the catalyst God uses to deepen appreciation for His covenant promises and to birth something new, as Hannah's anguish birthed Samuel
One-Line Action
Stop investing energy in assigning blame and instead turn to God, letting Him restore you so you can bring His hope to others trapped in impossible situations.
Memorable Quote
I would venture to say that if we spend all of our time spewing out bad news and figuring out who is to blame, we will not be doing what we are called to do and that is bring good news.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The rabbi opens by framing Rosh Hashanah around the Torah reading of Genesis 21-22, noting that both the miraculous birth of Isaac to 90-year-old Sarah and the binding of Isaac represent impossible circumstances where God kept His promises. He walks through the painful family dynamics—Abraham's distress at sending Ishmael away, Hagar's despair in the desert, and then the ultimate test of offering Isaac—showing that God had resolutions prepared behind the scenes in each case. He connects these ancient impossibilities to the cascading crises of 2020: pandemic, wildfires, locusts, and social upheaval, arguing that God uses impossible circumstances as catalysts to draw people back to Himself.

Drawing on Jeremiah 31's 'everlasting love' and Joel 2's call to repentance, he urges congregants not to get sidetracked by blame and political division but to be bearers of good news. He highlights the double meaning of Abraham's words—'God will provide Himself the lamb'—as pointing both to the ram caught in the thicket and to Messiah's substitutionary sacrifice. The sermon culminates with the sounding of the shofar, explaining the Tekiah, Shevarim, and Teruah blasts as expressing both weeping and joy, and calling hearers to introspection and restoration rather than accusation.

Hook / Wordplay

'God will provide Himself the lamb'—read one way, God supplies the sacrifice; read another, God Himself becomes the lamb. The impossible provision was embedded in the promise all along.

2020-09-27

Ha'azinu: Shabbat Shuvah (The Sabbath of Return)

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When I catch myself speaking from accusation — about politics, people, or situations — I should recognize that posture mirrors the adversary, not God; consciously shift to a restorative voice.
  • 2 Chronicles 7:14's call to 'humble yourselves and pray' is aimed at God's people, not the world outside — self-examination must come before any critique of others.
  • God's pattern across Scripture is always judgment-then-restoration; even when consequences are severe, the promise to 'restore the years the locusts have eaten' (Joel 2:25) means no season of loss is final if I return.
One-Line Action
This week, audit whether your words about the people and situations around you sound more like the Accuser or the Restorer, and deliberately choose the voice of restoration.
Memorable Quote
And as is our custom, we begin with the sounding of the shofar, calling our hearts to the attention of God's presence.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Delivered on Shabbat Shuvah during the Days of Awe, the rabbi opens by sharing that Ha'azinu was his bar mitzvah portion 55 years ago, though he only grasped its meaning years later when he preached it as his first message at a Messianic congregation. He walks through Moses' farewell song in Deuteronomy 32, which begins as an indictment against Israel for forgetting God and sacrificing to non-gods, yet pivots to God's tender desire to hover over His people like an eagle over her young. He connects this eagle imagery to Yeshua's lament in Matthew 23, where Messiah weeps over Jerusalem wanting to gather her children like a hen gathers chicks — but they refused.

Drawing on Hosea 14, he emphasizes that God's command is not merely to say the right words but to let those words produce genuine action and return. He applies 2 Chronicles 7:14 directly to believers rather than to outsiders, arguing that God calls His own people to humble themselves first before expecting national change. He warns that speaking from a posture of accusation emulates the adversary rather than God, and that revenge is never equivalent to repentance.

From Joel 2–3, he highlights God's promise to restore the years the locusts have eaten and to pour out His Spirit on all flesh, showing that even the darkest prophetic passages resolve in restoration. He closes by urging the congregation to return wholeheartedly and let their unity and love become the visible evidence of God's kingdom.

Hook / Wordplay

Moses' song in Ha'azinu was 'not something you could dance to' — it was an indictment disguised as a song, yet its real message is that God's harshest wake-up calls always contain an invitation to return.

2020-09-27

Highlights from Kol Nidre

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When stuck and lacking direction, pray for wisdom and then move forward in trust rather than freezing—God's guidance often becomes clear only in retrospect, not as an instant download.
  • Blame-shifting offers only temporary relief; real freedom from guilt comes through honest confession before God, not finding a scapegoat.
  • Isaiah 57:14 reframes obstacles: the thing blocking my path is usually me, so self-examination must precede any outward blame.
One-Line Action
This week, identify one area where I am blame-shifting instead of confessing, bring it honestly to God, and ask for His wisdom to move forward.
Memorable Quote
But as we move forward in our trust of God, He begins to open up the door for us. And I find myself looking back and saying, How did you do that?
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi opens by explaining the historical context of Kol Nidre—how persecuted Jews needed vows nullified when circumstances made fulfillment impossible—and recites the traditional prayer. He teaches from Leviticus 16 on the two goats of Yom Kippur, one sacrificed and one released, and connects this directly to Yeshua as the Kapparah through Romans 3:25. He cites a Talmudic tradition that the red ribbon on the scapegoat stopped turning white after Messiah's execution, arguing the ultimate atonement had been made.

From Matthew 5:33-37 he urges letting yes be yes rather than over-promising and exaggerating to cover guilt. He turns to James 1:2-8, teaching that when we lack wisdom in stressful circumstances we should ask God who gives generously without reproach, but we must ask in trust, not double-mindedness. He shares a personal testimony of praying for wisdom, moving forward without clarity, and looking back amazed at how God opened doors he could not see.

He connects 2 Chronicles 7:14 to a national call for repentance, referencing a 50,000-person prayer gathering in Washington D.C. He notes that blame-shifting only offers temporary relief while honest confession before God lifts the real weight. He closes with Isaiah 57:14—the obstacle in our path is usually ourselves, not others—and previews Isaiah 57-58 for the Yom Kippur morning service.

Hook / Wordplay

The Matrix 'download kung fu' moment—we want instant wisdom uploaded to our brain, but God's wisdom comes as we walk forward in trust and only becomes visible in hindsight.

2020-10-04

Sukkot Message by Rabbi Jan 2020-10-03

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Stop compartmentalizing God into approved areas of life; give Him full access to every 'room'—the sukkah has no locked doors, and neither should our inner life.
  • Embrace dependence on God as the core Sukkot message: when comfort and success come, remember 'God is the one who gives these things to us' rather than claiming self-made achievement.
  • Be a carrier of light rather than a critic of darkness—don't define, identify, or explain the darkness, but let God's Shekinah shine through you to expose and liberate.
One-Line Action
Sit in a quiet place this week, look upward, and ask God which room of your life you have kept off-limits to Him.
Memorable Quote
We are like earthen vessels and God puts His treasure in us. We are like Sukkot, booths, frail, not like a big steel and brick building, but fragile.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi Jan teaches on Sukkot as one of the three major appointed times when Israel was commanded to go up to Jerusalem, grounding the festival in Leviticus 23 with its commands to dwell in booths, take the four species, and celebrate for seven days. A central theme is that believers are like sukkot—fragile earthen vessels in which God places His precious treasure—underscoring total dependence on Him rather than self-sufficiency. Drawing from John 1, he highlights that the Word became flesh and 'tabernacled' among us, connecting the incarnation of Messiah directly to Sukkot imagery.

He presents a timeline argument for Yeshua's birth during Sukkot, tracing through Yochanan the Immerser's priestly lineage and the six-month gap to Miriam's conception. He reads from Zechariah 14, showing the prophetic vision of nations attacking Jerusalem, God's dramatic deliverance, and the ultimate outcome that all surviving nations will come up yearly to keep Sukkot. He connects the festival's themes of light and water to Yeshua's declarations in John 7—'I am the light of the world' and 'rivers of living water'—both spoken during Sukkot.

He shares his personal testimony of coming to faith during Sukkot in a Pentecostal church and the ripple effect through his family, including his brother and nephews becoming messianic rabbis. He warns against compartmentalizing God, saying we put ourselves in a box that limits what God wants to do rather than giving Him full access. The sermon closes with the call that God desires to make His habitation in us, transforming frail vessels into carriers of His light and redemption to a world in darkness.

Hook / Wordplay

The Greek word for 'dwelt' in John 1:14 literally means 'tabernacled'—so the Incarnation itself is a Sukkot event: God pitched His fragile booth in human flesh.
