2020-10-11

What Is Your Power Source?

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Treat Scripture like a love letter: read it repeatedly rather than once-and-file, expecting fresh revelation each pass—'sometimes people read the Bible like they're reading someone else's mail.'
  • When spiritually dry or overwhelmed by circumstances, go to God's Word and prayer first—Yeshua's 'if you're thirsty, come to me and drink' is an invitation to reconnect to the power source before trying to fix external problems.
  • Let God's light purify you internally before trying to shine outward—Matthew 5:14-16 shows the sequence is receive the light, then reflect it so others glorify the Father, not self.
One-Line Action
Recommit to daily, repeated Scripture reading as your primary spiritual power source, approaching it as a love letter to be savored rather than a textbook to be checked off.
Memorable Quote
Sometimes I think people read the Bible like they're reading someone else's mail. But God wants us to understand that the reason we rejoice over His Word is because His Word becomes alive in us.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi teaches on Shemini Atzeret, the eighth day following Sukkot, emphasizing that this festival uniquely commands fullness of joy. He connects Sukkot to the birth of Messiah, noting that the manger/booth wordplay and the angel's announcement of 'great joy' in Luke 2 align with the festival's character. Moving into John 7-8, he recounts Yeshua declaring Himself the living water and light of the world during Sukkot, amid conflict with religious leaders who judged by surface appearances.

The rabbi develops a love-letter metaphor for Scripture: just as one reads a love letter repeatedly and discovers new nuances, so God's Word reveals fresh insights each time we return to it. He introduces the sermon's central image—spiritual power outages happen when we disconnect from God, the power source—and argues that staying in His Word keeps the lights on.

Drawing from Matthew 5, he shows that Yeshua first calls Himself the light of the world, then tells His followers they are the light, meaning believers receive illumination and then reflect it outward. He closes by urging the congregation to celebrate God's indwelling presence year-round, not only during festivals, and to yield to the Spirit so rivers of life can flow unhindered.

Hook / Wordplay

Power outages vs. the Power Source—stay plugged into God's Word and the lights never go out; disconnect and you sit in spiritual darkness.

2020-09-28

Noonday in the Night

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When I see moral or social decay around me, treat it as a symptom of spiritual drift rather than an occasion for condemnation—ask what restorative action I can take instead of just complaining.
  • Examine whether my annual spiritual rhythms (fasting, confession, repentance) have become rote recitations or genuine heart-transformation—let each conviction actually land before moving on.
  • Check whether my speech in the past week has carried more accusation or restoration; if I find myself sharing mostly bad news, I have drifted from the good news I am supposed to carry.
One-Line Action
Stop trying to turn off darkness by complaint and instead let God ignite your transformed heart as noonday light for those still bound.
Memorable Quote
God doesn't have to have his arm twisted to do good. He just needs our twisted minds to be transformed so that he can then bring about and release what we have put a wall up to keep from happening.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The rabbi opens Yom Kippur by leading the Avinu Malkeinu and Al Chet confessional prayers, urging the congregation to let each sin listed penetrate their hearts rather than recite by rote. He walks through the Yom Kippur Torah readings—Leviticus 16 (the Azazel goat and the red ribbon that stopped turning white around the time of Yeshua), Leviticus 18 (sexual immorality and child sacrifice to Molech), and Leviticus 17 (the life is in the blood)—arguing these are not random selections but a unified warning about what happens when a nation drifts from God. Connecting to Romans 1, he frames homosexuality, abortion, and social violence not as isolated sins to condemn but as downstream symptoms of a people who have stopped considering God worth knowing.

He pivots to Isaiah 57-58, contrasting empty ritual fasting with the fast God truly wants: releasing the unjustly bound, feeding the hungry, and clothing the naked. The payoff is Isaiah 58:10—when we fast with transformed hearts and act justly, our light rises in darkness like noonday.

He ties this to Josephus's account of Sukkot torches so bright they made night look like noon, and to Yeshua declaring Himself the light of the world. The sermon closes with a call to stop merely complaining about darkness and instead become carriers of God's light through bold, restorative action.

Hook / Wordplay

Josephus said the Sukkot torches made it like noonday in the middle of the night—Isaiah 58 promises the same when we fast God's way: our light rises in darkness like noon.

2020-10-18

B'resheet: The Creative Power of Light

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Each person must individually examine what is 'plaguing their conscience'—corporate worship and group repentance are necessary but insufficient without personal accountability before God for the specific darkness each of us harbors.
  • Stop ruminating over past events and grievances (Isaiah 43:18); the fastest way to become irrelevant to what God is doing today is to stay anchored in yesterday's wrongs and rights.
  • Shift focus from pursuing spiritual power or importance to knowing God better—'when you know me better you'll trust me more'—because deeper relationship produces the trust needed to walk through dark, undefined seasons.
One-Line Action
Identify one specific thing plaguing your conscience this week and bring it before God's light rather than letting it stay hidden in darkness.
Memorable Quote
He said, He said, He said, He said, He said, He said, He said, He said, These words which I command you this day shall be in your heart.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The rabbi opens the annual Torah cycle with Parashat B'resheet, connecting the Sukkot festival's torchlight celebrations—where Yeshua declared 'I am the light of the world'—to God's first creative act of separating light from darkness. He distinguishes between 'barra' (creation from nothing) and 'asa' (forming what exists), suggesting Genesis 1:2's formless void may reflect a prior judgment before the ordered creation. Drawing from Solomon's temple dedication in 1 Kings 8, he emphasizes that corporate prayer is insufficient without each individual confronting what plagues their own conscience.

Through Isaiah 42, he presents the calling to be 'a light to the nations,' opening blind eyes and freeing those imprisoned in darkness, and from Isaiah 43 he urges the congregation to stop dwelling on past events and move forward. In Matthew 15, he addresses how religious tradition can become spiritual blindness when it nullifies God's actual commands, and in Matthew 6 he links the 'good eye' (generosity) with being full of light and the 'evil eye' (stinginess) with darkness. He uses a nuclear reactor metaphor to illustrate how God's power requires reaching a spiritual 'critical mass' before breakthrough occurs.

He shares a story about a professor who learned that knowing God better leads to trusting Him more, reframing the pursuit of spiritual power as a pursuit of relationship. The sermon concludes that God's creative word can speak 'let there be light' into any darkness, calling believers to yield to that light and become its extensions to others.

Hook / Wordplay

God's power is like a nuclear reactor with unlimited potential—nothing visible happens until it reaches 'critical mass,' and then the light floods everything.

Confidence

2020-10-25

Noah: From Violence to Rest

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Sin's progression from desire to sin to death (James 1) has recognizable stages — learn to spot the 'enticement' phase early before being dragged away, rather than assuming you will be the exception.
  • Messiah is not a temporary lifeboat but a permanent indwelling presence — daily acknowledge His right to stay, rule, and reign in every area, not just during crisis moments.
  • Anger physically shrinks the hippocampus and destroys rational capacity — when you feel rage rising, treat it as a warning that you are about to lose the ability to see clearly, and pause before acting.
One-Line Action
Identify one area of your life where you have kept God at arm's length and consciously invite His presence into it this week.
Memorable Quote
This is the difference between Yeshua as our ark of safety and a ship. You can get off of a boat.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The rabbi teaches from the Torah portion Noach (Genesis 6:9ff), examining how humanity's corruption escalated until all creation was affected, noting that the Hebrew word for violence is 'Hamas.' He traces a wordplay linking Lemech's name to God's sorrow (Nakham), showing how the genealogy itself echoes the era's longing for rest. Drawing from James 1, he outlines sin's progression from desire to death, then connects the flood narrative to Isaiah 54's promise that God's compassion will never depart. Using Matthew 24, he parallels Noah's day with the present, arguing that anger and moral blindness prevent people from recognizing judgment approaching.

He emphasizes that God granted 120 years of grace before the flood as Noah preached righteousness. The sermon's central turn reframes Messiah as the permanent ark — unlike a physical ship you can exit, Yeshua comes to dwell within us.

He shares a personal testimony of God saying 'I knew those things were there before I called you,' illustrating unconditional divine pursuit. He closes urging believers to let the King actually reign rather than merely celebrating the kingdom.

Hook / Wordplay

The Hebrew word for violence is 'Hamas' — and the Lemech/Nakham wordplay reveals that even the genealogical names echo the sorrow and longing for rest pervading that era.

2020-11-01

Lekh L'kha: To What & To Whom Are You United?

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Audit the voices and influences I consume (news, social media, relationships) and honestly assess whether they are pulling me closer to God or entangling me like Lot in Sodom.
  • When facing high-stakes decisions, check whether anger or fear is driving the choice—pause, pray, and return to biblical principles before acting.
  • Don't mistake cultural affinity or shared outrage for genuine spiritual unity; true union is with Messiah and produces overflowing thanksgiving, not insatiable discontent.
One-Line Action
This week, identify one influence or entanglement competing with your union with God and deliberately step back from it.
Memorable Quote
If he did that for us, why do we choose not to really live like we've been set free?
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi teaches from the Lech Lecha portion, showing how God called Abraham to leave entanglements behind and journey to the promised land. He traces how Abraham refused the king of Sodom's goods to protect his covenant integrity, while Lot's family was gradually corrupted by soul ties to Sodom's culture. The rabbi draws a direct parallel to modern life, warning that who and what we unite ourselves with shapes us profoundly.

He addresses the pre-election cultural moment, arguing that when anger levels rise sound reason disappears, and urging congregants to evaluate policies on biblical merits rather than reacting to personalities or propaganda. He reframes 'systemic racism' as systemic sin requiring repentance and redemption rather than political revolution.

Moving to Colossians 2-3, he presents Messiah as God's 'secret formula' in whom all wisdom is hidden, and warns against being taken captive by philosophy and empty deceit. He closes by calling believers to remain united with Messiah, focus on things above, and be agents of light and liberty rather than subjects of worldly fear.

Hook / Wordplay

"Lech Lecha" (get yourself out) becomes a call to disentangle from every voice, tie, and influence that competes with union with God—because when anger levels rise, sound reason disappears.

2020-11-08

Vayera: Believing Is Seeing

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When facing uncertain circumstances, resist the urge to 'dally' like Lot—act on God's direction promptly rather than lingering to see how things play out before committing.
  • Identify habitual fear patterns carried from past experiences (like Abraham's repeated 'she is my sister' lie) and break free from them rather than rationalizing them as technically true.
  • Practice 'come and see' faith by taking action steps toward God before full understanding arrives, since faith is substantive trust built through relationship, not wishful thinking.
One-Line Action
This week, identify one area where you are dallying in indecision and take a concrete step of trust before you can see the outcome.
Memorable Quote
Sometimes what you see is not what really is. We need to look through the eye of faith to see what is really going on.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon inverts the common phrase 'seeing is believing' into 'believing is seeing through the eye of faith.' Drawing from the Vayera Torah portion (Genesis 18-22), the rabbi traces how Abraham, Sarah, Lot, and Hagar each struggled with faith when circumstances seemed impossible. He highlights Lot's 'dallying' when angels urged him to flee Sodom—lingering because he could not believe catastrophe was imminent. Abraham's repeated lie about Sarah being his sister is presented as a habitual fear pattern carried from early in his journey, showing how past decisions rooted in uncertainty persist even as faith grows.

The sermon pivots to Hagar's despair in the wilderness, where God opened her eyes to see a well of water already there, connecting it to the earlier 'well of the one who lives and sees.' The Akedah is framed through rabbinic tradition as a type and shadow of death and resurrection pointing to Yeshua. Moving to the New Testament, the rabbi connects Yeshua's invitation to 'come and see' in John 1 with the healing of the blind man in John 9 and the raising of Lazarus in John 11 to show that active trust precedes spiritual sight. He concludes that faith is not wishful thinking but substantive trust built through relationship, urging the congregation to stop being double-minded and stand steadfast through uncertain times.

Hook / Wordplay

Flips 'seeing is believing' into 'believing is seeing'—faith is not the result of evidence but the lens through which God's work becomes visible.

2020-11-15

Hayyei Sarah: Never Live Below God's Best

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Don't frustrate God's grace by insisting on your own plan—yield to His direction even when you cannot see the full picture, because His pathway develops and builds you in ways self-direction never can.
  • Like Abraham's servant who refused food and comfort until his mission was complete, identify what is pulling you off-task spiritually and refuse to let delay, distraction, or comfort sidetrack you from God's calling.
  • Examine what voices—family pressure, personal ambition, cultural norms, anger—are dragging you below God's best, and put them in proper balance rather than letting them quietly take hold.
One-Line Action
Identify one area where you are settling for your own plan instead of yielding to God's direction and surrender it this week.
Memorable Quote
He answered with the words of the Shaman, said the following.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The Torah portion Chayei Sarah opens with Sarah's death yet begins with the words 'Sarah lived,' which the rabbi frames as a celebration of a life fully lived. He introduces the theme 'never live below God's best,' arguing that God has made more available to us than we imagine but we consistently sell ourselves short. Using Abraham's servant as the central illustration, he highlights how the servant refused food, comfort, and delay to fulfill his mission of finding a bride for Isaac—a model of undivided devotion.

He explains Abraham's insistence that Isaac not return to his family's land as wisdom about the pull of familiar ties and influences that drag us below God's standard. Transitioning to Galatians 2–5, he traces Paul's confrontation with Peter's compromise and warning to the Galatians against returning to legalism, emphasizing that we 'frustrate grace' when we try to achieve God's best under our own power.

He connects the fruit of the Spirit as the foundation for the life God intends and warns that snapping at one another and internal division destroy what God is building. He closes with a personal story about his stepfather surviving D-Day because he bent to tie his shoe, asking whether we live worthy of the sacrifice Yeshua made for us.

Hook / Wordplay

A portion about Sarah's death begins with 'Sarah lived'—the real question is whether at the end of our own lives, it can be said that we truly lived at the level God intended.

2020-11-22

Tol'dot: Does The End Really Justify The Means?

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Evaluate what I 'sell my birthright' for — identify the small comforts, shortcuts, or emotional reactions I trade away long-term spiritual purpose for, the way Esau traded his for a bowl of stew.
  • When facing opposition or conflict, resist both aggressive and passive-aggressive means; instead pursue God's peace, remembering that bitterness contaminates even when I 'grin and bear it' on the surface.
  • Trust the master weaver with my mistakes — rather than despairing over flawed decisions, yield the tangled threads back to God, who weaves errors into His larger tapestry of good.
One-Line Action
This week, identify one area where you are forcing your own means toward an end, and consciously yield it to God's direction instead.
Memorable Quote
I was thinking theologic versus human logic. Theologic, the logic of God versus our own human logic.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon examines the Torah portion Tol'dot through the question of whether the ends truly justify the means. Rabbi contrasts 'theologic' (God's logic) with human logic, arguing that our impatience drives us to cut corners with lasting consequences. He traces this pattern through Sarah's plan with Hagar producing Ishmael and Esau selling his birthright for a single meal, showing how both decisions created ripple effects still felt today.

He connects this dynamic to modern cancel culture, where people pursue their agenda at any cost because they don't believe forgiveness or redemption exists. Drawing from Paul's letters in Romans 8 and 2 Corinthians 1, he shows that Paul endured suffering beyond his own strength yet found God's grace sufficient. The sermon's turning point is the assertion that only with God do the ends justify the means — when we yield to His methods, He produces results beyond our comprehension.

He introduces the metaphor of a master weaver who takes our flawed, rushed tapestry and weaves the errors into something beautiful. The message concludes with Romans 8:28, urging listeners to surrender their own means and trust that God works all things together for good.

Hook / Wordplay

"Theologic versus human logic" — God's logic (theologic) produces the right ends through the right means, while human logic leads us to cut corners and reap consequences we never anticipated.

Repentance and Return

High
2020-11-28

Vayetze: When We Realize God Is With Us

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Ask God daily to reveal blind spots rather than discovering them a decade later—the rabbi's twelve-year music procrastination shows how 'false perfectionism' quietly replaces obedience.
  • When feeling spiritually barren or unfruitful, resist the urge to pout or strive in your own strength; yield to God and trust His timing to turn barrenness into fruitfulness.
  • God's promise 'I am with you wherever you go' is not geographically limited or event-based—maintain daily awareness of His presence instead of only crying out during crisis moments.
One-Line Action
Ask God today to show you one blind spot you have been sleeping through, and take one step of obedience without waiting for perfect conditions.
Memorable Quote
Nothing we do will shock God. Nothing we do will be a surprise to him.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi teaches from Torah portion Vayetze, centering on Jacob's realization at Bethel: 'God is in this place and I didn't know it.' He traces Jacob's journey from fleeing home through Laban's repeated deceptions, showing how the patriarchs were ordinary people facing real family dysfunction rather than spiritual superheroes. He connects Jacob's awakening to Romans 13's call to 'rouse to reality' and warns against spiritual slumber—letting circumstances, self-reliance, or comfort lull us into forgetting God's presence. He parallels Hosea 12's wrestling imagery and Revelation 3's rebuke of Laodicea, who thought themselves rich but were spiritually bankrupt.

A vulnerable personal story about procrastinating on music albums for over twelve years illustrates how a 'false sense of perfectionism' can become a hidden blind spot. He ties Jacob's ladder vision to John 1 where Yeshua tells Nathanael he will see angels ascending and descending on the Son of Man, completing the Immanuel thread from Genesis through Matthew 1. The sermon's core challenge: God is always with us, but are we with Him—and are we willing to ask Him daily to reveal what we cannot yet see?

Hook / Wordplay

Jacob's stone pillow gets a 'My Pillow' joke—but the real point is that God uses uncomfortable resting places to expand our vision beyond what comfort would allow.

Prayer and God's Presence

Confidence
High
2020-12-06

Vayishlach: The Power of Restoration

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Don't perform false confidence before God—Jacob was 'greatly afraid and distressed' and told God exactly that; honesty in prayer is not weakness, it's the doorway to receiving God's promise.
  • Recognize the 'mental bars' (worry, rumination, fear, replaying worst-case scenarios) as real prisons that stifle growth, and actively cast down those imaginations by rehearsing what God has said He will do.
  • Difficult seasons serve a purpose: they create the capacity to fully appreciate and engage with God's restoration rather than taking blessings for granted—don't waste the desolation.
One-Line Action
When fear or desolation hits, bring it honestly to God and rehearse His specific promises rather than faking faith you don't feel.
Memorable Quote
There is no limit to what God can do when we give Him access to everything we are. He'll make available everything He is.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi teaches from Parashat Vayishlach on Hashlamah—the Hebrew concept of restoration that shares a root with Shalom and means completeness or equanimity. He examines Jacob's fearful return to face Esau after 21 years, highlighting how Jacob brought his honest fears to God rather than performing false faith, and rehearsed God's promises. He traces the restoration theme through Rachel's burial at Bethlehem—a place of grief that became Messiah's birthplace—and then surveys Isaiah, Jeremiah, Psalms, and New Covenant passages showing God consistently transforms desolation into restoration.

He identifies 'mental bars' of worry, rumination, and imagination as prisons more binding than physical ones, connecting this to the spiritual warfare language of casting down imaginations. He explains that the Greek word sozo encompasses healing, salvation, and wholeness, matching Hashlamah's fullness.

He uses the Son of Sam story to illustrate that true liberation is internal—freedom in Messiah even within physical confinement. He closes by emphasizing that God's restoration flows through union with Messiah and that our role is to receive that wholeness and extend it to others through a ministry of reconciliation.

Hook / Wordplay

Hashlamah (restoration) shares its root with Shalom (peace)—to be restored is to be made complete and at peace. The sermon builds on a poetic chain: desolation→transformation, desecration→rededication, desperation→inspiration.

High
2020-12-13

Hanukkah Lights & Rededication

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When spiritually demoralized, the path forward is not aligning with worldly causes but reconnecting with God's presence — He fans smoldering embers back into flame rather than letting them extinguish
  • Letting your light shine means emanating God's presence through union with Messiah, not broadcasting opinions or identifying with ideological camps — 'let your head shine' is not the same as letting your light shine
  • Spiritual power multiplies through unity — just as the Maccabees needed brothers standing together to rekindle a nation, isolated faith is insufficient for the transformation God intends
One-Line Action
This week as you light each Hanukkah candle, rededicate one area of your life where spiritual lethargy has crept in and ask God to fan it back into flame.
Memorable Quote
We can think that darkness is its own substance that has a way of crushing light. But it doesn't crush the light.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The rabbi centers the sermon on the phrase 'then came Hanukkah' from John 10:22, where Yeshua declares His oneness with the Father during the feast of dedication in Jerusalem. He traces the historical oppression under Antiochus Epiphanes, showing how Greek pagan culture slowly eroded Jewish identity and drove people into hiding, depression, and spiritual lethargy. The Maccabees' zeal rekindled courage in demoralized Israelites hiding in caves, sparking a collective uprising that led to miraculous deliverance.

Drawing from Zechariah 3-4, he shows God cleansing Joshua the high priest and presenting the vision of a menorah fed by two olive trees, emphasizing that restoration comes 'not by force and not by power, but by my Spirit.' He parallels ancient oppression with modern cultural pressures that demoralize believers and blur spiritual boundaries. He connects Hanukkah to Sukkot, noting that the eight-day celebration was partly a delayed Sukkot observance, and that Yeshua declared Himself the light of the world in a Sukkot context. The sermon closes with a call to let God's light shine through believers naturally through relationship with Messiah rather than through opinion-broadcasting or cause-alignment.

Hook / Wordplay

'Then came Hanukkah' — the repeated pivot from John 10:22, framing Hanukkah as the moment God's light breaks through precisely when darkness and oppression seem most total.

2020-12-20

Miketz: Why Does The End Take So Long To Get To?

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • God's training often comes disguised as mundane or painful circumstances—like Joseph managing Potiphar's house and the prison, skills were being built for a role he couldn't yet see, so trust the process in current challenges.
  • Trying to shortcut God's timeline actually delays reaching the goal; yielding to His direction, even when the path seems indirect, is the fastest route to where He wants you.
  • Spending intimate time with God transforms character the way apprenticeship transforms a novice—we take on the nature of the One we align ourselves with.
One-Line Action
Stop trying to shortcut God's timeline—yield to the process and trust that every challenge is preparation for something greater than you imagined.
Memorable Quote
Why does the end always take so long to get to? Because we try to make it shorter.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The Torah portion Miketz (Genesis 41:1) opens with 'at the end of two years,' prompting the central question: why does the end always take so long to reach? Rabbi Rosenberg traces Joseph's journey from favored son to slave to prisoner to ruler, showing how each setback was actually preparation for a role far greater than Joseph imagined. Like the Karate Kid learning defensive moves through mundane chores, Joseph developed leadership and administrative skills without realizing God was training him.

The sermon draws parallels between Joseph and Yeshua—both rejected by their own, both learned obedience through suffering, both became the source of redemption for those who rejected them. Judah's transformation from the brother who sold Joseph into slavery to the man willing to sacrifice himself for Benjamin illustrates how suffering can reshape character. Turning to John 14, the rabbi connects Yeshua's declaration 'I am the way, the truth, and the life' to the theme of yielding to God's process rather than forcing our own timeline.

He argues we actually delay the end by trying to make the journey shorter and more convenient, when yielding to God's path is the shortest route. The sermon urges listeners to allow God full residence within them, taking on His character through intimacy—like an apprentice learning from a master craftsman—trusting that God's plan is always bigger than our own.

Hook / Wordplay

Miketz means 'at the end'—so why does the end always take so long to get to? Because every shortcut we take actually makes the journey longer.

2020-12-27

A Son Set Apart To Restore

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When going through difficult seasons, reframe them as God's wake-up calls preparing me for a purpose I cannot yet see, rather than punishments or dead ends.
  • Follow Joseph's example of not broadcasting past wounds to others—move forward without animosity so God can use the situation for restoration when the appointed time comes.
  • Pursue knowing God (not just knowing about Him) as the foundation for everything else; exploits and purpose flow from union with Him, not independent effort.
One-Line Action
Stop negotiating with dark circumstances and instead draw near to God, trusting that being set apart is preparation for restoration.
Memorable Quote
They who do know their God will be strong and do exploits, not outside of him, but in union with him. This is the key.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon explores Parashat Vayigash through the theme of being set apart by God for restoration. Rabbi Rosenberg notes the unique convergence of the day after Christmas and the 10th of Tevet, a minor fast day commemorating Nebuchadnezzar's siege of Jerusalem. He teaches that hardships and fast days are wake-up calls meant to draw us back to God, not punishments.

The central narrative follows Judah's emotional approach to Joseph, offering himself as a slave in Benjamin's place—foreshadowing Messiah's substitutionary sacrifice. Joseph's tearful revelation to his brothers demonstrates the culmination of being set apart: what was meant for evil, God used for family restoration. Rabbi Rosenberg draws parallels between Joseph and Yeshua, referencing the rabbinic concept of Mashiach ben Yosef and Mashiach ben David as one Messiah coming twice.

Through Ezekiel 37's two sticks becoming one, Colossians 1's 'secret' of Messiah united with believers, and Yeshua's prayer for oneness in John 17, the sermon builds to its climax: God's ultimate purpose is union with Him and with each other. The recurring refrain is that being 'set apart' is not being 'set aside'—God uses trials to prepare us for purposes beyond what we can imagine.

Hook / Wordplay

Being set apart is not the same as being set aside—God sets us apart for a purpose, not into isolation.

2021-01-03

Vayechi: What Will Be Done In 2021

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Reframe hardship seasons as God's preparation ground rather than wasted time—2020-style upheaval can be the demolition that precedes God's rebuilding (Jeremiah 1:10 pattern).
  • Before trying to tear down what is wrong or build something new, make sure God's word is settled in my heart first—Jeremiah received the word before the commission to act.
  • Distinguish between genuine resolve and mere good intentions: when I commit to something before God, treat failure as a restart point rather than a reason to quit until next year.
One-Line Action
Identify one area where I have been making good intentions instead of true resolutions, and commit to pressing through failure rather than abandoning the goal.
Memorable Quote
He wasn't weeping because he had a penitent moment. He's weeping because they spent all of these years harboring this idea that the grace extended to them was not real.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi opens the new year by pushing back against the impulse to simply discard 2020 as a terrible year, arguing that difficult seasons are God's tools for preparation, not just suffering to endure. Drawing from 1 Peter 1, he emphasizes that God's grace and shalom come 'in full measure' and that trials test faith the way fire tests gold, producing something of eternal value. He turns to the closing Torah portion Vayechi, where Jacob blesses his sons—including the messianic prophecy over Judah—and highlights Joseph's brothers fearing revenge after Jacob's death.

Rabbi reinterprets Joseph's weeping not as a penitent moment but as grief that his brothers never truly believed the grace he had extended was real. From Jeremiah 1, he stresses that God placed His word in the prophet's mouth before commissioning him to tear down and build up, establishing the principle that God's word must be settled in us before we act. He frames 2020 as God leveling the field so that building and planting can begin, urging the congregation to fix their eyes on Yeshua rather than on circumstances.

He weaves in 2 Corinthians 4 to reframe afflictions as 'light and momentary' when measured against God's eternal purpose, and Hebrews 12 to call believers to run with endurance while looking away from circumstances to Yeshua. He closes with a practical distinction between New Year's resolutions and mere good intentions: a true resolution means pressing forward even through failure, not abandoning the commitment at the first stumble.

Hook / Wordplay

People don't break New Year's resolutions—they break New Year's good intentions. A resolution means you're resolute; a good intention is just a wish that strays.

2021-01-10

SHEMOT: When A New King Rises

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When facing political turmoil, arm yourself with 'weapons of light'—doing what is right, loving neighbors—rather than adopting the adversary's tactics of rage, argument, and partisan warfare.
  • Don't disqualify yourself from God's calling because you feel inadequate; Moses started at 80, stammered, begged God to send someone else, and still became the great deliverer—God builds the person into the purpose.
  • Stop labeling fellow believers as enemies over political allegiance; the world should know us by our love for one another, not by which side of a partisan divide we fall on.
One-Line Action
Arm yourself with the weapons of light by actively loving your neighbor and speaking God's truth with boldness, rather than engaging in partisan arguments or fighting darkness with darkness.
Memorable Quote
If we have the authority of Messiah living in us, we have an authority that the world does not know what to do with and is afraid that it's going to challenge their power base.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Opening the Book of Shemot (Exodus), Rabbi Rosenberg examines what happens when a new king rises who does not know Joseph, drawing pointed parallels to the incoming Biden administration just days after the Capitol breach. He traces how Pharaoh's fear of Israel's growing numbers led to escalating oppression, noting how complacency and a Stockholm-syndrome dynamic kept the Israelites from recognizing their eroding freedom. He highlights Moses' deep reluctance to answer God's call, showing that God equips unlikely, self-doubting people for extraordinary missions.

A striking textual observation emerges: God told Moses from the very beginning that He would kill Pharaoh's firstborn if he refused—this was not a last resort but a stated judgment from the start. Moving to Romans 13, he teaches that believers should submit to governing authorities while actively arming themselves with 'weapons of light' rather than fighting darkness with darkness.

Through Acts 3-4, he shows how Peter and John deflected credit for healing to Yeshua's name and spoke with bold authority before the Sanhedrin despite being untrained 'people of the land.' He uses deliberate comedic misreadings of Acts 4:23-29 to contrast the human instinct for revenge with the disciples' actual response of prayer and worship. The sermon warns against partisan division among believers and calls instead for humble, bold witness through love, trusting that God remains sovereign over all rulers and nations.

Hook / Wordplay

'Shemot means names—what's in a name and who do you know?' frames the sermon's throughline: from Pharaoh who didn't know Joseph's name, to the Sanhedrin demanding 'in what name did you do this,' to Yeshua's commission carrying all authority in His name.

2021-01-17

Va-era: The Trial of the Ages

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Before responding to any conflict, pause and ask 'God, what do you want me to do?' rather than letting emotion or a sense of righteous indignation dictate the response.
  • Winning an argument can still cost you your witness—how observers perceive your conduct in conflict shapes whether they see Yeshua in you or just another combatant.
  • Moses' only punishable failure was deviating from God's specific instruction (striking the rock); every complaint others heaped on him never diminished his standing—obedience to God's command is the only metric that matters.
One-Line Action
This week, in any disagreement or frustration, deliberately pause before responding and ask God what He wants you to say or do before acting on your own impulse.
Memorable Quote
You can win an argument and still sacrifice your witness. You can win an argument and still turn people away.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Christian Montoya frames the Exodus confrontation between Moses and Pharaoh as 'the trial of the ages,' casting Moses as God's attorney arguing before a genocidal dictator. Despite having no qualifications—a shepherd, a stutterer, a fugitive—Moses' defining strength was doing exactly what God told him to do, nothing more and nothing less. The Israelites blamed Moses for making things worse under Pharaoh's retaliation, yet their complaints never diminished his standing before God.

Moses' only failure came later when he struck the rock instead of speaking to it as commanded—the single time he deviated from God's specific instructions. Drawing on Ephesians 6 and Romans 12, the speaker argues that believers' battles are not against flesh and blood and that we must live in peace with all people, including enemies. He introduces the concept of 'sacrificing your witness': one can win an argument but destroy one's testimony by how one treats others in the process.

Every conflict has observers—like a jury—who form opinions based on conduct, not just outcomes. The call is to respond to conflict as Moses did: seek God's direction, maintain composure, say only what God commands, and let Him bring the victory. Rabbi Jan closes by reinforcing the message with Paul's words: 'Do not be conquered by evil, but conquer evil with good.'

Hook / Wordplay

Moses as God's attorney in 'the trial of the ages'—unqualified on paper but unbeatable because he argued exactly the case God gave him, nothing more.

2021-01-23

Being Distracted When It's Time To Go

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When God gives a clear directive, don't negotiate it down to 85%—Pharaoh's incremental compromises were designed to keep a hold on Israel, and partial obedience is still disobedience.
  • True humility is not self-degradation but standing in awe before God's greatness; that posture of submission is what opens the door for His power to lift us up.
  • Master your thoughts before they master you—the heaviest burdens we carry are mental, and whoever controls our thinking controls our direction.
One-Line Action
Identify the one thing you keep negotiating away from God's clear call and commit to stop compromising on it this week.
Memorable Quote
When we try to take charge, instead of letting God show us how to prepare to go, we end up being in the midst of desolation.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi Gene teaches from Parashat Bo, focusing on the theme of being distracted when God calls us to move forward. He traces Pharaoh's progressive negotiation tactics with Moses: first refusing entirely, then allowing only the men to go, then permitting families but demanding the livestock stay behind. Moses refused every compromise because God's mandate was total departure.

Drawing from 1 Peter 5, the rabbi distinguishes true humility—recognizing God's supreme authority and submitting to it—from false humility that is really disguised pride or self-degradation. He connects Pharaoh's stubbornness to Yeshua's lament over Jerusalem in Matthew 23, highlighting the phrase 'God is abandoning your house to you' as both judgment and embedded hope: when we insist on running things ourselves, desolation follows. He references the Holocaust as a sobering historical example of the cost of not leaving when the window to go is open.

From Philippians 3, Paul models releasing every advantage to pursue knowing Messiah. The rabbi closes with a bird metaphor: left wing and right wing don't control flight—the mind does—and likewise our thoughts, submitted to God, determine our true direction.

Hook / Wordplay

A supplier's dog named 'Bo' reminded the rabbi of the Torah portion meaning 'Go'—leading him to joke he might be 'barking up the wrong tree' but appreciating how small connections open doors to share faith.

2021-01-31

B'shallach: Exposing the Spirit of Amalek

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Grumbling and complaining are not just bad attitudes — they create spiritual vulnerability by pulling you away from the group and the protection of God, like a straggler at the back of the camp exposed to Amalek.
  • When confronting injustice, follow Yeshua's model: He called out hypocrisy not to destroy people but to purge what corrupted them and redirect them to their purpose — truth to power aimed at redemption, not accusation.
  • Don't mistake posting and opining for action; God wants people of action who expose the adversary's work by demonstrating the redemptive alternative, not by engaging in the same divisive anger.
One-Line Action
Identify where grumbling or fear has isolated you from community and draw near to God and others rather than engaging division with the world's anger.
Memorable Quote
If we approach it with the same vitriol and anger that the world is doing, all we're doing is feeding into the adversary's desire to separate and destroy.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi Rosenberg expounds on Exodus 17 where Amalek attacks Israel's stragglers at Rephidim, framing it as the introduction of a recurring spiritual force that preys on the vulnerable and isolated. He notes that Israel's grumbling and complaining in the verses immediately prior created the spiritual vulnerability that allowed the attack, drawing an analogy to lions isolating a young calf from the herd. Drawing from Jude, he identifies the adversary's toolkit as money, greed, hatred, and power — the patterns of Cain, Balaam, and Korah.

He applies this framework to contemporary cultural dynamics: censorship, market manipulation, cancel culture, and ideological group-think, arguing that the real privilege is 'elite privilege' that sets groups against each other while operating behind the curtain. He quotes Elie Wiesel on the danger of neutrality and extends Niemöller's poem to show how people don't merely stay silent but actively buy into the oppressor's narrative for self-protection.

Crucially, he insists believers must not respond with the world's vitriol; Yeshua's model was exposing hypocrisy for the purpose of redemption, not destruction. He closes by calling believers to vigilance, unity, and action rooted in God's love — speaking forth life, salvation, and healing rather than being consumed by partisan anger.

Hook / Wordplay

Amalek is not just an ancient king but a spirit — a recurring force that isolates the vulnerable from the herd so they can be devoured. Grumbling is the drift that makes you a straggler.

2021-02-07

Yitro: Hearing A Voice From Heaven

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Train myself to be slow to speak and quick to listen in prayer and Scripture—God gives the ear of a disciple before He gives the voice of one.
  • When circumstances are confusing or God is doing something I don't understand, resist the urge to take charge like Peter; instead, be still and listen for the directive.
  • Don't let the initial emotional commitment to God ('whatever You say I will do') substitute for daily, tested obedience—let God's Word be planted deep enough to survive the wilderness challenges that follow mountaintop moments.
One-Line Action
This week, practice deliberate listening—in prayer, pause longer before speaking; in Scripture, sit with a passage before rushing to apply it—building the ear of a disciple.
Memorable Quote
He gives us the voice of a disciple, but it starts with him giving us the ear of a disciple.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The Torah portion Yitro begins with Jethro advising Moses to delegate leadership, illustrating that God speaks through wise counsel and not only through dramatic signs. The sermon then turns to the theophany at Sinai where God's thunderous voice terrified the people, yet they pledged 'everything Hashem said we will do'—a pledge they soon broke with the golden calf. Rabbi Rosenberg introduces the concept of bat kol (daughter of the voice), explaining that rabbinic tradition considers the spectacular heavenly voice a lesser form of God's communication compared to His Word internalized in the heart.

He traces the bat kol through Isaiah's throne-room vision, Yeshua's baptism, the Transfiguration, and John 12, showing a consistent pattern: God speaks, but hearers must move beyond emotional agreement to genuine obedience. Peter's impulsive offer to build shelters at the Transfiguration illustrates the danger of speaking before listening, prompting the Father's directive: 'Listen to Him.' The story of Ahimaaz running eagerly to King David but arriving with no message underscores the need to receive God's word before rushing to deliver it.

The sermon warns against hardened hearts, favoritism, and self-generated solutions, drawing on James 2 and Yeshua's wilderness temptation where He answered every challenge with Scripture. The central call is to cultivate the ear of a disciple so that God's voice becomes as familiar as the shepherd's voice the sheep recognize—not by sound alone but by demonstrated love and faithfulness.

Hook / Wordplay

Bat kol means 'daughter of the voice'—rabbinic tradition calls it a softer, secondary voice, suggesting that the thunder from Sinai was actually the lesser way God speaks; the greater voice is His Word planted quietly in the heart.

Mishpatim: Don't Just Follow The Crowd

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Before reacting to a cause or controversy, apply Proverbs 18:17—hear both sides and cross-examine before forming a position, rather than accepting the first narrative presented.
  • Audit the 'crowd' I run with: are their values drawing me toward God's standards or subtly pulling me past boundaries I've already set? Identify one area of drift and correct it.
  • When I encounter someone I disagree with or consider an opponent, look for a practical way to 'return their donkey'—extend unexpected kindness rather than defaulting to hostility.
One-Line Action
This week, pause before joining any group consensus and ask whether it aligns with God's word or just with the crowd's momentum.
Memorable Quote
Those words will be spoken by the same one who said on the execution stake, it is finished.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi teaches from Parashat Mishpatim, focusing on Exodus 23:1-9 which commands Israel not to follow a crowd to do evil or pervert justice. He connects this to modern peer pressure and group think, illustrating how people compromise values to fit in—from office water-cooler gossip to large-scale crowd movements where participants cannot even articulate what they are protesting. He warns about lashon hara (the evil tongue), explaining how slander murders reputations and creates filters through which we view others unfairly.

Drawing from Matthew 7's Sermon on the Mount, he parallels Moses' pre-Sinai instructions with Yeshua's kingdom regulations: remove the log from your own eye first, enter the narrow gate, and beware false prophets known by their fruit. He shares a personal story of a false prophet nearly leading congregants away, and another about critics of Messianic Judaism who discovered their assumptions were wrong once they actually talked to believers.

The sermon concludes with John 16:12-13, emphasizing that the Spirit of truth guides into all truth, and Matthew 9:36-38, where Yeshua had compassion on harried crowds like sheep without a shepherd. The call is to stop following crowds and instead lead others by following the Lord.

Hook / Wordplay

The Yogi Berra-style fork in the road: when you come to a fork in the road, take it—but which direction? The popular one where everybody's going, or the narrow path God sets before you?

2021-02-21

T'rumah: Don't Let God Go Homeless

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Inviting God to dwell in me requires active cooperation and obedience, not a do-it-yourself approach to spiritual life — His presence is conditional on making room, not just acknowledging Him.
  • The innkeeper who turned away Yeshua didn't recognize who was at his door; I should treat every encounter as potentially significant rather than dismissing people based on appearances.
  • When I default to blaming and accusing rather than building up and restoring, I'm behaving like a 'roommate' of the adversary rather than someone in whom God dwells.
One-Line Action
This week, consciously make room for God's presence in one area of life where I've been running my own 'DIY project' without Him.
Memorable Quote
Otherwise you can go to PayPal online at BethSign.org or buy the old fashioned snail mail, PO Box 807 Jackson, New Jersey 08527.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The Torah portion T'rumah details the contributions for building the tabernacle, but the rabbi focuses on God's deeper intention: not the materials themselves, but the declaration in Exodus 25:8 — 'Make me a sanctuary so that I may live among them.' He frames this as God's 'housing project,' arguing that the Creator of everything has paradoxically been homeless, seeking a dwelling place among His people since Eden. Yeshua embodied this literally — born with no room at the inn, telling followers that foxes have holes and birds have nests but the Son of Man has no home of his own. The rabbi traces a thread from Adam and Eve choosing the adversary as a 'roommate' through to Yeshua on the cross, who looked out and saw spiritually homeless people and said 'Father, forgive them.' John 14's promise to prepare a place is connected to the Jewish wedding tradition where the groom builds a home for his bride.

Solomon's temple in 1 Kings 6 receives God's conditional promise: live by my commands and I will dwell among you and not abandon Israel. The sermon culminates in Revelation 21, where the New Jerusalem descending is identified as the bride herself — God making His permanent home with His people. The call is to stop the DIY approach to life, recognize our own spiritual homelessness, and let God take full residency within us.

Hook / Wordplay

The Creator of everything was literally homeless — and the twist is that we, who think we have it together, are the ones truly without a home.

2021-02-27

Are We In Such A Time, or What!

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Don't be a spectator in spiritual battles—Esther demanded the people fast and pray alongside her, putting 'skin in the game' rather than waiting passively for someone else to act.
  • Resist mental slavery to the world's standards; Romans 12 says to keep letting your mind be renewed and transformed rather than conforming to the patterns around you.
  • If you bench yourself from God's purposes, He will raise deliverance from somewhere else—but you'll miss the moment you were positioned for.
One-Line Action
Identify one area where you've been a passive spectator and take an active step of faith—prayer, speaking up, or serving—this week.
Memorable Quote
Bitterness and hatred are contagious. But so is hope.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The rabbi connects the Purim narrative to the present day, arguing that believers face a form of mental slavery when they conform to worldly standards and silence their convictions. He traces how Mordecai refused to bow to Haman, provoking a genocidal decree against all Jews, and how Esther was challenged to risk her life by approaching the king unsummoned. Mordecai's key insight was that God's deliverance does not depend on any one person—if Esther failed to act, relief would come from elsewhere, but she and her family would perish.

Esther called on all the Jews of Shushan to fast for three days, demanding that the people have skin in the game rather than remain passive spectators. The rabbi draws a parallel to Uncle Tom's Cabin, highlighting how Tom's refusal to compromise his biblical convictions despite brutal oppression mirrors Mordecai's stand. He connects this to Romans 12's call not to be conformed to the world but to be transformed by the renewing of the mind.

The sermon emphasizes that bitterness and hatred are contagious, but so is hope, and that God orchestrates circumstances for deliverance when His people stand firm. He closes by urging believers not to bench themselves but to actively participate in God's purposes, standing against tyranny through obedience, unity, and a message of restoration rather than destruction.

Hook / Wordplay

"Are we in such a time, or what?"—riffing on Mordecai's challenge to Esther ("for such a time as this") and turning it into a direct question to the congregation about their own era.

2021-03-07

Golden Calf Confusion

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When under pressure and God seems silent, resist the urge to manufacture a solution—recall His track record and give Him time to fulfill what He started.
  • Leaders bear responsibility not just for their own faith but for holding the line when the crowd pressures them to compromise; Aaron's failure shows the cost of yielding to mob fear.
  • Self-discipline of thought is the first battleground—if I don't control what I think, I cannot control what I do; aligning thoughts with God's promises prevents the fear-to-idolatry spiral.
One-Line Action
Next time fear pressures you to take matters into your own hands, stop, recall God's track record, and wait on Him before building your own golden calf.
Memorable Quote
People are called to lead. If we abandon our calling to lead because a mob has affected us with fear, we end up doing some very terrible things.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi Jan teaches on Parashat Ki Tissa, centering on the golden calf incident in Exodus 32 and what he calls 'Golden Calf Confusion Syndrome'—the pattern where fear displaces faith and drives people to create substitutes for God. He examines how the Israelites' impatience during Moses' forty days on the mountain spiraled from murmuring into mob pressure on Aaron, who capitulated rather than standing on what God had already spoken. He draws a direct parallel to Elijah in 1 Kings 18, who went from mocking the prophets of Baal in total confidence to fleeing Jezebel in terror, showing that even the strongest believers are vulnerable to fear.

Rabbi Jan connects the ancient pattern to Romans 1's description of exchanging truth for falsehood, arguing that modern cultural pressures function as idol-worship by replacing God's authority with human agendas. He broadens the definition of idolatry to include anything—including pornography and self-reliance—that captures our core allegiance away from God. The sermon's prescription is to master our thoughts by aligning them with Scripture, trusting God's track record rather than manufacturing our own solutions, and walking in the Spirit so that our testimony remains credible when others watch us face trials.

Hook / Wordplay

Golden Calf Confusion Syndrome (GCCS): when fear replaces faith, sound reason disappears and we build our own gods to lead us.

Trust vs Fear

2021-03-14

How Much Is Too Much?

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Examine the motivation behind every act of generosity—identical giving can be fear-driven (golden calf) or heart-stirred (Tabernacle), and only the latter honors God.
  • Resist the 'what's the least I can do to get by' posture; settling for the minimum keeps you in perpetual need, while yielding to God opens abundance beyond what you can imagine.
  • When someone points out a sin or hard truth, don't treat them as an enemy—ask God 'what truth is in this?' before reacting.
One-Line Action
This week, before giving time, money, or effort to anything, pause and honestly name the motivation driving it—fear, obligation, reputation, or love—and only proceed from the last one.
Memorable Quote
Love is the one commodity that you could give it all the way and never run out. Because His love is an everlasting love.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi Rosenberg opens with the Torah portion where Moses assembles Israel to collect offerings for the Tabernacle, and the people give so freely that the craftsmen have to tell Moses to stop them—a situation no organization ever faces. He contrasts this heart-stirred generosity with the fear-driven giving of the golden calf just forty days earlier, showing that identical outward acts can flow from opposite motivations. Drawing on 2 Corinthians 9 he reinforces that God loves a cheerful giver and will multiply the harvest of righteousness, then pivots to 1 Corinthians 13 to argue that even giving away everything you own profits nothing without love.

Proverbs 30:8-9 supplies the prayer for neither poverty nor riches, a guardrail against both arrogance and desperation. He applies the 'too much' lens to anger, fear, and self-effort—each destructive when unchecked—while arguing that God's love is the one thing that can never be too much.

Paul's transformation from applauding Stephen's stoning to writing most of the New Covenant letters illustrates how yielding to Messiah converts misguided zeal into kingdom fruitfulness. The sermon closes with a call to stop settling for the minimum and instead let God pour out the abundance He intends.

Hook / Wordplay

The refrain 'too much' pivots across domains: the Tabernacle offerings were literally too much, God's love is never too much, and our fear, anger, and self-effort are always too much—turning a childhood memory of a housekeeper saying 'too much, too much' into the sermon's recurring lens.

2021-03-21

Vayikra: Visiting God's Place

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When accusation or guilt arises, don't argue with the adversary or wallow—immediately redirect attention to God and say 'Lord, if I'm guilty, show me, and let Your provision make a way for me.'
  • Stop rehearsing past offenses and injustices; dwelling on them keeps you living in the past instead of moving forward with a clean heart into what God is doing new.
  • Don't compartmentalize God's access to your life—the rooms you close off from Him are the ones He already sees; the only person fooled by the cover-up is you.
One-Line Action
When guilt surfaces, stop rationalizing and bring it honestly to God, who has already prepared the provision for your restoration.
Memorable Quote
And our calling is to declare Messiah to the Jewish Heart Essential Jersey and to share the truth of Yeshua through Jewish eyes.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi Rosenberg teaches from Parashat Vayikra, noting that after the tabernacle was completed in Exodus, Leviticus reveals its purpose: God wanted to dwell with His people. He frames the tabernacle as 'God's place' and asks what etiquette applies when visiting—using a humorous cat house-rules poster before turning serious. The repeated phrase 'they are guilty' throughout Leviticus 4-5 shows that even inadvertent sin must be acknowledged, not dismissed with 'I didn't know.' God as gracious host provides every provision for atonement, even scaled offerings for those who cannot afford a lamb.

Drawing from Isaiah 43-44, he warns that rationalizing sin leads to self-deception, like the man who worships the other half of the log he cooked dinner with. He shares a deeply personal Bible school moment where he felt God say 'I knew those things were there before I called you and I called you anyway,' calling it the most transformative moment of his life. Connecting to Romans 8, he emphasizes there is no condemnation in Messiah and that God's home is meant to be our home—we are not just visiting but living in God's place, seated in heavenly places.

Hook / Wordplay

'Visiting God's Place'—every house has its rules, and the Owner of this house makes the rules. But the twist is God doesn't want you to just visit; He says 'my home is your home.'

2021-03-27

Tzav: Secret Greatness of Shabbat Hagadol

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Examine whether I am more devoted to my doctrinal positions and being 'right' than to genuinely loving God — if doctrine outranks relationship, I am off balance and missing the mark.
  • When Yeshua overturns uncomfortable things in my life, recognize it as purification rather than destruction — He cleanses to restore, not to condemn.
  • Stop looking for the lowest common denominator of obedience and instead yield to the abundant flow of what God is doing, submitting with genuine heart rather than minimum compliance.
One-Line Action
Identify one area where doctrinal correctness or personal comfort has displaced genuine love for God, and surrender it this week.
Memorable Quote
If I'm more focused on my doctrinal position, than I am on him, I am missing the mark. I am off out of balance, not in the right focus.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi Rosenberg teaches on the Torah portion Tzav and the significance of Shabbat Hagadol, the Great Sabbath before Passover. He connects Yeshua's triumphal entry into Jerusalem with the Malachi 3 prophecy about the Lord suddenly coming to His temple to purify the sons of Levi. The temple cleansing was not an angry outburst but a prophetic fulfillment — the religious leaders recognized this, which is why they asked 'by what authority' rather than dismissing Him as disruptive.

The rabbi explains that the Malachi haftarah had just been read that very Shabbat, so the leaders understood exactly what Yeshua was enacting. He traces Yeshua's interactions through Matthew 21-23, showing how He silenced the Sadducees, answered the Pharisees' trap about the greatest commandment, and then posed His own unanswerable question about David calling Messiah 'Lord.' The 'secret greatness' of Shabbat Hagadol is that it pointed to Messiah coming to cleanse hypocrisy and restore genuine relationship with God. Yeshua recentered worship away from doctrinal power plays by declaring the greatest commandment is to love God wholeheartedly.

Rabbi Rosenberg shares a personal story of studying with an Orthodox rabbi who recognized the authority of Messiah's Torah clarification without realizing its source. The sermon concludes that true greatness comes from humbling ourselves before God rather than elevating our own positions, allowing Yeshua to overturn whatever corrupts our worship.

Hook / Wordplay

The 'secret greatness' of Shabbat Hagadol is not merely that the Sabbath is great — it is that this specific Shabbat unveiled the greatest purifier, Messiah himself, who came to overturn the tables of hypocrisy so God's love could flow unobstructed.

2021-04-04

Pesach Day 7: The Emerging Of A Resurrected People

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Resurrection power is not only future hope—invite God daily into the dry, deadened areas of life where spiritual vitality has been lost, because He specializes in restoring what is 'very dry.'
  • Being a 'resurrected person' means others should see God's life modeled in you; if they cannot, go back to the drawing board and ask God what is missing rather than assuming past commitment is enough.
  • The Omer counting models intentional anticipation—don't rest on the deliverance already experienced but actively move toward the next outpouring of the Spirit by seeking God's face daily.
One-Line Action
Identify one 'dry bones' area in your spiritual life this week and specifically invite God's breath to bring resurrection life there.
Memorable Quote
You know what real estate he really wants? He wants our hearts.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The seventh day of Pesach coincides with synagogue readings about resurrection—Ezekiel 37's dry bones and the messianic passages of Isaiah 11–12—and Rabbi Rosenberg shows why resurrection is woven into the entire Passover week. He frames Israel's deliverance from Egypt as a national resurrection: a slave people reborn into covenant nationhood. The Torah portion Shemini (Leviticus 9–10) illustrates both God's glory appearing and the severe consequences when Nadab and Abihu brought unauthorized fire, underscoring the seriousness of representing God faithfully from the start.

The commonly misquoted 'lion lying down with the lamb' is corrected to Isaiah 11's actual text—wolf with lamb—and connected to Revelation 5, where the expected Lion of Judah appears instead as a slaughtered Lamb, the only one worthy to open the scroll. Yeshua as the first fruits of resurrection opens the way for those dead in sin to experience resurrection power now, not only in the afterlife. The counting of the Omer points forward from Passover deliverance toward the outpouring of the Spirit at Shavuot, modeling intentional spiritual anticipation.

Rabbi Rosenberg uses a Hasidic story of a rabbi leaping across a pit during the Holocaust—holding the hands of his ancestors—to illustrate faith that transcends impossible circumstances. He urges the congregation not to settle for spiritual dryness, reminding them that God specializes in restoring what is very dry when He speaks His creative word of power. The sermon closes with a call to emerge as resurrected people who demonstrate God's presence and love to a world in desperate need.

Hook / Wordplay

People say 'the lion will lie down with the lamb'—but Isaiah 11 actually says 'the wolf will live with the lamb.' And in Revelation 5, the Lion of Judah appears not as a lion but as a slaughtered Lamb.

2021-04-11

Jeannie's Family Story - Part 2: On The Move Again

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Spoken blessings carry real power across generations — Jeannie's grandmother sought only a rabbi's blessing rather than honor, and her mother invoked it after every miracle, reminding us that life and death are in the power of the tongue.
  • Choosing family unity over self-preservation can be the path to collective deliverance — the father's 'either everybody goes or nobody goes' and the family's refusal to separate even when it meant years more in DP camps models radical commitment to togetherness.
  • Faith means moving forward without full knowledge — like Abraham, Jeannie's family kept going not knowing where they were headed but trusting that God would make a way where there seemed to be none, and the testimony shows that trust was rewarded repeatedly.
One-Line Action
Speak intentional blessings over your family this week, knowing that words of faith carry forward across generations.
Memorable Quote
We're so excited to have part two with Jeannie here with us.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

This service centers on Part 2 of Jeannie Parn's Wexler's Holocaust family story, with Marlene introducing her and the Rabbi providing biblical framing. Jeannie recounts her family's flight deeper into Russia, near-starvation in Uzbekistan where her father killed a bird to feed his dying children, and her mother's resolve that they must escape or perish. She describes the perilous return journey, bribing border guards with American cigarettes, and her father's insistence that all 22 refugees cross together or none at all.

Life in multiple DP camps followed — schools, communal bathrooms, her mother's recurring lung illness that delayed immigration for five years. The family finally reached Canada through sponsorship, and Jeannie shares the wrenching moment at Halifax when officials balked at their large family and her mother demanded to know which child Hitler should have killed.

The Rabbi ties the testimony to Hebrews 10-12, drawing a direct parallel between the family's journey and Abraham going out not knowing where he was going but trusting God. He highlights the grandmother's blessing from her rabbi — invoked by Jeannie's mother after every miracle — as evidence of the power of spoken blessing and generational faith that carried the family through.

Hook / Wordplay

De-location and relocation — always on the move again, like Abraham who went out without knowing where he was going but knew who he was going with.

2021-04-18

A Nation Born in a Day

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Stop rehearsing past failures like pulling at a scab; take what you've learned and press forward, trusting that God is doing something new even when you can't yet see it (Isaiah 43:18-19).
  • When God restores something in your life, resist the impulse to claim credit — remain in the humility that attracted His favor in the first place, a 'poor and humble spirit who trembles at His word.'
  • We are not called to argue people into faith; live as a disciple whose love is so visibly different that others say 'I want in' — transformation of hearts comes through God's hand extended through how we live, not through debate.
One-Line Action
Identify one area of your life you've written off as too desolate, bring it to God in humility, and ask Him to make rivers in that wasteland.
Memorable Quote
You may think it's all over. You may think it's too desolate.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon celebrates Yom Ha'atzmaut by exploring Isaiah 66:8's question — can a nation be born in a day? — through the lens of Israel's 1948 independence. After a video documenting ten fulfilled prophecies (ingathering from 100+ nations, land restoration, Hebrew revival, technological and agricultural achievements), the rabbi grounds the message in Isaiah 66:1-2, emphasizing that God looks not for greatness but for a poor and humble spirit that trembles at His word.

He traces the prophetic thread from Isaiah 43:18-19 (stop dwelling on the past; God is doing something new) through Ezekiel 36:24-28 (physical return leading to spiritual renewal with a new heart and new spirit) and into the New Covenant where Yeshua Himself prophesied both Jerusalem's destruction and its restoration. The rabbi draws a sustained parallel between Israel's national desolation-to-restoration arc and the believer's personal journey, arguing that the same God who turned Mark Twain's barren wasteland into a flourishing nation can breathe life into any desolate situation.

He highlights Israel's contributions — GPS, medical devices, disaster relief — to counter BDS rhetoric, but pivots to declare Yeshua as the greatest gift God brought through the Jewish people. The sermon closes with a call to pursue personal 'independence day' — yielding to God in humility so He can liberate and restore, living with love so evident that others are drawn in.

Hook / Wordplay

Israel's Independence Day becomes your independence day — the same God who birthed a nation in a day can liberate your soul in a moment.

Life Is In The Blood

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Aaron's sons show that treating atonement casually at the outset corrupts everything downstream—take the sacrifice seriously because it shapes how you value what God accomplished through Messiah.
  • 'Love your neighbor as yourself' is not passive tolerance; it includes not slandering, not standing idle when someone is threatened, rebuking frankly without grudges, and practicing equal justice regardless of status.
  • Evaluate your spiritual walk by what you spend your time talking about—if accusation outweighs restoration, you are following the pattern of the accuser rather than Messiah.
One-Line Action
This week, identify one harbored grudge or unspoken grievance and address it frankly with the person, so you stop carrying their sin and open the door to genuine fellowship.
Memorable Quote
I cannot tell by looking a vile of your blood what race you are. I can't tell anything if you're tall, short, fat, thin.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi Rosenberg teaches from the double Torah portion Acharei Mot-Kedoshim, anchoring the sermon on Leviticus 17:11—the life of the creature is in the blood. He begins with Aaron's sons, whose casual treatment of the sacrificial system cost their lives because compromising the atonement at its inauguration would have undermined the type and shadow of Messiah's future sacrifice. He explains that blood in Torah carried sacred significance as the means of atonement and as a marker of covenantal alliance, which is why God forbade consuming it or offering it to false gods.

He then pivots to the biological reality that blood is racially indistinguishable, arguing from Acts 17:26 that humanity is one race and one blood. The 'love your neighbor as yourself' command in Leviticus 19 is unpacked in its full context: do not slander, do not stand idle when a neighbor's life is at stake, rebuke frankly rather than harboring grudges, and practice equal justice without favoritism.

He connects this to Galatians 5, contrasting the self-focused deeds of the flesh with the relationally-oriented fruit of the spirit, noting that every fruit—love, patience, kindness—requires interaction with others. Closing with 1 John 1, he challenges listeners that walking in the light produces genuine fellowship, while acknowledging sin—rather than denying it—opens the door to purification through Messiah's blood.

Hook / Wordplay

'Life is in the blood' operates on four levels: biologically blood sustains the body, covenantally it atones for sin, relationally it unites all humanity as one race, and messianically Yeshua's shed blood purchases eternal life.

2021-04-30

Rabbi Jan & Marlene on The Hart of NY TV

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Dramatic spiritual experiences cannot sustain faith long-term; daily grounding in Scripture is what keeps us—build the habit of returning to the Word as foundation, not chasing emotional highs.
  • When challenged on belief, rather than getting defensive, redirect: 'Have you ever studied it yourself?' Genuine inquiry disarms dismissiveness more effectively than argument.
  • Transformation is a long-term process measured in years of daily devotional life—Marlene didn't recognize herself after one year in the Word; consistency compounds.
One-Line Action
Ground your faith in daily Scripture study rather than relying on past spiritual experiences to sustain you.
Memorable Quote
It cost me something. It cost me family.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi Jan and Marlene share their testimonies on Peter Hart's TV show. Rabbi Jan recounts a near-death experience at age three and a half that sparked lifelong God-awareness, leading through Orthodox Judaism to a dramatic power encounter with God in a Black Pentecostal church in North Philadelphia. He describes the family fallout—being sent to rabbis, psychiatrists, and psychologists—and how returning to his bar mitzvah Bible anchored his faith in Scripture rather than experience alone.

He addresses the common objection that one cannot be Jewish and believe in Yeshua, flipping the 'brainwashed' accusation back on critics who never studied the question themselves. Marlene shares how losing her mother at age eleven set her on a spiritual search, how her sister's Jehovah's Witness witnessing introduced Isaiah 53, and how reading John 1:1 in a college course crystallized her conviction that Yeshua is the Jewish Messiah. She describes being led to a Messianic synagogue two hours away where she finally accepted him, followed by 47 years of transformation through daily devotional life.

The episode also highlights Rabbi Jan's music ministry spanning ten albums of prophetic devotional worship. Two original songs, 'Carry On' and 'Save Us All Our Lord,' close the broadcast.

Hook / Wordplay

The 'brainwashed' reversal: Rabbi Jan flips the accusation—'It cost me family, it cost me friends. Cost you nothing to criticize… who's brainwashed?'
