2021-05-02

Emor: Be Holy, No Matter Where You Live

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Holiness is not something I manufacture — it flows from yielding to God, and the more I grow spiritually the more natural and less performative my faith should become.
  • When I see someone struggling or failing, my instinct should be restoration (Galatians 6:1), not judgment or exposure — that restorative heart is the mark of genuine spiritual maturity.
  • I should stop letting my current circumstances define what God can do through me; short-sightedness is overcome by looking to the One who knows the end from the beginning.
One-Line Action
Yield daily to God's transforming presence rather than trying to manufacture holiness through self-effort or religious performance.
Memorable Quote
We don't see what we could be because we limit God. We need to expand and open up our hearts to him so that we could see God do exploits in our lives.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Built on the Torah portion Emor, the sermon centers on the phrase 'no matter where you live' from Leviticus 23:21, teaching that holiness is not a denomination or self-generated effort but something God requires and provides. Rabbi connects the high priestly standards for the Kohanim to every believer's calling, stressing that God Himself makes us holy through union with Messiah. He frames the counting of the Omer as an incubation period anticipating spiritual harvest, linking the first fruits offering to Yeshua's resurrection and the outpouring of the Holy Spirit at Shavuot.

Drawing from Ezekiel 44's sons of Zadok and 1 Chronicles 12's sons of Issachar, he highlights the importance of faithfully representing God and understanding the times. Through Romans 6 he contrasts slavery to sin with slavery to righteousness, arguing that yielding to God naturally produces holiness rather than a religious facade.

He quotes his Bible school professor that 'the more spiritual you become, the more natural you become,' urging believers not to project false holiness but to let God's transforming presence become a natural expression of relationship. He closes by warning against the short-sightedness that limits what God can do, calling the congregation to expand their hearts so God can do exploits through them.

Hook / Wordplay

'No matter where you live' — pulled from Leviticus 23:21 as a permanent regulation, then paired with a quote about humanity's biggest defect being short-sightedness: we can't see what we could be, but God sees beyond our circumstances.

2021-05-09

Yerushalayim—Ascending & Descending

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Being a 'watchman' means taking responsibility to speak and act when I see spiritual need—passive observation and merely documenting problems without intervention makes me complicit, not faithful.
  • Spiritual disciplines like prayer cannot be reduced to annual events; the day after the National Day of Prayer matters as much as the day itself, because moment-by-moment yielding to God is the actual standard.
  • 'Stay close to the floor'—pursue humility not as a posture to display but as a daily orientation that prevents pride from building, keeping the focus on God's work rather than personal accolades.
One-Line Action
Identify one situation where I have been an observer instead of a watchman and take a concrete step to speak or act this week.
Memorable Quote
I say, stay close to the floor. Because if you fall, it's not going to hurt so bad.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Delivered on Yom Yerushalayim, the sermon explores the biblical motif of ascending and descending through the recurring pattern of the number 40. Rabbi draws a threefold parallel between Moses' 40 days on Sinai receiving Torah, Yeshua's 40 days of wilderness testing, and Yeshua's 40 post-resurrection days imparting kingdom understanding to His disciples before ascending. He connects the Year of Jubilee's restoration principle to God's heart for all people, emphasizing that the land's value lay in its yield rather than mere accumulation.

The ascension in Acts 1 becomes the sermon's pivot: Yeshua ascended and promised the Spirit would descend, empowering believers as witnesses from Jerusalem to the ends of the earth. The rabbi sharply contrasts watchmen who take action with modern observers who merely film crises on their phones, challenging the congregation to accept prophetic responsibility. He ties 2 Chronicles 7:14 to daily, moment-by-moment obedience rather than treating prayer as an annual event.

The descending New Jerusalem in Revelation is reframed as the bride of Messiah—believers collectively functioning in their God-given calling. The sermon concludes that we must descend in humility before God so His Spirit can cause us to ascend into the fullness of kingdom authority, reflecting Yeshua's presence to the world.

Hook / Wordplay

Yeshua ascended so the Spirit could descend; we descend in humility so we can ascend in His power—the kingdom moves in both directions at once.

2021-05-16

Bamidbar & Shavuot: Count Me In!

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Being 'counted in' demands ongoing obedience, not just initial affirmation—Israel declared 'we'll do whatever God says' yet built the golden calf before Moses descended; check where my own follow-through has stalled.
  • The Pentecost outpouring happened in the Temple during the Shavuot morning service while Ezekiel 1 was being read aloud—God works through appointed times and gatherings, so show up expectant at His appointed moments rather than waiting for a private mystical experience.
  • When facing opposition or hatred, respond with love rather than retaliation—like Wilkerson telling Cruz 'every piece will say God loves you'—because fear-based power collapses when met with Spirit-led courage.
One-Line Action
Identify one area where you have been passively agreeing with God but not actively obeying, and take a concrete step of obedience this week.
Memorable Quote
Bless me his name, whose glorious kingdom is forever a man.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The rabbi connects the Torah portion Bamidbar, which opens with a census of Israel's fighting men, to the Shavuot theme of being 'counted in' to God's purposes. He explains that the omer counting from Passover to Shavuot was a countdown toward harvest and empowerment, not merely a numerical exercise. He corrects the widespread misconception that the Pentecost outpouring occurred in the upper room, arguing from Luke 24:53 that the disciples spent their time in the Temple courts, where the Ezekiel 1 haftarah—describing fire, rushing wind, and wheels within wheels—was being read when the Spirit fell.

He draws vivid parallels between the fire on Mount Sinai, the Ezekiel vision, and the Acts 2 manifestation to show God's consistent pattern of dramatic self-revelation at appointed times. Using John 15, he teaches that love, unity, and fruit-bearing—not mere verbal agreement—prove genuine discipleship.

He illustrates fearless obedience through the David Wilkerson and Nicky Cruz story, where Spirit-led courage broke the power of intimidation. He connects contemporary events in Israel to God's sovereign protection and calls the congregation to yield to the Holy Spirit, pray for Israel and Jerusalem, and actively participate as laborers in God's harvest.

Hook / Wordplay

'Count me in' plays on three layers: the census counting in Bamidbar, the omer counting toward Shavuot, and the personal commitment to be numbered among God's obedient people rather than mere spectators.

2021-05-30

B'ha'alotkha: the Ultimate Set Up

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Monitor the 'leaking effect' of complaint: when I hear others grumble or catch myself doing it, recognize it as the first domino in a cascade that erodes unity and displaces spiritual focus, just as it moved from the mixed multitude to Israel to Moses to Miriam.
  • When fighting injustice or adversarial pressures, regularly check that the fight hasn't displaced my first love—the relationship with God that made the fight meaningful in the first place, as happened to the Ephesian congregation.
  • Practice spiritual 'smell checks'—step back to honestly assess whether I've grown numb to my own condition the way we grow nose-blind to a bad scent, because awareness is the prerequisite for God's restorative work.
One-Line Action
This week, catch the first complaint before it cascades—pause, redirect it to prayer, and re-anchor in your first love.
Memorable Quote
Knowing Him makes all the difference. Knowing every detail doesn't help a whole lot because we could never know enough, but knowing Him is enough.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The Torah portion B'ha'alotkha opens with the menorah's seven lamps casting light forward, which Rabbi Rosenberg frames as God's perpetual forward motion toward His purpose. He traces a cascading pattern of complaint through Numbers 11–12: the mixed multitude craves easier living, Israel demands meat instead of manna, Moses buckles under the burden and begs God to kill him, and Miriam and Aaron criticize Moses over his Ethiopian wife—really a power struggle over authority. Each episode erodes the unity the menorah represents, just as leprosy physically dismembers the body—connecting lashon hara to the disintegration of community.

Drawing from Zechariah 3, the rabbi contrasts Joshua the high priest standing in dung-covered garments, 'smell-deaf' to his own condition, with the risen Yeshua who overcame through sacrifice and offers restoration. In Revelation 2, the Ephesian congregation is praised for perseverance against wickedness but warned their menorah will be removed if they neglect their first love. The central wordplay is 'set apart versus set aside': God will set our candlestick aside if we fail to address what sets us apart.

Yet God's 'ultimate setup' is not destruction but restoration—trading death-ridden lives for wholeness, snatching the disheveled priest from the fire. The sermon closes with a call to stop selling out for 'melons and garlic' and instead let collective light shine forward into a darkened world.

Hook / Wordplay

Set apart vs. set aside: God will set your candlestick aside if you neglect what sets you apart—but His ultimate setup is restoration, not cancellation.

2021-06-06

Sh'lakh Lekha: A Discovery Tour

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When facing daunting obstacles, recall God's track record rather than cataloging the size of the 'giants' — the Canaanites were terrified for 40 years while Israel sat paralyzed by their own fear.
  • Words of discouragement can devastate an entire community; before reporting challenges to others, filter through faith so the report builds courage rather than spreading despair.
  • Discovery of God's Word must move beyond note-taking to internalization and outward action — share it as a discovery experience with others by asking questions and opening dialogue, not just dispensing information.
One-Line Action
This week, when you encounter a fear-driven narrative, counter it by sharing a specific testimony of God's faithfulness with someone who needs encouragement.
Memorable Quote
You can never turn off the dark, but you can turn on the light. And God makes available to us something that transcends all of the powers of darkness.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The rabbi teaches on Sh'lakh Lekha (Numbers 13), reframing the spy mission as a 'discovery tour' by connecting the Hebrew word latour to the concept of touring and exploration. The 12 tribal leaders were sent not for military reconnaissance but to witness the abundance God had prepared, yet they returned with a fear-driven report about giants and fortified cities that plunged the entire nation into despair. He contrasts this with Joshua's later approach of sending only two unnamed spies to Jericho, who discovered the Canaanites had been terrified of Israel for all 40 wilderness years.

Drawing on Zechariah 3-4, he shows how God transforms the dung-covered high priest Joshua into a vessel of illumination, illustrating that God lifts people from depression into purpose. Through Hebrews 3, he warns against hardening hearts through bitter quarreling and losing trust in God's promises. He applies the pattern to modern fear-based cultural narratives, arguing that the same spirit of discouragement that defeated Israel operates through media, politics, and ideological movements today.

From Philippians 2, he calls believers to humility and unity rather than rivalry, urging them to shine like stars in a twisted generation. The sermon closes with a charge to bring discovery of God's Word to others through encouragement rather than accusation.

Hook / Wordplay

The Hebrew word 'latour' (to spy/survey) connects to 'tour' — Israel's Ministry of Tourism even uses the spies-carrying-grapes image as its logo. God's mission was a discovery tour of abundance, not a military threat assessment.

2021-06-13

Korach: How Does Smart Become So Dumb?

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When blessings or authority come, check whether they are lodging in your heart producing humility or going to your head producing entitlement—the difference determines whether you stay wise or drift toward foolishness.
  • Like Moses falling on his face for his accusers, intercede for those who oppose you rather than defending yourself—Yeshua's command to pray for enemies was modeled centuries earlier in the wilderness.
  • Do not give God only the room at the end of the hall—give Him access to every area of life so His Spirit can course-correct before small drift becomes full rebellion.
One-Line Action
Identify one area of life you have kept under your own control and consciously surrender it to God this week.
Memorable Quote
Part of it is having received a blessing, and then letting the blessing go to your head instead of finding lodging in our hearts.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon examines the Torah portion Korach, tracing a downward progression of rebellion in Israel from the mixed multitude's complaints through the spies' evil report to Korach's direct challenge of Moses and Aaron's authority. Rabbi Rosenberg highlights the irony that Korach and his 250 accomplices were leaders of reputation who had witnessed God's miraculous power firsthand, yet let authority go to their heads instead of their hearts. After God dramatically judged the rebels by opening the earth, the very next day the entire community complained against Moses and Aaron again, illustrating how quickly smart becomes dumb.

The rabbi reinterprets the placement of the tzitzit commandment just before Korach's rebellion: the issue was not the wrong color blue on the fringes but Korach's heart following its own eyes, exactly what the tzitzit were meant to prevent. He connects this pattern to Romans 1:21-28 where Paul describes people who knew God yet became futile in their thinking, exchanging divine truth for their own constructs. Paul's rebuke of the Galatians for starting in the Spirit but trying to be perfected by their own efforts extends the same theme.

Moses modeled the right response throughout, falling on his face to intercede for those who opposed him, prefiguring Yeshua's teaching to pray for enemies. The sermon argues the real question is not how wisdom becomes foolishness but how to prevent the slide: by drawing near to God daily, staying in His Word, and giving Yeshua full access to every area of life rather than relegating Him to the room at the end of the hall.

Hook / Wordplay

How does smart become so dumb? People who saw God split the earth to judge rebels turned around the very next day and complained against the same leaders God had just vindicated.

2021-06-20

A Rock of Offense

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When anger rises, pause before acting—Moses' single moment of rage cost him the Promised Land; acting out of anger in our own strength makes us lose sight of what God is trying to accomplish through us.
  • The pattern of the rejected stone becoming the cornerstone (Jephthah, Yeshua) means rejection is not final—trust God's process of turning exclusion into a foundation for deliverance.
  • Stay 'in union with' Messiah as the cornerstone rather than taking matters into your own hands; the repeated Ephesians phrase 'in union with him' is the antidote to the rash self-reliance that tripped up Moses.
One-Line Action
Before reacting in anger this week, pause and speak to the Rock instead of striking at it.
Memorable Quote
We're so glad you're here today and for all those who are watching on the broadcast as well.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

From Torah portion Hukkat, Rabbi focuses on Numbers 20 where Israel complains about having no water in the wilderness and God instructs Moses to speak to the rock. Instead, Moses delivers a short angry speech calling the people rebels and strikes the rock twice with his staff, costing him entry to the Promised Land for failing to honor God before the people. Rabbi connects this to Judges 11, where Jephthah was rejected by his half-brothers but later became Israel's military chief and deliverer—the rejected one becoming the leader.

Yeshua's parable of the vineyard tenants in Matthew 21 extends the pattern further: greedy tenants reject and kill the owner's son, yet the rejected stone becomes the chief cornerstone. The sermon warns that acting out of anger, even briefly, can cause us to dishonor God and disrupt His purposes, exactly as Moses did in one fatal moment. Drawing from Ephesians 2:13-22, Rabbi teaches that Yeshua the rejected cornerstone holds the whole building together, uniting all believers into one spiritual dwelling place.

A contemporary application connects the biblical pattern to modern cancel culture, urging listeners toward restoration rather than cancellation. The sermon closes with an appeal to remain in union with Messiah as the foundation stone rather than letting emotions drive us to strike at the very source of our deliverance.

Hook / Wordplay

Moses made the rock that symbolized Messiah into his personal rock of offense by striking it in anger—dishonoring the very cornerstone of salvation. 'Cancel not that you be not canceled—God cancels, then He restores.'

2021-06-27

The Loss of Reason

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Check your motives before pursuing what you claim God has endorsed — Balaam was doctrinally correct but morally compromised, finding a 'back door' around obedience.
  • Make prayer and Scripture a first resort rather than a last resort; the remedy for lost reason is not more argument but renewed minds yielded to God.
  • Resist the trap of spending more energy diagnosing evil than producing good news — 'if you spend more time regurgitating bad news, all you're presenting is bad news.'
One-Line Action
Audit one area where personal desire may be clouding obedience, and bring it before God in prayer before acting further.
Memorable Quote
We cannot combat evil by just pointing out evil. We have got to be in a position where we are bringing forth the good news.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon opens with the Balak/Balaam narrative from Numbers 22-25, using the subtitle 'blinded by poor thinking' as a wordplay on Pe'or, the site of Israel's tragedy. Rabbi traces how Balaam's greed caused him to lose the ability to reason — he argued with his talking donkey without recognizing the absurdity, and ultimately counseled Balak to seduce Israel through Midianite women, leading to idolatry and a plague killing 24,000. He connects this to 2 Peter 2 and Jude, which warn of false teachers who promise freedom while being slaves of corruption, 'mouthing grandiocities of nothingness.' The sermon then draws parallels to modern cultural decline, citing quotes from John Adams, Marx, Stalin, and Lenin about undermining morality, family, and faith as pillars of society.

He references Matthew 21's parable of the wicked tenants as another example of greed-induced delusion — they thought killing the heir would gain them the inheritance. The message pivots to Micah 6:8 and Isaiah 1:18, arguing that believers must combat darkness not by pointing out evil but by bringing good news and loving one another. He closes with 2 Chronicles 7:14 as the prescription: humble prayer, seeking God's face, and turning from wickedness, citing the Jesus Movement of the 1960s-70s as proof that God can birth national revival from small, faithful remnants.

Hook / Wordplay

'Blinded by poor thinking' — a double meaning where 'poor' references both deficient reasoning and Pe'or (Baal Peor), the place where Israel's blindness led to catastrophic sin.

Pinchas: What Are The Odds?

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When tempted to go silent under opposition, remember Jeremiah's fire-in-the-bones — God's word is meant to be spoken forth, not suppressed out of discouragement.
  • Audit how much time I spend rehearsing bad news versus declaring the good news God has given; redirect that energy toward hope and restoration.
  • Trusting God is never a gamble — approach uncertainty not with anxiety over odds but with confidence in His sovereign purpose and presence.
One-Line Action
Stop calculating worldly odds and speak God's word boldly, trusting that His presence makes you the majority.
Memorable Quote
You see with trusting God it's never a gamble.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi opens the Pinchas portion briefly but pivots to the Haftorah in Jeremiah 1, using the concept of odds as his central frame. He contrasts lottery odds (1 in 300 million) with the probability of being born (1 in 400 quattrillion) to argue that our very existence reflects God's intentional design, not randomness. Jeremiah 1:5 anchors the message: God knew us before the womb and appointed us with purpose.

The rabbi traces Jeremiah's journey from calling through deep discouragement in chapter 20, where the prophet wanted to quit speaking but found God's word was like fire imprisoned in his bones. He draws parallels to Paul's perseverance through suffering, Jonah and Nineveh's surprise repentance, and the Emmaus road encounter to show that God consistently overturns unfavorable odds. A personal testimony about his own unlikely conversion in a Black Pentecostal church reinforces that God surprises even those praying for the impossible.

The sermon calls believers to stop rehearsing bad news and instead carry the good news boldly, loving enemies and standing strong under opposition. He closes with Psalm 139's invitation for God to search our hearts, urging listeners that trusting God is never a gamble but always a sure foundation in Messiah.

Hook / Wordplay

"What are the odds?" — lottery odds are 1 in 300 million, but being born is 1 in 400 quattrillion; with God, the odds are always in our favor.

2021-07-11

An Incorruptible Inheritance

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Don't let present circumstances ('it's livestock country and we have livestock') dictate what God's best provision is—press into the full promise rather than settling for what's convenient.
  • The value of the inheritance is rooted in the One who promised it, not in a checklist of blessings; prioritize knowing Him over cataloging what He gives.
  • Stay connected to community—Reuben and Gad did the right thing initially by helping settle their brothers, but their eventual isolation made them the first tribes lost.
One-Line Action
Spend daily time in God's Word to internalize the value of your inheritance in Messiah rather than settling for lesser, more immediate substitutes.
Memorable Quote
The value is not always in the inheritance. The value is in the one who promised the inheritance.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The rabbi connects the double Torah portion Matot/Masei to the theme of an incorruptible inheritance. He recaps how the tribe of Shimon plummeted from the third largest to the smallest after 24,000 died in a plague triggered by Zimri's sin—right before the census that would determine each tribe's land allotment. This illustrates how a moment of instant gratification can forfeit generational inheritance.

He then examines how Reuben, Gad, and half of Manasseh nearly repeated this pattern by wanting to settle east of the Jordan because it was 'livestock country,' choosing what looked convenient rather than pressing into the Promised Land. Moses warned that such a choice would discourage the entire nation, echoing the earlier failure of the ten spies. Pivoting to 1 Peter 1:3-4, the rabbi describes an inheritance that 'cannot decay, spoil, or fade' and argues the key problem is not ignorance of the inheritance but failure to appreciate its value.

He draws from Ephesians 1:7-13 to emphasize that this inheritance is held 'in union with' Messiah and sealed by the Holy Spirit. The practical call is to stay in God's Word and in community to remain 'fully vested' in what God has provided rather than trading it for lesser substitutes. He closes by noting that Reuben and Gad's eventual isolation from the other tribes led to their being the first lost—a warning against spiritual separation.

Hook / Wordplay

Uses a financial 'fully vested' metaphor: just as profit-sharing requires staying employed to maturity, staying in union with Messiah is how we become fully vested in our spiritual inheritance.

2021-07-18

Words That Demand Action

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When discouraging voices dominate—whether from others or from circumstances—filter them against God's promises rather than absorbing the fear; the spies' negative report was powerful only because the people chose to receive it over God's word.
  • Agreeing with God's word is not the same as obeying it; the Israelites repeatedly said 'we will do' but acted differently, so examine where verbal assent has substituted for actual follow-through.
  • When conviction comes, resist deflecting blame onto others—finding a scapegoat is never a solution; instead, humble yourself before God and ask what specific action His word is demanding of you right now.
One-Line Action
Identify one specific command from God's word you have been agreeing with but not acting on, and take the concrete step this week.
Memorable Quote
God's word is not simply to say, yes, I agree. Amen and so be it.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon connects Parashat Devarim with the eve of Tisha B'Av, building on the Hebrew meaning of 'devarim' (words) to argue that God's words carry authority and demand concrete action. Rabbi recounts how the spies' evil report on Tisha B'Av discouraged Israel from entering the land, triggering forty years of wilderness wandering—the first in a chain of catastrophes tied to that date including both Temples' destruction, expulsion from England and Spain, and the onset of World War I. He turns to the Haftarah in Isaiah 1, where God rejects hollow worship yet pivots to the invitation 'let us reason together,' showing that judgment always carries a doorway to restoration.

Drawing from John 15, he teaches that abiding in Messiah means letting God's word lodge so deeply that it bears fruit through action, not mere verbal agreement. From Hebrews 3-4 he warns against hardening hearts through blame-shifting and self-reliance.

He illustrates with Nineveh's repentance—a pagan city with no scripture that nonetheless turned and received mercy. He closes with Zechariah's promise that the fast days of mourning will become festivals of joy, and calls the congregation to yoke themselves with Messiah so His Spirit produces the obedience their own strength cannot.

Hook / Wordplay

'Devarim' means 'words'—but in Torah, words are never passive information; they carry authority that demands a response, and the gap between hearing and doing is where every calamity in this portion began.

2021-07-25

Va'etchanan — Reflections That Bring Comfort

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When God exposes my shortcomings it is not condemnation but an invitation — He knew those things before He called me; my job is to let Him address them, not spiral into shame.
  • Stop trying to do what God said He will do; focus on the obedience He asked of me and let Him handle the outcomes He promised.
  • Use this reflective season to honestly ask Psalm 139's question — 'Search me and know my heart' — specifically targeting the areas I think I already have under control, since those are where God most often challenges us.
One-Line Action
Each morning this season, ask God to reveal one blind spot in an area you consider a strength, and yield that area to Him before acting on it.
Memorable Quote
You don't try to do what God said he will do. You let God do what he's going to do by doing the things he told us to do.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon opens the seven-week Haftarah cycle from Isaiah that leads to Rosh Hashanah, framing this season as a time of reflective comfort after Tisha B'Av. Rabbi draws a detailed parallel between Israel's 40 years in the wilderness and Yeshua's 40-day temptation, showing that every Scripture Yeshua quoted against the adversary came from Deuteronomy — the very portions being read in synagogues during that liturgical period. He teaches that true comfort comes not from circumstances but from allowing God's Word to take deep root, becoming 'flesh in us.' A vulnerable personal story from Bible school anchors the message: feeling unworthy, he told God to give up on him, and heard clearly, 'I knew those things were there before I called you — and I called you anyway.' He warns against the paradox of becoming proud of one's humility, and notes that God tends to challenge what we think are our strong points rather than our known weaknesses.

Using Paul's willingness to count his impressive credentials as refuse and Joseph's unjust hardships as unknowing preparation, he urges listeners to yield their abilities to God rather than trying to accomplish what God said He would do. The call is to enter this reflective season with honest self-examination — not self-condemnation — letting God search and prepare hearts for what He wants to do next.

Hook / Wordplay

Isaiah 40's valleys filled and mountains lowered become God's 'biblical infrastructure' project — potholes, highways, leveling — but the construction site is inside our own hearts.

2021-08-08

What You See Is What You Get

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When facing uncertainty, check whether I am 'counseling with the adversary' — rehearsing worst-case narratives — instead of going to God for direction, since pessimism becomes a self-fulfilling prophecy.
  • The pool of Bethesda story illustrates how fixating on one avenue of solution can blind me to the answer standing right in front of me; broaden spiritual perception before concluding something is impossible.
  • During the Elul concept of 'the king is in the field,' actively look for divine appointments — even a simple hello — to speak comfort into someone's life this week.
One-Line Action
This week, catch yourself mid-pessimism and consciously choose to see the blessing God has set before you instead of defaulting to the curse.
Memorable Quote
If we see through the eyes of the Lord, if we see through Jewish eyes, if we see through Messiah's eyes, we will see opportunities that God lays out before us.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Opening from Deuteronomy 11:26, the rabbi frames Parashat Re'eh as God presenting Israel with a fundamental choice between blessing and curse, emphasizing that what we choose to see shapes what we receive. He warns that pessimism and fear become self-fulfilling prophecies, citing the pattern of people who receive a blessing but immediately expect it to fail. Drawing from Deuteronomy 13, he addresses false prophets who perform signs and wonders yet lead people astray, calling this a 'bait and switch' and connecting it to his own post-conversion crisis when rabbis challenged his faith in Yeshua.

He links this to 1 John 4's command to test the spirits and warns against the spirit of anti-Messiah that operates not as one figure but as a pervasive force throughout history. Transitioning to the Elul season theme of 'the king is in the field,' he teaches that Yeshua came out to meet us where we are, just as the king leaves his palace to be accessible. The climactic illustration is the healing at the pool of Bethesda in John 5, where a man's fixation on one narrow path to healing — getting into the stirred water first — blinded him to the healer standing directly before him.

The rabbi draws the parallel that our skewed perception keeps us stuck, and Yeshua's instruction to 'see, you are well' retrains spiritual sight. He closes with John 5:19, where Yeshua says the Son does only what he sees the Father doing, challenging listeners to align their vision with God's rather than pursuing their own plans and hoping for blessing.

Hook / Wordplay

The Torah portion Re'eh means 'See' — Rabbi flips the cliché 'what you see is what you get' into a spiritual challenge: what you get depends on what you choose to see.

2021-08-15

Shoftim: When The King is in the Field

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Accessibility is the core of servant leadership — come out of personal comfort zones and doctrinal towers to identify with people where they actually are, not where you think they should be.
  • During Elul, treat the season as an active invitation to fast, pray, and go into the field to seek out people who feel unseen or disconnected, rather than waiting for them to come to you.
  • The miraculous was the byproduct, not the method — Yeshua's real work was mingling, listening, and being present; genuine presence among people is what opens the door for God to meet needs through us.
One-Line Action
This week, step out of your routine to be genuinely present with someone who feels unseen — listen first, then let God show you how to meet their need.
Memorable Quote
When we are accessible to the people, we can begin to see not what everyone is doing wrong, but how we can restore such a one, how we can be there available.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon connects Parashat Shoftim with the Elul tradition that the King is in the field, asking what it means for a king to leave his palace and become accessible. The rabbi contrasts insulated leaders — David staying home and falling with Bathsheba, Marie Antoinette disconnected from her starving people — with the biblical requirements for a king to hand-copy Torah and read it daily as a boundary marker. He traces how Yeshua fulfilled the Deuteronomy 18 prophecy of a prophet like Moses by entering the world and laying aside royalty to dwell among the people.

Walking through Matthew 9, he highlights encounter after encounter: healing a paralytic, calling Matthew the tax collector, touching the hemorrhaging woman, restoring sight to blind men — each showing the King accessible in the field. The Pharisees, insulated in their own bubble, could only see threats and blasphemy.

The rabbi draws a modern parallel to Eugene Rivers, a Boston pastor who transformed his violent neighborhood by living among the people. He closes by challenging the congregation to leave their own ivory towers during Elul, become laborers in the harvest, and extend the same identification with suffering that Yeshua modeled.

Hook / Wordplay

The lion of the tribe of Judah came not to devour us as an entrée but to protect us — and unlike a celebrity book signing, this King set up a tent in the field where anyone could approach regardless of dress code or status.

2021-08-22

Everyone Belongs in the Family of God

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Receiving God's mercy and extending it to others are inseparable — the family of God is defined not by genetics or social circles but by this cycle of mercy received and mercy given.
  • The four-word counsel 'let Him love you' names the core struggle: believers and seekers alike resist God's mercy, and the breakthrough comes from simple openness rather than effort.
  • The obligation in Deuteronomy 22 not to ignore a brother's fallen animal applies spiritually — you cannot pretend not to see someone struggling and still claim family loyalty.
One-Line Action
Identify one person you have been 'behaving as if you hadn't seen' their struggle and actively reach out this week.
Memorable Quote
The family of God is made up of people who respond to God's mercy and extend it to others.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Guest speaker Ibarz Uchkun, a longtime Beth Zion congregant now in Seattle, delivers the message centered on 'Everyone belongs in the family of God.' He develops the Hebrew concepts of mishpachah (family/clan) and kesher (deep connection) to define God's family as people who receive His mercy and extend it to others. Ibarz shares his dramatic testimony: born to a Muslim Afghan-Turkish family, he spiraled through drug addiction and arrest before encountering Yeshua at a messianic congregation in Philadelphia at age 23, then was planted at Beth Zion under Rabbi Jan's mentorship where community transformed his life. Using Luke 23:32-43, he identifies with both thieves on the cross—the mocker and the mercy-seeker—arguing God extends mercy in both states.

He challenges listeners who resist God's love due to comfort, fear, or past hurt to open themselves to it. Rabbi Jan closes by connecting to the Ki Tetze Torah portion (Deuteronomy 22: you must not ignore your brother's fallen animal) and the Haftarah (Isaiah 54: God's everlasting compassion after brief abandonment), then reveals a private story of arranging for Ibarz to reconcile with his father—reinforcing that God pursues reconciliation like a father calling a son home.

Hook / Wordplay

"From Muslim to Messianic: A funny thing happened on the way to the mosque" — Ibarz's planned autobiography title frames his testimony arc from Islam through addiction to faith in Yeshua.

2021-08-29

Ki Tavo — The Rise of God's Kingdom

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Don't rest on spiritual inheritance passively—go in, possess it, and settle there by actively engaging with what God has given you in purpose and calling.
  • Be a field laborer who tills and cultivates, not just a seed-scatterer: invest in relationships and prepare people's soil before expecting a harvest from God's word.
  • When circumstances look like gloom, obey the command to 'arise and look up'—shift focus from what is failing around you to what God is assembling and restoring.
One-Line Action
Identify one person whose spiritual soil is parched and invest in cultivating that relationship this week before trying to plant any seed.
Memorable Quote
Where is the kingdom? Wherever you allow the king to reign.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi Yann teaches on Parashat Ki Tavo (Deuteronomy 26–29), framing the Torah portion as a blueprint for the rise of God's kingdom. He emphasizes that God gives an inheritance—land, purpose, promises—but commands us to actively possess and settle it rather than passively rest on it. The kingdom of God exists wherever the King is allowed to reign; without the King actively reigning, any kingdom decays.

Drawing from the blessings and curses in Deuteronomy, he notes they are overwhelmingly agricultural, connecting the land's true value to what it produces rather than what you build on it. He bridges to Matthew 13, where Yeshua's parables of the sower, mustard seed, and leaven use the same agricultural imagery to describe kingdom dynamics. The four soils represent not fixed categories of people but conditions we can change—God calls laborers not only to scatter seed but to till ground, clear thorns, and cultivate the field.

He illustrates with modern Israel: Mark Twain declared the land total desolation, yet today it is a leading global agricultural producer, proving God restores what seems beyond repair. From the Haftarah (Isaiah 60), he draws the command to arise and shine—to look up from gloom and see what God is assembling. He closes by urging the congregation to let the King reign in their hearts first, then become cultivators who prepare others' soil to receive God's word and produce an abundant harvest.

Hook / Wordplay

A tale of two kingdoms—blessed and cursed—that becomes a tale of four kingdoms when you map the four soils onto it. The value of the land is not what you build on it but what you grow from it.

2021-09-05

Are We In Good Standing?

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Taking a stand is not the same as complaining — it requires putting on the full armor of God (truth, righteousness, readiness, trust, deliverance, the Word) and letting His strength work through weakness rather than merely lamenting the state of the world.
  • Genuine compassion — stepping aside from your own agenda to meet someone's real need — is the practical expression of good standing with God, more powerful than doctrinal correctness alone.
  • The word of life is already near, in your mouth and heart (Deut. 30:14 / Rom.
One-Line Action
Move from complaining to standing: put on the armor of God daily and take one concrete step to serve someone who cannot stand for themselves.
Memorable Quote
The compassion we show is more than the doctrines we present.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The Torah portion Nitzavim opens with all Israel standing before God to enter His covenant, and the rabbi builds the entire sermon around what it means to be 'in good standing' — not with the world, but with God. God presents the choice between life and death, blessing and curse, and explicitly tells His people to choose life so that they and their descendants may flourish. The rabbi highlights Deuteronomy 30 as the Torah's 'love chapter,' emphasizing God's commitment to pursue and restore even those scattered to the far end of the sky.

Connecting Deuteronomy 30 to Romans 10, he shows that the word of life is not far away but already near — in our mouths and hearts — and that trusting in Yeshua and publicly acknowledging Him brings deliverance. Through a Curtis Sliwa campaign story, he illustrates what genuine compassion looks like: stepping away from your own agenda to meet someone in real need. A personal high school story about confronting a bully demonstrates that taking a stand can produce unexpected ripple effects, including the bully's eventual life transformation.

Drawing on Ephesians 6, the rabbi catalogs the armor of God — truth, righteousness, readiness from the good news, the shield of trust, the helmet of deliverance, and the sword of the Word — as the equipment for standing firm against spiritual forces. He closes by distinguishing between mere complaining and truly taking a stand, urging the congregation to let God's strength work through their weakness in this season of return.

Hook / Wordplay

The Torah portion name 'Nitzavim' means 'standing,' and the rabbi builds every angle of the sermon around it — standing before God, being in good standing, taking a stand for truth, and ultimately standing firm in the armor of God.

2021-09-12

Shabbat Shuvah: Return To Sender…

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When holding a grudge, recognize you are 'benching yourself'—sitting in a self-made prison. Forgiveness releases you, not the offender, from ruminating over what should no longer control you.
  • When someone provokes you, mentally picture them as an eight-year-old: not to condescend, but to access the compassion Yeshua modeled when he said 'they don't know what they're doing.'
  • The annual holidays are not redundant repetition—they are calendar bookmarks God set so every generation can rehearse His faithfulness and pass it on, which is why the Torah says 'when your children ask you, tell them.'
One-Line Action
Release one grudge you are holding this week and consciously 'return to the Sender' by spending time in His word and extending forgiveness.
Memorable Quote
People think that forgiving someone else is letting them off the hook. But in reality it lets us off the hook.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Delivered on Shabbat Shuvah during the Ten Days of Awe and the 20th anniversary of 9/11, the rabbi frames the entire sermon around the postal metaphor of 'return to sender'—except God always knows our address. He traces the theme of return through Deuteronomy 31, where Moses warns Israel they will go astray even before entering the land, and commissions Joshua with the words 'be strong and bold.' Hosea 14 and Joel 2 show the fulfillment of that warning: Israel strayed, suffered consequences, yet God calls them back with the command to 'take words and return.' The rabbi connects this to Acts 2, where Peter at Shavuot quotes Joel's prophecy and the crowd, 'stung in their hearts,' asks 'what should we do?'—the same response God anticipated in Deuteronomy. He emphasizes that forgiveness is not letting others off the hook but freeing ourselves from self-imposed imprisonment.

The biblical holidays are presented not as redundant rituals but as God-designed calendar bookmarks ensuring each generation remembers His faithfulness. He reflects on the 9/11 anniversary as a parallel to the cycles of national crisis, brief spiritual awakening, and gradual drift that Scripture repeatedly describes. The sermon closes with James 5:7-9, linking patience and courage for the Lord's return to the practical command not to grumble against one another.

Hook / Wordplay

'Return to sender, address unknown'—but God always knows our address. We are returning to the Sender who sent blessings, sent Messiah, and sent redemption, and He will not mark us 'moved, not forwardable.'

2021-09-19

Ha'azinu: A New Song…To Live Long

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Be mindful of what 'songs' (media, narratives, content) I absorb daily — they shape thinking subliminally even when I'm not consciously processing the message, just like kids singing lyrics they don't understand.
  • When feeling distant from God, remember He hasn't moved from the driver's seat; the drift is mine — return through worship and singing spiritual songs rather than trying to fix things in my own strength.
  • Replace passive screen consumption (binge-watching, endless scrolling) with intentional time in God's presence — Ephesians 5 says to 'redeem the time, for these are evil days.'
One-Line Action
Replace one daily screen-time habit with singing or listening to worship music that actively draws you back into God's presence.
Memorable Quote
We're so glad you're here with us today and we're going to worship the Lord.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The Torah portion Ha'azinu (Deuteronomy 32) presents the Song of Moses, composed as Israel prepared to enter the Promised Land. Rabbi Jan frames Moses as trading his staff for a pen, becoming a songwriter whose composition was meant to be memorized and rehearsed by the people. The sermon explores the power of songs to shape us subliminally, noting how children absorb lyrics without understanding and how music captures the heart beyond what laws can achieve.

The Song of Moses declares God's perfect righteousness while acknowledging the defect lies in His children — a generation drawn to other sounds and influences. Yet judgment is never God's final word; He always provides an avenue of return, illustrated by the metaphor that God never moves from the driver's seat — we are the ones who drift away. Drawing from Deuteronomy 32:47, the title's meaning emerges: adhering to God's words is 'not a trivial matter but your life' through which you will live long.

Ephesians 5 is brought in to contrast darkness and light, urging believers to be filled with the Spirit by singing songs, hymns, and spiritual songs rather than being lulled to sleep by screens and worldly distractions. Romans 12 reinforces the call to bless rather than curse, to conquer evil with good rather than adopting the adversary's weaponry. The sermon closes by noting that even Moses included his own failure in the song, demonstrating that no one — not even leaders — is exempt from accountability before God.

Hook / Wordplay

Moses trades his staff for a pen — the ultimate golden oldie is a song God wants you to memorize, because the chorus always comes back to return and restoration.

2021-09-26

1st Day of Sukkot Outdoor Service — Part 1

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Sukkot is the joyful reward after the hard introspective work of the High Holidays—don't skip the celebration after doing the repentance; the joy of the Lord is the intended destination of teshuvah.
  • When overwhelmed by external darkness or chaos, the response is not protest or panic but yielding to God's light, which darkness cannot suppress—be a reflector of that light to others.
  • The four species of the Lulav represent people with different strengths (study, deeds, both, neither) who all belong together—community means embracing every type, not just those like you.
One-Line Action
Identify one area where you have drifted and take a step back toward God this week—not by striving harder, but by humbling yourself and letting Him restore.
Memorable Quote
As we drift away, we become more hopeless. But God wants us to believe and to be transformed by the power of his spirit.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The rabbi opens the first day of Sukkot by explaining how the festival is the joyful culmination of the penitential High Holiday season from Rosh Hashanah through Yom Kippur. He reads Leviticus 23:39-44, reviewing the commands to dwell in booths, take the four species, and celebrate for seven days as a memorial of Israel's wilderness journey and God's provision. He emphasizes that the sukkah reminds us of humble beginnings, warning against the temptation to credit our own hands when we live in comfort.

Drawing on John 1:1-14, he argues that Yeshua was likely born around Sukkot and that the Word became flesh and 'tabernacled among us,' linking the incarnation directly to the festival's themes. He references 2 Chronicles 7:14 with a vivid sales analogy: God already told us what to do—humble ourselves and pray—so stop asking 'but what should we do?' He invokes Josephus's account of the torch-lit final day of Sukkot to connect with Yeshua's declaration 'I am the light of the world' from John 7-8. The Lulav and Etrog are explained through rabbinic teaching: the four species represent four types of people—those who study and do, study but don't do, do but don't study, and neither—all unified in community.

He frames the entire season as God's persistent call to return, noting that even if we drift to the ends of the sky, God commits to go and bring us back. The service closes with the Lulav shaking ceremony, the Aaronic benediction, and fellowship in the sukkah.

Hook / Wordplay

Like a salesman who keeps pitching after the customer is ready to buy—God already gave the answer in 2 Chronicles 7:14: just humble yourselves and let Him move.

2021-09-26

Shabbat Sukkot Message - Part 2

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • The woman caught in adultery is a legal teaching, not just a mercy parable: 'whoever is without sin' challenges the witnesses' willingness to carry testimony through a proper trial, reframing the passage from generic tolerance to Torah-rooted justice.
  • We are 'frail tabernacles'—our value comes not from our own strength but from God placing His treasure and presence within us, like the Shekinah filling the wilderness Tabernacle.
  • Sukkot's command to build temporary shelters is a guard against self-sufficiency: when you live in brick houses and enjoy vineyards you didn't plant, remember it was God's hand, not yours.
One-Line Action
Sit in a sukkah this week—or any simple, stripped-down space—and thank God for the blessings you didn't build by your own hand.
Memorable Quote
All the darkness around you, whether it's in your heart or surrounding you, is not enough to dispel the light. But the light will dispel the darkness and I am the light of the world.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi teaches Sukkot as the only festival designated the Festival of Joy and connects its themes to Messiah. He explains the Tabernacle as a giant sukkah where God's Shekinah dwelt among Israel, and how our personal booths recall God's provision in the wilderness. He builds a timeline case for Yeshua's birth at Sukkot using Luke's priestly course chronology, the six-month gap between Yohanan and Yeshua, and the 'about 30 years old' phrasing.

He walks through John 7-8 where Yeshua declared 'whoever is thirsty, come to me and drink' during the water libation and 'I am the light of the world' as the Sukkot torches blazed. He offers an extended reframing of the woman caught in adultery, arguing Yeshua was not making a generic statement about universal sinfulness but asking whether the witnesses were willing to carry their testimony through a proper Torah trial—and by looking away, he gave them room to save face.

He ties this act of mercy back to Sukkot's core: joy born out of redemption, light shining into darkness. He concludes that Yeshua took on a 'human sukkah'—a frail temporary body—to bring God's dwelling presence among us, and that God places His treasure in earthen vessels, giving us our true value.

Hook / Wordplay

Yeshua took on a 'human sukkah'—a fragile, frail human body—to tabernacle among us, making our temporary booths the dwelling place of an eternal presence.

2021-10-03

Creating a High Calling

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • True calling is relational union with God, not vocational identity—when asked 'what do you do?' the deeper answer is 'I walk with God,' and everything else flows from that.
  • When we see through a glass dimly and are tempted to take the wheel based on partial knowledge, remember God deliberately redirects us because He has a bigger view—yield to His redirects instead of resisting them.
  • The adversary's Eden strategy hasn't changed: offer to 'be like God without God.' Watch for modern versions of that lie in choices that promise self-sufficiency apart from dependence on Him.
One-Line Action
Identify one area where you have been steering by your own understanding instead of yielding to God's direction, and consciously surrender it this week.
Memorable Quote
And you shall love the Lord your God with all your heart, with all your soul and with all your might.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Opening the new Torah cycle with Beresheet, the rabbi frames the creation narrative as the origin story of God's calling—intimate, face-to-face fellowship with humanity. He highlights the contrast between light and darkness in Genesis 1 as emblematic of two competing callings: God's invitation to walk in His likeness versus the adversary's counterfeit offer to be like God without God. Adam and Eve chose the false proposal, and that pattern of self-directed detours persists in every generation.

Drawing from the Haftarah in Isaiah 42-43, the sermon emphasizes that God leads the blind on roads they don't know and calls each person by name—making the calling deeply personal. The rabbi argues that true calling is not vocational but relational: it is union with God, not a job title. Paul's testimony in Philippians 3 of counting all achievements as refuse compared to knowing Messiah illustrates pressing toward this higher calling daily.

Through 2 Corinthians 5, the message of reconciliation is presented as the practical fruit of that union—we become ambassadors because we walk with Him. The sermon closes with a warning from 2 Peter 2 against those who promise freedom while being slaves themselves, reinforcing that no one can offer a higher calling than what God has already given.

Hook / Wordplay

'Creating a high calling'—the word 'creating' ties back to Beresheet/Creation itself: we must go to the Creator for our calling rather than trying to create our own.

2021-10-10

Noah, As In The Day

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Audit which human voices I have functionally elevated to idol status—experts, media figures, political leaders—and measure their claims against Scripture rather than their charisma or credentials.
  • When cultural pressure to conform feels overwhelming, remember Noah preached and built for 120 years under mockery; steadfastness comes from hearing God's voice, not from crowd validation.
  • Practice daily 'spin-proofing' by immersing in Scripture and prayer before consuming news or social media, so that God's framework is the first lens rather than a corrective afterthought.
One-Line Action
Identify one authority figure or cultural voice you have been trusting uncritically this week and test their claims against what Scripture actually says.
Memorable Quote
The only vaccination is through Messiah. It's a blood transfusion.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The rabbi opens the Torah portion of Noach by reframing Yeshua's warning in Matthew 24—'as in the days of Noah, so shall the coming of the Son of Man be'—as a lens for examining persistent human corruption patterns. He traces the root problem to Genesis 2-3, identifying two 'image makers': God, who made us in His likeness, and the adversary, who seduced humanity into a counterfeit image defined by self-desire, blame-shifting, and wanting to be like God without God. He argues this corrupted image-bearing explains why every generation elevates human authorities—athletes, scientists, politicians, evangelists—into quasi-idols whose credibility expands far beyond their expertise.

Using vivid modern illustrations (a high-pressure baby-product sales pitch, vaccine line-cutting, Charlie Brown's sincere but losing baseball team), he shows how the same crowd-following impulse that filled Noah's world with violence operates today through media spin, fabricated narratives, and cultural pressure. Drawing from 2 Peter 2, he highlights the warning against false teachers 'mouthing grandiocities of nothingness' who seduce people back into bondage after they have tasted freedom. He then pivots to hope through Isaiah 54-55, where God promises compassion after judgment and calls the wicked to return, and concludes that the same narrative arc—corruption, warning, avenue of escape—runs from Noah through the apostles to today, with Messiah as the only reliable 'vaccination' against the sin nature.

Hook / Wordplay

"As in the days of Noah" is not distant prophecy but a recurring operating system—Eden's second image maker wrote the code, Noah's generation ran it, and we are running the same program today under different brand names.

2021-10-17

Challenges That Grow Righteousness

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Faith is built in layers through challenges — each difficulty you survive builds capacity to handle the next one in stride, even when the later trial is objectively harder.
  • When tempted to judge someone's spiritual failure, remember your own repeated failures and extend the same compassion God showed you — be a restorer, not an accuser.
  • God doesn't explain the training in advance (Karate Kid principle) — the mundane, frustrating seasons you want to escape may be exactly what's building the core strength you'll need later.
One-Line Action
Trust God through the current challenge you want to escape, knowing He is building capacity in you for what's ahead.
Memorable Quote
Think back when you did and have the same compassion on them that God had on us and see if God will give us the ability to be restorers and not just accusers.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi teaches from the Torah portion Lekh Lekha, tracing Abraham's journey as a model for how righteousness grows through challenges rather than arriving fully formed. He walks through Abraham's incremental steps of faith: stopping at Haran instead of fully obeying, lying about Sarah being his sister out of fear, the conflict and separation with Lot, refusing the king of Sodom's spoils, and the long wait for the promised son. He highlights that Abraham repeated the same failure with Abimelech decades later, and even Isaac copied the tactic, showing that spiritual growth is not linear.

Using the Karate Kid illustration, he explains that God trains us through seemingly mundane or painful circumstances without explaining the purpose in advance, building muscle memory of faith for greater challenges ahead. He connects this to Romans 3-4, showing that Abraham's trust was credited as righteousness while still uncircumcised, opening this promise to all nations. He closes with Colossians 2 on living in union with Messiah, warning against self-righteousness and calling the congregation to be restorers rather than accusers.

Hook / Wordplay

Lekh Lekha means 'get yourself out' — get out of yourself, out of the familiar, out of what stifles what God wants to do in your life.

2021-10-24

History Repeats Itself

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When God has given a clear promise, resist the instinct to 'help' it along with deception or shortcuts—Abraham lied about Sarah the same year he received the promise of Isaac, nearly forfeiting what God guaranteed.
  • Recognize the adversary's playbook as replication and bait-and-switch: amazement at signs leads to surrendering decision-making authority, whether through fortune tellers, celebrity culture, or ideological movements.
  • God's redemptive history repeats as faithfully as destructive history—focus less on mapping prophetic timelines and more on yielding to His repeated invitations to return.
One-Line Action
The next time fear tempts me to take a shortcut around a promise God already made, stop and remember Abraham at 99—the promise was one year away.
Memorable Quote
In the same way that these destructive elements repeat themselves, we can allow God's promises to repeat themselves over and over again if we yield to Him instead of our own old nature.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi opens with a montage of quotes about history repeating itself, setting up a wordplay: history repeats itself 'a lot'—both frequently and through the character Lot. He traces Abraham's journey in Vayera: the promise of Isaac, the negotiation over Sodom, Lot's rescue and his daughters' corruption shaped by their environment. He highlights Abraham repeating his 'she's my sister' lie with Abimelech just after receiving the promise of a son—questioning how someone with such revelation could default to fear.

The sermon pivots to Revelation 13, arguing the adversary's strategy is always counterfeiting: a fatal wound healed mimicking resurrection, 'who is like the beast' echoing 'mi kamocha,' and the mark on hand and forehead paralleling tefillin. He draws a throughline from the flood to Sodom to modern ideologies that promise freedom but deliver slavery, warning against the 'bait and switch' of familiar spirits and celebrity worship. He closes by insisting that God's redemptive pattern repeats just as reliably as destruction—whoever calls on the Lord shall be saved—and urges the congregation to walk in union with Messiah rather than trying to decode prophetic timelines.

Hook / Wordplay

'History repeats itself a lot'—a double meaning on frequency and on the biblical Lot, whose story embodies the cycle of compromise, judgment, and God's persistent rescue.

2021-10-31

A Vision of Biblical Loyalty

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Loyalty is proven through patience and follow-through on commitments, not through declarations — like Eliezer refusing leisure until his mission was complete, even after an exhausting journey.
  • True loyal friendship means speaking hard truths wisely (as Nathan did with David via parable), not just being a 'yes person' — and it requires knowing the person well enough to reach their heart, not their defensiveness.
  • There is a critical gap between quoting and reciting scripture versus allowing it to transform behavior — the 'full knowledge of truth' means being set free by its reality working in your life, not just knowing the words.
One-Line Action
Identify one relationship where you can deepen loyalty by showing up consistently and speaking truth wisely when needed, rather than defaulting to comfortable agreement.
Memorable Quote
And these words which I command you this day shall be in your heart.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon examines the Torah portion Hayyei Sarah through the theme of biblical loyalty, beginning with Abraham's negotiation to purchase the cave of Machpelah after Sarah's death, which demonstrated the trust he had built over 25 years in the land. The central narrative follows Eliezer's mission to find a wife for Isaac, highlighting his extraordinary loyalty: he prayed before acting, waited in silence to confirm God's guidance, and refused rest or delay until his task was complete. The rabbi notes that Eliezer's loyalty extended beyond Abraham to the generational vision, evidenced by his calling Isaac 'my master' rather than 'my master's son.' The Haftarah in 1 Kings 1 provides a counter-example in Adonijah's power grab, where Joab shifted loyalty to self-interest while Nathan, Zadok, and Solomon remained faithful to David's vision.

Nathan's earlier confrontation of David over Bathsheba illustrates that true loyalty includes the courage and wisdom to speak hard truth — using a parable to reach David's core values rather than triggering his defensiveness. Drawing from 2 Timothy 2–3, the rabbi warns that end-times conditions erode loyalty through self-love, arrogance, and pleasure-seeking, even among those retaining an outward form of religion.

He draws a sharp distinction between learning scripture and living it, confessing his own past habit of quoting Watchman Nee to appear spiritual without personal transformation. The sermon closes with the assurance that God remains faithful even when we are faithless, and calls the congregation to build relationships where loyalty is tested, refined, and grown across generations.

Hook / Wordplay

"Loyalty goes both ways — sometimes you can get what you want, but you may not want what you get."

Tol'dot: An Antidote for Defective DNA

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When despair hits, recognize it as the old corrupted DNA trying to reassert control—respond by relying on God who raises the dead rather than your own capacity to manage the crisis.
  • Before judging Esau for selling his birthright for a meal, audit where you have sold out for even less—momentary comfort, avoidance, or convenience—and reclaim what you traded away.
  • Stay connected to God's ongoing spiritual infusion; the moment you pull out the IV and try to handle things in your own power, you frustrate the very grace that is rewriting you from the inside out.
One-Line Action
Identify one area where you are operating in self-reliance this week, name it honestly, and consciously yield it back to God.
Memorable Quote
We worry about the future, we cry over the past and we neglect the value of what God gives us today.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon on Parashat Toldot explores the concept of defective spiritual DNA, arguing that humanity's original design was fellowship with God but the serpent corrupted that identity in the garden. Rabbi examines the family dynamics of Isaac, Rebecca, Esau, and Jacob, offering fresh chronological observations: Isaac was 40 at marriage, 60 when the twins were born, at least 100 at the blessing scene, yet didn't die until 180—meaning Rebecca's urgent decision to send Jacob away was based on a premature assumption Isaac was near death. He notes Rebecca's deceptive streak mirrors her brother Laban's character, suggesting family DNA at work, and debunks the rabbinic tradition that she was three years old at marriage by pointing to contextual impossibilities.

Pivoting to Paul's writings, he frames despair as a symptom of the sin-infection and argues that God's antidote is a spiritual blood transfusion through Messiah—restoring the original DNA rather than creating something entirely new. Through Hebrews 12 he shows that discipline and suffering are training tools, not punishment, and that faith means trusting God whether outcomes are miraculous or painful. He closes sharply with Galatians 3, where Paul calls the Galatians stupid for beginning in the Spirit and trying to finish under their own power, driving home that self-reliance frustrates the grace that rewrites our spiritual code.

Hook / Wordplay

Our spiritual DNA was hijacked in the garden; Messiah doesn't give us a new identity—He restores the original one. The first Adam broke the code, the second Adam rewrites it.

2021-11-14

Unseen Steps to Victory

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When something doesn't fit my plan, remember that the waiting period is God's investment—He is developing something in me for what's coming that I can't yet see.
  • Stop trying to piece together the puzzle and force an outcome from partial glimpses; let God reveal the full picture in retrospect rather than finagling forward.
  • Focus on the Giver rather than the gift—having Him as the cornerstone matters more than understanding the timeline or controlling the result.
One-Line Action
This week, when something feels off-plan, pause and say 'God is in this and I don't know it yet' instead of forcing the outcome.
Memorable Quote
But the mistake we make is thinking that what we see is all there is.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon explores Parashat Vayetze through the lens of God's unseen work, using Jacob's departure from Beersheva as its anchor. Jacob fled Esau's death threats toward Haran not knowing his journey would last over twenty years, filled with Laban's deception, changed wages, and the wrong bride. Genesis 28:16 captures the core revelation: the Lord was in this place and I didn't know it.

The rabbi shares his own parallel—believing God would reveal his ministry in seven years, only to wait twenty-one years before Beth Zion materialized, with agonizing false starts and a candidating mix-up along the way. He contrasts Leah's character with Rachel's outward beauty, arguing that our eyes are weak like Leah's, leading us to judge by appearance rather than substance. Drawing from Hosea, he warns that prosperity breeds pride and forgetfulness of God, and from Revelation 3 that self-sufficiency blinds us to spiritual poverty.

The Nathanael encounter in John 1 illustrates how Yeshua sees what is hidden—character and calling—before we can perceive it ourselves. Culminating in 2 Corinthians 4:17-18, he frames all trials as light momentary afflictions that work for us, not against us, building an eternal weight of glory. The call is to concentrate not on the seen but the unseen, trusting that God is actively orchestrating every unseen step toward victory.

Hook / Wordplay

Leah's 'weak eyes' become a mirror for our own weak spiritual vision—we misjudge by outward appearance just as Jacob's own father favored Esau, repeating the same blindness across generations.

2021-11-21

Get Up, Go Out, Move On

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Don't let the last negative encounter with someone freeze your expectations—Jacob feared Esau for 20 years based on an outdated threat, but Esau had moved on and embraced him; our reference points distort over time.
  • Before entering God's next phase, actively identify and bury the 'household idols'—familiar securities that aren't actually from God—because Rachel surrendered hers too late and never made it to Bethel.
  • Philippians 3 reframes spiritual progress: you haven't arrived, but Messiah has taken hold of you for a purpose, so forget what's behind and strain forward rather than ruminating on past failures or fears.
One-Line Action
Identify one familiar false security you cling to, name it honestly, and take a concrete step to lay it aside this week so you can move forward into what God has next.
Memorable Quote
You can't have resurrection unless you die. You can't have restoration unless you've been severed from what it was that seemed to be so important.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon follows Torah portion Vayishlach, focusing on Jacob's return after 20 years and God's command in Genesis 35 to get up, go up to Bethel, and dwell there. Rabbi traces Jacob's fear of meeting Esau, whose last words were a death threat, and the wrestling encounter at Peniel where Jacob prevailed and was renamed Israel. Before journeying to Bethel, Jacob commands his household to bury all foreign gods, purify themselves, and put on fresh clothes—a template for spiritual transition.

Rachel, who had stolen Laban's household idols, surrendered them but died in childbirth on the road and never reached Bethel, serving as a warning against delayed obedience. The rabbi connects this to Paul in Philippians 3 counting all former advantages as garbage compared to knowing Messiah, and pressing toward the mark of God's upward calling. He uses the class reunion illustration to show how our reference points become distorted over time, just as Jacob's 20-year-old fear of Esau was unfounded when Esau ran to embrace him.

First Peter 5 rounds out the message: cast anxieties on God, stay sober and alert, stand firm in trust, and God Himself will restore, establish, and strengthen you. The three-part framework—get up from the familiar, go out from hindrances, move on to God's next phase—applies to every season of the believer's life.

Hook / Wordplay

Three imperatives from Genesis 35:1 become a life framework: Get up (from comfort or even dysfunction), Go out (bury the idols and hindrances), Move on (press toward God's upward calling).

2021-11-28

When Great Miracles Happen

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Thanksgiving and Hanukkah both call for active gratitude—use the overlap as a prompt to name specific miracles (personal deliverances, provisions) rather than offering only generic thanks.
  • Yeshua's miracles point beyond the miraculous event itself to the Father behind it; when witnessing God's work, look through the miracle to the character of the One performing it.
  • The phrase 'I am not saying on my own initiative' (John 14:10) models a posture of dependence—align personal actions and words with God's initiative rather than self-generated effort.
One-Line Action
This week during Hanukkah candle-lighting, name one specific miracle God has done in your life and thank Him for it aloud.
Memorable Quote
It's interesting that with the gift that God gives us, with the miracles, we say that when great miracles happen, we know that Yeshua did miracles.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi opens the Thanksgiving Weekend Service noting that Hanukkah begins the following evening, creating a rare convergence of thanksgiving and the festival of miracles. He recalls 2013 when Thanksgiving and the first day of Hanukkah fell on the same day, an alignment said not to recur for roughly 70,000 years. Paul Wilbur joins the service as a guest worship leader.

The sermon bridges the theme of thanksgiving from 2 Corinthians 9 into the Hanukkah idea that God performs great miracles. Rabbi connects the Hanukkah declaration—when great miracles happen—to the miracles Yeshua performed. He draws on John 14:8-10 where Philip asks Yeshua to show them the Father, and Yeshua responds that whoever has seen Him has seen the Father.

The teaching frames Yeshua's miracles not as independent acts but as works done on the Father's initiative, reinforcing that seeing Yeshua at work is seeing God Himself at work. The service closes with the Aaronic blessing.

Hook / Wordplay

"When great miracles happen"—the Hanukkah declaration (nes gadol haya sham) reframed through Yeshua's miracles as the ultimate revelation of the Father.

2021-12-05

When Unlikely Becomes More Than Likely

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When facing impossible circumstances, examine whether self-reliance ('I'm going to make this happen') is the actual barrier—yielding that grip is what opens space for God to act.
  • Spiritual drift operates like the frog in slowly heating water: small compromises accumulate invisibly until you're in full captivity, so identify and address the small drifts now.
  • Even the tiniest act of faith or courage can ignite a movement—the Maccabees' single act of defiance rekindled an entire nation, so don't despise small beginnings in dark seasons.
One-Line Action
Identify one area where you are white-knuckling control and consciously release it to God this week, trusting Him with the impossible.
Memorable Quote
Lord, I want to have the faith and the joy that I know I'll have in retrospect, I'd like to have it in advance.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The rabbi connects Torah portion Mikketz—Joseph interpreting Pharaoh's dreams after two forgotten years in prison—with the Hanukkah narrative of the Maccabees facing an impossibly powerful occupying army. Both Joseph and the Maccabees were trapped in situations no human strategy could resolve. He introduces a central wordplay: remove 'I'm' from 'impossible' and it becomes 'possible,' arguing that self-driven effort often blocks divine intervention.

Solomon's dream in the Haftarah reinforces the pattern—wisdom was granted but not followed, and the kingdom fractured. The rich young ruler in Luke 18 illustrates how possessions and attachments become chains that obscure God's light, prompting Yeshua to teach that what is humanly impossible is possible with God. The Maccabees' righteous indignation at the altar's desecration sparked courage across a despondent, cave-hiding nation, achieving military victory no one thought achievable.

A personal anecdote about his flashlight illuminating a blacked-out movie theater concretely demonstrates that even the smallest light dispels total darkness. The sermon closes by urging listeners to yield control, allow God to rekindle faith's flame, and trust that 'at the end' is often where God's new beginning starts.

Hook / Wordplay

When 'I'm' is removed from 'impossible,' it becomes 'possible'—sometimes we are the obstacle standing between the impossible and what God wants to do.
