2021-12-12

Breaking the Sin Within

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • God uses personal suffering and loss to build empathy and break selfishness—Judah could not sacrifice for Benjamin until he himself had lost two sons and understood a father's grief
  • Sin blinds us to truth and blocks us from seeing what God is doing; Joseph in all his brilliance could not see God's full purpose until Judah's brokenness opened his eyes
  • Freedom from sin's control comes not through willpower but through walking in union with Messiah—Romans 8 shows the spirit's power dissolves what our own effort cannot break
One-Line Action
Identify the habitual sin you keep justifying or ignoring, stop making excuses, and yield it specifically to Messiah in prayer this week.
Memorable Quote
We don't know what will break loose when we open up our hearts to God and allow him to transform us.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon follows the Torah portion Vayigash, where Judah approaches Joseph and offers himself as a slave in place of Benjamin. The rabbi traces Judah's transformation from the instigator who sold Joseph into slavery to a man willing to sacrifice himself, explaining that Judah's own devastating losses—two sons dying—gave him the empathy to understand what losing another child would do to his father. Judah's self-sacrifice broke something in Joseph as well, enabling him to finally see the full scope of God's purpose and declare 'what you meant for evil, God meant for good.' The rabbi connects this to Ezekiel 37's prophecy of the two sticks of Judah and Joseph being made one, then draws extensively from Jeremiah 2 to show how sin blinds people into abandoning God, the fountain of living water, for broken cisterns that hold nothing.

Through Romans 7-8, he illustrates the internal war between the old nature and the spirit, showing that union with Messiah Yeshua is the only power that breaks the chains of sin. He closes with the story of Zacchaeus, whose encounter with Yeshua instantly broke his attachment to ill-gotten wealth, noting that 'Zacchaeus' means innocent—a picture of how God takes the guilty and makes them clean.

Hook / Wordplay

Zacchaeus, the guilty tax collector despised by everyone, bears a name that means 'innocent'—and God's work is to make the guilty truly Zacchaeus, truly innocent, because Messiah took our place.

2021-12-19

Vayechi: True Vision Accountability

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Comfort and acceptance can slowly erode vision—like Israel's favored status in Egypt eventually led to slavery, small compromises compound into spiritual captivity.
  • Being 95% prepared is still unprepared; procrastinating on spiritual readiness (like the five foolish virgins) means the door can close while I'm still 'getting oil.'
  • Hard words from God or from accountability partners (like Yeshua to Peter, Paul to Peter, Jacob to his sons) are redirection, not rejection—receive correction as vision-sharpening.
One-Line Action
Identify one area where comfort or distraction has dulled your accountability to God's calling and take a concrete step this week to realign.
Memorable Quote
They had done everything 99, 95% complete, but they did not allow themselves to be held accountable to the full extent of what the vision represented.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi teaches from Parashat Vayechi, focusing on Jacob's deathbed charge to Joseph: 'Don't bury me in Egypt.' He reframes this as a metaphor—don't let comfort, technology, acceptance, or worldly allure bury you away from God's vision. Jacob spent 17 years in Egypt with Pharaoh's favor, yet refused to let that privilege replace the promised land. The rabbi traces how Jacob's sons compromised their callings—Reuben defiled his father's bed, Simeon and Levi chose violence—and lost their firstborn standing, while Judah received the messianic scepter promise.

He parallels this with David's charge to Solomon, where Adonijah's subtle attempt to acquire David's concubine nearly undermined the kingdom. Drawing from John 13-14, he connects Yeshua's 'if you love me, keep my commands' to Jacob's 'if you truly love me, don't bury me here.' Peter's bold pledge to die for Yeshua and subsequent denial illustrates the gap between emotional commitment and tested accountability.

The sermon culminates with the parable of the ten virgins: all were prepared, all were pure, but five lacked oil—they were 95% ready yet found the door closed. The call is to maintain daily, vigilant accountability to God's true vision, guided by the Holy Spirit as a compass pointing true north.

Hook / Wordplay

"Don't bury me in Egypt" — not just a burial request but a plea against letting comfort and worldly acceptance bury us away from God's promised vision.

2021-12-25

Birthing Sons of Promise

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When I say 'I can't' or 'send someone else' about a calling, I'm not being humble—I'm undermining God's authority to equip me; recognize that reflex and reframe it as a trust issue.
  • God forms purpose in us before birth (Jeremiah 1:5); my job is to yield and discover what He's birthing, not to manufacture or dismiss my own calling.
  • Birth pangs and difficulty in spiritual growth are not signs something is wrong—they're the expected process, and joy follows the labor just as it does after a child is born.
One-Line Action
Identify one area where you've been deflecting a calling with 'send someone else' and step into it this week, trusting God to supply what you lack.
Memorable Quote
When we downplay it and say, no, send someone else, what we are saying is you don't have the ability God to do what you said you will do. It was a depreciating not of himself, but of God's authority and power to do what he says you will do.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi opens the Shemot Torah portion on Christmas Day by connecting Pharaoh's oppression of Israel and slaughter of male children to Herod's massacre of boys in Bethlehem, showing both tyrants feared a deliverer rising from the people they controlled. He traces the theme of 'sons of promise' through Moses, Jeremiah, Yohanan HaMatbil, and Yeshua, noting each was called by God before or from birth for a specific divine purpose. He argues that Yeshua was likely born during Sukkot rather than December 25th, using timeline evidence from Zechariah's temple service rotation, Elizabeth's pregnancy, and the wilderness testing readings from Deuteronomy.

He critiques secular documentaries that portray John the Immerser as a wilderness rebel, pointing out that his father was a high priest and that immersion was a standard priestly cleansing ceremony. The sermon pivots from physical births to spiritual application: God is birthing purpose and calling within each believer, and the labor pains of that process should not cause us to quit.

He challenges the congregation that saying 'send someone else' is not humility but a depreciation of God's power to equip those He calls. He closes with the invitation from John 1—as many as received Him were given the right to become children of God—urging listeners to let Messiah take root and be birthed within them.

Hook / Wordplay

'Birthing' operates on two levels: the physical births of Moses, Jeremiah, Yohanan, and Yeshua under hostile rulers—and the spiritual birth God is laboring to produce inside every believer right now.

2022-01-02

Va'era: Turning Discouragement into Encouragement

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When feeling inadequate, remember that God's calling is based on His purpose, not my resume — focusing on my limitations actually shrinks my view of what God will do.
  • When facing uncertain situations, invoke James 1:5 specifically: ask God for wisdom expecting He gives 'liberally and without reproach,' then move forward trusting it will arrive even before I see it.
  • Discouragement is not cured by forced positivity but by reopening spiritual ears — deepening knowledge of God so His quiet voice overpowers the loud voices of accusation and circumstance.
One-Line Action
When discouragement deafens you, stop self-evaluating and ask God for wisdom with the specific expectation that He gives liberally — then move forward.
Memorable Quote
We're going to begin by blessing the name of the Lord together.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi examines the Torah portion Va'era, where both Moses and the Israelites suffer deep discouragement — Moses feels inadequate as a speaker and the people are too crushed by slavery to listen. He identifies 'spiritual deafness' as the core problem: when we fixate on our own limitations, we become unable to hear God's voice. He contrasts Moses' self-depreciation with Pharaoh's self-deification, showing both needed humbling from opposite directions.

Drawing from James 1, he teaches that asking God for wisdom without doubting is the antidote — God gives liberally and without reproach. He highlights Exodus 4:23, where God telegraphed the tenth plague before the first, showing God's plan was always complete. From Joshua 1, he connects strength and success to meditating on God's word and trusting His presence.

Using Colossians 2, he reframes New Year's resolutions: true change requires 'resolute firmness of trust in Messiah,' not mere good intentions. The sermon concludes that we are 'self-sufficient in His sufficiency,' and as we know God better we trust Him more, becoming ambassadors who turn others' discouragement into encouragement.

Hook / Wordplay

People say they break New Year's resolutions, but they never made a resolution — they made a good intention. A resolution means being resolute, immovable, the same 'resolute firmness of trust' Paul describes in Colossians 2.

2022-01-09

Bo — How Much Longer… Till We Go?

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Don't let crisis push you into 'social distancing from God' — the instinct to withdraw spiritually in hard seasons is the opposite of what brings deliverance.
  • Watch for tunnel vision: when your focus on one narrative becomes so sharp that all surrounding reality blurs into deception, you've lost perspective.
  • God's patience is not indifference — reframe every unanswered 'how long?' as evidence He is still making room for transformation, not that He has abandoned you.
One-Line Action
This week, replace one daily session of news-scrolling with time in Scripture and prayer, deliberately closing the distance between yourself and God.
Memorable Quote
Sometimes our focus can become so clear that all of reality around us becomes a blur and a deception.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The Torah portion Bo ('go') opens with Moses and Aaron confronting Pharaoh: 'How much longer will you refuse to submit?' — and remarkably Pharaoh's own servants echo the same plea, asking how much longer this man will be a snare to them while Egypt is destroyed. Pharaoh attempts to negotiate by letting only the men leave while keeping the children, revealing a timeless pattern of controlling the next generation to control the future. Rabbi Gonzalez draws a parallel to the Spanish Inquisition, where Jewish families hid their identity so completely that fathers wrapped sons in a talit at age thirteen without knowing why — a haunting image of generational memory surviving without understanding.

Psalm 13's repeated cry of 'how long, O Lord?' connects Israel's ancient anguish to modern frustrations with pandemic restrictions, political control, and cultural despair. From Matthew 24 and the destruction of Jerusalem in 70 CE, the sermon shows how siege conditions turned people against each other rather than uniting them against a common enemy — a warning against self-destruction through internal hatred. Second Peter 3 reframes God's apparent delay: His patience is not slowness but deliverance, giving people time to turn from sin.

Paul's conversion becomes the culminating illustration — struck blind on the road, his literal blindness removed his spiritual blind spot, showing that transformation can happen in a single moment. The sermon closes with a call to stop 'social distancing from God' and instead draw near, trusting that 2022 could become a year of revival and deliverance if God's people seek His face.

Hook / Wordplay

Bo means 'go' — sounds like the name of a dog somebody down in the South might have. Come on, Bo. Let's go.

2022-01-22

Exposing the Spirit of Amalek

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Grumbling about circumstances I don't understand makes me spiritually vulnerable — the Israelites' complaining about water opened the door for Amalek's attack on the stragglers.
  • Speaking truth to power must aim at redemption, not accusation — Yeshua called leaders 'blind guides' and 'hypocrites' not to destroy them but to clear their vision so they could experience God's redemptive hand.
  • Refuse both passive silence and reactive vitriol — neutrality enables the oppressor, but fighting with the world's anger feeds the adversary's desire to divide and destroy.
One-Line Action
Speak out against injustice with redemptive intent rather than reactive anger, staying close to the group and to God so the adversary cannot isolate you.
Memorable Quote
When people talk about the idea of white privilege.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi Rosenberg opens with the Torah portion B'shallach, focusing on Amalek's attack on the vulnerable stragglers at the rear of Israel's camp after the Exodus. He notes that just before Amalek struck, the people had been grumbling against Moses about water, placing themselves in a spiritually vulnerable position — like a calf separated from the herd on the African plains. He connects this to Jude's warning about divisive people driven by greed, hatred, and lust for power, citing the patterns of Cain, Balaam, and Korah.

The sermon identifies a recurring 'spirit of Amalek' that operates through history by dividing groups, silencing dissent, and using elite manipulation to pit people against each other while real power brokers work behind the scenes. He quotes Elie Wiesel on the danger of neutrality and Martin Niemöller's famous warning about silence in the face of injustice.

Turning to Yeshua's confrontation with the money changers and his rebukes of hypocritical leaders, he argues that speaking truth to power must always aim at redemption rather than accusation, lest we become 'accusers of the brethren.' He warns that both passive silence and reactive vitriol serve the adversary's purposes, and that the real privilege driving division is not racial but elite. The call is to expose the adversary's tactics while proclaiming a message of life, restoration, and hope through Messiah.

Hook / Wordplay

Amalek attacked the stragglers who drifted from the group — like lions on the African plains isolating a young calf from the herd, the adversary targets those who have separated themselves through grumbling and spiritual drift.

2022-01-30

Hearing A Voice From Heaven #2

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Train your ear to hear God through daily engagement with His word rather than waiting for dramatic moments—the bat kol is secondary to the word taking root in your heart.
  • Practice listening before responding in conversations and in prayer; resist the urge to formulate your answer while someone is still speaking, because 'he who answers a matter before he hears it, it is folly and shame.'
  • When challenges come after a spiritual high, respond with Scripture already planted in your heart the way Yeshua did in the wilderness—don't default to your own solutions.
One-Line Action
This week, read one passage slowly each morning and sit in silence afterward, training the ear of a disciple before speaking.
Memorable Quote
What is most important is not the volume of the voice from heaven, but the words and understanding what God is saying and that we absorb and take in those words and respond to it with action.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon (part 2 of Hearing A Voice From Heaven) centers on the Torah portion Yitro (Exodus 19-20), where God speaks audibly from Mount Sinai amid thunder, lightning, and shofar blasts. Rabbi Rosenberg traces multiple biblical instances of the bat kol—at Sinai, in Isaiah's temple vision, at Yeshua's baptism, during the Transfiguration, and in John 12—revealing a pattern of God breaking through to gain human attention. He explains that rabbinic tradition considers the bat kol a secondary, softer way God speaks, arguing the real power lies not in dramatic manifestations but in God's word taking root in our hearts.

Drawing from Peter's interruption at the Transfiguration, he warns against speaking before fully listening, noting that when your mouth is open your ears close. He connects Yeshua's wilderness temptation to show that every response to the adversary came from Scripture already planted in his heart, not from personal reasoning. The story of Ahimaaz running to King David without a message illustrates the danger of zeal without genuine understanding of what God has said.

Israel declared everything God says we will do yet quickly built a golden calf, proving emotional agreement without sustained obedience is hollow. The sermon concludes that believers must develop the ear of a disciple before they can have the voice of a disciple, walking in union with Messiah to let His word become living and active.

Hook / Wordplay

Bat kol means 'daughter of the voice'—rabbinic tradition calls it a lesser way God speaks, yet we chase the thunder when God's real power is His word quietly planted in our hearts.

2022-02-06

Overcoming the Power of Bitterness

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When holding bitterness toward someone, practice Yeshua's command daily: bless their job, family, ministry, everything about them—Marlene testifies freedom came after about three weeks of this discipline.
  • Unforgiveness defiles and destroys the one who holds it more than the offender; make a conscious decision to say 'I will not live like this' and ask God for help rather than feeling entitled to anger.
  • Surrender the desire for personal vengeance to God—'vengeance is mine, says the Lord'—and trust that He will make things right, freeing you to walk in authority rather than bondage.
One-Line Action
Identify one person you hold bitterness toward and begin praying specific blessings over them every day this week.
Memorable Quote
We do not want to hurt other people and we do not want to defile and to disabilitize ourselves. We have to make a decision and to say, I will not live like this.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Marlene, filling in for Rabbi Jan who is recovering from COVID, teaches on overcoming bitterness using the Exodus narrative of the bitter waters at Marah, where God had Moses place wood into the water to make it drinkable—a foreshadowing of Yeshua's cross transforming bitterness into life. She warns from Ephesians 4:26 that unresolved anger gives the enemy a foothold and defiles us from within more than it harms the offender. She shares a personal testimony of being deeply betrayed by a longtime friend and finding freedom by obeying Yeshua's command to bless those who curse you—praying daily blessings over the person until, after about three weeks, the bitterness lifted and that very person returned with an unexpected blessing.

She recounts the story of Holocaust survivor Rose Price forgiving a concentration camp guard from Auschwitz who asked her directly for forgiveness. She surveys biblical examples of how bitterness and jealousy devastate families: Sarah and Hagar, Jacob's favoritism producing Joseph's brothers' murderous envy, Saul's jealousy driving him to hunt David, and Absalom's rebellion rooted in David's rejection.

She holds up Joseph as a Messianic forerunner who chose forgiveness over revenge when he had total power over his brothers. She closes by urging believers to surrender vengeance to God, walk boldly in the authority Yeshua has given, and be watchmen who proclaim reconciliation rather than perpetuate generational bitterness.

Hook / Wordplay

The bitter waters of Marah become drinkable when God places wood in them—a picture of how the cross of Yeshua transforms our bitterness into something life-giving.

2022-02-13

Tetzaveh: Revealing Real Dignity & Splendor

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Practice seeing God's hand in mundane daily moments—errands, routine work, small interactions—rather than reserving spiritual awareness only for formal prayer or worship times.
  • When anxious about career, finances, or appearance, consciously redirect focus to 'seek first the kingdom' and trust that provision follows alignment with God's purposes.
  • Evaluate whether personal pursuits of status or recognition are building self-made splendor that isolates, or yielding to God's splendor that serves and blesses others.
One-Line Action
This week, intentionally pause during one ordinary daily task to recognize God's presence in it, practicing Brother Lawrence's discipline of finding splendor in the mundane.
Memorable Quote
Let's begin by blessing his name together.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The Torah portion Tetzaveh describes the priestly garments made for Aaron, which were to express God's dignity and splendor. Rabbi contrasts this divine splendor with human splendor, which is consistently compromised—Aaron's sons Nadab and Abihu brought strange fire, Eli's sons corrupted the priesthood, and Isaiah catalogued society's breakdown when leaders stray from God. Isaiah 3–5 traces how self-made splendor decays into 'a stench instead of perfume,' yet God promises to restore glory like a chuppah over Zion.

Isaiah 6:1 shows that only when the human king died could Isaiah see the Lord high and lifted up—we too must look past failed human leadership to God's throne. Yeshua's woes in Matthew 23 echo Isaiah's, calling hypocritical leaders to repentance while offering return through 'Blessed is he who comes in the name of the Lord.' In Luke 12, Yeshua teaches that Solomon in all his splendor was not clothed like the lilies—God's provision surpasses our anxiety about material dignity. The parable of the rich man and Lazarus in Luke 16 reveals that true splendor was with the beggar, not the man living in opulence.

Drawing on Brother Lawrence's 'Practicing the Presence of God,' the rabbi urges the congregation to find God's glory in every mundane detail rather than chasing self-made splendor. First Peter 1 seals the point: grass withers and flowers fade, but the word of God and those who do His will stand forever.

Hook / Wordplay

"That's not splendor like the sugar-free—this is splendor. This is the manifest presence of God." A playful Splenda/splendor distinction that sets up the sermon's core contrast between artificial human glory and God's genuine majesty.

2022-02-19

Gold Standard Leadership

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When facing pressure to compromise, recall Aaron's failure: rather than caving to the crowd, fall on your face before God and call on Him to intervene—leadership means absorbing the discomfort rather than deflecting it.
  • Do a regular spiritual 'census'—an honest self-assessment not of financial or worldly accomplishments but of whether you are yielding to God's leading and building on the foundation of Messiah.
  • When trials and testing come, reframe them as the refiner's fire that reveals genuineness—count it joy because what survives is more valuable than anything perishable.
One-Line Action
Take an honest inventory of where you are building on your own gold versus God's, and yield one area of self-reliance back to Him this week.
Memorable Quote
The gold standard wasn't to make it of gold. The gold standard was to stand strong in doing what was right and what God had ordained.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi reframes the Ki Tissa census as a call to honest self-assessment of where we stand spiritually. He contrasts two leadership models in the same Torah portion: Bezalel and Oholiab, already filled with God's spirit and gifted for holy work, versus Aaron, who caved to crowd pressure and fashioned the golden calf while Moses was on the mountain. Aaron's deflection—blaming the people and claiming the calf simply walked out of the fire—illustrates how failed leaders dodge responsibility and compromise their calling.

Moses, by contrast, offered to be blotted from the book of life to intercede for the people, embodying sacrificial leadership. The sermon then moves to Acts 3, where Peter declares silver and gold are worthless compared to what God's spirit can do, healing the lame man at the temple gate. First Peter teaches that faith tested by fire is far more precious than perishable gold, and 1 Corinthians warns to build on the only sure foundation—Messiah—because fire will reveal each person's work.

A brief excursion to Elijah shows how even a powerful leader crumbled when peer pressure shifted, underscoring the need for sustained vigilance. The rabbi closes by pointing to Yeshua as the ultimate gold standard of leadership: one who laid down his life while we were still rebels, who restored Peter after triple denial, and who calls us to transparent, humble, yielded lives rather than self-made résumés.

Hook / Wordplay

The golden calf was literally gold but represented the lowest standard of leadership; God's gold standard is character refined by fire—not what you accumulate, but what survives the furnace.

2022-02-27

More Assembly Required

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Spiritual growth is never 'done'—there is always more assembly required, more parts God is fitting together in my life, so resist the temptation to coast on past growth.
  • Giving (time, resources, self) should flow from the joy of participating in what God is building, not from obligation or competition—like the Israelites who gave so much Moses had to tell them to stop.
  • Don't neglect assembling with community; mutual encouragement and building each other up cannot happen in isolation, and Hebrews 10:25 frames it as increasingly urgent as the day approaches.
One-Line Action
Identify one area where you have been holding back from wholehearted giving—time, resources, or commitment—and give cheerfully this week.
Memorable Quote
Love is that one commodity, you can give it all away and there's more that comes in. It's like a continuous filling up, filling up of God's love and we give it away and in the process we experience it more and more.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon unpacks the Torah portion Vayakhel (Exodus 35-36), where Moses assembles Israel to build the tabernacle, exploring multiple layers of 'assembly.' The people gathered, gave so generously Moses had to stop them, and assembled the tabernacle so God could dwell among them. The rabbi contrasts this wholehearted giving with the earlier golden calf episode, where the same people gave eagerly for the wrong thing. Using 2 Corinthians 9, he teaches that cheerful giving overflows in thanksgiving and that God's love is a commodity that never runs out.

Through Hebrews 9-10, he connects the tabernacle to Yeshua's sacrifice, which opened direct access to God's presence and purified the conscience. Ephesians 4 frames the body of Messiah as an ongoing assembly where each part must work together, building itself up in love.

He applies the theme to current events—ordinary Ukrainians and Canadian truckers giving wholeheartedly for something bigger than themselves—as modern illustrations of sacrificial commitment. He closes with Hebrews 12, urging the congregation to run with endurance and assemble joy, drawing a spiritual line of wholehearted commitment.

Hook / Wordplay

'More Assembly Required' layers the consumer-product cliché into three spiritual dimensions: assembling as community, assembling God's dwelling place, and God assembling each believer—none of which is ever finished. He also found a 'supply chain' in 1 Kings 7:17's 'chained wreaths,' joking there was no supply chain problem when God was the provider.

2022-03-06

P'kudei: Properly Balancing Your Life Account

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Practice contentment as a discipline: whether in seasons of abundance or scarcity, the secret is staying in union with Messiah rather than letting circumstances dictate inner peace.
  • Shift the ratio of encouragement to correction—name the God-given strengths you see in people regularly so that when adjustment is needed, it lands in a context of affirmation rather than isolation.
  • When facing obstacles beyond your capacity, treat them as invitations to deepen trust rather than signals to retreat; God uses impossible situations to build faith that gets credited to your account.
One-Line Action
This week, identify one person whose strengths you have never verbally acknowledged and speak a specific encouragement to them.
Memorable Quote
How much better to have God come in as the accountant in our life and line up these things in advance for us and help us to understand what the balance, the proper balance is.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The parasha P'kudei (accounts) describes the completion of the tabernacle exactly as God commanded Moses, prompting the rabbi to frame spiritual life through an accounting metaphor: everything must be balanced. When Israel obeyed fully, God's Shekinah presence filled both the tabernacle and later Solomon's temple so powerfully that the priests could not minister—a divine sign-off on their faithful work. Drawing from Romans 4, the rabbi explains that Abraham's trust was credited to his account as righteousness, not through works but through faith in God's promises even when circumstances seemed impossible.

He connects this pattern to David facing Goliath, the Maccabees in hiding, and Esther's Purim deliverance—each time God turned impossible odds into victories for those who trusted Him. Philippians 4 provides the key to balance: Paul learned contentment whether in abundance or need, declaring he could do all things through Messiah who strengthens him. Paul's deepest concern was not the gifts he received but increasing the credit balance of others' spiritual accounts.

The rabbi applies this to congregational life, teaching that believers are lively stones fit together by God, and that consistent encouragement of others' God-given gifts produces transformation far more effectively than only pointing out faults. Good deeds and works are the byproduct of walking in union with Messiah, not the basis of our identity before God.

Hook / Wordplay

The Hebrew name P'kudei (accounts) becomes a double metaphor: the tabernacle's material inventory mirrors the spiritual ledger God keeps for each life, where faith—not works—is credited as righteousness.

2022-03-13

How Courage Grows (Esther Style)

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Courage is not a prerequisite for obedience — Esther acted despite fear and courage grew through the process, so step forward even when you feel unready.
  • Mordechai's faith that deliverance would come 'by another way' shows that trusting God's sovereignty frees you from the paralyzing belief that everything depends on you alone.
  • Encourage others not just with words but by investing your presence and attention in their lives, because courage is contagious when demonstrated through relationship and action.
One-Line Action
When fear paralyzes you, take one concrete step of obedience and let God grow the courage you need through the process.
Memorable Quote
To be able to see our courage grow, we have to be in situations where our courage is challenged, where our faith is challenged, and we hold firm to the things that God says.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The rabbi opens with the Torah portion Vayikra and highlights Leviticus 4:13, where even inadvertent communal sin carries guilt, though God provides a path of atonement. He connects Israel's exile in Shushan to their violations of God's commands, setting the stage for the Esther narrative. The central thesis is that courage is not a trait people possess in advance but something that grows through the very crises that demand it.

Esther began as a quiet, sheltered young queen who initially tried to avoid involvement, but Mordechai's blunt warning in Esther 4:13-14 catalyzed her transformation into bold action. Mordechai himself displayed deep faith by asserting that deliverance would come regardless, trusting God's faithfulness even if Esther refused. The rabbi draws modern parallels to Rosa Parks and Martin Luther King, who did not set out seeking courage but found it rising within them when circumstances required it.

He then anchors the message in Hebrews 10:35-39, urging the congregation not to throw away their courage because the One who made the promise is trustworthy. The sermon closes with the exhortation that courage grows not from self-reliance but from yielding to God, spending time in His Word, and investing in one another through presence and action rather than mere words.

Hook / Wordplay

Courage is not a supply you stockpile in advance; it grows Esther-style, forged in the furnace of the very crisis that demands it.

2022-03-20

Tzav: Sin, Forgiveness & Restitution

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When facing accusation, shift the dialogue from the adversary to God: instead of spiraling in guilt, say 'Left to myself I'm capable of worse, but I'm not left to myself' — then ask God to reveal what He actually wants to show you.
  • Don't settle for minimum-viable obedience; keep the fire burning on the altar continually — the Shabbat candle principle of letting devotion burn fully rather than blowing it out early applies to daily spiritual life.
  • Forgiveness is not the finish line but the starting point: the progression runs sin → forgiveness → restitution → restoration → reconciliation, and our calling is to carry that reconciliation forward to others.
One-Line Action
This week, when guilt or accusation surfaces, redirect the conversation to God and ask Him what He actually wants to reveal rather than engaging the accuser.
Memorable Quote
Doesn't matter what the accusation is, I say, left to myself.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi teaches from Torah portion Tzav (Leviticus 6:1), which commands Aaron and his sons regarding the burnt offerings and the perpetual fire on the altar. He connects this to the sin offerings in Leviticus 4, emphasizing that even inadvertent sins carry guilt — ignorance is no excuse before God. The sacrificial system was not designed to leave people trapped in guilt but to open an avenue of return, forgiveness, and restoration.

Drawing from Matthew 5, he teaches that Yeshua raised the standard beyond outward action to the heart and commanded making restitution with a brother before offering gifts at the altar. He shares powerful personal testimony of experiencing God's unconditional love — hearing God say 'I knew those things were there before I called you, and I called you anyway.' He offers a practical framework for handling spiritual accusation: shift the conversation from the adversary to God, responding 'Left to myself I'm capable of worse, but I'm not left to myself.' From 1 John 1, he teaches that acknowledging sin rather than hiding it is the path to purification and freedom.

He connects Isaiah 53's 'asham' (guilt offering) language to Yeshua as the suffering servant whose death provides atonement and whose resurrection establishes restitution for all that was lost. The sermon traces a full arc from sin through guilt, forgiveness, restitution, restoration, and ultimately to the ministry of reconciliation — we who are reconciled are called to reconcile others to God.

Hook / Wordplay

The Hebrew 'Ola' (burnt offering) literally means 'what goes up' — praise ascending to God mirrors the offering rising from the altar, and the fire must never go out.

2022-03-27

Sh'mini: Being Called Is Not Enough

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Being invited to the banquet is the beginning, not the destination—I need to 'put on the garments' God provides by actively building goodness, self-control, and love into daily life rather than resting on the identity of being called.
  • Anything that blurs the line between holy and common—whether literal substances or over-absorption in work, ego, or comfort—functions like the intoxication that compromised Nadav and Avihu; I should regularly audit what is dulling my spiritual discernment.
  • Saying 'I know, I know' is often the exact moment I am most at risk of not doing—genuine knowledge shows up in action, not in deflecting reminders.
One-Line Action
Identify one area where you have settled for the title of 'called' without actively pursuing the wholeness that calling demands, and take a concrete step this week.
Memorable Quote
Being called the great power is not enough. Being called a man of God is not enough.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi Rosenberg teaches from Parashat Sh'mini (Leviticus 9–10), where Nadav and Avihu were consecrated as priests yet brought unauthorized fire before God—likely because intoxication compromised their ability to distinguish holy from common. He draws a parallel to Uzzah steadying the Ark with good intentions but the wrong prescribed method (2 Samuel 6), and to Yeshua's wedding feast parable where an invited guest refused the garments the king provided (Matthew 22). He extends the pattern through Ananias and Sapphira, who lied about their offering while appearing generous (Acts 5), and Simon the sorcerer, who tried to purchase the Holy Spirit's power (Acts 8).

The unifying thesis is that being called by God is only the starting point—integrity, from the word 'integer' meaning whole numbers, requires wholeness of commitment. From 2 Peter 1 he traces the progression: faith furnished with goodness, knowledge, self-control, perseverance, godliness, brotherly affection, and love. He closes with 1 Peter's charge to become holy in our entire way of life, stressing that God always wants to do more in us than we settle for, and we must not treat His unlimited resources as though they have a ceiling.

Hook / Wordplay

'Integrity' comes from 'integer'—whole numbers, not fractions. God doesn't want a fractional commitment; He wants the whole number.

2022-04-03

Birthing A Nation

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Prayer is not begging God to act but an intimate space where He births understanding and vision—make withdrawing to pray a deliberate practice like Yeshua did.
  • God uses discomfort and stirring as spiritual contractions; when the nest is disturbed, recognize it as His way of pushing you into a new phase rather than a sign something is wrong.
  • Be physically and spiritually present where God directs—Simeon almost missed seeing Messiah, Peter could have skipped the temple on Shavuot; positioning yourself in obedience is how breakthroughs arrive.
One-Line Action
Set aside a regular time this week to withdraw and seek God's face in prayer, treating it as the place where He births new purpose in you.
Memorable Quote
Prayer is not a time of begging God to do something. It's the place of intimacy with Him where He begins to birth something in us that we're unable to fully picture or unable to fully fathom.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi opens the month of Nissan by connecting the Torah portion Tazria (she conceived) to the theme of birthing a nation, noting that Passover marks Israel's emergence from Egypt as a true nation rather than merely a captive family. He frames the plagues and Exodus as birth contractions that brought forth a people with a constitution (Torah) and a destiny. He parallels this national birth with personal spiritual rebirth, emphasizing that God continually births new things in believers and uses uncomfortable circumstances as contractions pushing us into new phases.

He illustrates through Naaman's healing in 2 Kings 5, showing how anger and unmet expectations were birth pangs of transformation. He then traces Yeshua's own presentation at the temple where Simeon received the fulfillment of a lifelong promise simply by being where the Spirit directed him.

Drawing from Luke 5, he highlights Yeshua's practice of withdrawing to pray as the source of His ability to do what the Father was doing. He closes with a personal testimony from Bible school where God told him He knew his flaws before calling him, describing this as the deepest birthing moment of understanding unconditional love.

Hook / Wordplay

Tazria means 'she conceived'—the rabbi extends the birth metaphor across every scale: a nation born at Passover, a body of Messiah born at Pentecost, and new life continually born in each believer through prayer and obedience.

2022-04-10

Removing Hametz with Light

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • During the pre-Passover chametz search, treat it as a spiritual inventory—identify one specific area where a 'small' compromise has been quietly expanding like leaven and bring it before God honestly.
  • When God exposes an uncomfortable truth about my behavior or attitudes, receive it as refiner's fire meant to restore and purify, not as condemnation meant to destroy—He is purging dross so I can come forth as gold.
  • Practice the distinction between destructive gossip (lashon hara) and necessary intervention: speak up when someone's wellbeing is at stake, but refuse to chatter merely to accuse or spread rumors about others.
One-Line Action
This week before Passover, name one specific area of spiritual chametz—a compromise or habit you have been dismissing as small—and honestly surrender it to God for removal.
Memorable Quote
If God is not in control in our lives, somebody else is and it's not us.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon opens with the Torah portion Metzora (Leviticus 14), framing the purification process after tzara'at as a transition from darkness into light, and connecting the traditional rabbinic link between tzara'at and lashon hara (evil tongue). Since this Shabbat is Shabbat HaGadol, the week before Passover when chametz is removed from homes, the rabbi extends the metaphor to removing spiritual leaven—selfishness, deception, and compromise—from our hearts. Drawing from 1 Corinthians 5, he shows Paul warning the Corinthian community that tolerating sin, specifically condoning sexual immorality in their midst, is like letting a little chametz leaven the whole batch of dough.

He turns to the Malachi 3 haftarah for Shabbat HaGadol, where God promises to purify the sons of Levi like a refiner's fire—not to destroy them but to restore them to righteous service. Connecting this to Matthew 21, he demonstrates that Yeshua's cleansing of the temple during the week before Passover directly fulfilled the Malachi prophecy the people had just heard, which explains why the leaders asked 'by what authority' rather than dismissing him as deranged. Through the parable of the wicked tenants and Matthew 23's woes, he illustrates how sin progressively distorts reasoning until people believe their corruption is justified, just as the tenants convinced themselves they could kill the heir and inherit the vineyard.

He clarifies that Yeshua's rebuke was directed at hypocritical Pharisees specifically, not all Pharisees, correcting the common misunderstanding that 'Pharisee' means 'hypocrite.' The sermon culminates with Yeshua weeping over Jerusalem—not from self-pity at rejection, but from compassion for the people's blindness to their own moment of redemption. The closing call is that the same Passover season urging us to clean chametz from our homes is urging us to let God clean the leaven of sin from our hearts so we can move from darkness into His marvelous light.

Hook / Wordplay

'Removing hametz with light'—the same candlelight used to search kitchen crevices for crumbs before Passover is the spiritual light God uses to expose and purge the leaven of sin hiding in the crevices of our hearts.

2022-04-23

Pesach: Why Is This Shabbat Service Different Than Any Other Service?

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Approach the Passover Seder 'as if we ourselves were truly there'—the participatory framework is what unlocks the deeper meaning of both the Exodus and Messiah's sacrifice
  • When encountering Yeshua's difficult 'this is my body' language, place it back into the Seder context of the Afikoman rather than treating it as an isolated theological puzzle
  • Don't wait passively for God to act—the Reb Schmouel teaching that 'Messiah is waiting for us' means we can hasten redemption by opening the door of our hearts now
One-Line Action
Open the door of your heart to Messiah now rather than waiting—He is already knocking and waiting for you.
Memorable Quote
You will never go wrong giving Messiah full access to our hearts. He will bring things beyond our imagination, beyond our expectation.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The service was a full interactive walkthrough of the Messianic Passover Haggadah, with the congregation participating in readings, songs, and symbolic foods. Rabbi guided the assembly through each Seder element—the four cups of wine, matzah, bitter herbs, charoset, and the ceremonial washings—explaining both their traditional Jewish significance and their Messianic parallels. He emphasized that the Seder's structure itself illuminates Yeshua's actions at his last Passover, from reclining at the table to identifying Judas specifically at the dipping of matza in bitter herbs.

The Afikoman received extended treatment as a profound Messianic symbol: the middle matzah broken, wrapped in white linen, hidden away, and redeemed before the third cup—mirroring Messiah's affliction, burial, and resurrection on the third day. He addressed the centuries-old communion debate by arguing that placing the event back into its original Jewish Seder context resolves the mystery of Yeshua's words 'this is my body.' The rabbi highlighted that traditional Jewish Haggadot themselves contain overtly Messianic language, including prayers for the days of Messiah and the Afikoman's role at the great future Seder.

He shared an Orthodox rabbi's account of Reb Schmouel in Jerusalem declaring 'Messiah is waiting for us,' bridging traditional Jewish longing with Messianic fulfillment. The service closed with a call to open our hearts fully to Messiah and the singing of Hatikvah, looking toward ultimate redemption.

Hook / Wordplay

The title echoes the Mah Nishtanah's 'Why is this night different from all other nights?' reframed for a Shabbat Seder service that walks the congregation through the Haggadah experientially rather than just teaching about it.

2022-04-24

Reset: Do You Want to Start Over?

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When things start coming together but aren't yet complete, resist the urge to 'take it from here'—yield to God through the entire process, not just the desperate parts.
  • Don't evaluate your spiritual life by circumstances alone; the Lazarus story shows God works beyond every human threshold of 'too late.'
  • Practice daily micro-resets through moment-by-moment relationship with God so that course corrections stay small rather than requiring a full restart from scratch.
One-Line Action
Each morning, consciously yield the day's plans and anxieties to God as a deliberate reset rather than waiting for a crisis to force one.
Memorable Quote
You haven't missed it if you're in union with him. You haven't missed it if God is in control.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The rabbi frames the post-Passover season as fundamentally about resurrection, connecting the Ezekiel 36-37 dry bones haftarah to God's desire to continually reset and restore His people. He begins with Moses in Exodus 33 asking God to continue showing favor, illustrating that relationship with God requires ongoing pursuit rather than a one-time encounter. The Ezekiel dry bones vision shows God breathing life into what seems completely dead and beyond hope, modeling how God revives both nations and individuals.

Turning to John 11, the rabbi emphasizes that Yeshua waited four days past the Jewish traditional threshold of possible revival to demonstrate no situation is too far gone. Martha's limited theological response is reset by Yeshua's declaration 'I am the resurrection and the life,' reframing resurrection from a distant future event to a present relational reality. The sermon connects to Matthew 28's Great Commission, noting that Yeshua delegates His authority and commissions disciples to immerse others into the reality of relationship with God.

A Colonel Sanders illustration grounds the abstract resurrection concept by showing how the death of a lifelong business at age 65 became the reset that launched something far greater. The rabbi urges daily yielding to God rather than waiting for crisis, arguing that consistent relationship means resets require only small course corrections rather than starting from scratch.

Hook / Wordplay

The sermon title 'Reset: Do You Want to Start Over?' appeared on screen like a computer error prompt, fooling congregants into thinking there was a technical glitch—mirroring how God's resets can look like system failures before revealing new life.

2022-05-01

Are You Going Back to Where You Used to Live?

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When nostalgia for 'the old days' surfaces, recall the full picture — Israel remembered the leeks and garlic but forgot the slavery; evaluate what you were delivered from, not just what you miss.
  • Spiritual drift happens in tiny increments (one degree off course, one paper cut) that compound over distance; regular realignment with God's Word prevents arriving somewhere unrecognizable.
  • Both sin and spiritual fruit are communal, not private — they manifest in how you treat others; invest in honest community as the proving ground where your walk with God becomes real.
One-Line Action
Identify one area where you are quietly drifting back toward an old pattern and deliberately resubmit it to God this week.
Memorable Quote
Humility is that little something you lose the moment you discover you have it.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon centers on Leviticus 18:3, where God warns Israel not to return to the practices of Egypt where they used to live, nor adopt the ways of Canaan where they are headed. The rabbi frames this as a timeless question: are we drifting back toward the old nature we were delivered from? He uses Ezekiel 22 to catalogue the consequences of communal moral decay — violence, corruption, gossip, oppression of the vulnerable — and notes God's haunting words that He sought someone to stand in the gap but found no one.

The sermon transitions to Galatians 5, where Paul warns believers who started well but allowed small compromises to leaven the whole batch. The contrast between the works of the flesh and the fruit of the Spirit is presented as inherently relational — both lists describe how we treat others, not private spiritual states. Matthew 24's warning that love will grow cold because of increased distance from Torah is reframed as practical instruction for any era, not only end-times prophecy.

Several vivid illustrations — a one-degree navigation error, a paper cut disabling the whole body, a broad jumper who still falls short of a thirty-foot chasm — make the abstract concept of spiritual drift concrete. The rabbi closes by urging the congregation to order their lives day by day by the Spirit, drawing near to God rather than coasting on past experiences, so that the fruit of their lives becomes attractive testimony to those who do not yet know Him.

Hook / Wordplay

"Where you used to live" operates on two levels — the literal land of Egypt that Israel left behind, and the spiritual territory of the old nature that believers are constantly tempted to revisit.

2022-05-08

Kedoshim: Building a Neighborhood

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Being a true neighbor means engaging rather than crossing the street to avoid involvement; even stopping to pray for someone is better than turning a blind eye.
  • Frank honesty requires earned relationship first—without genuine connection, correction registers as judgment and shuts down communication entirely.
  • When someone's life is at stake, silence is the sin, not speaking up; bring concerns to the right people with humility rather than discussing them with everyone else under the guise of avoiding Lashon Hara.
One-Line Action
This week, notice one person on the margins of your daily path and take a concrete step to include them—share a meal, ask a frank caring question, or simply make eye contact and acknowledge their humanity.
Memorable Quote
Love can be honest, love can be frank, but you can't have love without relationship.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon explores Parashat Kedoshim (Leviticus 19), arguing that 'love your neighbor as yourself' does not mean passive tolerance but active, honest, invested community. Through the Good Samaritan parable in Luke 10, Rabbi shows that the priest and Levite who passed the beaten man robbed him a second and third time—of dignity, personhood, and acknowledgment. He shares a personal story of inviting a homeless man into a pizza shop, illustrating how small acts of inclusion restore a person's sense of belonging.

Walking through Leviticus 19's specific commands—don't defraud, don't curse the deaf, don't obstruct the blind, judge without favoritism—he frames each as a building block of genuine neighborhood. He distinguishes slander from righteous concern: don't spread gossip, but also don't stand idly by when a neighbor's life is at stake, and bring concerns to the right people rather than discussing them with everyone else. The 'Fred in the mall' illustration shows how avoiding frank conversation breeds resentment over imaginary offenses.

Drawing on Amos 9, he connects neighborhood-building to God's promise to restore the fallen booth of David and rebuild the ruins. He closes with a powerful encounter with a woman who challenged him about homosexuality and hell, where his response—'you can go to hell for a lot less; all of us are headed there, but God made a provision and none of us have to go'—opened real dialogue by placing himself in the same category rather than above her.

Hook / Wordplay

Riffs on Mr. Rogers' 'Won't you be my neighbor?'—joking about bringing a cardigan and changing into sneakers—then redefines neighborhood through Leviticus 19 as holy community built on frank love, not polite distance.

2022-05-15

Designated Times for a Designated People

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When facing a season of difficulty or discomfort, stay in position rather than bolting—God may be building strength through the struggle that only becomes visible later, as illustrated by the wholesale job story.
  • If you're going to do something difficult or serve someone anyway, do it early with joy and enthusiasm rather than grudgingly after internal debate—the outcome is the same but the heart posture transforms you.
  • Spiritual maturity is not claiming to be a teacher but continuously exercising the ability to distinguish good from evil; don't assume you've graduated past the basics when Hebrews warns that many who should be teachers still need milk.
One-Line Action
Stay faithful in the uncomfortable season you are in right now, trusting that God is using this appointed time to build strength and discernment you cannot yet see.
Memorable Quote
The cornerstone of our faith from Devahreem to the Lord.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi Yann teaches from the Torah portion Emor (Leviticus 21-23), focusing on the Kohanim's responsibilities and the designated appointed times. Drawing from Ezekiel 44, he highlights the Sons of Zadok who stayed faithful when Israel strayed, stressing that priestly leadership means teaching by demonstration, not just instruction. Using Matthew 5:38-48, he explores Yeshua's call to go beyond the letter of the law—turning the other cheek, giving more than demanded, and loving enemies—illustrated vividly by the story of Dan who handed his money to an anti-missionary challenger.

He references Richard Wurmbrand praying for his prison persecutors to show genuine love for enemies in practice. From Galatians 4 he explains that at the appointed time God sent Messiah, warning against turning holidays into empty ritual divorced from relationship. His personal story of enduring a miserable wholesale job in Philadelphia demonstrates how God's appointed seasons of difficulty build unseen strength.

He concludes with Hebrews 5, connecting Yeshua learning obedience through suffering to our growth from spiritual milk to solid food—the capacity to distinguish good from evil. The sermon ties the arc together: God's designated times are the process by which He transforms His designated people into mature, discerning leaders.

Hook / Wordplay

Designated times for a designated people—God uses His calendar appointments not just to mark holidays, but to shape who we become through the process of walking them out.

2022-05-22

The Cure for Dystopia

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • The Jubilee was ancient Israel's social safety net: when someone fell on hard times, the community absorbed the loss and restored them — a model for how congregations should function rather than exploit vulnerability.
  • Jeremiah buying a field during captivity teaches that investing in God's future promises is an act of faith even when present circumstances look hopeless — press toward the mark rather than fixating on the darkness.
  • 'Do not become slaves of other human beings' applies not only to physical bondage but to allowing others to manipulate how we think, speak, and act — guarding mental and spiritual freedom is an ongoing discipline.
One-Line Action
This week, identify one neighbor or community member going through a hard time and take a concrete step to restore them — a meal, a conversation, practical help — embodying the Jubilee principle of not exploiting but lifting up.
Memorable Quote
From Devariim due to Rana Mi 6 4, the cornerstone of our faith.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi opens the B'Har portion from Leviticus 25, explaining the Jubilee year system where land reverted to original owners, debts were forgiven, and community members were restored rather than exploited. He frames these laws as God's designed antidote to dystopia, then surveys an extraordinary range of dystopian examples from film (Soylent Green, The Matrix, Hunger Games, Meet John Doe, Westworld) and history (Holocaust, Mao's famine, Rwandan genocide, the siege of Jerusalem in 70 CE) to show that societal collapse is not science fiction but a recurring pattern when people abandon God's principles. He highlights how Jeremiah was told to buy a field during captivity as an act of faith in future restoration.

Drawing from Luke 4, he connects Yeshua's Jubilee proclamation of freedom for captives to the same restorative vision. The core argument is that dystopia emerges whenever humans dehumanize one another, seek control, and drift from God, and the cure is not political revolution but returning to God's word, loving neighbors practically, and allowing the Spirit to transform communities one person at a time.

Hook / Wordplay

Dystopia is not a disease you see on a pharmaceutical commercial — but there is an antidote, and it was prescribed in Leviticus 25.

2022-05-29

B'Chukkotai — Regulations

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When I notice myself rationalizing small compromises ('it's not really that bad'), recognize that as the exact pattern of boundary-erosion the rabbi describes — name it and course-correct before the lines blur further.
  • Internalize Ecclesiastes 8:11 as a diagnostic tool: when I see consequences delayed (in culture or in my own life), understand that the absence of accountability emboldens the wrong direction — don't mistake mercy for permission.
  • Actively seek 'divine appointments' during the week to share hope with people in dark places, rather than waiting for them to show up at services — transformed hearts start with personal encounters, not institutional programs.
One-Line Action
This week, identify one boundary you've been quietly blurring and reestablish it before the darkness of compromise sets in.
Memorable Quote
It's not just a matter of deadening the feeling of hopelessness that will give people hope. It's giving people hope that will destroy hopelessness.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The Torah portion B'Chukkotai opens with extraordinary covenant blessings for obedience — rain, harvest, security, victory — then pivots to devastating consequences for disobedience: terror, defeat, exile, and paranoia. Rabbi frames the central thesis as 'regulated deregulation of God's regulations,' arguing that when society removes God's moral boundaries it does not achieve freedom but merely installs darker regulators in God's place. He connects Ecclesiastes 8:11 — that delayed punishment emboldens evil — to the current crisis of violent crime, mass shootings, and judicial leniency.

Drawing on 1 John 2 and John 14-15, he shows that obedience to God's commands is the pathway to joy, love, and clear spiritual sight, while Romans 1 maps the downward spiral of a culture that suppresses truth. He addresses the Uvalde school shooting and broader cultural decay, arguing that pills, legislation, and political posturing cannot substitute for genuine hope rooted in relationship with God. The sermon closes with 2 Chronicles 7:14, calling believers to humble themselves and seek God's face so that transformed hearts — not more laws — can heal the land and restore hope to the hopeless.

Hook / Wordplay

Regulated deregulation of God's regulations — society doesn't eliminate rules, it replaces God's protective boundaries with its own darker regime.

2022-06-04

Bamidbar / Shavuot: Countdown to Empowerment

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Treat each day as preparation for an empowerment you cannot predict—worship and yield now so you are ready when God opens an unexpected door.
  • God's most powerful moves come through people who did not plan or rehearse them; stop measuring your adequacy and focus on availability.
  • Unity with other believers is not optional atmosphere—it is the 'critical mass' precondition that releases God's power, as seen in Acts 2:1.
One-Line Action
Each morning this week, pray specifically for God to open one divine appointment you cannot anticipate, and walk into your day expecting it.
Memorable Quote
It was watching God do something that I could never have done on my own and I'm so glad that he let me be a part of it.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The rabbi opens with Parashat Bamidbar, noting that the wilderness (midbar) is where God isolates us from distractions to take account of where we stand. He frames Shavuot as the culmination of a fifty-day countdown from Passover, moving from deliverance to empowerment, likening it to a ketubah—a marriage covenant defining the relationship between God and His people. He corrects the common misconception that the Acts 2 outpouring occurred in an upper room, demonstrating from Luke 24:53 that the disciples were worshiping daily in the Temple.

He highlights that the Shavuot Haftarah reading from Ezekiel 1, with its fire, rushing wind, and glory hovering over living creatures, was read just before strikingly similar manifestations broke out in the Temple that day. Peter's bold sermon was entirely unplanned—God opened the door no human scheduling could have arranged—and thousands responded. Drawing on the Hosea Haftarah, the rabbi shows God betrothing an unfaithful people back to Himself in righteousness and faithfulness.

He illustrates unexpected empowerment through a personal story of a subway encounter where God gave him a prophetic word for a stranger. The overarching call is to yield daily to God's preparations, anticipate His movement even when the form is unknown, and trust that divine empowerment exceeds any human ability or resume.

Hook / Wordplay

Shavuot as a 'countdown to empowerment'—just as the Omer counts down grain measures toward harvest, the disciples counted down toward an outpouring they could not have scripted or scheduled.

2022-06-12

Naso: Consecration of a Nation

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Consecration is not suffering or ritual deprivation—it is deliberately separating from distractions to feast on God's presence, like Mother Brit's first-week-of-the-month shut-in prayer time.
  • We are alarmingly susceptible to external voices (one comment can derail confidence); maintaining a consecrated posture means anchoring identity in God's word rather than others' opinions.
  • National and communal healing (2 Chronicles 7:14) depends on individuals who personally own their commitment to God—you cannot inherit or grandfather your way into the kingdom.
One-Line Action
Choose one recurring distraction to fast from this week—not to suffer, but to redirect that time toward seeking God's presence and purpose.
Memorable Quote
The consecration of a nation starts with the consecration of an individual.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon explores the Nazirite vow of Numbers 6 as a lens for understanding personal and national consecration to God. Rabbi notes that the vow applied to both men and women and was not about ritual mechanics but about intentionally separating from everyday life to draw near to the Lord. He examines Samson as a cautionary example—someone consecrated from birth who never personally owned the commitment, playing frivolously with his anointing and ultimately motivated by revenge rather than God's glory.

Paul in Acts 21, by contrast, demonstrates a truly consecrated life, navigating false accusations and mob violence with wisdom and authority rooted in his relationship with God. Romans 12 frames consecration as offering ourselves as living sacrifices, being transformed by renewed minds rather than conformed to the world. The sermon connects Daniel 9:24's anointing of the especially holy place to the Shavuot outpouring of the Spirit, arguing the disciples' ten days of prayer in the Temple were an act of consecration that preceded God's powerful response.

The Aaronic benediction closing Numbers 6 reveals God's heart toward His people—blessing, grace, and peace—which naturally motivates the individual to consecrate in return. The central thesis is that national transformation begins with individual consecration: moment-by-moment yielding to God rather than going through religious motions.

Hook / Wordplay

Nazir (Nazirite) vs. Natzeret (Nazareth)—artists misread the Hebrew and gave Yeshua long hair, but He drank wine and touched the dead, so He was no Nazirite.

2022-06-19

B'ha'alotkha: Bad Before Good

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When I catch myself chronically complaining or blaming others, I am emulating the accuser rather than God—redirect toward gratitude and trust in His provision.
  • Hardships are passages on the journey, not permanent destinations; resist the urge to 'go back to Egypt' by romanticizing past situations that were actually bondage.
  • Adopt honest faith language in daily life: 'always better than I know and getting better every day'—because God does exceeding abundantly beyond what I can ask or imagine.
One-Line Action
This week, catch every complaint before it leaves your mouth and reframe it as recognition of something God is already working for your good.
Memorable Quote
If you find that you're in the category of constantly complaining or accusing someone else, we are emulating not God's mercy and love, but the accuser of the brethren. We are emulating a different father.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon explores Torah portion B'ha'alotkha (Numbers 8–12), centering on the Israelites' pattern of complaining despite God's miraculous provision. Influenced by the mixed crowd, the people grumbled about manna and romanticized Egyptian food—conveniently forgetting they were slaves. Even Moses doubted God could feed 600,000 men, yet God provided quail in overwhelming abundance.

Miriam's leprosy after criticizing Moses illustrated that even leaders face consequences for lashon hara, and the whole nation waited for her restoration. The Haftarah from Zechariah presents the high priest Joshua standing in dung-covered garments before God, who strips away the filth and reclothes him in royal splendor—a vivid picture of divine transformation. Paul in 2 Corinthians 4 reframes crushing hardships as 'light momentary afflictions' that produce an eternal weight of glory, and Romans 8 declares nothing can separate us from God's love.

The central question—'How bad does it get before it gets good?'—is answered with the insight that when we grasp God's commitment, even the bad is already good because we are passing through, not settling there. The sermon closes by calling believers to stop emulating the accuser through chronic complaint and instead trust God's transformative process.

Hook / Wordplay

How bad does it get before it gets good? It starts getting good the moment you realize God's goodness is better than any world bad.

2022-06-26

Shlakh L'kha — A Lifeline from God

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When facing an uncertain outcome, catch yourself ruminating on worst-case scenarios and consciously redirect attention to God's track record of faithfulness rather than your own narrow read of the situation.
  • Treat the small daily reminders of faith (prayer, Sabbath practice, community) the way the tzitzit functioned — as guardrails that keep you from drifting before you realize you have drifted.
  • Look back at past crises that felt impossible but resolved; let those build a resource of courage for the next challenge rather than treating each new trial as if no precedent of deliverance exists.
One-Line Action
When fear spirals begin, pause and recount one specific time God came through for you, then act from that memory instead of the fear.
Memorable Quote
I've discovered that the love of God is so powerful that even if it seems like a narrow thread, maybe even to the dimensions of a hair.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The Torah portion recounts the 12 spies sent to survey Canaan; 10 returned with a demoralizing report that led Israel to 40 years in the wilderness, while Caleb and Joshua urged trust in God's promise. Numbers 15 then commands Israel to wear tzitzit with a blue thread as a tangible reminder not to follow their own hearts into destruction. The Haftorah from Joshua 2 reveals that for those same 40 years, the people of Jericho lived in terror of Israel — the very enemies the spies feared were themselves afraid.

Rahab's scarlet cord hung from her window became a literal lifeline for her family, paralleling the tzitzit as a thread connecting people to God's faithfulness. Rabbi Schwartz connects this to the woman healed by touching Yeshua's tzitzit and to Peter walking on water, noting that in both stories Yeshua's response begins with 'courage.' He warns against the pattern of ruminating on worst-case scenarios until fear hardens the heart against trust.

Drawing from Ecclesiastes, he teaches that a threefold cord — God, spouse, community — carries a strength no single strand can match. The sermon closes with a call to let God weave His presence into every detail of life rather than unraveling under pressure.

Hook / Wordplay

A lifeline from God — or hanging on by a thread? The tzitzit fringes and Rahab's scarlet cord are both literal threads that became lifelines, reframing fragility as divine provision.

2022-07-03

Rebellion: When Existence Ceases To Be

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Rebellion against God often begins not from oppression but from dissatisfaction with an already privileged position — examine areas where I have been given much but want more instead of stewarding what I have with gratitude.
  • The way to resist the adversary is not through direct confrontation but by drawing near to God — prioritize closeness to God as the primary defensive strategy when facing spiritual opposition.
  • Moses and Aaron interceded for the very people attacking them — when others oppose or accuse me, the godly response is to fall on my face before God for their sake, not to celebrate their downfall.
One-Line Action
This week, when tempted to assert your own way, pause and ask whether you are drawing near to God or drawing near to yourself.
Memorable Quote
Let's be the Lord who is blessed, let's be the Lord who is blessed forever and ever.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon explores the Torah portion Korach (Numbers 16–18), examining how Korah and 250 leaders of Israel rebelled against Moses and Aaron despite already holding positions of high honor. Rabbi draws out the irony that these were not oppressed masses but privileged insiders who wanted even more power, and that their distorted perspective led them to call Egypt 'the land of milk and honey.' He reframes the popular concept of 'existential threats': the true existential threat is rebellion against God, which causes our spiritual existence and purpose to cease to be. Using Proverbs 6:16–19, he catalogs the behaviors God detests — haughty looks, lying tongues, shedding innocent blood, sowing strife — and shows how these manifest in Korah's camp and in modern culture.

He contrasts two Sauls: King Saul, whose pride led him to care only about appearances before the people, and Paul, who humbly counted all his credentials as garbage compared to knowing God. Moses and Aaron's response to the rebellion models true spiritual leadership — they fell on their faces and interceded for the very people trying to destroy them, standing between the living and the dead.

The sermon closes with the tekelet (blue thread) teaching: Korah allegedly used his own shade of blue, symbolizing how substituting our own ideas for God's instructions leads to destruction. The antidote to rebellion is drawing near to God with humility, dying to self, and existing for Messiah and one another rather than for personal ambition.

Hook / Wordplay

'Ceasing to exist' works on two levels: Korah literally ceased to exist when the earth swallowed him, and we spiritually cease to exist when rebellion displaces humility — capped by a closing wordplay on Korah's 'wrong color blue' (tekelet dye) and the need for 'dying to self.'

2022-07-16

Blind Spots Message by Rabbi Jan at Messiah 2022

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Identify the 'precious thing' you are protecting that may be bottlenecking everything God wants to release beyond it—procrastination disguised as perfectionism is a common blind spot.
  • Stop accepting accolades for work not yet done; the pat on the back for an unfinished book or project can become a substitute for actually completing it.
  • Pray proactively for God to reveal blind spots now rather than waiting years—Rabbi Jan's prayer model: 'Lord, if there's another blind spot, I don't want to wait 10 years to find out.'
One-Line Action
Ask God this week to reveal one blind spot you have been unable to see on your own, and commit to one concrete step to address it.
Memorable Quote
Sometimes when you see God moving, your position is not to figure out what you're supposed to do, but ask God what it is he's doing.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi Jan teaches on blind spots—the hard-to-see areas in our lives that bottleneck God's purposes—using the practical analogy of adjusting car side mirrors to eliminate driving blind spots. He shares a deeply personal testimony of how a false sense of perfectionism caused him to procrastinate recording music for 12 years, and once God revealed that blind spot, he produced ten albums in the following decade. He walks through numerous biblical examples: Paul's literal and spiritual blindness on the Damascus road, Joseph's emotional blind spot about his father, Korah's fatal covetousness of Moses' role, the spies' fear-driven report, and Peter's impulsive response at the Transfiguration.

A recurring theme is that blind spots cause us to become like the idols we serve—eyes that cannot see, ears that cannot hear—echoing Psalm 115. He emphasizes that God reveals blind spots at His appointed time and that the breakthrough on the other side is always beyond what we could imagine.

He warns against accepting accolades for unfinished work, against going by the 'spin' rather than God's word, and against becoming despondent when circumstances don't match expectations. The sermon closes with the image of a master weaver taking over a tangled tapestry and creating a masterpiece, urging listeners to yield their knotted lives to God.

Hook / Wordplay

Adjusting your car mirrors to see approaching traffic instead of the side of your own car—a practical picture of how we can choose to address blind spots rather than accept them as inevitable.
