2022-07-24

Staying On Point

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When we compromise God's standards for momentary gratification, we cannot foresee the full cost — the tribe of Simeon lost over half its population and went from third largest to smallest, shrinking their inheritance permanently because of one leader's sin that the tribe stood in unity with.
  • A spirit of dullness sets in gradually after peak spiritual moments; Elijah outran a chariot one day and begged to die the next — staying on point requires vigilance precisely when the initial excitement fades.
  • The tzitzit's blue thread represents interweaving God's presence into every aspect of daily life as a tangible guard against blind spots; without deliberate reminders, our own passions blur every boundary until we cannot tell when we are crossing the line.
One-Line Action
Identify one area where you have been drifting off-point from what God has clearly said, and re-anchor to His word before the drift becomes a blind spot.
Memorable Quote
When we don't stand point, we miss the mark, which is the definition of sin to miss the mark.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon explores Parashat Pinchas, centering on how Pinchas's zealous act of putting a spear through Zimri and the Midianite woman Cozbi stopped a plague that killed 24,000 Israelites after they were seduced into idolatry. The rabbi draws a dramatic statistical comparison between the two censuses in Numbers, showing the tribe of Simeon plummeted from 59,300 (third largest tribe) to 22,200 (smallest), likely bearing the brunt of the plague because their leader Zimri initiated the sin. He frames 'staying on point' as a double meaning: Pinchas's literal spear-point and the spiritual discipline of keeping God's commands central.

Balaam serves as a cautionary example of blind spots — so consumed by greed he failed to notice the absurdity of having a conversation with his donkey. The tzitzit with the blue thread are presented as God's built-in reminder system to prevent the drift into compromise. Elijah's collapse in 1 Kings 19 illustrates how even someone who just witnessed God's supreme power on Mount Carmel can lose heart after a single threat from Jezebel.

Drawing from Romans 1, the rabbi traces a deterioration pattern: suppressing truth leads to futile thinking, darkened hearts, and eventually applauding wickedness. He closes with the assurance that the moment we turn back and stay on point with God, He promises healing and deliverance — every passage of judgment in Scripture has an avenue of return nearby.

Hook / Wordplay

"Stay on point or miss the big picture" — Pinchas's literal spear-point act of zeal becomes the metaphor for spiritual focus, where drifting off-point means missing the mark (the Hebrew definition of sin).

2022-07-31

Matot-Masei: Integrity — My Word Is My Bond

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Treat every verbal commitment — casual or formal — as a vow before God, since Torah treats spoken words as binding (Numbers 30)
  • Integrity is not passive honesty but an active stand; look for one situation this week where you can choose the harder right over the easier wrong
  • Connect the V'ahavta command ('these words shall be in your heart') to integrity: internalized truth naturally produces trustworthy speech
One-Line Action
This week, before making any promise or commitment, pause and ask: 'Am I prepared to treat this as a vow before God?'
Memorable Quote
My word is my bond. That's the statement that people make.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi introduces the double portion Matot-Masei and frames the sermon around the theme of integrity and the idea that 'my word is my bond.' He connects the Torah portion's opening laws on vows and oaths to a broader call for believers to take a stand in everything they do. The service opens with the Shema and V'ahavta, grounding the congregation in the foundational command to love God wholeheartedly. He references Yeshua's teaching that the greatest commandment is to love God and love your neighbor, linking personal integrity to relational faithfulness.

The rabbi emphasizes that saying 'my word is my bond' is more than a phrase — it is a declaration of standing for what is right. He transitions into a worship song the congregation has been learning, reinforcing the theme of taking a stand. The remainder of the sermon was not captured in the transcript due to audio-to-text limitations during the extended teaching and worship segments.

Hook / Wordplay

'My word is my bond' — a common saying reframed through Torah's laws on vows, where God holds your spoken word as a binding covenant.

2022-08-07

Preparing to Cross Over

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Don't fill in the details God hasn't specified—when a prophetic word or sense of direction comes, act on what God actually said to do (seek Him, pray, stay in the Word) rather than assuming the glamorous outcome and skipping to execution.
  • Preparation seasons that feel mundane or unproductive may be exactly what God is building—Joseph's prison years and Abraham's decades of waiting were not delays but essential formation for what came next.
  • When the crowd's pressure or popular opinion conflicts with what God is directing, be willing to leave the crowd behind, trusting that His presence in the boat is sufficient even when the storm is raging.
One-Line Action
Identify one area where you are rushing ahead of God's instructions and return to the preparatory step He actually asked you to do.
Memorable Quote
You shall teach them diligently to your children.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon opens Parashat Devarim (Deuteronomy 1), where Moses recaps Israel's wilderness journey as they prepare to cross the Jordan into the Promised Land. The rabbi highlights that an eleven-day journey took forty years due to disobedience and the spies' evil report. He connects this to Tisha B'Av, noting the tradition that the spies returned their negative report on the ninth of Av.

Using extended driving metaphors—lane lines, blind spots, phone distractions—he illustrates how spiritual distractions pull us off course. He shares vulnerable personal stories about misinterpreting God's timing, including assuming a relationship was God's will and waiting over twenty years for a ministry calling that unfolded on God's schedule. He draws parallels to Abraham and Joseph, who endured long preparation periods before seeing God's promises fulfilled.

Referencing Isaiah 1, he warns that religious observance mixed with compromise becomes repugnant to God, but repentance opens the door to restoration. From Mark 4, he highlights Yeshua's command to cross to the other side, emphasizing the need to leave the crowd behind and trust God's presence. The central thesis is that preparation—aligning with God's instructions rather than rushing ahead—matters more than the crossing itself.

Hook / Wordplay

"Crossing over" serves as a multi-layered metaphor throughout: crossing the Jordan, crossing lane lines while driving, crossover songs, and crossing into new spiritual territory—each illustrating the tension between staying in God's lane and veering off course.

Primary Theme
Trust vs Fear

Trust vs FearObedience and HolinessMessiah and Redemption

ReflectiveCautionaryVulnerableEncouraging
Season / Tag
Parashat Devarim / Tisha B'Av
Core Topics

2022-08-14

Building A Heritage

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Faith transmission requires active demonstration in daily routines—not verbal instruction alone or delegation to schools, devices, or extracurricular activities; the Shema embeds parenting into sitting, walking, lying down, and rising up.
  • The question 'What does this mean?' only arises when children are engaged enough to ask; be present enough that the question comes, and ready with a personal testimony of deliverance rather than inherited tradition.
  • When dismissed or looked down upon, the response is not confrontation but being a living example in speech, behavior, love, trust, and purity—people gravitate to demonstrated sincerity, not argued superiority.
One-Line Action
Choose one daily routine you currently do alone or passively and turn it into an active faith-demonstration moment with a child, mentee, or spiritual orphan in your life.
Memorable Quote
I tell them if you see something not good in me, you're reading a page that I wrote. Let's go to the things the pages God wrote.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi explores parashat Vaetchanan, opening with Moses pleading to enter the Promised Land only to be told the matter is settled—yet not finished, as God immediately commissions Joshua as the next generational leader. He argues that the Shema's command to love God includes the often-overlooked mandate to teach children diligently in every context of daily life—sitting, walking, lying down, rising up. He highlights Deuteronomy 4:9's instruction to make God's works known to children and grandchildren, warning that failure leads to corruption and national loss.

Drawing on Nathaniel's bar mitzvah preparation, Huxley's Brave New World, and modern technology displacing parental engagement, he illustrates the dangers of passive or delegated spiritual formation. He emphasizes that honoring parents is tied to parents actively demonstrating faith, not merely issuing instructions they themselves don't follow.

The Haftarah's comfort passage and Isaiah 40's contrast between fading grass and God's eternal word reinforce that only Scripture-rooted living endures. He closes with 1 Timothy 4's exhortation to be an example in speech, behavior, love, trust, and purity—showing that the answer to being dismissed is not argument but activated, visible faithfulness that builds a self-perpetuating heritage.

Hook / Wordplay

God told Moses the matter was 'settled'—but not finished. Immediately after closing Moses' chapter, He opened Joshua's, showing that every ending is a commissioning for the next generation.

2022-08-21

Ekev: Obedience is the Key to True Love

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When you know you will end up serving someone anyway, choose willing obedience from the start—responding with 'I'll leap over a wall for you' instead of grudging compliance transforms the same act from resentment into love.
  • Love is the root, obedience is the fruit: rather than forcing compliance through willpower, deepen your love for God and for others, and faithful action follows naturally.
  • You cannot obey what you have not read—invest regular time in Scripture so that your commitment to God is informed and engaged, not like signing a contract you never reviewed.
One-Line Action
This week, catch yourself in one moment of reluctant compliance toward God or a loved one and consciously reframe it as a willing act of love before you carry it out.
Memorable Quote
What I tell her is, I've always said, since we were married, I said, I'm loving you.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi teaches from Parashat Ekev, arguing that obedience to God's commands is the truest expression of love rather than a burdensome obligation. He frames Deuteronomy as the most love-filled of the five books of Moses, comparable to the Gospel of John among the Gospels. He highlights that all three of Yeshua's responses during the wilderness temptation came from Deuteronomy, which was being read in synagogues during that forty-day period.

He connects Moses's repeated calls to love, serve, and follow God with Yeshua's words in John 14-15 where keeping commands equals loving him. Using the personal anecdote of telling his wife 'I'll leap over a wall for you' when asked for small favors, he illustrates how willing obedience transforms duty into devotion. He draws from Hebrews 5 to show that even Yeshua learned obedience through suffering, and challenges the congregation to move from spiritual milk to solid food by actively applying God's word.

He warns against taking blessings for granted and forgetting the source of provision, echoing Moses's caution in Deuteronomy 8. The sermon closes with the exhortation that love is the root and obedience is the fruit, and that one cannot obey what one has never read.

Hook / Wordplay

Connects the portion name 'Ekev' (because) to the classic song 'Because You Come to Me,' noting he long misheard 'naught save love' as nonsense words—then realizes the lyric perfectly captures Deuteronomy's message: God comes to us with nothing except love.

2022-08-28

Avoiding the Traps in Life

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When facing hostility, resist the reflex to fight on the world's terms; choosing the world's weaponry (anger, rudeness, accusation) puts you at a spiritual disadvantage—respond with love instead.
  • Good intentions are not resolutions; Peter swore he would die for Yeshua yet denied him three times—daily immersion in God's presence, not emotional pledges, is what sustains faithfulness under pressure.
  • Fear of man is a snare (Proverbs 29:25), but perfect love casts out fear; when intimidation rises, shift focus from the accuser to the love of God that nothing can separate us from.
One-Line Action
Identify one area where you have been matching the world's tone or tactics, and this week consciously choose to respond with love and truth instead.
Memorable Quote
If we choose the weaponry that the world uses, we put ourselves at a disadvantage.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi teaches from Parashat Re'eh, where God sets before Israel a blessing and a curse, warning them not to be trapped into following the ways of the nations they displace. He connects Deuteronomy's warnings to Luke 22, where the adversary demanded to sift Peter like wheat—Peter's bold pledge of loyalty collapsed into triple denial, illustrating how good intentions fail without spiritual grounding. He draws on Proverbs 29:25 to show that fear of man is itself a snare, and Proverbs 7 to depict how seductive enticements lead to destruction without the victim even recognizing the danger.

He argues that believers must not use the world's weapons—anger, rudeness, accusation—because adopting the enemy's tactics puts us at a disadvantage. Instead, citing 1 John 4, he urges that perfect love casts out fear and that God dwelling in us is greater than the world's opposition. He references Isaiah 54:17's promise that no weapon formed against us will prevail, cautioning against becoming accusers ourselves.

He closes with Judges 17:6, where everyone did what was right in their own eyes, as the cautionary endpoint of ignoring God's guardrails. The central call is to become 'trap-proof' through daily surrender, immersion in God's word, and responding to the world with love rather than matching its hostility.

Hook / Wordplay

The 'trappings' of the world are literally traps—and the biggest trap of all is opening our own trap and speaking in the same angry manner the world uses.

2022-09-04

Shoftim: Presumptuous Justice

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Before forming a judgment about anyone's guilt or failure, check for 'mixture'—ask whether my motive is genuine justice or an ulterior desire to see a particular outcome.
  • Pray the Psalm 19:13 prayer regularly—'Keep your servant from presumptuous sins'—as a guard against slowly assuming God is fine with my compromises.
  • When wrongly accused or attacked, default to intercession rather than self-defense; Yeshua on the cross and Wurmbrand in prison both demonstrate that praying for persecutors breaks the cycle of presumptuous retaliation.
One-Line Action
Before judging any situation, pause and honestly ask whether you are pursuing justice or just wanting things your way.
Memorable Quote
We need to make sure that we don't have ulterior motives, that we lend ourselves to the Lord, that we are taking time to even if it hurts, to be honest with our hearts, honest with God.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi teaches from Parashat Shoftim on the distinction between presumptive justice (reasonable grounds for belief) and presumptuous justice (arrogant assumption that we already know the verdict). He examines how God's judicial system demands impartiality, freedom from bribery, and corroboration by two or three witnesses before any sentence. He reframes the story of the woman caught in adultery, arguing Yeshua's challenge about casting the first stone was not about general sinlessness but about whether the witnesses had ulterior motives or mixture in their accusations.

He illustrates through the Judges 17-18 account of Micah's stolen idol what happens when everyone does what is right in their own eyes—justice becomes absurd and self-serving. Drawing from Psalm 19, he highlights the psalmist's prayer to be kept from presumptuous sins, warning that we can slide into assuming God is 'winking' at our compromise.

He connects these ancient texts to modern dynamics of public opinion replacing due process, noting how spin and accusation replace impartial evaluation. He closes by calling believers to love their enemies and pray for persecutors, citing Yeshua's words from the cross and Richard Wurmbrand's example in prison, urging the congregation to yield to God as the ultimate judge rather than acting presumptuously.

Hook / Wordplay

Presumptuous vs. presumptive—two words that sound alike but diverge sharply: one is arrogant assumption, the other is reasonable grounds for belief. Justice demands we know the difference.

Justice and Mercy

Ki Tetze: The King Is in the Field and an Ox Is on the Road

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When I see someone struggling—their 'ox collapsed on the road'—I must not walk past, even if the person has been hostile toward me; Exodus 23:5 commands care for an enemy's fallen animal, and standing in that gap can be transformative.
  • God makes Himself accessible (the King comes to the field), so I should not be too distracted by lesser things to meet with Him; don't get sidetracked by 'small talk on the road' and miss divine appointments (Luke 10:4).
  • The yoke imagery in Matthew 11 means God is not watching me struggle alone—He is yoked alongside me, sharing the weight; my job is to accept that partnership rather than insisting on carrying everything myself.
One-Line Action
This week, notice one person whose 'ox is on the road'—someone visibly struggling—and step in to help without waiting to be asked.
Memorable Quote
Messiah then said, the second command is like unto the first, via haft'ul'rach'a'kum'ocha and you shall love your neighbor as yourself." All right, Baruch Hashem.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi teaches from the Ki Tetze Torah portion, weaving together the traditional Elul concept of 'the King is in the field' with the commandment to help a neighbor's fallen ox. He uses the recent British royal transition—Queen Elizabeth's death and King Charles's public engagement with crowds—to illustrate how a sovereign can personally connect with common people. The Deuteronomy 22 command not to ignore a brother's collapsed ox is extended through Exodus 23:5 to include helping even an enemy's animal, teaching that we must stand in the gap for everyone.

He touches on the camp hygiene laws as evidence that God cares about every practical detail of communal life. The divorce passage in Deuteronomy 24 is linked to Isaiah 54's theme of God temporarily abandoning Israel but always providing an avenue of return. Yeshua's invitation 'take my yoke upon you' is connected to the ox imagery—God yokes Himself alongside us so we don't carry burdens alone.

Ephesians 5's teaching on marriage mirrors the Messiah-congregation relationship, where sacrificial love naturally produces mutual respect. He closes by revealing that the first letters of 'Ani L'dodi V'dodi Li' spell Elul, encapsulating the sermon's central theme: God's desire for intimacy with His people.

Hook / Wordplay

The first letters of 'Ani L'dodi V'dodi Li' (I am my beloved's and my beloved is mine) spell Elul—the very month when the King leaves the palace to meet His people in the field.

How God's Blessing Removes Insanity

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Guard what occupies your attention daily—media, scrolling, and passive consumption slowly reshape thinking just as surely as active teaching does; deliberately replace idle screen time with Scripture engagement.
  • When cultural confusion presses in, refocus by returning to Philippians 4:8 as a diagnostic checklist: is what I'm consuming true, noble, pure, lovable, and praiseworthy?
  • During this season of teshuvah, actively demonstrate faith through lifestyle rather than argument—be the light that makes people ask 'what is different about you?' instead of trying to force belief.
One-Line Action
Each morning this week, before opening any app or news feed, read one passage from this parasha and pray Philippians 4:6-7 over your thought life.
Memorable Quote
God teaches us to have the mind of Messiah, to have the mind of God to listen to Him and to walk in the pattern that produces life and not destruction. Peace and not anxiety.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi Jan teaches from Parashat Ki Tavo, focusing on Deuteronomy 28:28 where disobedience brings 'insanity, blindness, and utter confusion,' and contrasts this with the abundant blessings promised for obedience. He argues that straying from God's commands doesn't trigger a punitive curse but naturally removes us from His protective presence, leaving us vulnerable to confusion and captivity. He connects this ancient warning to modern cultural phenomena—children's exposure to harmful digital content, TikTok's divergent content strategies between China and the West, the pronoun movement, and increasing reliance on medication over spiritual solutions.

He uses the pronoun discussion humorously, suggesting believers could adopt spiritual 'preferred pronouns' like 'fellow heir' or 'lover of all,' illustrating how identity disconnected from God leads to fragility and anger. Drawing from Isaiah 60 and Philippians 4, he presents the antidote: focusing thoughts on what is true, noble, and praiseworthy, and walking in union with Messiah so that God's peace guards heart and mind.

He emphasizes that believers are called to shine like noonday in the night—not by forcing change on others, but by living as demonstrations of God's transforming presence. The sermon closes with a call to lengthen the cords of our tents and reach communities in darkness during the season of return leading to Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur.

Hook / Wordplay

Deuteronomy 28:28 warns God will strike with 'insanity, blindness, and utter confusion'—the same three symptoms visible in today's culture. The cure isn't new medication; it's the old prescription of Philippians 4:8: think on these things.

The Power of Word of Mouth

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Audit what voices I'm repeating: am I passing on God's words or just recycled talking points and cultural assumptions? The whisper-down-the-lane illustration is a practical test for my own speech.
  • When someone has wandered far, resist the impulse to say 'you got what you deserved'—instead adopt the Deuteronomy 30:4 posture of 'I will go there and get you,' which Yeshua modeled even on the execution stake.
  • Approach God's word like Tova with the textbook: not 'what's the minimum required reading?' but 'if this has what I need to help people, I want in on it'—invest beyond the surface.
One-Line Action
This week, replace one habitual complaint or criticism with a specific word of encouragement spoken directly to someone who needs to hear God still pursues them.
Memorable Quote
God is able to take us no matter where we have ended up on the ash heap of life. And God is able to say, come on, I can use you.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi opens with the Torah portion Nitzavim (Deuteronomy 29:9-15), where God establishes a covenant not only with those standing before Him but with all future generations. He introduces the theme 'word of mouth,' noting its power in advertising because it carries the authority of personal experience, but also its danger when messages get distorted through repetition. He illustrates distortion with pop-culture misquotes—Dragnet's 'Just the facts,' Cary Grant's 'Judy Judy Judy,' Paul's non-existent horse—before pivoting to how Nazi propaganda weaponized word of mouth to dehumanize Jews, tracing a line from Luther's later writings through Hitler to Goebbels.

Turning to Deuteronomy 30, he highlights God's stunning promise that even after exile caused by listening to wrong voices, He will gather His people back—'even from the far end of the sky.' He connects this to Yeshua's reading of Isaiah 61 in the Nazareth synagogue (Luke 4), where Yeshua declared His mission to bind up the brokenhearted and set captives free, noting that Yeshua deliberately scrolled back to the chapter's opening rather than reading the assigned starting verse. He traces the 'word of mouth' theme through the crucifixion scene (Matthew 27), where mockers used words to dehumanize the very one who came to redeem them, yet Yeshua still offered paradise to the thief beside Him.

Drawing from Romans 1, he lifts up Paul's eagerness to bring the good news to all people—civilized and uncivilized—while warning that those who claim wisdom through tearing others down become fools. He closes with a challenge that believers must be extensions of Messiah's love, reaching people with words of life rather than dehumanizing those in rebellion, because transformation comes not through legislation but through the Spirit of God working through His people.

Hook / Wordplay

"Word of mouth" bridges the advertising concept—the most trusted form of testimony because you've experienced it yourself—with Deuteronomy 30:14's literal promise: "the word is very close to you; it is in your mouth, even in your heart."

Vayelekh: The Return of Abandoned People

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Don't accept criticism from someone you wouldn't take advice from — take correction to the Lord, who cares in a way no one else can, rather than internalizing words from voices that haven't earned that authority.
  • Reconciliation is not a destination but a commission: once reconciled to God, we inherit a ministry of reconciliation, becoming the avenue of access to God for people who feel abandoned.
  • Reframe abandonment actively — instead of sitting in the feeling of being left alone, abandon the distractions and encumbrances to God, giving Him first place, and let that relationship overflow into loving your neighbor.
One-Line Action
This week, identify one person in your life who seems to be drifting in a sense of abandonment and be a specific voice of encouragement to them — don't diagnose their problem, draw out the gift God placed in them.
Memorable Quote
The return of abandoned people carries with it not all the baggage of abandonment, but it carries with it all the hope and benefits that God wants to bestow upon his people, to bring deliverance, to Jew and non Jew alike, to bring that message of hope that we have through Messiah who laid his life down for us while we were yet rebels.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi teaches parashat Vayelekh on Shabbat Shuvah, anchoring on Moses's charge to Joshua: 'Be strong, be bold — God will neither fail you nor abandon you.' He identifies fear of abandonment as one of the deepest human fears and reframes Shabbat Shuvah as God's answer to it. Drawing on his own Bar Mitzvah Haftarah from Hosea 14, he highlights that God says 'return' even though we fell by our iniquities — not 'don't bother coming back.' He threads Isaiah 62's renaming of Israel from 'Azuvah/Abandoned' to 'Hephtzibah/My delight is in her,' showing God's posture is restoration, not punishment. Joel 2's call to solemn assembly and the subsequent outpouring of the Spirit illustrate the pattern: repentance precedes divine visitation.

Romans 5 provides the capstone — Messiah died for us while we were still rebels, with no guarantee of reciprocation. The rabbi then pivots the word 'abandon' itself: rather than being abandoned, we can abandon ourselves to God, shedding the encumbrances that hold us down. He closes by urging the congregation to extend this ministry of reconciliation outward, becoming voices of encouragement to others who feel abandoned.

Hook / Wordplay

Flips 'abandonment' from passive wound to active surrender — we are not abandoned by God; we abandon ourselves to God, dropping the encumbrances that weigh us down.

Ha'azinu: Moses' New Country Song

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When overwhelmed by competing cultural voices and news cycles, consciously return to God's 'melody'—His word and promises—rather than getting drawn into reactive debates or blame cycles.
  • Be a bearer of good news: instead of cataloging what's wrong in the world, lead conversations toward the hope and deliverance available through Messiah.
  • Recognize when you're 'scorning the Rock' by trusting unreliable sources or shortcuts over God's proven faithfulness—the adversary's track record is failure, yet we keep giving him 'one more try.'
One-Line Action
This week, catch yourself mid-complaint about the state of the world and pivot to sharing one piece of good news rooted in God's promises.
Memorable Quote
From Devariyaam Duturana Me 6-4, the cornerstone of our faith.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi frames the Torah portion Ha'azinu as Moses' 'new country song'—a double meaning because Israel was entering new country (the Promised Land) and because, like country music, the song mixes sorrow and hope, warning and love. Moses addresses heaven and earth, declaring God as the Rock whose work is perfect, while Israel repeatedly scorns that Rock to chase alien gods. The rabbi draws a vivid parallel between the biblical image of poisonous grapes and snake venom and the modern fentanyl crisis, where lethal drugs are disguised as candy to destroy children.

He warns that competing 'melodies'—cultural noise, political blame, government overreach into social care—distract believers from the foundational song God is singing over His people. Turning to Romans 10 and Isaiah 52, he emphasizes that trust comes from hearing, and hearing comes through the proclaimed word about Messiah.

The congregation is called to be people with 'beautiful feet' who announce good news rather than recycling negativity. He closes with the image from Revelation of Messiah standing at the door knocking, inviting intimate fellowship, and urges listeners to keep God's melody playing in their hearts so they can carry that song of deliverance to others.

Hook / Wordplay

Moses wrote a 'new country song'—country because Israel was heading into new country (the Promised Land), and country because, like the genre, the song mixes sadness and hope, warning and love.

2022-10-16

Sukkot Shabbat Outdoor Service

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • God deliberately puts his treasure in broken, imperfect people so the glory points to him—stop disqualifying yourself because of your flaws and start seeing yourself as his chosen dwelling place.
  • Reject escape theology that says the world doesn't matter; 'occupy till I come' means staying engaged as agents of transformation especially when hearts are most open during difficulty.
  • The Lulav binds together four species representing people of different gifts and knowledge—community means we belong to each other regardless of credentials or spiritual resume.
One-Line Action
This week, step into one dark or difficult situation as a carrier of God's light instead of retreating from it.
Memorable Quote
Lord, we thank You for this wonderful day, this day that You have made.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The rabbi offers a Sukkot recap for congregants who missed earlier services, walking through Leviticus 23's instructions for the feast of booths. He explains how the fall festivals build on each other—from Rosh Hashanah through Yom Kippur to Sukkot—each carrying a distinct message within a unified arc of repentance, celebration, and readiness. He highlights why the devastating judgment passages of Zechariah 14 and Ezekiel 38-39 are read during a joyful holiday: they serve as a sobering reminder that judgment is real, yet God's redemptive provision stands in the midst of it.

The sermon centers on Yeshua's Hoshana Rabbah declarations—'I am the light of the world' during the torch-lighting and 'if anyone is thirsty, come to me' during the water libation—as the fulfillment of what the festival pointed toward. He teaches that God places his treasure in earthen vessels, choosing imperfect people as his dwelling place just as the fragile Sukkah symbolizes. He pushes back against escape theology, insisting believers should occupy and transform rather than withdraw.

The Lulav's four species illustrate how God binds together people of different gifts and backgrounds into one community. He closes with the guards' response from John 7—'No one ever spoke the way this man speaks'—as evidence that Yeshua's words carried a presence beyond mere teaching.

Hook / Wordplay

The Sukkah is frail, temporary, a little cracked—and so are we. Yet God chooses these earthen vessels as the place to store his greatest treasure.

2022-10-23

B'resheet Creating A Touchpoint

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When I catch myself complaining, accusing, or gossiping, treat those behaviors as diagnostic indicators that I am reflecting the adversary's image rather than God's—and consciously course-correct.
  • Evaluate the track record and credibility of what I am trusting rather than being swayed by appealing circumstances; the adversary's strategy is repeated false promises that never deliver.
  • I can be a touchpoint for God not by generating my own light but by yielding to His presence and faithfully reflecting it so others are drawn out of darkness.
One-Line Action
This week, use moments of complaint or accusation as a mirror—ask whose image you are reflecting and consciously redirect toward God's.
Memorable Quote
You can't turn off darkness, but when you turn on the light darkness disappears.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi opens the first Shabbat after the High Holidays by returning to Parashat B'resheet, framing creation as God establishing a touchpoint with humanity. He introduces the concept of 'two image makers'—God creating humanity in His image and the adversary working to distort that image into his own. Drawing extensively from Romans 1, he argues that the root problem is not any particular sin but the exchange of God's glory for worship of created things, which leads to darkened thinking and moral breakdown.

He connects this to John 1, presenting Messiah as the Word made flesh—the ultimate touchpoint where God's invisible image became visible and accessible. Through Isaiah 42-43 he emphasizes God's desire to lead people from darkness into light, calling each person by name with intimate redemptive purpose.

Paul in Philippians 3 models the posture of counting all things as loss for the surpassing value of knowing Messiah and His resurrection power. The sermon concludes that believers can themselves become touchpoints—reflections of God's image that draw others out of darkness—and that the beginning can start the moment we choose to change.

Hook / Wordplay

Two image makers were present at creation: God forming humanity in His image, and the adversary seeking to distort that image into his own.

2022-10-30

Our Weapons Against Violence

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When facing conflict, resist the impulse to fight with worldly tactics; identify and use the spiritual skills God has specifically equipped you with, as David chose his sling over Saul's armor.
  • Audit your inner circle — invest in relationships where truth is spoken honestly and loyalty persists through misunderstanding, rather than depending on many voices that create confusion and paralysis.
  • Guard children's spiritual formation proactively; don't outsource it entirely to institutions, since early influence is the hardest to undo (the Lenin 'four years' illustration drives this home).
One-Line Action
Identify one area where you are fighting with borrowed 'armor' instead of the spiritual tools God has specifically given you, and switch weapons this week.
Memorable Quote
A real friend stands with you even if they don't understand what it is you're doing. They'll speak honestly to you and speak strongly to you, but they won't walk away from you.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon opens with Parashat Noach and the universal flood narrative, highlighting that the Hebrew word for violence filling the earth is 'Hamas.' Rabbi teaches that corruption spirals when people focus on self-interest and drift from God, as seen in Noah's generation and echoed in Romans 1. God's judgment through the flood was ultimately a consequence of humanity's own rebellion, yet corruption resurfaced quickly through Babel and beyond. The central thesis is that the weapon against violence is not more violence but spiritual warfare conducted with God's power, drawing from 2 Corinthians 10:3-5.

Using the David and Goliath narrative, he illustrates that believers must fight with the skills and spiritual equipment God has given them rather than borrowed worldly tactics. He addresses modern cultural threats against children and urges parents to reclaim their formative role, anchored in Isaiah 54:13.

The sermon explores authentic friendship through Proverbs 18:24 and Yeshua's experience in John 6, contrasting shallow connections with the Lord who sticks closer than a brother. He closes with 2 Corinthians 4:6-12, reminding the congregation that God's treasure resides in fragile clay vessels so that the overwhelming power is clearly from God, and believers endure troubles without being crushed because of Messiah's sustaining life within them.

Hook / Wordplay

The Hebrew word for violence is 'Hamas' — the Torah's description of a world filled with violence becomes the setup for God's counter-arsenal: spiritual weapons that demolish strongholds without a single fist thrown.

2022-11-06

Soar Like An Eagle

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When life detours from the plan, look for what God is cultivating in the waiting season—relationships, strength, and readiness for what's ahead, just as Abraham gathered people in Haran who would never have joined him otherwise.
  • Stop letting circumstantial evidence overrule God's promises; Abraham looked at his childlessness and concluded Eliezer would inherit, but God's word came back: 'Your heir will be from your own body.'
  • Rising above challenges means seeing them from God's vantage point, not avoiding them—the Oldfield story shows that going through suffering, not around it, is what builds the strength to handle even greater trials later.
One-Line Action
Identify one circumstance you have been interpreting as God's abandonment and reframe it as equipment for a future you cannot yet see.
Memorable Quote
From Devarim to the Rani Mi 6 4, the cornerstone of our faith.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi opens the Lekh L'kha portion by showing that Abraham's call to leave everything familiar mirrors the unknown journeys each of us faces. He traces Abraham's detour in Haran, noting that the delay was not wasted—God used it to gather people who would join Abraham's mission. He highlights Abraham's repeated lapses of faith (calling Sarah his sister, taking Hagar) to show that even the father of faith stumbled, yet God kept building him.

Pivoting to Isaiah 40:27-31, he addresses the complaint 'my way is hidden from the Lord,' insisting that those who wait on God will soar like eagles and have their strength renewed—even the ones who stumbled. He shares a powerful illustration about a pastor's wife who went catatonic for seven years after trying to do ministry in her own strength, and how her recovery through 'God is love' transformed her into a powerhouse of faith. He connects this to the congregation's own long wait for their property to develop, admitting he does not know God's full timeline but trusts the calling.

Drawing from Galatians and Hebrews, he ties Abraham's covenant to the Melchizedek priesthood and Messiah, arguing that inward transformation through trusting faithfulness matters more than outward expressions. He closes by urging the congregation not to hide from challenges but to rise above them and see from God's vantage point.

Hook / Wordplay

The Philadelphia Eagles' fight song became an accidental sermon anthem—he sent 'Trust and Prevail' to sports radio when they won the Super Bowl, but the real message was about mounting up on eagle's wings above every challenge.

2022-11-13

Vayera: How Compromise Affects Conviction

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Compromise on deep convictions doesn't happen all at once — it begins with circumstantial decisions (Lot choosing Sodom for economics) and gradually erodes moral clarity; audit where 'practical' choices are quietly moving you away from core values.
  • Abraham's failures (the sister deception, twice) didn't disqualify him — they were part of the learning journey that brought him to Akedah-level faith; past compromises don't define your trajectory if you keep yielding to God.
  • When firm-belief conviction weakens through compromise, it can be replaced by guilt-verdict conviction; drawing near to God is the mechanism that strengthens resolve and leaves no vacancy for the adversary.
One-Line Action
Identify one area where a 'practical' compromise is quietly eroding a core conviction and bring it back to God before it escalates.
Memorable Quote
And you shall love the Lord your God with all your heart, with all your soul, with all your strength.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The rabbi explores the dual meaning of 'conviction' — as firm belief and as legal guilt — through the lens of Parashat Vayera. He traces Lot's gradual compromise: choosing Sodom for economic reasons, becoming spiritually vexed yet staying, and ultimately offering his own daughters to a violent mob rather than surrendering his guests. Even after rescue, the effects of Sodom's moral environment showed in his daughters' incestuous act, though the rabbi notes the rabbinical view that their motive was continuing their father's line amid perceived total destruction.

He contrasts this with Abraham's own compromises — twice passing Sarah off as his sister — yet shows how Abraham grew through these failures toward deeper trust. Abraham's journey culminated at the Akedah, where he demonstrated uncompromising obedience, willing to sacrifice Isaac because he trusted God's promise of resurrection if necessary. Drawing from 2 Peter 2, James 1, and James 4, the rabbi shows how desire entices, compromise leads to sin, and sin leads to destruction.

He warns that moral accommodation follows a predictable escalation: tolerance, then acceptance, then dominance, then zero tolerance for dissent. Yet the sermon ends redemptively: just as Isaac's death sentence was commuted by a substitute ram, our guilt-conviction is commuted through Messiah's sacrifice, and we are called to draw near to God and walk in newness of life.

Hook / Wordplay

'Conviction' means both a firm belief and a guilty verdict — the rabbi traces how compromised convictions lead to guilty verdicts, while Abraham's uncompromised faith led to Isaac's death sentence being commuted by a substitute.

2022-11-20

He Called Him Master

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Don't wait for ideal conditions to obey — Eliezer refused comfort and delay when Rifka's family pressured him to stay; identify where you are postponing what God has called you to do and move now.
  • The mundane details of daily faithfulness form the fabric of who you are — being willing to 'take a bullet' means nothing if you are unwilling to be inconvenienced for twenty minutes.
  • Share your faith from lived intimate relationship with God, not as talking points or a book report — the difference between Eliezer's passionate testimony and a rehearsed pitch is personal knowledge of the Master.
One-Line Action
Identify one area where you are delaying obedience and take the first step this week without waiting for perfect conditions.
Memorable Quote
People will talk about willing to take a bullet for somebody, but not willing to be inconvenienced.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon centers on Genesis 24 and Eliezer's mission to find a bride for Isaac after Sarah's death at age 127. The rabbi highlights that Eliezer says 'my master' twelve times in this chapter when referring to Abraham, demonstrating extraordinary dedication. Crucially, when Rifka asks who Isaac is, Eliezer does not say 'my master's son' but 'he is my master,' recognizing the transfer of God's generational vision from Abraham to Isaac.

Eliezer refused to delay when Rifka's family asked him to stay longer, maintaining singular focus on his sacred calling. The sermon connects this to Yeshua's teachings in Luke 9, where potential followers wanted to first bury a father or say goodbye to family, and Yeshua declared no one who looks back is fit for the kingdom. The seventy disciples sent out in Luke 10 were similarly told not to stop and 'shmuz' along the road but to stay focused on proclaiming the kingdom.

In John 15, Yeshua elevates the relationship from slave to friend, saying a slave does not know what his master is about but 'I have called you friends.' The sermon's central insight is the paradox that we call Him Master yet He calls us Friend, with authority expressed through intimate relationship. Just as Eliezer's obedience flowed from deep knowledge of Abraham's heart and calling, our discipleship should flow from genuine friendship with Messiah rather than mere verbal commitment.

Hook / Wordplay

We call Him Master, but He calls us Friend — the paradox of divine authority expressed through intimate relationship.

2022-11-27

Tol'dot: Overcoming the Struggles of Life

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When overwhelmed by a struggle, follow Rebekah's model—go to God first to inquire what He is doing through it, rather than complaining or trying to solve it alone.
  • Refuse to let a current struggle become your identity; it is something you are passing through, not who you are, and your identity is found in God.
  • After God brings you through a difficulty, deliberately stop to thank Him and record the testimony rather than just wiping your face and moving on—accumulate evidence of His faithfulness.
One-Line Action
When the next struggle hits, pause before reacting and bring it to God in prayer, asking what He is producing through it.
Memorable Quote
Pain goes away. God's promises and his blessings do not.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon explores Parashat Toldot (Genesis 25:19–28:9) through the lens of overcoming life's struggles. Rabbi reframes Jacob not as a conniving manipulator but as someone who deeply valued the spiritual birthright, while Esau traded eternal inheritance for momentary gratification. He highlights Rebekah as a model for responding to struggles—she went to inquire of the Lord rather than complaining or despairing.

Drawing on 2 Corinthians 4:16-18, he argues that afflictions are light and momentary compared to the eternal glory God is producing through them. Romans 8:28-39 anchors the assurance that nothing can separate believers from God's love, and that God works all things for good. A key theme is that struggles should not become one's identity; they are what we pass through on the way to where God is bringing us.

The rabbi shares a personal account of anti-Semitic vandalism at the congregation's property around Kristallnacht remembrance, using it to model choosing faith over fear. He urges praying even for the perpetrators rather than fixating on the attack, pointing to God's transformative power. The sermon closes with the vivid reminder that pain is temporary and ultimately forgotten, but God's promises and blessings never stop growing.

Hook / Wordplay

The birthright meal deal: for Jacob it was a happy meal; for Esau later, it was not very tasty.

2022-12-04

Vayetze: Accessing Our Personal Bodyguard

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When life feels manipulated or trust is shattered—like Jacob's experience with Laban—remember that God's bodyguard promise means He is working through the hardship, not that He failed to prevent it.
  • Guard your heart actively (Proverbs 4:23): don't let emotional reactions redirect your journey; instead, bring anxieties to God in prayer and let His shalom keep your mind safe (Philippians 4:6-7).
  • Access the Ruach HaKodesh as a daily guide—He receives directives from the Father and leads into all truth, so pause to listen before counseling yourself or seeking only human advice.
One-Line Action
This week when anxiety rises, stop self-counseling and bring it to God in prayer—access the Bodyguard who already sees what is around the corner.
Memorable Quote
He answered with the words of the shaman, said the following.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi explores Parashat Vayetze through the extended metaphor of God as our personal bodyguard. As Jacob leaves Beersheva for Haran—fleeing Esau's threat—God appears in a dream at the ladder and promises to guard him wherever he goes. This divine promise becomes the sermon's central frame: God shadows, observes, and shields us the way a bodyguard protects a client.

The rabbi traces Jacob's twenty years under Laban's repeated manipulation—the switched bride, the changed wages ten times—showing that God's guarding does not prevent hardship but ensures safe passage through it. He connects Jacob's ladder vision to John 1, where Yeshua tells Nathaniel 'I saw you under the fig tree,' illustrating God's constant, unseen observation. Philippians 4:6-7 and Proverbs 4:23 expand the metaphor inward: God guards not only our circumstances but our hearts and minds in union with Messiah.

John 16 introduces the Ruach HaKodesh as the ongoing bodyguard presence—receiving directives from the 'home office' and guiding believers into all truth. The sermon culminates in the powerful parallel between a bodyguard taking a bullet and Messiah taking nails, with the crucial difference that Yeshua rose again and remains on duty. Rabbi closes by emphasizing that accessing this protection requires only prayer, openness to the Spirit, and allowing God to take up residence within us.

Hook / Wordplay

God as personal bodyguard—the rabbi dons earpiece and sunglasses mid-sermon as props, then lands the climactic turn: a bodyguard takes a bullet for his client, but Messiah took nails for us and rose again, still on duty.

2022-12-11

Rescued by a Promise

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When overwhelmed by fear, follow Jacob's prayer model: rehearse God's specific promises back to Him rather than cataloging worst-case scenarios.
  • Don't project old versions of people onto present encounters — just as Esau had changed in 20 years, God works transformation in others beyond what we can see.
  • Shift focus from knowing the promise to knowing the Promise Keeper; the credibility of the one who promises is what makes the commitment unbreakable.
One-Line Action
When fear rises, pause and pray by reciting a specific promise God has made rather than spiraling into worst-case thinking.
Memorable Quote
The promise is one thing, but the one making the promise is what gives its validity and its power.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon follows Jacob's return to face his brother Esau after 20 years away, drawn from the Torah portion Vayishlach. Jacob is terrified upon hearing Esau approaches with 400 men, but rather than complaining, he prays by rehearsing God's covenant promises back to Him. The rabbi uses a class reunion analogy to explain how Jacob was locked into his last memory of Esau's death threat, unaware that Esau had also changed.

He corrects the common misreading that Jacob hid behind his family, showing from Genesis 33:3 that Jacob actually walked ahead of them into danger. The reunion is one of embrace and forgiveness, not violence. The sermon then traces the Dinah-Shechem incident and Simeon and Levi's deception, showing that even amid his children's ruthlessness God upheld His protective promise.

Drawing on 2 Peter 1, 2 Corinthians 7, and Acts 1, the rabbi distinguishes between knowing a promise and knowing the Promise Keeper, arguing that God's credibility is what gives His promises their power. He closes with Hebrews 10:35-36, urging the congregation not to throw away their courage but to hold out for what God has promised.

Hook / Wordplay

"Rescue is not just a verb, it's a promise" — borrowed from a pet-rescue t-shirt and reframed as God's rescue plan for His people.

2022-12-25

Mikketz: Fruitful in a Land of Misfortune

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Misfortune is not the destination — Joseph's 13 years of hardship developed the exact administrative and interpretive skills God used to elevate him, so difficulty may be building capacity you cannot yet see.
  • Don't build a prison out of past guilt like Joseph's brothers did — they spent 17+ years after reconciliation fearing retribution that Joseph had already released, wasting years in self-imposed torment over something that no longer existed.
  • Stay united with Messiah to bear fruit (John 15) — fruitfulness is not produced by willpower or circumstance but by abiding connection, and Yeshua's specific commission is to bear lasting fruit through loving one another.
One-Line Action
When facing misfortune, stop viewing the difficulty as the destination and start looking for how God is cultivating fruitfulness in the midst of it.
Memorable Quote
The misfortunes that are there are not for us to commiserate over, but to be able to look to God to be the one who brings fruitfulness, who brings abundance, even out of those places of misfortune.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon focuses on parashat Mikketz and Joseph's rise from 13 years of slavery and imprisonment to second-in-command of Egypt after interpreting Pharaoh's dreams. The rabbi highlights how Joseph named his sons Manasseh ('causing to forget' his family's betrayal) and Ephraim ('fruitful'), declaring God made him fruitful in the land of his misfortune. He draws extensive parallels between Joseph and Yeshua as rejected figures who brought salvation to the very people who rejected them.

The Hanukkah context is woven in, showing how the Maccabees found light in darkness just as Joseph found fruitfulness in misfortune. The rabbi presents evidence that Yeshua was likely born during Sukkot rather than December 25th, while affirming the value of hearts being open during the Christmas season.

He examines how Joseph's brothers created their own prison of guilt for 17 years after reconciliation, fearing retribution that was never coming. The sermon closes with John 15's teaching on abiding in Messiah as the vine, emphasizing Yeshua's commission to bear lasting fruit through love rather than living imprisoned by misfortune.

Hook / Wordplay

Ephraim means 'fruitful' — Joseph named his son after the paradox that the land of his greatest misfortune became the land of his greatest success.

2023-01-01

Vayigash — A Vision Beyond Circumstances

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When I assume someone hasn't changed, I'm projecting my last reference point onto them; stay open to genuine transformation in others the way I hope they stay open to mine.
  • When circumstances seem clear-cut and I think I know what God's plan means, hold that interpretation loosely — Joseph had prophetic dreams and still couldn't anticipate what was unfolding.
  • Don't build convictions around circumstantial spin; stay anchored to the vision God spells out in Scripture and let Him reveal the details in His timing.
One-Line Action
Hold your circumstantial interpretations loosely and stay open to God revealing a bigger picture through people and events you didn't expect.
Memorable Quote
We know what's changed in our life, but we don't know what's changed in someone else's life. So our reference point is always to believe that we've changed, but they haven't.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon explores Vayigash (Genesis 44:18–47:27), centering on the moment Judah approaches Joseph and offers himself as a slave in Benjamin's place. Rabbi explains that Joseph had been planning to keep Benjamin away from his brothers to protect him, operating from his last memory of Judah as the one who proposed selling him into slavery. Judah's transformed heart completely blindsided Joseph, who broke down and revealed his identity to his brothers.

The rabbi draws out the principle that we freeze other people at our last reference point and assume they haven't changed, even though we know we have. Joseph's declaration that God sent him ahead to preserve life was itself a new revelation triggered by Judah's unexpected repentance. The sermon connects this to Ezekiel 37's two sticks becoming one and to Ephesians 2's teaching that Jew and Gentile are reconciled into one new humanity through Messiah.

The rabbi addresses the 70-versus-75 discrepancy between Genesis 46 and Acts 7, explaining that Stephen was quoting the Septuagint to a Greek-speaking Jewish audience. He closes with Hebrews 11–12 and 2 Corinthians 4, urging the congregation to trust God's vision over circumstantial evidence, since even the most mundane details may be part of God's preparation for things beyond imagination.

Hook / Wordplay

Vision is more than how you see things — Joseph had prophetic dreams and still couldn't see Judah's transformation coming, because his only reference point was a 20-year-old memory.

2023-01-08

Relationships & Choices That Create Our Destiny

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Evaluate whether my closest relationships reinforce fear and stagnation or push toward growth — commiserating with anxious people produces shared darkness, not shared solutions.
  • When caught in worry, write the feared outcome down and revisit it later; the bishop's exercise showed none of his anticipated worries materialized, and the energy saved was redirected toward trust.
  • Jacob's charge 'remember who I am and what I stand for' is a deathbed-worthy question to answer now: articulate what I stand for clearly enough that it could be passed to the next generation.
One-Line Action
Identify one relationship where commiseration has replaced encouragement and redirect the next conversation toward purpose and calling.
Memorable Quote
Sometimes when you commiserate with others of like thought, you end up in the same dark place.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The portion Vayechi records Jacob's final 17 years in Egypt, where despite prosperity in Goshen he never lost sight of the Promised Land as his family's true destiny. Jacob's life was defined by tenaciously holding onto God's calling through every injustice, and on his deathbed he transferred that vision to his sons through brutally honest blessings that functioned as tough love. Joseph's brothers spent those same 17 years paralyzed by fear that Joseph would take revenge after Jacob's death, even though Joseph had already forgiven them.

Their choice to commiserate with each other in anxiety rather than seek reconciliation created a destiny of fear instead of growth. The Haftarah in 1 Kings 2 reinforces the theme: Adonijah, Shimei, and Joab each made fatal choices driven by self-serving relationships, while Barzillai's loyalty to David was rewarded. A passage from Deuteronomy 32 laments that the people lacked the wisdom to understand their own destiny.

The rabbi connects this to 1 Peter's teaching that believers are a chosen people called out of darkness, and to Revelation 21's stark binary between the fountain of life and destruction. The overarching warning is that complacency in Goshen's comfort eventually led to centuries of slavery — settling for what is good can forfeit what is destined. The sermon urges cultivating a primary relationship with Yeshua that then shapes all other relationships toward encouragement and calling rather than fear and stagnation.

Hook / Wordplay

Vayechi — 'and he lived.' Jacob didn't just survive Egypt's comfort; he lived with unrelenting grip on destiny, and the verb challenges us: are we truly living or merely settling in our own Goshen?

2023-01-15

Shemot: I Am Identity

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When we allow others to define us we become enslaved to whoever holds that power; actively anchor identity in God's word rather than cultural labels or peer expectations.
  • God told Moses the final outcome (death of the firstborn, Exodus 4:22-23) before a single plague began—trust God's declared endgame even when present circumstances show zero progress.
  • Like fleas that won't jump past a removed lid, we internalize imposed limitations long after the constraint is gone; yielding to God breaks those invisible ceilings.
One-Line Action
Let the great I Am define your identity and calling instead of accepting the labels, limitations, and categories others place on you.
Memorable Quote
When we allow others to define us, we become a slave to the one who defines us. And we lose sight of who we really are.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon opens the book of Exodus (Shemot), tracing how Israel went from prospering in Goshen to enslavement under a Pharaoh who did not know Joseph. Rabbi Rosenberg shows how Israel gradually accepted a slave identity, forgetting Jacob's charge to remember who they were. He follows Moses through three identity crises: Egyptian prince, fugitive shepherd, and reluctant deliverer, highlighting that God answered Moses' insecurity by revealing His name Ehyeh Asher Ehyeh—I Am Who I Am.

A key textual observation is that God told Moses before the first plague that the confrontation would end with the death of Pharaoh's firstborn, declaring the outcome from the start. The haftorah connection to Jeremiah's call shows the same pattern: a young man protesting inadequacy whom God overrides with divine commission.

The sermon pivots to Yeshua's bold I Am declarations in John 8, where He refused to back down from His identity even under threat. The conclusion lands in Colossians 3, where Paul teaches that our identity is hidden with Messiah in God, calling believers to strip off the old self and clothe themselves in love and shalom as their heart's decision-maker.

Hook / Wordplay

'I Am Identity' plays on the double meaning: our personal 'I am' assertion versus God's divine name—true identity is found not in 'I am special' but in THE I Am.

2023-01-22

How To Avoid Child-Like Stubbornness

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Audit where my sense of identity actually comes from: am I defending a self-constructed image for others, or resting in what God declares about me?
  • When temporary relief from a struggle arrives, don't slide back into old patterns the way Pharaoh did after each plague lifted—use the reprieve to cement real change.
  • Pursue knowing Messiah himself rather than accumulating credentials and accolades that become the substitute identity Paul warned about in Philippians 3.
One-Line Action
Identify one area where you are stubbornly defending a self-made identity and surrender it to God's definition of who you are.
Memorable Quote
The cornerstone of our faith is Shemaad, from Devarim to around the six-foot.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon explores Parshat Va'era (Exodus 6–9) through the lens of identity and stubbornness, asking how destructive childlike stubbornness develops and how believers can avoid it. Rabbi tracks three groups whose identities shift throughout the Exodus narrative: Moses grows in confidence, Pharaoh hardens progressively, and Israel vacillates between hope and Stockholm syndrome with their captors. Pharaoh's self-constructed identity as a god made him unable to concede even as plagues systematically dismantled every Egyptian deity he represented.

A personal anecdote about the rabbi's toddler son refusing to leave a store—fully convinced he was in charge despite having no grasp of reality—illustrates how absurd adult stubbornness looks from a higher vantage point. The sermon draws a parallel to modern identity crises where people construct self-images and then stubbornly defend them even when reality contradicts those images. Paul's testimony in Philippians 3 provides the antidote: counting former identity markers as loss compared to the supreme value of knowing Messiah.

Romans 7 honestly acknowledges the internal struggle between old nature and spiritual identity, while Romans 8:1 offers freedom from condemnation through union with Yeshua. The rabbi concludes with 1 Corinthians 13:11, calling believers to put away childish stubbornness and embrace spiritual maturity. Humility before God—not self-promotion—is presented as the key vaccine against the pride that hardened Pharaoh's heart and ultimately cost him everything.

Hook / Wordplay

Pharaoh acted not like a god but like a spoiled child refusing to leave the toy store—stubbornly clutching an identity that was crumbling around him with every plague.

2023-01-29

How Much Longer?

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When I ask 'how long, Lord?'—flip the question and examine whether I am the one delaying by not stepping into what God has already made available.
  • A hardened heart is not forced by God; circumstances reveal what is already inside—tend to heart posture before crises expose it.
  • Do not underestimate being a small light; one flashlight lit an entire dark theater, so show up faithfully even when the contribution feels insignificant.
One-Line Action
Stop asking 'how long' and take hold of one promise or calling you have been delaying on this week.
Memorable Quote
We're waiting for God and God often is waiting for us. Waiting for us to respond.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon explores Parashat Bo through the lens of 'how much longer?'—a question asked from multiple directions throughout Scripture. Moses confronts Pharaoh on God's behalf while Pharaoh's own servants begin pressing him to let Israel go. Rabbi examines whether God is truly to blame for hardening Pharaoh's heart, arguing that God brought plagues which triggered Pharaoh's already-hardened disposition, while the servants proved hearts can change by becoming favorably disposed toward Israel.

He draws parallels to Psalm 74 (how long will the enemy insult?), Joshua 18 (how long will you delay taking the land?), Mark 9 (the father who cries 'I trust, help my lack of trust'), and John 10 (the crowd demanding Yeshua declare Himself Messiah while refusing to see what was already evident). The recurring theme is that 'how long' often depends on us rather than on God—we wait on Him while He waits for us to respond. Using a vivid personal story of a flashlight lighting an entire dark movie theater, he teaches that even a small amount of light dispels darkness and believers are called to be that light.

He connects Exodus's plagues to Revelation's parallel imagery of locusts, darkness, and martyrs crying 'how long,' showing the question echoes across all of Scripture. The sermon closes with Joshua's challenge: how long will you delay taking hold of what God has already given you?

Hook / Wordplay

"Bo" means both "go" and "come"—if you don't know whether you're coming or going, you can say Bo. The question "how much longer?" keeps reversing direction: Moses asks Pharaoh, the servants ask Pharaoh, we ask God, and God asks us.

2023-02-05

Protective Pillars of Cloud, Fire & Manna

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When circumstances box you in, God's command is 'go forward'—the slave mentality prefers familiar hardship over unknown blessing, and recognizing that reflex is the first step to breaking it.
  • Cloud, fire, and manna map onto protection, purification, and provision—three dimensions of how God sustains us daily, not just in crisis moments.
  • God's purpose in allowing overwhelming situations is to strengthen faith and reveal His power; the enemies panicking on the other side of the sea were more afraid of Israel than Israel was of them (foreshadowing the spies' report).
One-Line Action
Identify where you are retreating to familiar 'slavery' out of fear and instead take one concrete step forward, trusting God's daily provision.
Memorable Quote
Just because the circumstance has seemed difficult, doesn't mean that God has gone and taken a nap somewhere or forgot about fulfilling what he said he would do. He neither sleeps nor slumbers.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The rabbi teaches Parashat B'shallach, focusing on how God led Israel with pillars of cloud by day and fire by night after Pharaoh released them. He explains that God deliberately set up Pharaoh's pursuit at the Red Sea to demonstrate His authority over all rival powers, not out of ego but as a cornerstone event in redemptive history. The people panicked and preferred slavery's familiarity over the unknown, but Moses commanded them to stop being fearful and move forward.

The rabbi introduces a novel framework: alongside the pillars of cloud and fire, manna served as a third pillar of stability—a daily reminder of God's faithful provision. He connects manna to John 6 where Yeshua identifies Himself as the bread of life, and to 1 Corinthians 10 where Paul reveals the spiritual rock that followed Israel was Messiah.

He traces God's Shekinah presence from the wilderness cloud to the tabernacle dedication to the Revelation 15 sanctuary filled with glory. The sermon concludes that God always provides a way of escape and that Messiah is not just a pillar but the chief cornerstone and foundation of all belief.

Hook / Wordplay

Manna wasn't literally a pillar, but the rabbi reframes it as a third pillar—cloud protected, fire purified, manna sustained—three columns of God's faithfulness all fulfilled in one Person.

2023-02-12

Where Do I Still Fall Short?

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Identify the specific thing I place between myself and deeper relationship with God — whether security, opinions, comfort, or prestige — and honestly confront it rather than hiding behind general obedience.
  • Don't declare 'whatever God says I will do' without follow-through; match intention with action, especially when the cost is uncomfortable and the answer isn't what I wanted to hear.
  • Stay focused on the finish line — when circumstances feel overwhelming, remember that breakthrough may be closer than I think, like the fog clearing just beyond where I stand.
One-Line Action
Ask God specifically where you still fall short, listen honestly for the answer, and act on it rather than just declaring good intentions.
Memorable Quote
All Yeshua did was hold a mirror up to the man and said, here's where your conflicts of interest are. Here's where you're being stifled, where you're not allowing it to come down into the core of your being because these other things have control over you.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon draws from the Torah portion Yitro (Exodus 18-20), where Israel witnesses God's power at Sinai and declares 'whatever God says we will do.' Rabbi pivots to Matthew 19 where the rich young ruler asks Yeshua 'Where do I still fall short?' despite keeping all the commandments. Yeshua reveals the man's wealth as the specific impediment blocking deeper relationship with God. The rabbi emphasizes that the impediment differs for each person — it could be wealth, opinions, prestige, career, or how we idolize our children.

He draws a parallel to Isaiah 6, where Isaiah only received his full prophetic calling after King Uzziah died and that stumbling block was removed. Citing Romans 3:23, he affirms that all fall short but God has made provision through Yeshua's faithfulness. Using Paul's athletic metaphors from 1 Corinthians 9 and Hebrews 12, he urges the congregation to run straight for the finish line without being sidetracked.

He illustrates the danger of giving up too soon with a movie scene where a father makes a tragic decision just moments before safety was within reach. The sermon closes with a call to ask God directly where we fall short and then act on the answer rather than merely declaring good intentions.

Hook / Wordplay

The rich young ruler's question 'Where do I still fall short?' becomes a mirror held up to every believer — we all have unnamed impediments, and Yeshua's answer is always specific to what controls us.

2023-02-19

Mishpatim — Whose Rules Do You Follow?

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Reframe God's commands as boundary markers that show where danger lies rather than restrictions that stifle—obedience is protection, not limitation.
  • When tempted to handle problems in my own strength or by my own invented rules, remember that self-made rules are unenforceable and leave me subject to the world's systems instead of God's provision.
  • Let genuine encounter with God produce generosity and restitution from the heart—like Zacchaeus who gave four-fold without being told—rather than checking off boxes of external compliance.
One-Line Action
This week, identify one area where I have been following my own rules instead of God's boundary markers and consciously submit it back to Him.
Memorable Quote
Devarim, Deuteronomy 6, the cornerstone of our faith, the Shema.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The portion Mishpatim (Exodus 21–24:18) lays out rulings God gave Israel, and the sermon asks whose rules we truly follow. The rabbi reframes the Hebrew slavery laws not as condoning slavery but as a social safety net designed to restore people after financial loss within six years. He draws on Jeremiah 34 where the Israelites released their servants as God commanded but then recaptured them, ultimately losing everything they tried to protect.

The command to honor parents is connected to Yeshua's rebuke of the Pharisees' Corban loophole in Matthew 15, showing how man-made traditions can void God's commands. The eye-for-eye principle is elevated through Yeshua's teaching in Matthew 5 about turning the other cheek and loving enemies—going to the heart behind the law rather than mere letter-keeping. The Zacchaeus story in Luke 19 is contrasted with the rich young ruler to illustrate genuine heart transformation versus external compliance.

Drawing from Colossians 2 and 2 Peter, the rabbi argues that making your own rules paradoxically enslaves you to the world's systems, while submitting to God's boundaries brings true freedom. The central message is that Torah means instruction, all of it rests on loving God and loving neighbor, and following the Rulemaker's boundaries is for our benefit and liberation.

Hook / Wordplay

"Whose rules do you follow?" — making your own rules doesn't set you free; it makes you subject to every other rule of this world. True freedom comes from the Rulemaker whose boundaries mark where the danger is, not where the fun stops.

2023-02-26

Terumah: God's Design for Family Living

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Stop hedging on obedience by keeping 'backup plans' — the Luke 9 pattern of 'I'll follow you, but first let me…' reveals a divided heart that treats God as one option among several.
  • Take time to explain the 'why' behind spiritual disciplines to children and newer believers rather than demanding compliance — Ephesians 6 frames irritating children as dismissing their questions instead of engaging them.
  • Recognize that the spiritual blessings described in Ephesians 1 are present-tense inheritance, not future hope — actively identify where I am living below what God has already provided.
One-Line Action
Identify one area where you are 'looking back' instead of plowing forward, and surrender it to God this week.
Memorable Quote
We tend to live way below what it is God has built around us and built us for.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon centers on Parashat Terumah and God's command to build a sanctuary so He may dwell among His people (Exodus 25:8), identified as the key verse. Rabbi traces this dwelling theme from the Tabernacle through Solomon's Temple, showing in 1 Kings 6:12-13 that God's presence is conditioned on obedience to His commands. He highlights David's humble response in 2 Samuel 7 when told his son would build the Temple, noting God builds the house around those who yield to Him.

Moving to the New Covenant, he connects Yeshua's words in John 14:23 — 'we will come to him and make our home with him' — to the same Tabernacle principle of obedience enabling God's dwelling. Using Luke 9's three would-be followers, he illustrates how half-hearted commitment and looking back, much like Israel in the wilderness, disqualifies people from Kingdom service. He applies Ephesians 6 to show God's design for family living requires Him at the center, with parents raising children through patient explanation rather than antagonism.

The sermon closes with Ephesians 1, emphasizing the vast spiritual inheritance believers possess in Messiah but rarely walk in. The overarching message is that God's design for family living means He is the core of the household, and walking in obedience opens the door for His transforming presence.

Hook / Wordplay

There's a difference between a house and a home — you have a house you live in, but a home is what you build with your family and community. God wants to do a 'home makeover' in us.
