2023-03-05

Esther: A Study in God's Timing

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Mordechai's confidence that deliverance would come 'from a different direction' if Esther declined is a model for not limiting God to one solution — trust the outcome even when you can't control the instrument.
  • The Esther timeline (4 years to the throne, 12 years to the crisis) is a corrective against impatience; God's background orchestration often spans years before the moment of deliverance arrives.
  • Esther didn't act as a lone hero — she called the entire community to fast and unite before she approached the king, a reminder that major spiritual battles require collective investment, not solo courage.
One-Line Action
Trust that God is orchestrating your circumstances even when you cannot see His hand, and invest yourself in His purposes rather than anxiously trying to control the timeline.
Memorable Quote
Sometimes the traps that the enemy lays out are traps that he himself falls into.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The rabbi presents the Book of Esther as a study in God's timing, emphasizing that the story spans far longer than most readers realize — four years between Vashti's removal and Esther becoming queen, and twelve years before Haman's genocidal decree. He highlights that God's name never appears in the text, yet divine orchestration is unmistakable in the sequence of events: the king's insomnia, his reading of the records crediting Mordechai, Haman's arrival at precisely the wrong moment, and Esther's carefully staged banquet revelations. Mordechai's faith is held up as exemplary — he told Esther that if she failed to act, deliverance would come from a different direction, showing absolute confidence in God's faithfulness regardless of the human instrument.

The rabbi connects this to Yeshua's teaching in Matthew 6 not to worry about provision, Paul's testimony in Philippians of contentment in all circumstances, and Habakkuk's instruction to wait for the vision at its appointed time. A parallel is drawn to the Jesus Movement of the late 1960s, when a generation written off as lost experienced unexpected revival through a small church that welcomed everyone — a movement that also sparked the renewed Messianic Jewish movement. He points to contemporary signs like the Asbury revival and urges the congregation to invest themselves in God's purposes rather than anxiously watching the hourglass run down, trusting that God is still orchestrating appointed times today.

Hook / Wordplay

"Like sands through the hourglass, so are the days of our lives" — the rabbi reframes Esther as God's slow-motion orchestration across 12 years, not the quick pageant we imagine.

2023-03-12

Spark of Life or Fool's Gold

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When circumstances become uncertain, resist the impulse to grasp at material or emotional substitutes (fool's gold) for genuine trust in God—Israel could not wait even 40 days before replacing Him with a counterfeit.
  • Measure worth not by net worth or worldly portfolio but by how faithfully you carry God's message forward to others; let that reframe what 'value' means in daily decisions.
  • Like Bezalel's God-given craftsmanship, each person has a unique spark of life imparted by God's Spirit for a specific purpose—take a personal census to evaluate whether that spark is being fanned into flame or quietly neglected.
One-Line Action
Identify one area where you are chasing fool's gold—material security, status, or comfort—and consciously redirect that energy toward the spark of life God has placed in you.
Memorable Quote
Israel was delivered out of slavery and out of Egypt, and they could not get Egypt out of them.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon contrasts God's genuine spark of life with the fool's gold of material and spiritual counterfeits, drawn from the Torah portion Ki Tissa. Rabbi explores how Bezalel was simultaneously filled with God's Spirit for sacred craftsmanship in Exodus 31 while the people below demanded Aaron make a golden calf in Exodus 32. He highlights the bitter irony that God delivered Israel from calf-worshipping Egypt, yet they credited a golden calf for their deliverance the moment Moses was out of sight.

Joshua's quiet faithfulness halfway up the mountain is contrasted with Aaron's capitulation under pressure and his absurd excuse that the calf simply came out of the fire on its own. The rabbi draws a parallel to Elijah confronting 450 prophets of Baal, showing a spark of life that burned brilliantly but later flickered under Jezebel's threat. From Matthew 5-6, he warns that pursuing material security creates a darkness that masquerades as light, and no one can serve two masters.

Through 1 Peter 1, he teaches that genuine faith tested by fire is far more valuable than perishable gold, and that our trials serve a purifying purpose pointing toward the revealing of Messiah. The application throughout is to let God's imparted spark fan into an enduring flame rather than settling for fool's gold—the counterfeit comforts that promise security but draw us from God.

Hook / Wordplay

Gold threads through every scene: Bezalel's sacred gold craftsmanship for God's tabernacle versus the people's golden calf, fool's gold versus fire-tested genuine faith, and heaven's streets of transparent gold—same metal, opposite allegiances.

2023-03-19

Vayak'hel Pekudei – What's Your Next Step?

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Seasons that feel like a waste of time may be preparation God is using to equip me for the next thing—trust the process even when it seems pointless, because the equipping only becomes visible in hindsight.
  • The adversary's main tactic is distraction through chaos; when I'm consumed sorting out problems I'm not moving toward God's next step—refocus on yielding to God rather than managing chaos.
  • Knowing something needs to change is not the same as activating the solution—move past 'I know, I know' to actually drawing near to God and taking the concrete next step He's placed before me.
One-Line Action
Identify one area where I've plateaued or become preoccupied with chaos, and take one concrete step toward what God is calling me into next.
Memorable Quote
I found that there were situations where I thought this is a waste of my time. And later I looked back and said, that was a preparation that I didn't know I was in the middle of.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The double Torah portion Vayak'hel-Pekudei recounts Moses assembling the people to build the Tabernacle, where the people gave so generously they had to be told to stop, and completed the work exactly according to God's pattern until His glory filled it. Rabbi uses this as a springboard for the central question: What is your next step? He illustrates through Luke 17's ten lepers that only the Samaritan returned to give thanks and received the deeper blessing, connecting the next step to the Kingdom of God manifesting wherever we allow it to reign.

In Acts 1, the disciples fixated on political restoration while God's actual next step was the outpouring of the Holy Spirit at Shavuot, where Peter stood up completely unprepared and thousands responded. The transfiguration in Matthew 17 showed three disciples a vision of the kingdom they could not yet comprehend, yet God kept advancing them toward the next step. Drawing from 2 Peter 1, Rabbi outlines a progression of spiritual qualities—faith, goodness, knowledge, self-control, perseverance, godliness, brotherly affection, love—as concrete steps toward maturity.

He warns that the adversary's primary strategy is keeping believers preoccupied with chaos so they never take God's next step. The sermon closes with Proverbs 3:5–6 and the assurance that the steps of a righteous person are ordered by the Lord, even when the destination is unclear.

Hook / Wordplay

Every biblical hero faced a next step they couldn't fully understand—Moses descending the mountain, Peter at Shavuot, the leper turning back—yet God kept moving them forward; the question isn't whether the step makes sense but whether we trust the One directing it.

2023-03-26

Guilty But Forgiven

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When the adversary brings accusation, redirect the conversation to God rather than defending yourself—acknowledge your capacity for failure but declare you are not left to yourself.
  • Stop trying to justify or minimize guilt; honest confession before God is the only pathway that actually removes the weight of shame and sets you free.
  • As Passover approaches, actively look for people around you who are trapped in shame and don't know redemption exists, and be willing to speak that hope into their lives.
One-Line Action
Bring your guilt honestly before God instead of hiding behind excuses, and let His forgiveness remove the weight you were never meant to carry.
Memorable Quote
Being guilty is not a bad thing before the Lord. Because he also has another word that keeps coming up, which is he will forgive.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Opening the book of Vayikra, Rabbi Greg highlights how the words 'guilty' and 'forgiven' appear repeatedly across Leviticus 1–5, revealing that guilt is not the end of the story but the setup for God's redemptive provision. He explains that even inadvertent sin carries real consequences, yet God established the sacrificial system as an avenue of return and reconciliation. He contrasts this biblical model with modern cancel culture, calling it 'the catechism of the world—no redemption, just guilt.' Through Romans 7–8 he traces the believer's struggle with sin to the declaration that there is no condemnation for those in union with Messiah Yeshua.

He examines Yeshua's healing of the paralyzed man in Luke 5, where forgiveness of sin preceded physical restoration, and the blind man in John 9, where religious leaders missed the miracle because of doctrinal rigidity. He urges honest confession before God rather than excuse-making, arguing that justifying guilt actually blocks the deliverance God wants to pour out. With Passover approaching, he calls the congregation to carry this message of freedom to people trapped in shame who do not know redemption is available.

Hook / Wordplay

Leviticus pairs 'guilty' and 'forgiven' side by side throughout its opening chapters—guilt is not the verdict but the doorway to God's provision of return.

2023-04-02

What's So Great About Shabbat HaGadol?

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When I catch myself mentally cataloging others' failures, redirect that energy into examining whether I am actually paying attention to what God is asking of me specifically.
  • Recognize when I am 'committed to my plan working' more than committed to following God through uncertainty — the rabbi's warning about substituting our own solutions for God's unfolding direction applies to career, relationships, and spiritual life.
  • Let the Passover season function as the text intends: not just historical memory but a live audit of what still holds me in bondage and where I need God's refining fire to purify rather than my own effort to fix.
One-Line Action
This week, identify one area where you have been doing things your own way while telling yourself it is God's way, and yield it back to Him.
Memorable Quote
Pointing out what other people are doing wrong is not the same thing as doing what is right because the real power of what happens for people who are not doing what's right is when they see the work of God moving in the hearts of people and they say, how can I buy into this?
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon explores why Shabbat HaGadol — the Great Sabbath before Passover — carries special significance, anchoring in the Torah portion Tzav and its command to keep the altar fire burning continually. From Jeremiah 7, the rabbi draws the theme that God valued obedience and attentiveness over the mechanics of sacrifice: 'Pay attention to what I say, then I will be your God.' Moving to the Shabbat HaGadol Haftarah in Malachi 3, he shows that God's refiner's fire was aimed at purifying the priesthood — not destroying them — so they could bring upright offerings, yet the priests responded with self-deception, asking 'How have we robbed you?' while doing things their own way. He parallels this with Yeshua cleansing the temple in Matthew 21, where leaders saw miracles yet only worried about silencing the crowd, and with Yohanan's warning in Matthew 3 that fruitless trees face the fire.

The central insight is that the same fire of God either purifies or consumes, and the difference hinges on whether we yield to Him or cling to our own plans. He warns against the trap of self-righteously condemning others while neglecting personal responsiveness to God. Closing in Romans 13, he frames love as Torah's foundation and urges wakefulness because the final redemption draws nearer, connecting Passover's past deliverance from Egypt to the ultimate future redemption promised through Messiah.

Hook / Wordplay

"What's so great about Shabbat HaGadol?" plays on gadol (great) to probe why this pre-Passover Sabbath carries such weight — landing on the answer that it previews God's pattern of purification before redemption.

Leadership and Responsibility

2023-04-09

A New Look at Jewish Resurrection

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Resurrection power is not reserved for physical death—bring God into the 'dead' situations (finances, relationships, hope) and expect transformation rather than resignation.
  • Paul's Mars Hill method: understand where people stand before trying to lift them up; use their own frameworks as bridges to truth rather than leading with condemnation.
  • The Passover mandate is not just to remember past deliverance but to actively look around for the displaced and oppressed and bring them a message of restoration.
One-Line Action
Identify one 'dead' situation in your life or community this week and actively invite God's resurrection power into it rather than accepting it as permanent.
Memorable Quote
It is impossible for man. It is impossible for humankind.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The Shabbat following Passover traditionally features the Ezekiel 37 dry bones passage, making resurrection its central theme. The rabbi argues that resurrection is fundamentally a Jewish concept carried into Christianity, not the reverse. He traces this through Israel's national resurrection—from enslaved family to free nation at the Exodus, and from scattered diaspora to reestablished state in 1948.

The raising of Lazarus in John 11, occurring just before Passover, served as a prophetic preview of Yeshua's own resurrection days later. Paul's teaching on Mars Hill in Acts 17 shows how resurrection was presented to the Greek world, where some scoffed while others believed. The Pharisee-Sadducee divide over resurrection in Acts 23 further demonstrates it was a live debate within Judaism itself.

The rabbi corrects a common misreading of Gethsemane, arguing Yeshua was not trying to avoid the cross but was experiencing the physical weight of death in the garden and was willing to die there if God willed it. He emphasizes that resurrection is not merely doctrinal but experiential—God breathes new life into situations that seem dead, much as spring renews what winter stripped bare. The call of Passover extends beyond historical remembrance to actively seeking restoration for the oppressed and hopeless.

Hook / Wordplay

What is resurrection but restoration? As spring renews bare winter trees with buds and blossoms, God breathes resurrection life into what seems dead—and Passover falls right in that season of renewal.

2023-04-16

Don't Get Burnt By Strange Fire

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Address 'strange fire' when it is still a spark — compromises in leadership, ideology, or personal life grow into consuming blazes if left unchecked, so early discernment is critical.
  • Use God's Word as the primary gauge for truth rather than popular consensus or political alignment, since both sides can propagate lies and we need an unchanging standard.
  • God delivers us not just for our own sake but so we become extensions of His deliverance — actively snatch others from destructive fires rather than watching passively.
One-Line Action
This week, identify one area where you may be passively consuming 'strange fire' — unexamined information, compromised values, or unholy alignments — and consciously realign it with Scripture.
Memorable Quote
God doesn't deliver us just to make us delivered, but so that we then become an extension of His deliverance power, and help to deliver others who are in need, and to see God bring transformation into their hearts.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The rabbi teaches from Parashat Sh'mini, where God's fire gloriously consumed the burnt offering, but then the same divine fire consumed Nadab and Abihu when they offered unauthorized 'strange fire' while apparently intoxicated. He emphasizes that this occurred at the inauguration of a new move of God's spirit, making the compromise especially dangerous to the entire nation. He draws a parallel to Uzzah, who died touching the Ark when it was transported contrary to God's prescribed method—even well-intentioned actions done outside God's order carry consequences.

A New Testament parallel follows with Ananias and Sapphira, who lied about their offering at the birth of the early messianic community and died on the spot, again at a pivotal inaugural moment. The rabbi extends the 'strange fire' metaphor to modern life, urging discernment about what information and ideologies we consume, warning that totalitarian control mechanisms throughout history begin as small sparks before becoming consuming fires. He connects this to Holocaust Remembrance Day, tracing how indoctrination, attacks on children, and erosion of family foundations led to catastrophic destruction.

Drawing from Jude, he calls believers to 'snatch others out of the fire' rather than abandon them, and to stay anchored in God's Word as the reliable gauge for discerning truth from deception. The sermon closes with a call to pursue holiness, discern between clean and unclean, and become extensions of God's deliverance power to those around us.

Hook / Wordplay

Watch what you consume so it doesn't consume you — the same divine fire that consumed the offering in glory consumed Nadab and Abihu when they brought strange fire.

2023-04-23

Words That Heal Broken Lives

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Words carry physical and spiritual weight: negativity and anger literally exacerbate affliction (the Miriam example, the eczema analogy), so choosing encouragement over criticism is a discipline of spiritual health, not mere niceness.
  • God's law for the afflicted opens with the day of cleansing, not permanent exile—when I encounter someone struggling, my posture should anticipate their restoration rather than writing them off as the modern cancel mentality does.
  • The four lepers' pivot from hoarding abundance to sharing it with starving Jerusalem saved a city; when I find provision or insight in my own difficult season, the instinct to keep it for myself is the wrong move—sharing multiplies the blessing.
One-Line Action
This week, intentionally speak a specific word of encouragement to someone who seems isolated, and catch yourself before speaking any lashon hara about others.
Memorable Quote
You think it's a make sure he stays away from everybody, don't ever let him back in. But it doesn't say that.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi connects the Torah portion on tzaraat (leprosy) with lashon hara (evil speech), demonstrating that what we speak and absorb has physical and spiritual consequences. He reframes the quarantine laws not as permanent exclusion but as a pathway to restoration, emphasizing that Leviticus 14:1 opens with the day of cleansing rather than permanent cancellation. He ties the theme to Yom Ha'atzmaut, paralleling Israel's 2,000-year exile and miraculous national restoration with personal affliction and healing.

Through the narrative of Gehazi receiving Naaman's leprosy after deceitful greed, he shows how wrong motives invite affliction. The four lepers of 2 Kings 7 become unlikely heroes by pivoting from self-interest to sharing their discovery with starving Jerusalem, illustrating how outcasts can bring deliverance. He traces Yeshua's healing encounters—the hemorrhaging woman's desperate faith, the ten lepers where only one returned to give thanks—to show that trust and gratitude are inseparable from restoration.

Drawing on James 3, he warns that the same tongue produces blessing and cursing, and that this contradiction must be confronted. He concludes that just as God restored Israel nationally after millennia of exile, He desires to restore broken individuals through words of healing, genuine community, and yielding to His transforming power.

Hook / Wordplay

Tzaraat (leprosy) and lashon hara (evil tongue) are traditionally linked—what comes out of the mouth manifests on the skin; the same tongue that curses can also heal broken lives.

2023-04-30

Acharei Mot — Finding Our Voice In This World

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Small consistent acts of compassion toward hostile or lost people have cumulative transformative power—don't underestimate offering 'half your sandwich' to someone in darkness
  • The posture toward people living contrary to Scripture is not condemnation or protest but modeling light; Paul says judging outsiders is not our business, but maintaining integrity within community is
  • Loving your neighbor as yourself requires honest, frank relationship—not passive affirmation of everything—which means investing in real relationships where truth can be spoken without offense
One-Line Action
This week, show compassion to someone difficult without trying to correct them—let the light do the work.
Memorable Quote
Finding our voice in the world is finding our place in God. Finding our place in Him gives us an extension of His voice to be the voice going out to the people that draws all people to Himself, not to us, not to create the disciples of our own, but to bring people to know Him who is to know His life eternal.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Beginning from Leviticus 16:2's instruction that Aaron must not enter the Holy Place 'at just any time,' the rabbi expands this into a framework: not just any time, not just any way, not just anywhere—God prescribes how we order our walk. The double portion Acharei Mot-Kedoshim moves from atonement rituals to warnings against adopting the sexual practices of Egypt and Canaan, which the rabbi frames as attacks on family structure and generational continuity, drawing parallels to modern cultural pressures. He connects the ancient practice of child sacrifice to Molech with contemporary disregard for the next generation.

Turning to Ezekiel 22, he highlights how internal moral decay—greed, injustice, forgetting God—causes civilizations to collapse from within, citing the fall of Rome through political instability, economic problems, and weakened borders. The sermon pivots to John 17, where Yeshua prays that believers be 'in the world but not of the world,' serving as extensions of God's love rather than enforcers of condemnation. A powerful illustration follows: a former neo-Nazi skinhead was gradually transformed not by arguments but by cumulative small acts of compassion—a Black coworker sharing his sandwich, a Jewish employer seeing beyond the hatred.

Paul's teaching in 1 Corinthians 5 provides the practical framework: believers should not judge outsiders but should maintain integrity within their own community. The sermon closes with Leviticus 19's 'love your neighbor as yourself' in its full context—honest rebuke within relationship, not passive celebration of everything—urging the congregation to find their voice by finding their place in God.

Hook / Wordplay

'Not just any time'—from God's instruction to Aaron at the Holy of Holies, expanded into: not just any time, not just any way, not just anywhere, not just anyone. Finding our voice means finding God's timing and place for us.

2023-05-07

Emor: Finding God's Time, Place & Purpose

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • True identity is established in union with Messiah, not through cultural markers or self-constructed labels — the Galatians 3 'neither Jew nor Gentile' passage is about unity in Him, not erasure of distinction.
  • The Zadok principle: when everyone else compromises, the standard is to refuse — holiness is demonstrated by life, not taught by lecture alone.
  • Even when we cannot see God's timing or purpose, yielding in obedience positions us exactly where He needs us, as the Isaiah 62 fast story showed — the leader resisted, but God moved through millions anyway.
One-Line Action
This week, identify one area where your reference point has drifted from God's standard and consciously realign it.
Memorable Quote
If you don't know something exists, you can't be longing for it. You can't be striving for it.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi teaches from Parashat Emor (Leviticus 21-23), beginning with the priestly standards of holiness required of the Kohanim and extending that call to all believers as a royal priesthood. He highlights Ezekiel 44 and the faithfulness of Zadok's priestly line, who refused to compromise even when everyone else went astray, and presents them as the gold standard for ministry. Turning to Matthew 5, he shows how Yeshua went to the heart of Torah commands like 'eye for eye' and 'love your neighbor,' pushing beyond legalistic surface readings to expose the core values underneath.

Through Galatians 3-4, he explores how God sent His Son at the appointed time into a culture of legalistic perversion, drawing a pointed parallel to modern cultural distortions of law and identity. He argues that true identity comes from union with Messiah, not from external markers or cultural conformity.

Using a vivid analogy of a child raised in a basement who has no concept of the world beyond, he illustrates how sin confines our perspective and prevents us from even knowing what freedom looks like. He closes by urging the congregation to align with God's timing, position themselves in His presence, and trust His purpose even when the picture is unclear, citing the Isaiah 62 fast movement as an example of God's timing overriding human reluctance.

Hook / Wordplay

Three coordinates for walking in God's will: His time, His place, His purpose — miss one and you're navigating blind.

2023-05-14

B'har-B'chukkotai: Sow & Reap

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Don't 'dig up the seed to check on it'—anxiously monitoring or forcing spiritual growth sabotages the harvest; trust God's timing even when nothing visible is happening yet
  • The Jubilee/Shemitah model shows that God's provision requires trust through seasons of apparent inactivity—the land produced enough beforehand to cover three years, a pattern for trusting God's advance provision in our own lean seasons
  • When personal causes, career, or even family become the ruling priority over God, they become a form of slavery (1 Cor 7:23)—identify what has become a 'taskmaster' and recalibrate
One-Line Action
Identify one area where you are anxiously 'digging up seeds'—trying to force results—and commit to trusting God's timing for the harvest.
Memorable Quote
If you keep digging up what is buried, you may find yourself without any harvest. We need to trust the Lord of the harvest to bring in that harvest.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The double portion Behar-Bechukotai describes God's commands for letting the land rest every seventh year (Shemitah) and the Jubilee cycle, with blessings for obedience and consequences for disobedience. The rabbi frames the sermon around Galatians 6:7—you reap what you sow—emphasizing that we choose whether to invest in our old nature or in God's Spirit. He explains the Jubilee as an agricultural safety net where God owns the land and provides for His people through trust, not self-reliance.

Drawing from Jeremiah 17, he highlights that the person who trusts in Hashem is like a tree planted near water, unshaken by drought. He connects Yeshua's vine metaphor in John 15 to the Torah's covenantal promises: stay united with God, keep His commands, and bear fruit. Using Hosea and Zechariah, he warns that sowing the wind reaps the whirlwind, and building palaces by our own strength while forgetting God leads to desolation.

He ties the counting of the Omer season and worldwide prayer movements for Israel to the theme of planting seeds for a coming spiritual harvest. The central call is to deliberately sow into God's kingdom and trust Him for the harvest rather than anxiously forcing results or relying on human effort.

Hook / Wordplay

'Sow into what you want to reap'—a forward-looking reframe of 'you reap what you sow' that shifts the emphasis from passive consequence to intentional spiritual investment.

2023-05-21

Bamidbar — It All Adds Up

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When life feels like a wilderness, resist calculating meaning from a purely human perspective; God uses desolate seasons to strip distractions and speak directly to the heart, not as a final destination but as a passage toward abundance.
  • Stop qualifying Romans 8:28 with exception clauses — 'all things work together for good' carries no asterisks; trust the full promise even for the hardest circumstances rather than carving out the painful ones.
  • Follow the disciples' model in the ten days before Shavuot: act in obedience and praise during the waiting season rather than staring into the sky paralyzed by what you cannot yet see.
One-Line Action
Release your human calculations about difficult circumstances and walk in obedience through the wilderness, trusting that God's math always resolves for your good.
Memorable Quote
He doesn't bring us there to make us desolate. He brings us there to bring about the contrast of the abundance of what God wants to produce in our lives as we yield ourselves to Him.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon opens the book of Numbers (Bamidbar) by noting that the Hebrew title means 'wilderness,' not 'numbers,' and uses the census as a springboard for the theme 'It All Adds Up in God's Calculation.' Through Hosea's marriage to an unfaithful wife, the rabbi shows how God moves from 'not my people' to full restoration, wooing Israel into the desert to speak to her heart. He connects the Roman census in Luke to God orchestrating circumstances for Messiah's birth amid seemingly inhospitable conditions. In 1 Corinthians 12 he stresses that the body of Messiah requires every part coordinated together, warning against a 'selfie generation' that fractures unity.

Romans 8 anchors the sermon's core claim: all things work together for good without qualifications, illustrated by Richard Wurmbrand's rebuke that believers wrongly add exception clauses to the promise. The Acts 1 passage shows the disciples still miscalculating God's timetable even after witnessing the resurrection, expecting immediate political restoration instead of the spiritual harvest of Shavuot.

Matthew 16 reinforces the point through Peter's rebuke — thinking from a human perspective rather than God's. The sermon ties together Yom Yerushalayim, the Omer count approaching Shavuot, and the disciples' obedient worship in the temple during the ten-day wait as evidence that God's math always resolves, even when ours cannot follow it.

Hook / Wordplay

'It all adds up — in God's calculation.' The census-counting of Bamidbar becomes a metaphor: God's math works differently from ours, and what looks like desolation on our chalkboard resolves perfectly on His.

2023-05-28

Shavuot Shabbat Service

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Don't rely on evangelism formulas (four steps, spiritual laws) alone—seek genuine spiritual empowerment and anointing before sharing faith, because the disciples were told to wait for power from on high before going out.
  • When someone braces for confrontation about their lifestyle, disarm them by placing yourself in the same position of need—'all of us were headed there but God made a provision'—and lead with shared human condition rather than pointing out sin.
  • God's major moves often begin in small, obscure, unified settings rather than large organized campaigns—focus on heart-readiness, unity, and availability rather than scale or resources.
One-Line Action
This week, when an opportunity arises to share faith, lead with God's provision for all of us rather than anyone's specific failing.
Memorable Quote
People brace themselves for confrontation when God wants to fill us with His spirit and make His life happen in the hearts of people everywhere.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The rabbi connects the traditional Shavuot Torah readings—the Sinai theophany in Exodus 19 and Ezekiel's throne-chariot vision—to the events of Acts 2, showing that the Pentecost manifestations of wind, fire, and lights over heads directly paralleled what the congregation had just read in the Shacharit service. He challenges the popular 'upper room' narrative, arguing from Luke 24:53 that the disciples were joyful and continually in the Temple courts, not hiding in fear, which explains how thousands could witness and respond to Peter's message. He frames God's pattern of 'signing off' on pivotal covenant moments with Shekinah glory—at Sinai, at the Tabernacle and Temple dedications, and now at Pentecost—as divine authentication of each new era.

He offers a detailed reading of Daniel 9's seventy weeks as pointing directly to Messiah's sacrifice and the subsequent anointing of the Temple, rejecting a 2,000-year gap interpretation. He reframes Yom HaBikurim from agricultural first fruits to the spiritual harvest of souls, emphasizing that effective evangelism requires genuine empowerment from on high rather than just methods or talking points. He draws a parallel to the Jesus Movement of the late 1960s–70s, where a small obscure church sparked a worldwide revival producing Vineyard and Calvary Chapel.

He references the Saving Private Ryan grave scene to urge living worthy of the sacrifice made for us. He closes with a personal anecdote about responding with grace to a woman who expected condemnation, modeling the love-first evangelism the sermon advocates.

Hook / Wordplay

God didn't launch the ship with a bottle—He launched every new covenant era with a manifestation of His power, signing off on it like a graphic designer's approval stamp.

2023-06-04

Naso: A Nation of Trans People

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Audit which voices—media, culture, social platforms—are shaping my thinking more than God's Word, and consciously elevate Scripture as the primary influencer
  • Don't coast on inherited faith like Samson; the Nazirite vow required personal choice, and so does genuine commitment to God
  • When opposition or setbacks come, respond like Paul—move forward as 'more than a conqueror' rather than settling into blame or victimhood
One-Line Action
Identify one worldly influencer consuming your attention this week and replace that time with focused prayer or Scripture study.
Memorable Quote
God has no pigeonholes. God is always wanting to expand, always wanting to increase, always wanting to manifest His power and His influence in our lives.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon draws from Parashat Naso, covering the Levitical census and the Nazirite vow in Numbers 6. Rabbi uses the provocative title to redefine 'trans' as the spiritual transformation every believer undergoes, pivoting to the central question: who is your influencer? He examines Samson as a cautionary Nazirite who never personally chose his commitment, played at the edges of his vow, and ultimately lost God's anointing without realizing it.

Paul is presented as the counter-model—transformed on the Damascus road, he became an influencer even from prison, never claiming victimhood despite immense suffering. Drawing from Romans 12:1-2, the rabbi urges believers not to conform to worldly standards but to be transformed by the renewing of the mind, developing a sober self-estimate based on trust. He warns that worldly influencers spin narratives before facts emerge, dividing communities and inflating self-importance.

The body of Messiah metaphor from Romans 12 reinforces that different gifts must coordinate rather than isolate. He closes by connecting the Aaronic benediction's placement directly after the Nazirite vow in the Torah portion to the theme of personal devotion leading to God's blessing.

Hook / Wordplay

"A Nation of Trans People"—not gender transition, but spiritual transformation. Everyone is constantly being transitioned; the real question is who is your influencer.

2023-06-11

Follow the Cloud, Not the Crowd

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When facing hardship, bring complaints directly to God as requests for strength rather than venting among others, which spreads discouragement and can erode even strong leaders like Moses.
  • Resist the pull of nostalgia that romanticizes past bondage — recognize God's current provision (the manna) even when it feels monotonous or insufficient.
  • Stay anchored to God's direction rather than being swayed by loud cultural voices offering substitute frameworks for meaning; ask whether a movement points toward God or toward human self-worship.
One-Line Action
This week, when a loud voice or internal complaint tries to redirect your focus, pause and ask whether you are following the cloud or the crowd.
Memorable Quote
From Devarim, Deuteronomy 6 for the cornerstone of our faith.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The Torah portion Bahalotra describes how Israel was to follow the cloud of God's presence — moving when it moved, stopping when it stopped. Rabbi connected this to the Canadian wildfire smoke covering the US that week as a timely entry point into the text. In Numbers 11, the mixed multitude began complaining about hardship and romanticizing life in Egypt, conveniently forgetting they had been slaves. Their complaining influenced even Moses to despair, illustrating how negative voices erode faith across an entire community.

Citing Exodus 23:1-2, the rabbi warned against following the crowd when it does wrong, contrasting this with Hebrews 12's great cloud of witnesses whose faith testimony encourages perseverance. Paul's response to suffering in 2 Corinthians 12, where God's power is perfected in weakness, was presented as the antithesis of Israel's complaining spirit. The Haftarah from Zechariah reinforced the theme through Joshua the high priest's restoration and Zerubbabel's promise that God finishes what He starts — not by force nor by power but by His spirit.

The sermon addressed modern cultural substitutes for God, particularly the elevation of environmental activism to a quasi-religious status that shifts worship from Creator to creation. Romans 8 was used to show that creation itself waits not for humanity's elimination but for the revelation of God's children, whose redemption brings freedom to all creation. The closing call was to follow God's cloud of presence and purpose rather than the loud voices of cultural crowds.

Hook / Wordplay

Canadian wildfire smoke blanketed the US the same week as the Torah portion about Israel following God's cloud — 'Follow the Cloud, Not the Crowd.'

Trust vs Fear

2023-06-18

Who Do You Represent?

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When facing situations beyond my ability, resist the grasshopper mentality — remember that God's commitment, not my résumé, determines the outcome, just as the Canaanites were actually terrified of Israel for 40 years.
  • Represent Messiah through demonstrated action, not just talking points — like Paul, whose credentials were his suffering and transformed life, not his rhetoric alone.
  • Cast anxiety on God in real time rather than only thanking Him after the crisis passes; make 'where do we go from here?' the reflex instead of 'that was close.'
One-Line Action
Identify one situation where you have been retreating like a grasshopper and step into it this week trusting God's empowerment rather than your own strength.
Memorable Quote
God doesn't call us to do what we think we have the capability of doing. He calls us normally to do things that are beyond our capability so that our reliance is on him and not on ourselves.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon opens with Parashat Shlach Lekha, where God tells Moses to send men to reconnoiter Canaan — not to decide whether to enter, but to prepare for what God had already promised. The twelve spies returned with abundant fruit but added a faithless 'however,' focusing on the giants and fortified cities rather than God's commitment, and their grasshopper self-image cost an entire generation the promised land. The rabbi connects this to the concept of the Shaliyach — an emissary sent with divine credentials and authority — showing how Yeshua sent His disciples as sheep among wolves, equipped not by their own strength but by the Spirit.

Drawing on Peter's restoration in John 21, Moses' reluctance at the burning bush, and Paul's transformation from persecutor to apostle, the sermon demonstrates that God consistently calls people beyond their natural ability so their reliance stays on Him. The tzitzit (fringes) commanded in Numbers 15 served as a physical reminder to obey God rather than follow human impulses. From 1 Peter 5, the rabbi urges humility, sobriety, and casting anxiety on God while standing firm against the adversary.

The woman with the issue of blood illustrates that encountering Messiah transforms us from receiving His representation to becoming His representatives. The central challenge is that how we represent God matters as much as claiming to represent Him — through yielded, Spirit-empowered lives rather than self-reliant effort.

Hook / Wordplay

Yeshua sends us as sheep among wolves not as a menu item but as a divine strategy to 'take the teeth out of the wolf' and turn predators into companions.

2023-06-25

Korah: At The Heart of Rebellion

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Rebel against sin in your own heart first; making the focal point of your existence pointing out how others miss the mark is itself a redirect away from God's calling on your life.
  • Don't abandon a high calling to grasp for someone else's position—Korah was one of the chief leaders yet destroyed himself reaching for what wasn't his, proving that self-importance blinds you to the calling you already have.
  • Filter the noise: word battles and cultural controversies accomplish nothing useful and eat away at the hearer like gangrene; stay anchored in God's word rather than hyper-focusing on any one cultural category.
One-Line Action
Examine where pride or self-importance may be blinding you to the calling God already placed on your life, and redirect that rebel energy toward conquering sin in your own heart.
Memorable Quote
From Devareem, Deuteronomy 64, the cornerstone of our faith.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The rabbi teaches on Parashat Korach, exploring what lies at the heart of rebellion. He distinguishes righteous rebellion against tyranny from unrighteous rebellion against God's appointed order, arguing that Korah's uprising was driven by pride and self-importance despite Korah being a man of high standing. He traces a causal chain from the spies' evil report, which progressively eroded community trust and created the conditions for Korah's revolt.

He contrasts King Saul from the tribe of Benjamin, who deflected blame and sought public approval rather than genuine repentance, with Paul from the same tribe, whose encounter with Messiah produced authentic humility and contentment. Drawing on Jeremiah 17, he teaches that the heart is deceitful but God searches it and offers transformation through a new heart per Ezekiel 36:26.

He applies 2 Timothy 2 to warn against word battles and godless babbling that bring catastrophe to hearers, urging believers to rebel against sin in their own lives rather than appointing themselves judges over others. He closes by calling the congregation to approach the lost with a heart to restore and rescue rather than condemn.

Hook / Wordplay

"At the heart of rebellion" plays on the dual meaning of heart—the core of what drives rebellion and the literal deceitful heart of Jeremiah 17:9 that God alone can search and transform.

2023-07-02

Life Is A Journey

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When plans collapse, ask what God might be redirecting you toward rather than fuming at the change — the rabbi's 'missed' judo chance protected the most important year of his life.
  • Stop declaring 'God told me' with false certainty; present leanings as leanings and stay open to course correction, since misreading impressions as divine commands distorts faith.
  • Refuse to live as a 'time traveler' — replaying past what-ifs or projecting future anxieties steals the present, which is the actual gift God has given.
One-Line Action
This week, when a plan falls apart, pause before reacting and ask God what He might be navigating you toward instead.
Memorable Quote
God is better than GPS. He knows exactly where we're going, and He knows how best to get us there.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The double portion Hukkat-Balak frames a sermon on life's unexpected detours. Numbers 21:4 reveals that Israel's tempers grew short because of the detour around Edom, and the rabbi uses this as a springboard for the central thesis: don't fight detours, embrace God in them. He connects the Yiddish saying 'Man plans, God laughs' to multiple biblical examples — Moses striking the rock instead of speaking to it and forfeiting entry to the Promised Land, and Balaam's scheming to circumvent God's blessing on Israel.

Extended personal stories illustrate the principle: the rabbi nearly avoided Bible school (which became the most transformative year of his life) and years later reached the judo semi-finals only to see how winning earlier would have derailed his calling. He warns against living as 'time travelers' trapped in past regrets or future anxieties, citing James 4 on the arrogance of assuming tomorrow.

Proverbs 16:9 and Psalm 37:23-24 anchor the closing appeal: God directs our steps and holds us by the hand even when we stumble. The sermon concludes that discernment is needed to distinguish distracting detours from protective ones, and that yielding to God as navigator is the only reliable path.

Hook / Wordplay

God is better than GPS — He knows exactly where we're going and how best to get us there. The Yiddish 'Der mentsh trakht un Got lakht' (man plans, God laughs) threads the entire message.

2023-07-15

Numbering Men & Counting Women

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Consider consequences before acting impulsively—the tribe of Simeon's inheritance was permanently diminished by momentary sin; pause and seek God's guidance when the ramifications are unclear.
  • Approach conflict and authority the way the daughters of Zelophehad did—with honor, clarity, and respect—rather than with rebellion like Korah or the ten spies, whose brash confrontations brought disaster.
  • Speak words of hope and love to people rather than condemnation; the rabbi's response to the woman who asked if she was going to hell modeled reaching people through grace, not judgment.
One-Line Action
When facing a decision with unclear consequences, pause and bring it before God rather than acting on impulse.
Memorable Quote
We need to find the avenue to encourage people to move in a new direction instead of just condemning the direction that they're finding themselves in.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon opens with Pinchas receiving God's covenant of peace for stopping the plague by killing Zimri and Cozbi, then highlights the devastating consequence for the tribe of Simeon, which fell from third-largest to smallest due to the sin at Baal Peor. A census follows and inheritance is distributed by tribal size, illustrating how sin permanently diminished their portion. The rabbi pivots to the daughters of Zelophehad in Numbers 27, who approached Moses respectfully to request inheritance rights—contrasting their wise petition with the rebellious methods of Korah and the ten spies.

He elevates the biblical portrayal of women as spiritually discerning, citing Proverbs 31, Deborah, the women at Yeshua's crucifixion, and Hannah. He connects Romans 1 to the pattern of exchanging God for created things, drawing parallels to contemporary cultural shifts around gender. The sermon concludes with a call to emulate God's love and speak hope rather than condemnation, using a personal anecdote about responding with grace to a challenging question.

Hook / Wordplay

'Numbering Men & Counting Women'—a play on the census (numbering the tribes) while asserting that women truly 'count' in God's economy. Also riffs on 'BLM—But (Daughters') Lives Matter.'

2023-07-16

Don't Get Spoiled With Your Spoil

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Guard against letting material comfort or personal achievement quietly replace God as my source of identity — the giver matters more than the gift.
  • Practice simple integrity in commitments: let yes mean yes and no mean no, rather than over-promising or hedging with dramatic vows.
  • When I receive something good, ask how it can serve the community around me rather than defaulting to accumulation — stewardship is the antidote to entitlement.
One-Line Action
This week, identify one blessing you're holding tightly and find a concrete way to share it with someone in your community.
Memorable Quote
Don't get so caught up in what you possess that you miss out on possessing the things that he's given and bestowed upon us for our good.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi teaches from the double portion Mattot-Masei, focusing on how Israel handled the spoils of war against Midian and the lessons for us today. He opens with the Torah's laws on vows, connecting to Yeshua's teaching in Matthew 5 to simply let your yes be yes rather than swearing oaths. The bulk of the sermon examines Numbers 31-33, where Moses instructs the people on distributing spoils communally rather than through greed, and warns that failing to drive out corrupting influences will leave thorns in their eyes.

He connects the two-and-a-half tribes' request to settle east of the Jordan to the danger of prioritizing material comfort over communal obligation. Drawing from Jeremiah's calling and Israel's later abandonment of God, he shows how a nation exchanged its glory for worthless things. He pivots to Yeshua's teaching on storing treasure in heaven rather than earth, and Paul's warning in Galatians not to let freedom become license for the old nature.

The central exhortation is that possessions, achievements, and even victimhood can all become idols that displace gratitude toward God and love toward neighbors. He closes by urging the congregation to seek first God's kingdom, recognizing that everything we have is from Him and meant to be shared.

Hook / Wordplay

"Don't get spoiled with your spoil" — a double meaning where 'spoil' is both the blessings God gives (war booty, inheritance, provision) and the character corruption that comes from clutching those blessings too tightly.

2023-07-23

Devarim: Life in Review

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Conduct a personal 'life in review'—not measuring success by material comfort but by the depth of your relationship with God and service to others, since the Laodiceans' wealth blinded them to their spiritual poverty.
  • Stop investing energy in worry by adopting the minister's practice: write down what you would worry about, leave it with God, and redirect that energy toward kingdom purposes—most fears never materialize.
  • Reframe painful seasons as pruning rather than punishment; God cuts away what is unproductive not to destroy but to increase fruitfulness, so lean into discomfort as a sign of His active care.
One-Line Action
Evaluate one area of your life where material comfort may be masking spiritual complacency, and invite God to prune it back so genuine fruitfulness can grow.
Memorable Quote
Pruning is not destroying. It's taking off what is not productive to make the full plant more fruitful and productive.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon opens with Devarim (Deuteronomy), where Moses reviews Israel's 40-year wilderness journey on the cusp of entering the Promised Land, and draws a parallel to our personal need to evaluate where we've been and where we're heading spiritually. Rabbi highlights how Israel's failures—fear, rebellion, taking matters into their own hands—cost them the land, yet God continued to offer restoration rather than destruction. Through Isaiah 1 he shows God's consistent pattern: rebuke followed by an invitation to repentance and renewal, with the memorable appeal to 'come, let's talk this over together.' The John 15 vine-and-branches passage reinforces that fruitfulness comes only through union with Messiah, and that pruning is God's tool for growth, not punishment.

The Laodicean warning from Revelation 3 illustrates the danger of spiritual self-satisfaction—being materially rich yet spiritually bankrupt—with the rabbi noting that Laodicea's famous textile trade and eye-salve made Yeshua's rebuke especially pointed. Matthew 6 redirects focus from worry to seeking God's kingdom first, illustrated by a minister who wrote down his worries for a month and discovered none of them materialized.

Paul's testimony in Philippians 3 caps the message: everything is disadvantage compared to knowing Messiah, and the mature believer keeps pressing forward rather than assuming they've arrived. The overarching call is to live in genuine union with Messiah, serve one another, and allow God's Spirit to make ongoing course corrections throughout our lives.

Hook / Wordplay

'Devarim' means 'words' and names the book—Moses' review of Israel's journey connects to the power of our own words to acquit or condemn us (Matthew 12:36-37), framing the entire sermon as a reflection on what our life's words have spoken.

2023-07-30

VA'ETCHANAN: Tisha B'Av & God's Great Love

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • God's judgments are expressions of tough love meant to redirect us back to Him—interpret hardship as redirection, not abandonment
  • Leaning on your own understanding is functionally like idolatry, putting self in God's place; actively defer to His word over your own reasoning
  • Walk in the Spirit 'day by day,' not just Shabbat to Shabbat or holiday to holiday—let the Spirit order daily life consistently
One-Line Action
When hardship strikes, ask what God is redirecting you toward rather than fixating on what He seems to be taking away.
Memorable Quote
Even when we see elements of judgment, judgment is not always bad. It feels bad.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi connects the Torah portion Va'etchanan with the observance of Tisha B'Av, framing the apparent contradiction between the saddest day on the Jewish calendar and God's great love as the sermon's central thesis. He walks through Deuteronomy highlighting Moses's repeated expressions of God's love, desire for blessing, and heartfelt wish that the people's devotion would remain constant. He traces historical calamities associated with Tisha B'Av—the spies' evil report, both Temple destructions, and the Spanish Inquisition—framing them not as curses but as consequences of rebellion that serve as God's tough love to draw people back.

He connects Yeshua's prediction of Jerusalem's destruction in Luke 21 with His weeping over the city in Matthew 23, showing judgment always paired with the promise of restoration in 'Baruch Haba B'Shem Adonai.' He notes that the month of Av means 'father' and is also called Menachem Av (comforting father), linking it to Yeshua's promise of the Comforter. Using Galatians 5 he contrasts the old nature with the fruit of the Spirit, calling it an 'inoculation' against the disease of division. He closes urging believers not to settle for minimum spiritual effort but to press toward the fullness God has purchased for them.

Hook / Wordplay

The month of Av means 'father' and is traditionally called Menachem Av—'comforting father'—connecting directly to Yeshua's promise not to leave us without a Comforter, turning the saddest month into a season of consolation.

High
2023-08-06

Ekev: Let God Bring It!

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When facing challenges that built up over time, trust God's 'little by little' strategy rather than demanding instant resolution—He removes obstacles at a pace that prevents being overwhelmed by the aftermath.
  • Treat God's love as a commodity to actively circulate, not hoard; like a leaking vessel, I need to keep receiving and giving it away to see its transforming effect in my community.
  • Before crediting my own effort for any success, pause and acknowledge the One who gave the power to achieve it—Deuteronomy 8:17-18 is a daily humility checkpoint.
One-Line Action
This week, identify one person who seems despondent and intentionally bring them an expression of God's love through a concrete act of encouragement.
Memorable Quote
The one commodity that God gives us that is an unlimited resource is his love. And it's that commodity that, no matter how much you give it away, he always provides more, it's always there, there's a continual supply.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The Torah portion Ekev opens with God's covenant promise: because you listen and obey, He will keep His covenant of love and blessing. Rabbi frames the sermon around 'Let God bring it'—God wants to activate His full provision in our lives, while what the world brings leads only to bondage and confusion. He situates the portion within the seven weeks of Isaiah leading to the High Holy Days, noting this aligns with Yeshua's 40-day wilderness fast where He quoted directly from Deuteronomy to answer every temptation.

A key teaching is that God removes obstacles 'little by little' so we are not overwhelmed, and that we must not credit our own strength for what God provides. Drawing from Isaiah 49, he emphasizes God's promise never to forget His people, even engraving them on His palms. The sermon culminates in Romans 8, declaring that nothing—trouble, hardship, persecution, or any power—can separate us from God's love through Messiah.

Rabbi draws a parallel between Deuteronomy and the Gospel of John as books uniquely centered on God's deep love. He closes by calling God's love the one unlimited commodity: the more you give it away, the more He provides, and urges the congregation to become vessels carrying that transformative love into their communities.

Hook / Wordplay

'Let God bring it'—the street challenge 'bring it!' reframed as divine invitation: God wants to bring His full covenant power into your life, but what others bring, you won't want.

2023-08-13

Re'eh — The Choice is Yours

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When facing a major decision, resist acting on anger or emotion — pause, ask God for wisdom (James 1:5), and trust He gives liberally without holding back rather than reaching for the first available answer.
  • Practice love as action, not sentiment: esteem others above yourself in concrete daily ways, because love that covers a multitude of sins is active love, not passive feeling.
  • When you or someone else has strayed, remember that biblical judgment is almost always a wake-up call with an avenue of return — come alongside people with presence before counsel, as Job's friends did best in their first seven silent days.
One-Line Action
This week, at one decision point where emotion or convenience pulls you toward the wide road, stop and pray for wisdom before acting.
Memorable Quote
The choice is ours, but the choice is very simple. Choose the Lord's choice.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Teaching from Parashat Re'eh (Deuteronomy 11:26), the rabbi frames God's presentation of blessing and curse not as divine imposition but as natural outcomes of our alignment with or departure from His path. He likens straying from God to marital unfaithfulness, where neglecting sacred commitments leads to unforeseen entanglement with 'other gods' that never deliver on their promises. Connecting to Yeshua's teaching on the narrow gate in Matthew 7, he argues the path is narrow not because God cannot build wider but because we are easily sidetracked by alluring alternatives.

Drawing from the Haftarah in Isaiah 54-55, he highlights God's desire to restore storm-ravaged lives with everlasting compassion, even after abandonment. He weaves in James 1 on asking for wisdom without doubting, warning that anger and double-mindedness block our ability to hear God's voice. Micah 6:8's call to act justly, love mercy, and walk humbly is presented as God's beautifully simple requirement.

From Philippians 2 and 1 Peter 4, he urges active love over self-promotion, contrasting the emptiness of viral fame with genuine community. He recounts how Yeshua told Peter the adversary wanted to sift him like wheat, stripping away the good, yet prayed his trust would not fail. The sermon closes with an anecdote of a woman who, offered the choice between visible ministry and hidden influence, simply said 'Lord, You choose' — embodying the thesis that the best choice is to choose God's choice.

Hook / Wordplay

'The Choice is Yours — Where Will It Lead You?' frames life as a highway fork: the wide road of self-navigation versus the narrow path of yielding to God's GPS.

2023-08-20

Shoftim: What Is Equal Justice?

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When I judge a situation, check whether I'm pursuing God's standard of justice or just wanting the outcome that benefits me—'all of a person's ways are right in his own view, but the Lord weighs the heart.'
  • In the John 8 story, Yeshua modeled two responses: mercy toward the accusers (room to save face) and tough love toward the woman ('go and sin no more')—I should learn when each is appropriate rather than defaulting to one.
  • Stop outsourcing change: if I focus on letting God change me from the inside out rather than demanding the world rearrange itself, the influence will follow naturally.
One-Line Action
This week, identify one situation where you are demanding justice that conveniently serves your own interest, and submit it to God's standard instead.
Memorable Quote
He could have claimed injustice. And in the justice of God, he put his own life on the line for us so that we could receive true justice, true deliverance and being set free.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon opens with the Shoftim command to appoint judges who pursue righteous, impartial justice—'Tzedek, tzedek tirdof'—and observes that everyone wants justice but usually means justice that goes their way. Rabbi teaches that without a shared divine standard, competing personal 'truths' produce anarchy, echoing Judges 17 where everyone did what was right in his own eyes. He then reinterprets John 8 (the woman caught in adultery) through a Torah lens, arguing Yeshua was not dismissing the law but exposing the accusers' failure to follow proper judicial procedure—investigation, witnesses willing to testify through trial, and the Torah rule that witnesses cast the first stone.

Yeshua's writing in the dirt was not mystical but a deliberate refusal to make eye contact, giving the accusers room to save face and leave. The sermon moves through Matthew 5-6 on retaliation, favoritism, and materialism, reframing the stereotype of Jewish materialism by quoting Yeshua: pagans chase material things, but seek first the kingdom of God.

Drawing from Isaiah 51-53, the rabbi connects the suffering servant to the sermon's theme: the one person who had legitimate grounds to claim injustice chose instead to lay down his life so that those rebelling against him could receive true justice and freedom. The closing challenge asks whether we would lay down our lives for those who believe differently or simply say 'good for them' when they suffer.

Hook / Wordplay

Everyone wants change—but what they really mean is change the other guy. The song 'Change from the Inside Out' sets up the sermon's core tension: true justice starts with letting God transform us, not demanding others conform to our version of right.

2023-08-27

When Our World Seems to Collapse Around Us

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When someone's world is collapsing, avoid both extremes: don't dismiss their pain ('it'll get better') and don't merely validate without addressing deeper issues—instead, come alongside with genuine presence aimed at restoration.
  • Our capacity to help others in crisis flows directly from our own reconciliation with God through Messiah; we are ambassadors of reconciliation, not self-appointed humanitarians.
  • Performative compassion (sitting on a grating before returning to the penthouse) is posturing, not restoration—real ministry means bearing one another's burdens at personal cost.
One-Line Action
This week, identify one person whose life is collapsing and come alongside them with genuine presence and a heart to restore, not to fix or to judge.
Memorable Quote
But also never underestimate God's ability to successfully and victoriously bring us fully through to the goal that is there before us to what God has made available to us.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Drawing from the Ki Tetze Torah portion, the rabbi builds his sermon around Deuteronomy 22:4—the command not to ignore a brother's collapsed donkey—as a metaphor for coming alongside people whose lives are collapsing. He connects this to recent catastrophes including the approaching 9/11 anniversary, the Maui wildfires, and Hurricane Hilary to show how collapse is both physical and emotional. He warns against two equal failures: dismissing people's suffering as nothing, and superficial posturing that performs compassion without real engagement.

Through Isaiah 54, he demonstrates God's promise to restore even after abandonment, anchored by Joel 2:25's pledge to restore the years the locusts consumed. Turning to Galatians 6, he teaches that the spiritually mature must restore the fallen in humility, not accusation.

The theological climax arrives in 2 Corinthians 5:18-21, where he frames believers as ambassadors carrying a ministry of reconciliation—not reconciling people to a congregation but to God Himself. He closes with 1 John 4:4, insisting that despite how broken the world appears, God's indwelling power is greater, and believers must never give up on moving forward in that power.

Hook / Wordplay

Deuteronomy's collapsed donkey on the road becomes a picture of collapsed lives in our communities—'you may not behave as if you hadn't seen it.'

2023-09-03

God's Got You Coming & Going!

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Stop trying to plan and pack my own spiritual journey—yield the itinerary to God, who already has every step mapped out and promises to be with me 'even to the end of the age.'
  • When I feel like the only one struggling (Elijah's 'I alone am left'), remember God always preserves His remnant; pull back the veil before assuming isolation.
  • Hearing God's word intellectually is not enough—actively respond to what His Spirit is saying, like Nathanael who moved from skepticism to worship once he experienced Messiah's intimate knowledge of him.
One-Line Action
Identify one area this week where you are 'packing your own baggage' instead of yielding direction to God, and consciously surrender it to Him.
Memorable Quote
Praise be his name whose glorious kingdom is forever a man.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon draws on Parashat Ki Tavo ('when you come in') paired with the prior week's Ki Teitzei ('when you go out') to establish that God covers every direction of our lives. Rabbi walks through the first-fruits declaration of Deuteronomy 26, connecting it to the Passover narrative of God bringing Israel out of Egypt to bring them into promise. He surveys the blessings and curses of Deuteronomy 28, framing God's commands not as tyranny but as the pathway where blessing can flow.

The Deuteronomy 29:3 warning about eyes that do not see and ears that do not hear is traced forward through Yeshua's parable teaching in Matthew 13 and Paul's quotation in Romans 11, showing a persistent biblical theme of spiritual dullness. The Nathanael encounter in John 1 illustrates how Messiah speaks to our most intimate, unseen cries—knowing what happened under the fig tree when no one else was watching. The Great Commission of Matthew 28 is presented as echoing Deuteronomy's language of obeying everything commanded, but adding the relational promise 'I am with you always.' The rabbi emphasizes that all of Torah rests on two relational commandments—love God and love neighbor—not mere rule-keeping.

He urges the congregation to stop 'packing their own baggage' and yield direction to God, who has already mapped the journey. The sermon closes with a passionate prayer for a new wave of revival to transform homes and broken people in surrounding communities.

Hook / Wordplay

Ki Teitzei means 'when you go out' and Ki Tavo means 'when you come in'—so God's got you coming and going, covering every direction of your life.

2023-09-10

Nitzavim: Standing for God's Full Fashion Makeover!

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Choose life as an active daily decision — God presents blessing and curse plainly, but drifting back to old patterns happens subtly; the covenant requires intentional standing before Him.
  • Spiritual transformation requires removing old garments before putting on new ones — strip off falsehood, bitterness, and comfortable old habits before God can clothe you in righteousness.
  • Wear love as the outermost garment over everything else — it is the one commodity you can give entirely away and never exhaust, because God continually replenishes it as you yield to Him.
One-Line Action
Identify one 'old garment' — a habitual pattern of bitterness, falsehood, or anxiety — and consciously strip it off this week, replacing it with its opposite virtue.
Memorable Quote
From Devarim to Duranami 6, the cornerstone of our faith.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The Torah portion Nitzavim presents all of Israel standing before God to enter His covenant, with the charge to choose life over death and blessing over curse. The Haftarah from Isaiah 61 introduces the sermon's central metaphor: God clothing His people in salvation and robes of righteousness — a divine fashion makeover. The rabbi connects this to Yeshua reading Isaiah 61 in the synagogue, declaring it fulfilled in their hearing — anointed to bring good news, heal the brokenhearted, and free captives.

He develops an extended wardrobe metaphor: just as priests, kings, warriors, and brides each dress for their role, God provides appropriate spiritual attire for His purposes. Isaiah 63 adds the image of Messiah in royal garments stained crimson, linking to sacrificial atonement. Ephesians 6 frames the armor of God as the warrior's outfit within this spiritual wardrobe.

The sermon draws from Colossians 3 to emphasize stripping off the old self before putting on the new, and names love as the outermost garment that binds everything together. The closing call is to walk in this makeover — not as a superficial statement but as a transformative witness that draws others to God.

Hook / Wordplay

God is a divine tailor performing a full fashion makeover — stripping away tattered garments of sin and clothing us in salvation, righteousness, and praise, with a different outfit for every spiritual role.

2023-09-17

Substitution During the Season of Return

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • God's restoration is total, not partial — He calls His restored people 'virgin of Israel' as if the past never happened, which reframes how I view my own failures and fresh starts.
  • Love is the one commodity you can give entirely away and never run short; loving God fully does not diminish capacity for others but expands it.
  • The season of return is not a ten-day performance to 'get by' until Yom Kippur — it demands genuine internal transformation, which means honestly identifying where I have substituted my own preferences for God's commands.
One-Line Action
This week, identify one area where you have substituted your own way for God's design and take a concrete step of return.
Memorable Quote
When God fixes us in some way, he doesn't do it part way. He doesn't say this is a refurbished version.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Delivered on Rosh Hashanah, the sermon explores the concept of substitution through the lens of the Akedah (Binding of Isaac) from Genesis 22. The rabbi notes Abraham's faith-filled declaration that he and Isaac would both return from the mountain, reflecting trust that God would not abandon His child of promise. He highlights the traditional rabbinic view that Isaac was figuratively raised from the dead, connecting this directly to Yeshua as the ultimate substitutionary sacrifice.

Moving to Jeremiah 31, he emphasizes that God calls wayward Israel 'virgin of Israel' after their unfaithfulness, illustrating that divine restoration is complete rather than partial. He warns against modern cultural substitutions for God's design, using humor about sugar substitutes, bird-robot conspiracy theories, and his 'transparent' wordplay to illustrate how people swap God's calling for lesser identities.

Drawing from Joel 2-3, he ties the shofar blast to God's call for genuine assembly, repentance, and the promise to restore the years the locusts have eaten. He closes by insisting that the ten days of awe are not about temporary good behavior but about lasting transformation through yielding to God's Spirit.

Hook / Wordplay

I decided I am going to call myself transparent, because I am a parent and I am transparent. I want to be fully clear as to what it is we believe.

2023-09-24

The Power of A Song

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Songs and repeated Scripture embed in us before we understand them — keep engaging with God's word because understanding often arrives years later, just as the rabbi chanted Ha'azinu at his Bar Mitzvah without grasping it was about the very power of song itself.
  • God's commitment to restore is relentless and personal: 'If one of yours was scattered to the far end of the sky, He will go there and get you' — this is not abstract theology but a promise that no distance or failure puts us beyond reach.
  • Beware spiritual lukewarmness (Laodicea syndrome) — feeling self-sufficient and 'rich' while actually being blind; the antidote is hearing God's knock and opening the door rather than assuming everything is fine.
One-Line Action
Engage with Scripture the way you'd learn a song by heart — through repetition now, trusting that God will bring understanding and transformation when the moment is right.
Memorable Quote
Sometimes the answer is already in a song in our hearts. It's already there before us, we've seen it.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The rabbi explores Torah portion Ha'azinu, Moses' farewell song written so Israel would memorize it and carry it even when they strayed. He opens with personal stories — childhood jingles, his father embarrassingly singing 'Paint Your Wagon,' and teaching his kids their phone number through melody — to show how songs embed themselves in memory beyond our control. He reveals that this was his own Bar Mitzvah portion, which he chanted without understanding, yet now sees as profoundly fitting given his life as a songwriter.

The recurring image of God as 'the Rock' (Tzur) threads through Deuteronomy 32: perfect in His work, faithful despite Israel's corruption, and committed to restoration even from the far end of the sky. He connects this to Yeshua as the rejected stone becoming the chief cornerstone (Psalm 118:22) and to Paul's teaching on provoking Israel to jealousy through Gentile faith.

He traces the pattern through Peter at Shavuot recognizing Joel's prophecy — 'this is that' — showing how a familiar song suddenly becomes revelation. He closes with Revelation 3's Laodicea message: people who think they are rich but are spiritually blind, yet God still stands at the door knocking, offering restoration rather than destruction.

Hook / Wordplay

Stand on the rock for a return engagement — or let God rock your world. The rabbi plays on 'rock' (Tzur) as both God's character attribute and the foundation metaphor running through the entire portion.
