2023-10-08

Fire Light and Living Water

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Trusting Messiah 'as the scripture says' means grounding faith in obedience to God's word rather than vague spirituality—this is what opens the flow of living water from within.
  • Our value does not come from ourselves but from God choosing to dwell in us; like a sukkah, our frailty is the point—it makes His presence the treasure.
  • Possessing God's commands is not enough; keeping them is the act of love that invites God to reveal Himself and make His home within us (John 14:21-23).
One-Line Action
Yield yourself as a dwelling place for God's presence by not just knowing His word but actively keeping it, so His light and living water flow through you to others.
Memorable Quote
He looks at us and says, I like that tiny home. I'd like to make my residents in there.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

This sermon, delivered on Shemini Atzeret, focuses on Hoshana Rabbah and the eighth day of Sukkot, picking up from a prior week's message lost to technical difficulties. The rabbi connects the Sukkot water libation ceremony—where water was poured over the Temple steps as a prayer for rain—to Yeshua's declaration in John 7:37 that anyone who thirsts should come to Him and drink, with rivers of living water flowing from within. He then ties the brilliant torchlight of Hoshana Rabbah, which Josephus described as making night bright as noonday, to Yeshua's claim in John 8:12: 'I am the light of the world.' The central metaphor frames believers as tiny houses—fragile sukkot booths—in whom the Creator of all things chooses to dwell, placing His treasure of light, living water, and the Holy Spirit within earthen vessels.

The rabbi emphasizes from John 14 that keeping Yeshua's commands, not merely possessing them, is the true expression of love that invites God's self-revelation and indwelling. He shares his personal testimony of coming to faith during Sukkot, experiencing what he later recognized as the living water of John 7:38 rising within him. He briefly touches on eschatological visions of all nations coming to celebrate Sukkot, then closes by urging the congregation to be vessels of God's light and life to those in darkness.

Hook / Wordplay

God apparently likes tiny homes too—He could live anywhere in the universe, but He looks at our frail, falling-apart hearts and says, 'I can work with this. I can put my treasure there.'

2023-10-15

B'resheet: Creation & the Forgiveness of Evil

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Don't use the severity of others' evil to excuse my own shortcomings — comparing sins downward is itself a form of self-deception that blocks real teshuvah.
  • Guard against bandwagon thinking: when everyone chants the same slogans, that's precisely when I need to stop and reason through what's actually being said and what it means.
  • Be positioned to share hope rather than mirror hatred — the calling is to be God's light, not to extinguish darkness by becoming dark yourself.
One-Line Action
When confronted with evil in the world this week, resist the urge to self-justify and instead ask God to reveal the areas where I still need His forgiveness and light.
Memorable Quote
God doesn't create things desolate and void. They become desolate and void because we become desolate and void because we drift away from following him.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi opens the B'resheet portion by exploring how Genesis 1:1 may represent an original creation, with the seven days as God hitting a 'reset button' — recreating from chaos. He introduces the powerful framework of 'two image-makers' in the garden: God who made humanity in His image, and the adversary who injected deceptive spiritual DNA that corrupted human nature. In Genesis 6, he highlights that humanity's imagination was 'always of evil only' and that the Hebrew word for violence there is 'Hamas,' directly connecting the biblical text to the October 7 attack.

He shares the personal loss of his wife's cousin's grandson, killed at the Nova music festival, grounding the sermon in lived grief. He challenges the congregation not to use the severity of others' evil as a shield from examining their own sin, citing that all have sinned and fall short. He traces how normal people become radicalized through propaganda — from Holocaust-era churchgoers to modern university students — when they stop reasoning independently.

Using Paul's Damascus road transformation as an example, he argues that even those consumed by hatred can be reached by God's light. He emphasizes that forgiveness does not mean evil goes unpunished, but God's patience creates space for repentance. He closes by calling the congregation to be expressions of God's light rather than matching hatred with hatred, and to bring a message of hope to a broken world.

Hook / Wordplay

The Hebrew word for violence in Genesis 6 is 'Hamas' — the same word the terrorist organization chose as its name, meaning zeal and bravery in Arabic, but meaning destruction and violence in the Torah's original language.

Noah & God's First Clean Up Operation Against Hamas

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Groupthink destroys reasoning capacity—actively test repeated claims against primary sources rather than absorbing talking points uncritically, because the pre-flood generation and Tower of Babel builders were both undone by unified false narratives.
  • God demonstrated 120 years of patience through Noah before judgment came—persistent faithfulness to an unpopular message matters even when mocked, because you cannot see who will eventually respond.
  • Build refuge in God (Proverbs 18:10) rather than constructing towers of personal ideology—the Babel builders' self-monument was scattered, but those who run to the Lord's name find safety.
One-Line Action
Identify one talking point I have absorbed without tracing it to its original source, and investigate whether the full context supports the claim.
Memorable Quote
Noah was outside of the narcissistic bandwagon group-think box. He was doing what God said to do.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The rabbi connects the Torah portion of Noach to current events by noting that the Hebrew word for violence is Hamas, framing the flood as God's first cleanup operation against violence. He explores how pre-flood humanity was consumed by wickedness and groupthink, unable to hear Noah's 120 years of warnings because their talking points reinforced each other. He draws direct parallels to modern media echo chambers, specifically the October 2023 Israel-Hamas conflict and how misinformation about a hospital bombing spread virally before the truth emerged.

The Tower of Babel narrative is presented as humanity building monuments to themselves, united in one language of self-serving ideology, which God disrupted by confusing their speech. He then connects this to Acts 2 at Shavuot, where God restored communication through His Spirit, reversing the Babel fracture and enabling thousands to respond to Peter's message. He offers a scientific aside on post-flood atmospheric changes affecting fermentation and human lifespans, reframing Noah's drunkenness as encountering an unknown phenomenon.

He traces the groupthink pattern through history—from the flood to the Holocaust to modern campus protests—showing how people lose the ability to reason when trapped in ideological conformity. He closes by calling believers to be like Noah, standing outside the narcissistic bandwagon, trusting God as their strong tower rather than building towers of their own.

Hook / Wordplay

The Hebrew word for violence is Hamas—making the flood narrative God's first cleanup operation against Hamas, connecting a third-millennium-BCE Torah portion directly to October 2023.

Lekh L'kha: The Ties That Bind Us

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When I reach exhaustion from striving in my own strength, recognize it as God's invitation — 'I can work with you now' — rather than as failure, and yield the effort to Him.
  • Examine what 'Haran' I may be stuck in — a comfortable stopping point short of where God is actually calling me — and take the next step forward.
  • Yoking to Messiah (Matthew 11:28-30) means letting Him supply the strength for every forward movement rather than tying myself to human philosophies, strategies, or arm-of-flesh partnerships.
One-Line Action
Identify one 'Haran' in your life — a comfortable stopping point short of God's calling — and take one concrete step past it this week, trusting His strength rather than your own.
Memorable Quote
From Dvaryim due to Rn 6, the cornerstone of our faith.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon explores Torah portion Lekh L'kha through the dual lens of 'the ties that bind' — both the restrictive bonds that hold us back and the unifying bonds that draw us toward God. Avram was called to leave country, kinsmen, and father's house, but family ties delayed him at Haran until his father Terah died, illustrating how strong attachments can stall obedience. A parallel is drawn to Isaiah, whose prophetic ministry opened only after King Uzziah died, showing how dependence on human authority can block divine calling.

Avram's deception in Egypt revealed incomplete faith — he could not yet trust God's protection far from home — demonstrating that faith grows through failures. Lot's choice of Sodom showed how tying ourselves to worldly opportunity leads to captivity, while Avram's refusal to let anyone but God be credited for his wealth showed proper allegiance. Drawing from Matthew 11:28-30, the rabbi taught that yoking ourselves to Messiah provides rest and strength, unlike the exhaustion of self-effort.

Colossians 2 and Galatians 5 reinforce that Messiah wiped away the charges against us and freed us so we would not be entangled in bondage again. The sermon concluded that among all competing ties, only union with Messiah brings true freedom, renewed strength, and fulfillment of God's promises.

Hook / Wordplay

'The ties that bind' — explored as both the restrictive bonds (fear, flesh, sin) that hold us captive and the unifying bonds (covenant, yoke of Messiah, threefold cord) that propel us into God's purpose.

2023-11-05

Vayera: Responding to Trauma

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Identify recurring fear-based patterns in my own life (like Abraham's repeated 'she is my sister' deception) and bring them to God before they sabotage critical moments.
  • When facing crisis, resist the pull of groupthink and emotional bandwagons—pause to verify facts and seek God's perspective before reacting, as Lot's sons-in-law failed to do.
  • Practice Abraham-at-the-Akedah resolve: when God's direction is clear, act early and decisively rather than delaying like Lot, because a double-minded person is unstable in all his ways.
One-Line Action
When trauma strikes, bring it to God rather than fixating on it—let faith, not fear, dictate your next step.
Memorable Quote
When God appears, so does our vulnerability. When God appears, all the elements that are shakeable are shaken.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon examines the Torah portion Vayera through the lens of trauma, showing how biblical figures responded to overwhelming circumstances in vastly different ways. Sarah laughed in disbelief at the promise of a son, Lot's family had to be physically dragged out of Sodom because the culture had so paralyzed their decision-making, and Abraham repeatedly fell into the fear-driven pattern of calling Sarah his sister. The rabbi highlights that Lot's sons-in-law dismissed the warning of destruction as a joke, illustrating how immersion in a corrupt environment destroys the ability to reason clearly.

He draws a striking connection between Abraham's negotiation for ten righteous people and the possibility that Abraham was counting Lot's extended family. Despite his trauma-driven mistakes, Abraham's resolute obedience at the Akedah—rising early, not delaying like Lot—became the defining act God credited as righteousness. The rabbi connects the daily Orthodox recitation of the Akedah to the theme of God's provision and a foreshadowing of resurrection.

He then bridges to the October 7 Hamas attack, arguing that radicalization and generational trauma create the same inability to reason clearly seen in Sodom. The sermon closes with the thesis that responding to trauma rightly means turning to God's solution rather than fixating on the trauma itself, allowing faith to override the fight-or-flight paralysis.

Hook / Wordplay

Vayera means 'He appeared'—but when God appears, so does our vulnerability; everything shakeable gets shaken before He works it for good.

Primary Theme
Suffering, Hope, and Comfort

2023-11-12

Semper Fidelis Loyalty

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • True loyalty is tested not in comfortable times but at the first inconvenience—like Eliezer who refused to eat, drink, or rest until his master's mission was fulfilled, I should examine where I quietly withdraw when commitment becomes costly.
  • A genuine friend earns the right to speak hard truth in love, as Nathan did with David and Paul did with Peter; building that kind of honest relationship requires loyalty first, not just agreement.
  • Trying to accomplish God's purposes under my own power frustrates His grace (Galatians 3:3); trusting His plan through dark and uncertain seasons is itself an act of loyalty to Him.
One-Line Action
Identify one commitment or relationship where you have been a fair-weather friend and take a concrete step this week to show up when it is inconvenient.
Memorable Quote
There's not a one-state solution or a two-state solution, but there is a one-God solution.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon uses the Marine motto Semper Fidelis (always faithful) as its organizing frame, tying Veterans Day, Kristallnacht remembrance, and the Torah portion Hayyei Sarah together under the theme of covenantal loyalty. From Genesis 23-24, the rabbi highlights Abraham's servant Eliezer as the supreme model of loyal service—refusing even to eat until he had stated his mission to find a wife for Isaac, and resisting every delay tactic Laban offered. He contrasts this with Joab's shift of allegiance from the aging King David to Adonijah in 1 Kings 1, showing how decades of loyalty can evaporate when self-interest takes over.

Moving to the New Covenant, the rabbi traces a pattern from Judas's fatal betrayal through Peter's denial and later compromise at Antioch, where Paul publicly confronted him for acting disloyally toward Gentile believers. He draws from Proverbs 18:24 that a true friend sticks closer than a brother and can speak hard truth in love, as Nathan did with David over Bathsheba.

The sermon connects these biblical loyalty failures to modern fair-weather support for Israel, warning that saying 'never again' while remaining passive is not true commitment. He closes by urging the congregation to trust God's methods rather than their own understanding, framing Messiah's sacrificial death as the ultimate act of loyalty—laying down his life while humanity was still in rebellion.

Hook / Wordplay

The Marine battle cry 'Semper Fi' (always faithful) becomes the sermon's lens: biblical covenant loyalty measured against the same standard soldiers pledge to one another—never leave anyone behind.

2023-11-19

Tol'dot: Stopped Up Wells

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Examine whether my own fears or insecurities are blocking what God wants to flow through my life, rather than only looking for external enemies.
  • Build something spiritually durable — Jacob's well lasted thousands of years and became the site of a divine encounter; invest in depth, not convenience.
  • When confused or overwhelmed, do what Rebecca did: inquire of the Lord rather than ruminate on negative possibilities.
One-Line Action
Identify what is stopping up your spiritual wells — fear, bitterness, or self-reliance — and bring it to God so His refreshing can flow again.
Memorable Quote
From Devarim to Duranami 6, the cornerstone of our faith.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon explores the Torah portion Tol'dot, centering on the Philistines stopping up the wells Abraham's servants had dug and Isaac's effort to reopen them. Wells in the ancient desert represented prosperity, stability, and life itself, making their destruction an act of envy-driven sabotage. The rabbi draws a deliberate parallel between the biblical Philistines (Pelishtim) and modern conflicts, noting the Hebrew root 'palash' means invader or trespasser.

He then pivots inward: sometimes we ourselves stop up our own wells through fear, as Isaac did when he lied about Rebecca being his sister, or through bitterness and double-mindedness. The sermon connects to Yeshua meeting the Samaritan woman at Jacob's well in John 4, showing how a well dug centuries earlier became the setting for an offer of living water that wells up into everlasting life.

Hebrews 12 warns against roots of bitterness that contaminate many, and James 4 identifies internal desires as the source of quarrels. The rabbi closes with the striking image of Yeshua on the execution stake digging a well of life for all nations, and Isaiah 12's promise of joyfully drawing water from the springs of salvation.

Hook / Wordplay

"Stopped Up Wells by Local Palestinians" — a deliberately provocative title that is historically accurate (Pelishtim = Philistines = Palestinians), drawing listeners in before pivoting to the spiritual question: what is stopping up your wells?

2023-11-26

Thank God For His Faithfulness

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When circumstances feel overwhelming, recall that God's faithfulness operates beyond our perception—Jacob said 'truly Hashem is in this place and I didn't know it'—so resist the urge to interpret silence as absence.
  • The peace that passes understanding is not passive comfort but functional protection: it keeps emotions and thoughts safe in union with Messiah, preventing panic-driven decisions when everything seems to go wrong.
  • Share faith not for spiritual scorekeeping but because what God has done for us He can do for others—be willing to be the catalyst for someone else's awakening moment.
One-Line Action
Practice thanksgiving as a daily discipline by naming one specific faithfulness of God each morning, especially when circumstances feel uncertain.
Memorable Quote
Bahuadadonaihamevoorach, pa'urokhara na'amavorach, le'olambad.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Built around the Torah portion Vayetze (Genesis 28:10ff) and the Thanksgiving holiday, the rabbi traces Jacob's departure from Beer-sheba toward Haran and God's covenant promise: 'I am with you, I will guard you wherever you go, and I will bring you back.' He reframes Jacob not as a conniver but as a humble man repeatedly taken advantage of, enduring 21 years of Laban's deceptions while sustained by God's faithfulness. The rabbi connects Jacob's ladder vision to Yeshua's words in John 1:51 about angels ascending and descending on the Son of Man, revealing a typological bridge between the Torah portion and the Gospels. Drawing from Lamentations 3, he walks through the despair before arriving at 'great is your faithfulness—they are new every morning.' Through Philippians 4, he teaches that God's peace that passes understanding serves a specific purpose: keeping hearts and minds safe in union with Messiah, preventing fear-driven decisions.

He corrects the prosperity-gospel misuse of 'I can do all things,' showing Paul meant enduring both abundance and want without being moved. The sermon closes with a call to be catalysts for others' awakening, tying back to Matthew 1:23—Emmanuel, God with us—as the message that has been there all along.

Hook / Wordplay

Jacob 'awoke from his sleep' becomes a metaphor for spiritual awakening—'there are a lot of people that are like the walking sleep, functioning but asleep, not seeing what God has in store for us.'

2023-12-03

When God Says To Do...

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When facing a frightening decision that aligns with God's direction, rehearse His specific promises back to Him in prayer—as Jacob did at the Jabbok—rather than rehearsing the worst-case scenario.
  • Recognize that fear of the unknown often projects outdated information onto the present; the person or situation you dread may have changed entirely, as Esau had transformed from sworn enemy to weeping brother.
  • Don't let hesitation in a critical moment become a pattern—take the shot and move forward in obedience even when the outcome is uncertain, because inaction from fear guarantees the loss.
One-Line Action
Identify one God-directed step you've been avoiding out of fear, rehearse His promise of presence, and take that step this week.
Memorable Quote
When God says to do what your fear says you can't, we have to go with what God is saying.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi reframes Jacob not as a conniver but as someone consistently manipulated by those around him, from his mother Rebekah to his father-in-law Laban. Returning to Canaan at God's command, Jacob faces paralyzing fear of his brother Esau, who had vowed to kill him. In his distress, Jacob does the critical thing: he rehearses God's own promises back to Him, grounding himself in what God said rather than what circumstances suggest.

Alone at the Jabbok, Jacob wrestles with God and with his own fears, emerging with a dislocated hip, a new name (Israel), and a deeper confidence that God is truly with him. The dreaded encounter with Esau proves utterly different from what Jacob expected—Esau runs to embrace him with tears and kisses rather than violence. Rabbi draws from this the principle that our fear of the unknown freezes us because we project the past onto the future, illustrated by a personal wrestling story about hesitating on a fireman's carry and losing the match.

He extends the pattern through Mark 4 (disciples forgetting Yeshua is in the boat during the storm), Peter walking on water, and John 6 (Peter's declaration 'to whom would we go?'). Joshua 1, Proverbs 3, and 1 John 4 reinforce the repeated divine message: 'I am with you,' and perfect love drives out fear. The sermon concludes that when God calls us forward into something frightening, the key is not self-confidence but confidence in His presence, which will carry us through in ways we never expected.

Hook / Wordplay

Jacob names the wrestling site Peniel ('face of God'), then tells Esau 'seeing your face has been like seeing the face of God'—the feared enemy becomes the unexpected mirror of divine encounter.

Hanukkah: Overcoming All Terror & Tyranny

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Oppression often creeps in through gradual compromise (frog-in-water); stay vigilant about what you quietly accept as normal so you don't wake up locked into a defeated lifestyle.
  • Move from being a 'talker' to a 'doer' of the word—the Maccabees didn't deliberate endlessly, they stepped forward and trusted God to bring them through.
  • When despair tempts you to hide in a cave, remember that God's repeated promise is 'I am with you'—He never moved; we are the ones who drifted to the passenger door.
One-Line Action
Identify one area where you have gradually accepted defeat or compromise, and take one concrete step this week to reclaim it for God.
Memorable Quote
When we live in despair when God has provided abundance for us, has said he's with us to carry us through, we are choosing another counselor over God.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi connects the Hanukkah story to a universal message about overcoming terror and tyranny through trust in God. He traces how Israel fell into oppression under the Syrian-Greeks gradually, without realizing it, paralleling this with Egypt's enslavement and using the frog-in-water metaphor. The Maccabees—Matathias and Judah—rose from obscurity when they refused to sacrifice to foreign gods, sparking a rebellion that rekindled faith in despairing people hiding in caves.

He grounds the observance in John 10:22 where Yeshua celebrated Hanukkah and declared himself the Good Shepherd who lays down his life for his sheep. He draws a thematic chain of unlikely deliverers: Joseph sold by jealous brothers, Moses raised from the basket, the Maccabees from a Levitical family.

From Hebrews 12 he warns against bitterness taking root and distorting everything, and from Romans 8 he affirms that nothing can separate us from God's love. He closes with James on being doers of the word—the Maccabees did not just talk, they stepped forward—and challenges the congregation to bring God's light to those in darkness rather than hiding in caves of despair.

Hook / Wordplay

Every Jewish holiday summed up: They tried to kill us. God delivered us. Let's eat.

2023-12-17

Changes Ahead, But Not Always the Ones We Plan

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Recognize that mundane or frustrating seasons may be building skills for a purpose you cannot yet see, just as Joseph's prison years prepared him to govern Egypt.
  • When feeling stuck, look back at how far you have already come rather than fixating on how far the goal still appears, because the distance behind is often greater than the distance ahead.
  • Learn to discern whether an unexpected turn is a distraction or God redirecting your path, by staying yielded to Him rather than clinging rigidly to your own plan.
One-Line Action
When your plans shift unexpectedly, resist panic and ask what God may be building in you through the detour rather than demanding the original route.
Memorable Quote
God is not intimidated by the temper tantrums of human beings. He's not intimidated and he's not altering his course.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon explores Parashat Miketz through the lens of how life's changes rarely follow our plans. Joseph spent two forgotten years in prison before Pharaoh's dream suddenly catapulted him to second-in-command of Egypt, demonstrating how God's timetable can accelerate rapidly. The rabbi traces how Joseph's years as a slave and prisoner built administrative skills he never would have learned as his father's coddled favorite.

He examines the Yiddish proverb 'Man plans, God laughs,' showing it is not biblical but aligns with Proverbs 16 on God directing our steps. Solomon's story illustrates how even divinely granted wisdom can be squandered when focus shifts from God to accumulation. The rabbi shares a personal story of wanting to quit his wholesale job daily but waiting for God's release, only to discover later that the experience was essential preparation.

He draws on John 16 where Yeshua prepares confused disciples for changes they cannot yet comprehend, promising that grief will turn to joy. The sermon closes with news of vandalism at the Beth Zion barn, which the rabbi frames as a real-time illustration of the very message: changes come that we did not plan, but God remains in control.

Hook / Wordplay

'Man plans, God laughs' — a Yiddish proverb, not a Bible verse, but Proverbs 16:9 says the same: a person plans his path, but God directs his steps.

2023-12-24

Vayigash — When God Says Draw Near

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Drawing near to God is itself the defense against the adversary—no rebuking ritual needed; full spiritual occupancy leaves no vacancy for opposition (James 4:7-8).
  • Judah's transformation from the one who sold his brother into slavery to the one offering himself as a slave shows that personal suffering can reshape selfishness into sacrificial love—past sins do not define future capacity.
  • Don't live at the edge of 'what's the least I have to do to qualify'—occupy fully what God has called you to and bring others along into that nearness.
One-Line Action
Fill every space in your life with nearness to God so there is no vacancy left for anything else.
Memorable Quote
Now if you sent cards to the Messiah for his birthday, it's okay.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi teaches from the Torah portion Vayigash (Genesis 44:18), focusing on Judah's unexpected decision to offer himself as a slave in place of Benjamin before Joseph. He traces Judah's transformation from the brother who instigated selling Joseph into slavery to the one willing to lay down his freedom, arguing that Judah's personal losses—two sons dying in Genesis 38—gave him empathy for what his father would suffer losing another child. This substitutionary offer broke Joseph emotionally, causing him to reveal his identity and forgive his brothers.

The rabbi draws systematic Messianic parallels: Joseph rejected by brothers mirrors Yeshua rejected by his people; Judah offering himself foreshadows Messiah's substitutionary sacrifice; Joseph saying 'come near to me' echoes God's invitation in James 4:8. Addressing the Christmas season, he argues Yeshua was likely born during Sukkot and possibly conceived around Hanukkah, and that the real 'birth' that matters is when Messiah becomes real in our hearts.

He teaches from James 4 that drawing near to God leaves no vacancy for the adversary—spiritual resistance is not dramatic rebuking but yielding fully to God. He closes by urging believers not to settle for minimal faithfulness but to fully occupy their calling and draw others near to God's redemptive work.

Hook / Wordplay

'Vayigash' means 'he drew near'—Judah drew near to offer himself, which broke Joseph open to say 'come near to me,' mirroring God's own invitation: draw near and I will draw near to you.

2023-12-31

Vayechi: Who Are We… & What Do We Stand For

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Identity in God is not a static resume but a living impartation — like Jacob's blessing, I should consider what values and faith I am actively passing forward to those in my sphere of influence.
  • Judah's arc from selfish betrayer to substitutionary sacrifice shows that past failures do not define destiny; the slow progressive work of repentance and growth can radically transform who we become.
  • Paul counted his impressive credentials as loss compared to knowing Messiah — I should evaluate whether I am resting on past achievements or actively pressing toward God's high calling, since arriving is an illusion and pursuit is the posture.
One-Line Action
Identify one core value or conviction from your faith heritage and find a concrete way to impart it to the next generation this week.
Memorable Quote
He could tell you his resume, you could tell me your resume, but God looks at the heart to know who we really are.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon explores Genesis 48:16, where Jacob blesses Joseph's sons asking that they remember who he is and what he stands for, reframing the usual translation of 'let my name be named on them' as a call for generational impartation of identity and purpose. Rabbi highlights Judah's dramatic transformation from the brother who proposed selling Joseph into slavery to the one who offered himself as a substitute slave for Benjamin, showing that who we were need not define who we become. He connects this to the messianic prophecy of Genesis 49:10 about the scepter not departing from Judah.

Drawing from Philippians 3, he parallels Paul's journey of exchanging an impressive earthly pedigree for the supreme value of knowing Messiah, emphasizing that God looks at the heart rather than the resume. The sermon addresses a modern identity crisis, arguing that those who stand for nothing will fall for anything, with pointed commentary on rising anti-Semitism and cultural confusion. Using Psalm 8, Ephesians 6, and 1 John 3, he builds the case that believers' true identity is as God's children, equipped with spiritual armor to stand against deception.

He stresses that pressing toward the mark is an ongoing pursuit — arrival is never final, and humility before God is the posture that enables Him to lift us up. The sermon closes with the charge to carry the faith of spiritual ancestors forward, letting God expand His kingdom through transformed lives.

Hook / Wordplay

Jacob's blessing is usually translated 'let my name be named on them,' but the rabbi reframes it as 'may they remember who I am and what I stand for' — shifting the text from genealogical memory to generational impartation of identity, calling, and purpose.

Sh'mot: Messianic Makeover

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Don't let external forces or past failures redefine your identity — know what God has called you to be and refuse to let others write that script for you.
  • When God calls you to something beyond your capability, that's the point; Moses' 'I can't speak' was depreciating God's wisdom, not just expressing humility.
  • Watch for the slow slide of small compromises — Israel went from prosperous to enslaved not in one moment but through incremental concessions that reshaped their mindset.
One-Line Action
Let God define your identity rather than accepting the makeover your failures, culture, or others have imposed on you.
Memorable Quote
God doesn't call us because of the resume that we've acquired. He doesn't call us because we are so fantastic.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The Torah portion Sh'mot opens with the names of Israel's sons who came to Egypt, and the sermon uses this as a launchpad into how identities get reshaped — for better or worse. Rabbi traces how Israel slid from favor under Joseph into slavery under a new Pharaoh who didn't know Joseph, illustrating how small compromises accumulate into full bondage (the frog-in-the-pan analogy). Moses attempted his own makeover by killing an Egyptian guard, failed, and spent 40 years in the desert with a shattered self-image.

At the burning bush, Moses' refusal to go wasn't mere humility but a depreciation of God's wisdom in choosing him, as F.B. Meyer noted. God did the real makeover — commissioning Moses beyond his capability and even announcing the death of Pharaoh's firstborn before the plagues began.

The sermon then addresses false makeovers of Yeshua himself — the blonde-haired Renaissance image born from confusing Nazarite with Nazareth, and modern attempts to recast him as a Palestinian militant — arguing for restoring his Jewish identity in proper Torah context. Drawing on 2 Corinthians 5:17, the rabbi contrasts superficial self-reinvention with God's drastic spiritual surgery that transforms from the inside out. The message closes with the Holy Spirit as our ongoing 'makeup person' who convicts and guides us into the transformed image God intends.

Hook / Wordplay

Yeshua doesn't need a makeover — He already had a false one imposed on Him. We're the ones who need one, and only God can do it from the inside out.

2024-01-14

V'era: When God Appears, Everything Changes

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When feeling unqualified for something God is placing before me, recognize that self-doubt is actually doubting God's ability to equip—shift focus from my own resume to His resources.
  • Like Peter at Shavuot, boldness comes not from preparation but from yielding to the Spirit; stop waiting to feel ready and step into what has been placed before me.
  • Discouragement and harsh circumstances can deafen us to God's promises the way Israel's cruel slavery made them unable to hear Moses—actively choose trust over temporary pain by remembering God's track record of deliverance.
One-Line Action
Stop waiting to feel qualified and step into the calling God has already equipped you for.
Memorable Quote
We're not downplaying ourselves as much as we are downplaying God's ability to be everything necessary to complete our need.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon opens with Torah portion Va'era (Exodus 6:2), exploring how God's appearance to Moses came at a moment of deep discouragement after Pharaoh's harsh rejection and Israel's blame. Rabbi draws a parallel between Moses' reluctance and Martin Luther King Jr.'s midnight kitchen-table crisis in 1956, where God told him to stand for justice without fear. He broadens the reluctant-hero theme to Rosa Parks, showing God raises ordinary people in moments of weariness and doubt.

Moving to Luke 24, he traces how the Emmaus disciples failed to recognize the risen Yeshua until the breaking of matzah opened their eyes, transforming despair into overflowing joy. He connects this to 1 John 3:2, arguing that God's appearance causes us to appear as who we truly are meant to be. The sermon culminates in Matthew 28's Great Commission, where Yeshua's declaration of all authority empowers hesitant disciples to go make other disciples.

Peter's transformation at Shavuot is offered as the ultimate example: an unprepared man speaking with an authority not his own. The central message is that when we feel unqualified we are actually downplaying God's ability to equip us, and that yielding to Him changes both us and everything around us.

Hook / Wordplay

Va'era means 'I appeared'—and when God appears, we appear too. Moses appeared as a leader, Peter appeared as a vessel, MLK appeared as a voice for justice.

2024-01-21

Mastering the Art of Negotiation

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When in conflict, resist the urge to force your position; instead ask questions that help the other person discover the truth for themselves, as Yeshua did with the woman at the well and in John 10.
  • God's version of 'reasoning together' is not watering down truth into compromise — it is lovingly confronting reality so restoration can happen, like Isaiah 1:18's scarlet-to-snow promise.
  • Even when negotiation fails and people respond with hostility (stones, covered ears), stay faithful to the truth like Stephen did — the impact may land in someone's heart long after the moment passes, as it did with Paul.
One-Line Action
In your next difficult conversation, ask questions that help the other person discover truth rather than insisting on your own position.
Memorable Quote
Mastering the art of negotiation is allowing the master to teach us the art of negotiating, of how our broken hearts can be mended, how our soiled hands can be cleansed, how our hearts can be purified and that we can experience the wonder of God's power being made manifest in us as earthen vessels with the power of God being revealed, how exciting it is when God does these things.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The rabbi uses Parshat Bo — the final three plagues and Israel's exodus from Egypt — to explore what it means to master the art of negotiation. He traces the escalating back-and-forth between Moses and Pharaoh, highlighting the humor in Pharaoh's incremental concessions: first only men can go, then children can come but flocks must stay behind as collateral. Even Pharaoh's own servants broke ranks, urging him to let Israel go before Egypt was fully destroyed.

The sermon connects this pattern to Yeshua's dialogue with hostile Judeans in John 10, where He calmly reasoned from Scripture rather than losing composure when they picked up stones. Additional examples include Ananias and Sapphira's failed deception, the woman at the well's gradual awakening to truth, and Stephen's final testimony before being stoned. Drawing on Isaiah 1's invitation — 'come, let us reason together' — the rabbi argues that God's negotiation style is not mutual compromise but a call to confront truth and receive restoration.

The central thesis is that mastering negotiation means letting the Master direct the conversation rather than relying on our own emotions or insisting on our own plans. He closes with Matthew 11:28-30, framing Yeshua's offer of rest as the ultimate invitation for burdened souls to come to terms with truth.

Hook / Wordplay

"Mastering the art of negotiation" plays on master as skill-level versus Master as God — the real mastery is letting the Master be in charge of the negotiation.

2024-01-28

B'Shallach: Why Is It So Hard To Let It Go

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When I catch myself worrying, adopt the bishop's practice: write the worry down and revisit it in a month—most fears never materialize and the exercise exposes how much energy I waste on things I cannot control.
  • Identify what I am gripping so tightly that it is bottlenecking other areas of life; the thing I think I cannot release may be exactly what God is asking me to surrender so He can open something greater.
  • Reframe weakness as Paul did—not as victimhood but as the precise condition where God's power shows up most fully; stop trying to eliminate every struggle and instead lean into dependence on God through it.
One-Line Action
Name one thing you are holding onto out of fear or pride this week, consciously release it to God, and watch whether the feared outcome even materializes.
Memorable Quote
In reality, those things that we often hold on to so tenaciously that we cannot let go are holding back an outpouring of what God wants to do in our life.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The Torah portion B'Shallach opens with Pharaoh releasing Israel, then pursuing them to the sea—unable to let go of his pride and control, which led to total destruction of his army. Israel, despite witnessing God's mighty acts in Egypt, immediately reverted to a slave mentality at the sight of the Egyptian army, preferring bondage over the unknown. Rabbi draws a parallel to the rich young ruler in Matthew 19 who walked away sad because he could not release his wealth to follow Yeshua—illustrating that what we grip most tightly often bottlenecks God's outpouring.

He shares the story of a bishop who began writing down his worries and discovered a month later that none of them had actually come to pass, showing how worry disengages us from productive life. Using the image of releasing a balloon—you surrender control to the wind—he frames letting go as yielding to God's unlimited resources rather than clinging to our limited ones.

Paul's thorn in the flesh from 2 Corinthians 12 becomes the capstone example: Paul shifted from begging for removal to boasting in weakness so Messiah's power could rest on him. The sermon closes with Galatians 5:1 and Ephesians 3:20, urging the congregation that Messiah freed us for freedom and that God can do far beyond anything we ask or imagine when we finally let go.

Hook / Wordplay

'Let it go' operates on three levels: Pharaoh physically letting Israel go, Israel spiritually letting go of their slave identity, and us releasing the fears and attachments that bottleneck God's work in our lives.

2024-02-04

Yitro: How to Arrive on Time at God's Destination

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Respond to God's call like Isaiah — say 'here am I, send me' before demanding to know all the details; willingness precedes understanding of the assignment.
  • Don't be the five virgins who had everything ready except oil — spiritual procrastination in one critical area can close doors that were meant for you.
  • When life's delays and detours frustrate my personal timeline, look for what God may be working in the disruption rather than resenting the schedule change.
One-Line Action
Yield your personal schedule and agenda to God's appointed times, and respond to His call before you feel fully ready.
Memorable Quote
He had an attitude to want to respond to God before he knew what he was responding to.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Drawing from Exodus 18:23, where Jethro advises Moses that following God's direction will help all the people 'arrive at their destination peacefully,' the rabbi builds a sermon around surrendering our personal timelines to God's appointed schedule. He stresses that life's difficulties are never our final destination but waypoints on God's transformative journey. Through Isaiah 6, he highlights Isaiah's willingness to volunteer for God's mission before knowing what it entailed, responding to the manifest presence with 'Here am I, send me.' The parable of the ten virgins (Matthew 25) illustrates the danger of spiritual procrastination: they were prepared in every way except for lacking oil, having miscalculated the bridegroom's timing.

The parable of the talents warns against burying what God gives us out of fear, showing how a wrong attitude toward the Master leads to loss. From Galatians 4, Paul teaches that God sent His Son at 'the appointed time,' urging believers not to return to slavery by following their own schedules over God's. Hebrews 12 calls us to run looking to Yeshua, who endured the cross for the joy set before Him.

The rabbi weaves in a personal story about his daughter's flight delay that turned into unexpected blessings — a $500 voucher and a stranger's donation to her dog rescue — illustrating how God works even in disruptions. He closes with Jeremiah 12:5's challenge and the Ahimaaz story, urging believers to run their own God-appointed race rather than comparing themselves to others.

Hook / Wordplay

Life's GPS says 'you have arrived' — but difficulties along the way are never the final stop. God's ETA runs on appointed times, not your calendar app.

2024-02-11

Mishpatim: When Good Intentions Are Not Enough

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Audit where my commitments to God are actually conditional good intentions — especially where obedience would cost me something tangible (my 'bottom line').
  • God's covenant with me is firmer than my grip on it; when I fail, the path back is repentance and recommitment, not self-flagellation — 'when you have been converted, feed my sheep.'
  • True spiritual confidence is not self-confidence; it comes from God's investment in me, like Peter at the temple gate saying 'what I have, I give to you' after all his failures.
One-Line Action
Identify one area where you have been treating a covenant commitment as a conditional good intention and recommit to it this week regardless of cost.
Memorable Quote
When it comes to God, practical is obedience.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi opens the Mishpatim portion by framing God's rulings not as legalism but as the fabric of healthy community — covering servitude as a safety-net system, limits on retaliation, treatment of foreigners, and the prohibition of false rumors. He then introduces his central thesis: Israel declared 'everything Hashem says we will do,' but their resolution lasted only about forty days before the golden calf. He parallels this with Jeremiah 34, where the people freed their Hebrew slaves per covenant, then reversed course when it threatened their bottom line — and lost everything in captivity.

Turning to the New Covenant, he traces the same pattern through Yeshua's disciples: in John 6 crowds abandon Yeshua over hard sayings, in Matthew 20 Zebedee's sons ask for thrones moments after Yeshua describes His coming death, and in Luke 22 the Twelve argue over greatness at the Last Supper. Peter exemplifies the arc most vividly — confessing Yeshua as Mashiach, then rebuking Him, then denying Him three times. Yet Yeshua's response is not dismissal but recommissioning: 'When you have been converted, feed my sheep.' The sermon closes by arguing that true transformation comes not from self-confidence or good intentions but from submitting to God's covenant, which remains firm even when we falter.

Hook / Wordplay

You didn't break a resolution — you broke a good intention. A resolution means you adhere until it's accomplished; if you quit, it was never a resolution at all.

Obedience and Holiness

2024-02-18

T'rumah: Stayin' in the Loop

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Being 'in the loop' with God is not about having insider knowledge of the future but about maintaining wholehearted relational union with Him — even when circumstances are unclear, presence matters more than information.
  • When I feel spiritually 'out of the loop,' the remedy is entering God's presence (like the Psalmist entering the temple), not seeking more data — perspective comes from proximity to God, not from analysis.
  • God invites dialogue, not silent compliance — like Abraham negotiating for Sodom or the disciples asking questions, I can bring my concerns and confusion directly to God as a sign of relationship, not doubt.
One-Line Action
This week, identify one area where you feel spiritually disconnected or confused, and instead of trying to figure it out, bring it into God's presence through honest prayer and worship.
Memorable Quote
When we experience his presence we become an extension of who he is. He allows himself to be made manifest to us and through us to the people around us.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi opens Parashat T'rumah by noting that Moses ascended Sinai to receive detailed instructions for building the tabernacle while the people below had no idea what God was revealing — Moses was 'in the loop' and they were not. He draws a creative wordplay from Exodus 26's literal loops (lulot) that join the tabernacle curtains into a single unit, reading them as a picture of how God joins His people to Himself and to each other. He argues that being 'in the loop' does not mean knowing every future detail but rather staying united with God in relationship and presence, citing Psalm 91's 'secret place' and the Psalmist who only understood the fate of the wicked after entering God's house.

Moving to John 15, he equates 'abide in me' with staying in the loop — a place of dialogue, not robotic obedience, where God invites questions and even negotiation as He did with Abraham over Sodom. He traces the pattern through the disciples who oscillated between being in and out of the loop, culminating in Peter's denial and restoration, and the Shavuot outpouring when believers gathered in one accord.

From 2 Corinthians 5 and Colossians 2, he frames the new creation and reconciliation ministry as the fruit of being in union with Messiah. The sermon closes with an exhortation not to let empty philosophy or circumstances pull us out of the loop but to remain rooted in Messiah, serving as ambassadors of reconciliation.

Hook / Wordplay

The physical 'loops' (lulot) of blue thread in Exodus 26 that fasten the tabernacle curtains into one unit become a wordplay for 'staying in the loop' — remaining united with God and aware of His purposes.

Tetzaveh: Are You One of God's Fashion Statements?

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Focus on being 'clothed with Messiah' (Galatians 3:27) rather than external presentation — let inner character and a gentle spirit be what others perceive, since that imperishable quality is what God values most.
  • Like the priestly shoulder stones carrying Israel's names, take seriously the weight of interceding for your community — the calling is not for personal elevation but to carry others' burdens before God.
  • Adopt the Philippians 4:17 posture in relationships: seek to 'increase the credit balance of others' accounts' by ministering to their needs rather than calculating what you can extract from them.
One-Line Action
This week, examine whether your daily conduct reflects a 'God statement' — clothed with His righteousness and presence — rather than a self-focused fashion statement.
Memorable Quote
He saw not me or my clothing, but he saw how God had clothed me with his presence.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon explores Torah portion Tetzaveh and the detailed priestly garments God prescribed for Aaron and his sons, contrasting modern red carpet fashion culture—where clothing serves self-presentation—with the biblical purpose of priestly vestments: setting the priests apart to represent God's holiness before the people. The shoulder stones bearing the names of Israel symbolized the priests carrying the people's burdens before God, and the Urim and Thummim over Aaron's heart represented decision-making in God's presence. Drawing from Proverbs 31, the rabbi highlights being clothed with strength and dignity as an enduring quality that surpasses physical adornment.

Through Matthew 6 he emphasizes not worrying about physical clothing but seeking God's kingdom first, and from Galatians 3 he teaches that believers have clothed themselves with Messiah, creating unity across all distinctions. Ephesians 6 presents the armor of God as clothing for spiritual warfare, while 1 Peter 3 elevates the imperishable quality of a gentle and quiet spirit over external beauty. The rabbi coins the phrase 'Ruach originals' to reframe the Spirit of God as the ultimate designer who clothes believers for their calling.

He shares a personal story of his brother at Hofstra University sensing God's presence on him—not through appearance but through spiritual clothing—which became a turning point in his brother's journey to faith. The central message is that believers are living extensions of the Tabernacle, called to be 'God statements' rather than fashion statements, clothed with righteousness, humility, and divine presence so others can experience the God who desires to dwell among His people.

Hook / Wordplay

'Are you one of God's fashion statements?' — contrasting red carpet designer labels (Chanel, Versace) with 'Ruach originals,' reframing the priestly wardrobe as God's runway where clothing represents holiness rather than self-display.

Ki Tissa: Singled Out & Going for the Gold

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When God seems silent or slow, resist building your own 'golden calf' — substitutes for His presence born from impatience only produce fool's gold.
  • Being singled out means God has appointed you for specific honorable use; yield to His refining fire rather than resisting the heat, because the purification produces something of eternal value.
  • The Laodicean trap of self-sufficiency ('I'm rich, I have need of nothing') blinds us to spiritual poverty — stay humble, dependent on God, and willing to buy His gold rather than minting your own.
One-Line Action
Identify one area where you have built your own golden calf substitute and surrender it to God's refining fire this week.
Memorable Quote
Instead of setting God apart from us, we set ourselves apart to him. And we pressed toward the mark for the prize of the high calling of God.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon explores Parashat Ki Tissa by contrasting two uses of gold: the dishonorable golden calf fashioned by Israel's own hands out of fear, and the honorable gold crafted by God-appointed artisans like Bezalel, who was singled out and filled with the Spirit for sacred work. Rabbi notes that while Moses received revelation for 40 days, Joshua waited faithfully partway up the mountain, and the people below replaced God with an idol born of impatience. Using 2 Timothy 2, he distinguishes vessels of honor from dishonor, emphasizing that what matters is being set apart by the Master, not the material itself.

He connects this to 2 Corinthians 3-4, where believers carry God's treasure in clay jars, and to 1 Peter 1, where faith tested by fire is more valuable than perishable gold. Drawing from the Laodicean church in Revelation 3, he warns against spiritual self-sufficiency and reframes Messiah's words as advice rather than condemnation: buy from me gold refined by fire.

The sermon weaves Paul's athletic metaphor from 1 Corinthians 9 and 2 Timothy 4, urging believers to run the race to win, pressing toward the high calling. He concludes that each person has been singled out by God for an honorable purpose and must yield to His refining process rather than building substitutes of their own.

Hook / Wordplay

The double meaning of gold — Israel's self-made golden calf versus God's gold refined by fire — and 'singled out' as both census-counting and divine appointment for purpose.

Vayakhel: When Is Too Much Too Much?

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Generosity is not primarily about money—it encompasses how I pour into children, coworkers, family, and even adversaries; recognizing God's investment in me should make giving natural rather than forced.
  • The 'good eye' and 'evil eye' framework applies beyond finances: stinginess with forgiveness, grace, or time creates inner darkness that blocks my ability to see clearly and serve others effectively.
  • I must deal with my own 'logs' through honest self-examination before presuming to correct others; genuine ministry requires humility and clear spiritual sight, not just having the right answers.
One-Line Action
Identify one area where I am spiritually hoarding—whether forgiveness, time, or grace—and deliberately pour into someone this week without expecting return.
Memorable Quote
And yet he says no I want to bestow even more but he also wants us to understand that what we have we can give away the biggest gift that God has given us his love and we say this to it's the one thing that God has given us that we can give all of it away and never run out because God is continually pouring out his love for us continually pouring out and investing in us.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon explores Parashat Vayakhel, where Moses assembles the community and the people give so generously for the tabernacle that they must be told to stop—the rare case of 'too much.' The rabbi contrasts this willing generosity with the golden calf episode, noting the same people gave gold both times but with radically different hearts. He connects this to Paul's 'cheerful giver' teaching in 2 Corinthians 9, warning through the Ananias and Sapphira story that giving for image rather than from genuine heart corrupts the community. Drawing on 2 Peter 1, he argues that God's 'superlatively great promises' give us everything needed for life and godliness, yet complacency can cause us to forget our forgiveness and slip into spiritual blindness.

The Matthew 6 teaching on the 'good eye' versus 'evil eye' reframes generosity and stinginess as matters of inner light and darkness, not just finances. He threads this into Luke 6's log-and-splinter teaching: clear spiritual sight—gained through personal humility—is required before effectively helping others. The sermon concludes that God's love is the one gift we can give entirely away and never exhaust, and we should never say 'enough' to what God wants to pour into and through us.

Hook / Wordplay

The Israelites gave so much for the tabernacle they were told 'too much'—but when it comes to God's love poured into us, there is no 'too much,' only an unlimited resource to keep giving away.

P'kudei: Exactly What God Ordered

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When spiritual progress feels stalled, remember the critical mass principle: God may be assembling every piece according to His pattern, and the outpouring comes at His moment of completion, not on my timeline.
  • Focus on faithfully executing God's known pattern—Word, prayer, fellowship, outreach—rather than trying to manufacture spiritual breakthroughs through sheer effort or willpower.
  • The Tabernacle-to-Temple-to-believer progression shows God's dwelling place was always meant to be His people; take seriously the weight of being a living vessel for His Shekinah presence.
One-Line Action
Identify one area where I have been trying to force spiritual results on my own schedule and consciously yield it back to God's pattern and timing.
Memorable Quote
Not necessarily the way we think it's supposed to be, but at the moment that God sees everything line up properly, the outpouring of God's Spirit commerce.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon covers Parashat Pekudei, the final portion of Exodus, which details the accounting of all materials and labor that went into constructing the Tabernacle. Rabbi emphasizes that every detail—every socket, hinge, and fabric—was completed exactly as God had ordered Moses, and when the last element was in place, God's Shekinah filled the Tabernacle so powerfully that Moses could not enter. He draws a parallel to Solomon's Temple in 1 Kings, where the same outpouring occurred upon completion, and Solomon marveled that the heavens of heavens cannot contain God, let alone a house built by human hands.

The sermon then connects to Acts 2 and Shavuot, arguing that the Spirit's outpouring happened at the Temple itself—not an upper room—fulfilling Daniel's prophecy of anointing the most holy place after Messiah's death. He traces this pattern through Revelation, where God's Shekinah ultimately dwells permanently with His people, and New Jerusalem is described not as a physical city but as the bride of Messiah in architectural terms—God's true dwelling place is His people. The rabbi introduces the concept of spiritual critical mass: when all preparations align with God's pattern, His power flows, just as a power plant produces nothing until every element hits its mark.

He challenges the congregation with two questions—are you exactly what God ordered, and are you doing what you were ordered to do? He briefly addresses contemporary issues including political language manipulation and rising anti-Semitism, connecting them to 1 Corinthians 2's teaching that worldly leaders do not understand God's hidden wisdom. The sermon closes with a call to yield to God's revealed pattern—Word, prayer, fellowship, and outreach—trusting that God will bring about the outpouring in His timing.

Hook / Wordplay

"Just what the doctor ordered" becomes "exactly what the Great Physician ordered"—shifting from medical prescriptions to God's divine blueprint for the Tabernacle and for our lives.

2024-03-24

God's Purim Response to Anti-Semitism

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Look for divine appointments daily—like Mordechai sitting at the gate or Peter locking eyes with the beggar, position yourself to notice and act on encounters God has orchestrated rather than looking away.
  • When hatred or anti-Semitism surfaces, resist responding purely as a political movement; stand in union with God and let the Spirit work through you, because the battle is ultimately spiritual not partisan.
  • Shift default attention from cataloging how bad things are to identifying where grace is abounding—focus energy on what God is doing rather than being consumed by the sin that is abounding.
One-Line Action
Ask God each morning to reveal the divine appointments He has set for you, and be ready to act when He locks your eyes with someone in need.
Memorable Quote
But he also blots out those elements that are looking to bring destruction.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon connects the Torah portion Vayikra with the Purim story, tracing a thread from Amalek's attack on Israel through Haman's genocidal plot to modern anti-Semitism. Rabbi traces how King Saul's failure to execute the Amalekite king eventually produced Haman, and walks through the Esther narrative highlighting divine timing—Mordechai's unrewarded loyalty, Esther's strategic placement, and the king's sleepless night—as evidence of God orchestrating deliverance behind the scenes. He draws a striking parallel between Nazi-era propaganda (puppet master cartoons, blood libels, octopus imagery) and nearly identical modern anti-Semitic tropes that resurfaced after October 7th, showing the same spirit recycling across centuries.

He emphasizes that anti-Semitism is ultimately a spiritual attack on God's credibility: if the adversary can destroy God's chosen people, he undermines God's promises to everyone. Through Romans 7-8, the rabbi connects Haman's old-nature-driven hatred to the universal struggle between flesh and Spirit, and from Hebrews 11-12 frames the faithful response as endurance looking to Yeshua.

He notes that even after the king's counter-decree allowed Jewish self-defense, many still attacked—illustrating how irrational hatred persists despite clear evidence it will fail. The sermon closes by reframing Romans 5: rather than cataloging how sin abounds, focus on the grace that much more abounds, and stand in the gap to help others experience God's deliverance as Esther did for such a time as this.

Hook / Wordplay

God 'blots out' in two directions—He blots out the memory of Amalek (Exodus 17:14) and blots out our sins for His own sake (Isaiah 43:25). Same God, same power, applied to both enemy and beloved.

2024-03-31

The Transformative Power of Sacrifice

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Look at others with 'eyes of the heart'—see God's investment in them and ask what piece of yourself you can give to fill their gap, rather than comparing yourself favorably.
  • When going through a difficult season, step into ministry to someone else's need; the act of self-giving often lifts your own burden in ways you don't expect.
  • Practice 'looking away'—when circumstances pull you toward rumination and despondency, deliberately redirect focus to the joy set before you in Messiah.
One-Line Action
This week, identify one person who seems diminished or discouraged and offer a specific, genuine encouragement that names the gift you see God has placed in them.
Memorable Quote
It's the one thing, one commodity. You can give it all away and never run out because he is continually pouring into us more and more.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi teaches from Parashat Tzav, which outlines the sacrificial offerings—burnt, grain, sin, guilt, and peace—that Aaron and his sons were commanded to perform. He connects the sacrificial system to Jeremiah's message that God desired obedience and intimacy with His people more than ritual sacrifice alone. Using the image of two figures exchanging a puzzle piece, he illustrates how we are called to give of ourselves to complete what is lacking in others, echoing John the Baptist's declaration that he must decrease while Messiah increases.

Drawing from Romans 12, he frames believers as living sacrifices transformed by the renewing of the mind rather than conformed to the world. From Hebrews 12, he highlights that Yeshua endured the execution stake not by focusing on suffering but on the joy set before Him, and urges believers to look away from circumstances and toward Messiah. He unpacks Philippians 2 on regarding others as better than ourselves, arguing that we cannot see the full potential God has placed in another person.

The sermon's central thesis is that sacrifice is not merely powerful but transformative—it changes both the giver and the receiver. He closes by urging the congregation to give love freely, since it is the one commodity you can give away entirely and never exhaust.

Hook / Wordplay

Tzav means 'given order'—God ordered sacrifices, but more importantly He ordered His people to pay attention and obey, so that sacrifice would transform rather than become empty ritual.

2024-04-07

Sh'mini: So That God's Glory Will Appear!

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Modern 'intoxications' (phone scrolling, news outrage, herd mentality) impair spiritual judgment the same way wine impaired Nadav and Avihu — identify and limit the inputs that cloud my focus on what God actually commands.
  • Aaron's refusal to eat the offering because his heart wasn't right shows that pausing worship when my attitude is compromised is more pleasing to God than hollow performance — don't just go through motions.
  • Resist the pull to decode every earthquake or eclipse as prophetic sign; the 'last days' have been ongoing since Peter's time, and fixating on theories distracts from the clear call to obey and love.
One-Line Action
Audit my daily 'intoxicants' — news, phone, outrage loops — and cut what clouds sober-minded obedience so God's glory has room to appear.
Memorable Quote
From Devarim due to Rana Mi 6, the cornerstone of our faith, the Shema.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon opens with Leviticus 9:6 where Moses tells the people to do what God commanded so that the glory of the Lord will appear. When they obeyed and performed the prescribed offerings, God's glory manifested visibly — fire consumed the offerings and the people fell on their faces. Immediately after, Nadav and Avihu offered unauthorized fire and were themselves consumed, and the rabbi notes the subsequent prohibition on intoxicating drink suggests they may have been impaired.

He extends the concept of intoxication to modern distractions — phones, news cycles, herd mentality — arguing these cloud spiritual judgment just as alcohol did. Aaron's refusal to eat the sin offering after his sons' deaths is presented as a model of right attitude: better to pause than perform worship with a compromised heart, and Moses accepted this. The rabbi then contrasts two kinds of glory through New Covenant texts: the glory of man, like grass that withers, versus the glory of God revealed through obedient suffering (Romans 8), self-emptying union with Messiah (Philippians 2), and resurrection hope (1 Corinthians 15).

He cautions against fixating on end-times speculation or earthquake theories, insisting the call is to obey what God has clearly commanded. The sermon closes with the encouragement that we are sown in weakness but raised in strength, and that staying steadfast and immovable in union with Messiah is how the glory of God appears in our lives.

Hook / Wordplay

"Don't drink and contrive" — a play on "don't drink and drive," connecting Nadav and Avihu's likely intoxication to their unauthorized improvisation before God.

2024-04-14

Tazria: Clear Skin & A Clean Heart

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When I sense spiritual uncleanness causing me to withdraw or isolate from community, recognize that as the Torah's 'living outside the camp' pattern and bring it to God rather than hiding.
  • Stop treating sin and inner struggles with surface-level cover-ups (busyness, image management); follow David's Psalm 51 model of honest confession that addresses root causes.
  • Practice esteeming others above self-image concerns — the shift from self-focused insecurity to sacrificial love is what opens the door to genuine spiritual transformation.
One-Line Action
Identify one area where you are managing appearances instead of pursuing heart-level cleansing, and bring it honestly before God this week.
Memorable Quote
If we present ourselves as a sacrifice to others, if we esteem others better than ourselves, we start to see a move of God's spirit that transforms us like nothing else can.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi opens the Tazria portion (Leviticus 12–13) on tzara'at (leprosy) and reframes it through the lens of modern skin-condition commercials, noting how people obsess over outward blemishes while ignoring deeper spiritual ailments. He observes that perceived physical flaws create self-worth problems and isolation, paralleling the Torah's command that the unclean person must live outside the camp. He connects this to Yeshua's willingness to touch and heal lepers and the woman with the issue of blood, demonstrating that Messiah breaks through the barrier of uncleanness.

The Naaman story from 2 Kings 5 illustrates how pride and self-importance can be a disease worse than leprosy, while Gehazi's greed transferred the very condition Naaman was cured of. Rabbi draws the Jewish interpretive link between tzara'at and lashon hara (evil speech), citing Miriam's leprosy after speaking against Moses. Psalm 51 anchors the sermon's core message: David's cry for a clean heart after his sin with Bathsheba models genuine repentance that addresses root causes rather than symptoms.

The Dorian Gray illustration dramatizes the danger of preserving outward beauty while inner corruption festers unseen. Rabbi contrasts pharmaceutical approaches that merely cover symptoms with God's offer of total cleansing through confession and the blood of Messiah, citing Isaiah 1:18 and 1 John 1:7-9. He closes by urging the congregation to seek the inner transformation that enables them to help set others free.

Hook / Wordplay

Pharmaceutical commercials promise 'clear skin can be yours' but list side effects including death — God offers cleansing that goes deeper than skin, with no disclaimers needed.

2024-04-21

Metzora 042024 & message from 040922

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Sin works like leaven—small compromises expand until they corrupt your entire capacity to reason clearly; address them before they distort your thinking into believing the distortion is normal.
  • When God exposes something painful in your life, receive it as refining fire, not rejection—His purpose is to purge the dross so you come forth as purified gold capable of fulfilling your calling.
  • Forgiveness is not letting the other person off the hook—it is letting yourself off the hook, freeing you from being controlled by emotions tied to people who are no longer even in your life.
One-Line Action
This week before Passover, search your heart for one compromise you have been dismissing as small, bring it honestly before God, and ask Him to purge it like a refiner purifies silver.
Memorable Quote
From Devariim to the round of 6th, the cornerstone of our faith, the Shema.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon connects the Torah portion Metzora—about purification from tzara'at—with Shabbat HaGadol, the great Sabbath before Passover. The rabbi explains that the physical removal of leaven from homes parallels the spiritual removal of sin, which like yeast starts small but expands to corrupt everything. He links the Leviticus 14 purification rituals to Malachi's prophecy of the Lord suddenly coming to his temple to purify the sons of Levi like a refiner's fire.

He then shows how Yeshua fulfilled this by cleansing the temple in Matthew 21, and the leaders understood his authority because they had just read the Malachi passage on that very Shabbat. The parable of the wicked tenants illustrates how sin corrupts reasoning until people believe their distorted thinking is viable. The rabbi clarifies that 'Pharisee' does not mean hypocrite—Yeshua addressed specifically hypocritical Pharisees, not all of them.

He emphasizes that God exposes sin not to condemn but to restore, purging us like gold so we can fulfill our calling. The sermon closes with Yeshua weeping over Jerusalem—not from self-pity but from compassion for those who could not see that his visitation was the doorway to redemption.

Hook / Wordplay

Removing chametz with light: the metzora's journey from darkness to light mirrors how God illuminates hidden sin in our lives so we can be purified—cleaning the inside of the cup, not just the outside.

2024-04-29

Jewish Resurrection and the Meal of Messiah

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Resurrection is threaded throughout the entire Passover season (dry bones Haftarah, Lazarus narrative, Omer counting, Mashiach's Se'udah)—the Jewish liturgical calendar itself teaches the theology of new life if you pay attention to the readings.
  • God delivers us not for our own comfort but so we can comfort others with the same comfort we received—like the oxygen mask analogy, experience His power first so you can minister from that capacity.
  • Don't hoard the seed of faith God has planted; let Him expand it, because a seed that stays hidden produces nothing, but one that is planted yields a harvest far beyond the original.
One-Line Action
Identify one area of your life that feels like dry bones and actively invite God's resurrection breath into it this week.
Memorable Quote
Sometimes people think that resurrection is just something invented by Christians. But the fact is that resurrection was an unusual concept that was a Jewish concept.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The rabbi teaches that the Shabbat after Passover is specifically focused on resurrection, centered on the Ezekiel 37 dry bones vision where God breathes life into a hopeless situation. He frames Israel's deliverance from Egypt as itself a resurrection—a slave people reborn as a nation. He connects the counting of the Omer from Leviticus 23 to the anticipation of harvest, drawing a parallel to Yeshua's teaching in John 12 that a grain of wheat must die to produce abundance.

The raising of Lazarus in John 11, occurring just before Passover, further establishes resurrection as the season's dominant theme. He introduces the Chasidic tradition of Mashiach's Se'udah (Meal of Messiah) on the last day of unleavened bread, which reads from Isaiah 11-12 about the messianic branch from Jesse, showing that Messiah is already central to Jewish Passover observance. He argues that resurrection is fundamentally a Jewish concept carried into the wider world through the messianic community, not a Christian invention.

The seed metaphor is developed throughout: just as a seed contains a forest's potential, God's resurrection power in us is meant to expand and produce a harvest far beyond what we can imagine. He closes urging the congregation to plant the seed of faith rather than hoarding it, trusting God to produce abundance beyond what we can see in advance.

Hook / Wordplay

Inside every seed is the possibility of something much grander—Passover's resurrection thread runs from dry bones to Lazarus to the Meal of Messiah, proving resurrection is Jewish to its core.
