2024-05-05

Seasons & Cycles of Return

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Maintain daily spiritual readiness like the wise virgins rather than assuming provision will always be available later—complacency is its own cycle that closes doors.
  • Recognize recurring negative patterns in my own life (distraction, compromise, spiritual drift) as cycles that can be broken through vigilance and yielding to God, not willpower alone.
  • Forgiveness releases the forgiver as much as the forgiven—holding onto bitterness is a self-binding cycle, as illustrated by the Holocaust survivor who found freedom only when she chose to forgive her former guard.
One-Line Action
Identify one recurring negative cycle in your life this week—distraction, drift, unforgiveness—and take one concrete step to break it by yielding that area to God.
Memorable Quote
From Devariim to the Rani Mi 6, the cornerstone of our faith.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi teaches on the Torah portion Acharei Mot (After the Death), connecting it to Yom HaShoah week by identifying recurring cycles in both scripture and history. He traces Leviticus 16-18 from the Azazel scapegoat atonement ritual through warnings against sexual immorality and child sacrifice to Molech, framing these as ancient patterns that repeat in every generation. He draws explicit parallels between the Holocaust era and current global anti-Semitism, noting that survivors are watching the same groundwork being laid again and insisting 'never again' must mean action, not just words.

Through Romans 3 he shows how Messiah's sacrifice breaks the sin-enslavement cycle, while John 12 illustrates how spiritual blindness and love of human approval create their own recurring trap. Using Matthew 24-25 and the parable of the ten virgins, he warns against being lulled into complacency by everyday distractions.

He contrasts two Holocaust survivor responses—one consumed by hatred, another freed through forgiving her former guard—to show that forgiveness breaks the bitterness cycle. He closes with the image of seeds dying to produce an expanding harvest, urging the congregation to yield to God's redemptive cycle so that after every death comes restoration.

Hook / Wordplay

"Seasons and Cycles of Return" layers natural seasons, the Hebrew concept of teshuvah (return/repentance), and the historical repetition of both destruction and redemption into one unifying frame.

2024-05-12

Kedoshim: God's Promise to Restore

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Reframe 'loving your neighbor' beyond passive acceptance — it includes speaking truth frankly when genuine relationship and care are present, following Nathan's model with David rather than avoiding hard conversations out of false kindness.
  • When facing overwhelming circumstances, adopt Paul's perspective shift from 2 Corinthians 4: focus on unseen eternal realities rather than visible temporary afflictions, recognizing that God's restoration pattern (physical first, then spiritual) is at work even when progress seems incomplete.
  • Regularly audit what competes for first place in your life — career, finances, children, reputation can become subtle idols that pull you outside God's protective boundaries just as foreign gods did for ancient Israel.
One-Line Action
Identify one area where you have been merely surviving and ask God to begin His renovation work to move you toward thriving.
Memorable Quote
He's in the renovation business. He's able to fix what needs fixing and sometimes we don't realize how fixed we need to be.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon interweaves the Torah portion Kedoshim (Leviticus 19-20) with a celebration of Yom Ha'atzmaut, Israel's Independence Day. Rabbi opens by grounding holiness in God's character rather than human effort, noting the immediate link between 'be holy' and honoring family and Shabbat as community cornerstones. He reframes 'love your neighbor as yourself' as active communal responsibility — including frank rebuke and standing against injustice — using Nathan's confrontation of David as a model.

The message pivots to Israel's prophetic restoration, tracing Ezekiel 37's dry bones pattern: physical national reassembly followed by spiritual revival. He highlights the miraculous circumstances of 1948 independence, including the improvised Air Force story from the documentary Above and Beyond, and the geopolitical reality that most surrounding Arab nations gained independence in the same era. Drawing a striking parallel, he notes the Messianic Jewish movement emerged alongside Zionism in the late 1800s and exploded after Jerusalem's 1967 reunification, mirroring the Jesus Movement among young people.

From 2 Corinthians 4, he frames believers as clay jars carrying divine treasure — troubled but not crushed, persecuted but not abandoned, knocked down but not destroyed. He concludes that God's restoration pattern for Israel mirrors what He offers every believer: liberation not for independence from God but for fruitful purpose in Him.

Hook / Wordplay

"He's in the renovation business" — God restores what's broken, nationally and personally, following the Ezekiel 37 pattern: physical reassembly first, then spiritual breath.

2024-05-19

Making Appointments With God

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Make daily time with God the non-negotiable appointment—if Yeshua Himself withdrew before daybreak to be alone with the Father, we cannot afford to glide by without that same discipline.
  • Hold personal plans loosely and resist the rich-fool impulse to self-congratulate; God orchestrates appointments we cannot foresee, and retrospect consistently reveals their purpose.
  • Engage the biblical calendar (moedim) not as a ritual checklist but as active spiritual preparation—the Omer counting models how each season is meant to cultivate readiness for the next harvest God has planned.
One-Line Action
Set a daily non-negotiable appointment with God in prayer and His Word, trusting Him to orchestrate the divine appointments you cannot yet see.
Memorable Quote
We're living right now in the shortest part of eternity, the shortest part of eternity right now. And that means that in this place, we're able to make adjustments and make changes.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Built on Torah portion Emor (Leviticus 21-23), the sermon uses the concept of 'appointments' as its unifying metaphor. The rabbi opens with the priestly calling to holiness and pivots to Luke 12's parable of the rich fool, warning against self-focused planning that ignores God. He draws from Hebrews 5 to connect spiritual maturity—distinguishing good from evil through continuous exercise—to the priestly role of teaching the people.

Ecclesiastes 3 illustrates that God orchestrates every season of life, including the one appointment no one can miss. Through Galatians 3-4 he shows Messiah arrived at God's pre-set appointed time, cautioning against reducing faith to calendar observance without heart engagement. He highlights the sons of Issachar (1 Chronicles 12:32) as leaders who understood their times and acted.

The Omer count is reframed as active spiritual preparation rather than passive waiting. Closing with Habakkuk 2:2-3, he urges the congregation to trust God's timeline, illustrated by his own story of years at a hated job that later proved to be training for ministry.

Hook / Wordplay

Doctor appointments, divine appointments, God's holiday calendar, and the one appointment nobody can miss—the sermon threads every meaning of 'appointment' into a single call to put God first on our schedule.

2024-05-26

B'har: God's Investment Properties

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When facing loss or setback, remember the Jubilee principle—God builds reset points into life so that no season of poverty or failure needs to be permanent.
  • The value of our 'life property' is measured not by status or possessions but by the fruit it produces for others—ask what harvest your current circumstances are yielding.
  • Like a seed that must be buried to bear fruit, yielding control to God in uncomfortable or dark seasons is precisely what unlocks exponential growth and impact beyond what we could achieve alone.
One-Line Action
Identify one area where you feel 'buried' or at a dead end and reframe it as God planting you for a harvest you cannot yet see.
Memorable Quote
Each one of us is a precious commodity of the Lord. Each of us is a place that God invests His treasure in, even though we may be cracked vessels, leaking vessels, whatever it may be.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon explores Parashat B'har (Leviticus 25), which establishes the Sabbatical year and Jubilee laws governing land sales and redemption in ancient Israel. Rabbi frames these laws under the metaphor 'God's Investment Properties,' noting that land value was measured by crop yield rather than development potential. The Jubilee served as a divine reset button, restoring property to original families and preventing permanent despair from poverty.

He connects this to Jeremiah 32, where the prophet purchased his uncle's land during the Babylonian siege as a prophetic declaration that God would bring restoration and return. The sermon pivots to the spiritual application: God's true investment property is us—we are 'lively stones' in whom He places His treasure, even though we are fragile earthen vessels. Drawing from John 12, he uses the grain of wheat metaphor to explain that yielding ourselves to God, even when it feels like dying, produces abundant harvest.

He addresses the counting of the Omer, arguing it should be joyous anticipation of harvest rather than a mourning period rooted in a post-130 CE tradition. The sermon emphasizes that God is in the restoration business, watching over His property investments to bring forth dividends and value. He encourages the congregation that no circumstance is too hard for God, and that ministering to others transforms us in the process.

Hook / Wordplay

'God's Investment Properties'—reframes real estate language into spiritual terms: we are the land God has invested in, and our value is measured not by what we look like on the surface but by the harvest we produce.

2024-06-02

B'chukkotai: Keys to Fruitful Productivity

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When productivity stalls or life feels fruitless, check whether I have drifted from the covenant disciplines (prayer, word, obedience) rather than just working harder on my own terms.
  • Resist writing off difficult people; God's entire covenant arc bends toward restoration, and even those walking in darkness are candidates for return.
  • Ecclesiastes 8:11 is a practical leadership principle: when wrong behavior goes unaddressed, it escalates—apply this to setting and holding boundaries in relationships and responsibilities.
One-Line Action
During these ten days before Shavuot, commit to daily time in God's word and intentional obedience as the foundation for lasting fruitfulness.
Memorable Quote
The key to fruitfulness with God is to obey Him, to walk in His ways, to draw near to Him, to be in His Word, to allow His Word to work through us, to bring forth fruit that remains.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi teaches from the Torah portion Bechukotai (Leviticus 26), which outlines God's covenant blessings for obedience—rain in season, abundant harvests, security, and peace—alongside escalating consequences for disobedience, intensifying sevenfold at each stage of rebellion. He emphasizes that even after the harshest warnings, God promises restoration by remembering His covenant with the ancestors. The Haftarah from Jeremiah reinforces the contrast: cursed is the one who trusts in human strength, but blessed is the one who trusts in God, likened to a tree planted by water that bears fruit even in drought.

Drawing from Ecclesiastes 8:11, he makes a pointed application that when punishment for evil is not promptly carried out, people are emboldened to continue in wrongdoing, connecting this to modern social decay. He transitions to John 15's vine-and-branches metaphor, teaching that fruitful productivity depends on remaining united with Messiah.

From Romans 1, he traces the progressive deterioration that follows when people suppress the truth, exchanging God's glory for created things. He closes with Romans 8:28, affirming that God works all things together for good for those called according to His purpose, and notes the significance of being at day 39 of the Omer, with Yeshua's ascension on day 40 and Shavuot ten days away.

Hook / Wordplay

Fruitful productivity is not self-generated effort but covenant fruit—stay attached to the vine and God brings the increase.

2024-06-09

Shavuot Is a Numbers Game

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When you don't know what to do in a moment of God's overwhelming presence, don't manufacture a response—yield and listen, as Peter learned at the transfiguration.
  • God woos us back even from our worst compromises; He speaks to our hearts in desolate places and always keeps avenues of return open.
  • Encourage others to fulfill their unique calling rather than expecting conformity—the body functions when each part operates in its created purpose, not when every part tries to be the same.
One-Line Action
Surrender personal agendas and unite with fellow believers so God's Spirit can move through each unique part of the body toward a harvest of souls.
Memorable Quote
God wants to do something beyond what our best resume could come up with.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi connects the Torah portion Bamidbar (Numbers) with Shavuot through the theme that Shavuot is a 'numbers game'—the counting of the Omer, fifty days, and the census of Israel all converge. He walks through Exodus 19, where God descended on Sinai in fire and thunder, and Ezekiel 1, with its wheel-within-a-wheel manifestation, showing these were the traditional Shavuot readings in the temple. He argues compellingly that Acts 2 occurred in the temple during the morning service—not in a hidden upper room—because the disciples had just read those very passages when fire and wind manifested over them, and thousands could not have responded from a private house.

He draws in Hosea's theme of God wooing adulterous Israel back from the wilderness with betrothal language, and Jeremiah 31's New Covenant promise of Torah written on the heart. He links Daniel 9:24's anointing of the most holy place to the Shavuot outpouring in that same prophetic year. He recalls the 1970s Jesus Movement as a modern parallel where God used 'foolish things to confound the wise.' He closes with 1 Corinthians 12, urging that each member of the body has a unique role, and that unity and mutual encouragement—not conformity—release God's Spirit to reach the multitudes.

Hook / Wordplay

'Shavuot is a Numbers Game'—the Torah portion is literally the Book of Numbers, while Shavuot is built on counting: the Omer count, the 50th day, 3,000 then 5,000 responding.

2024-06-16

Core Values vs. Corpse Values

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Let life's hardships function as God's training regimen rather than resenting them — the Marine model shows that rigorous formation produces people who can stand when it counts
  • Demonstrate the kingdom by letting the King reign in your own life first; if He is not reigning in us, our message of hope to others rings hollow
  • When God equips you to do something beyond your natural ability, resist the reflex to credit yourself — 'Wow, I didn't realize I was that good' is the exact point of downfall
One-Line Action
Identify one area where you are playing around the edges spiritually and commit to pressing fully in, like a Nazarite choosing consecration rather than drifting.
Memorable Quote
What moves me more than talents and skills is when I see God do something in my life that's beyond me. And I stand in awe to see how God equipped me to do something that I would not have thought myself equipped to do.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi builds the sermon on a triple wordplay linking core values, the Marine Corps, and corpse (dead men's bones) from both the Nazarite vow in Parashat Naso and Yeshua's rebuke of hypocritical teachers in Matthew 23. He parallels the Marine Corps core values of honor, courage, and commitment with the spiritual formation God provides believers, emphasizing that esprit de corps — the spirit of the body — maps directly onto the body of Messiah working together corporately. He highlights the Roman centurion in Matthew 8 whose military understanding of authority produced a faith Yeshua said He had not found in all Israel.

The Nazarite vow is explored as a voluntary act of consecration, with Samson offered as a cautionary example of what happens when the vow is imposed rather than chosen. Rabbi traces Paul's navigation of hostile situations in Acts 21-23 as a case study of spiritual training producing real-time wisdom under extreme pressure. David's confrontation with Goliath is presented as the model of stepping into the gap when God's honor is at stake, trusting not in personal skill but in relationship with God.

The sermon warns against self-congratulation after God-enabled victories, insisting the credit belongs to God who equips us beyond our natural abilities. He closes by urging the congregation to internalize God's core values so deeply that they become the operating center of who we are, not just words on a shelf.

Hook / Wordplay

Core values, Corps values, corpse values — the same sound carries three meanings: the integrity God builds in us, the esprit de corps of the body of Messiah working as one, and the dead men's bones we must never let contaminate our walk.

2024-06-23

B'Ha'Alotkha: Clouds in Your Head

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When afflictions feel overwhelming, reframe them: they work FOR you, not against you, because God uses them to produce a glory that dwarfs the suffering — stop viewing hardship as evidence God has abandoned you.
  • Guilt clouds spiritual vision just like brain fog clouds thinking; accept that God has already addressed your guilt rather than staying in a spiritually disheveled state that the accuser exploits.
  • Follow God's timing with discipline — when the cloud stays, stay; when it moves, move — resist the impulse to run ahead or complain during seasons of waiting.
One-Line Action
When doubt or mental fog sets in, consciously redirect your focus from what you lack to the presence and provision of God, and obey His lead even when you cannot see the destination.
Memorable Quote
God believes that we can handle more than we think we can.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi teaches from the Torah portion B'Ha'Alotkha, focusing on the cloud that covered the tabernacle and guided Israel's movements through the wilderness. He draws a dual metaphor: having your 'head in the cloud' of God's presence is the ideal, while having 'clouds in your head' represents the mental fog, depression, and doubt that block spiritual clarity. He highlights how the mixed multitude's complaining about manna — remembering Egypt's free food while forgetting their slavery — mirrors how we take God's provision for granted.

From the Haftarah in Zechariah, he shows Joshua the high priest standing in filthy garments before the accuser, depicting how guilt and the adversary leave us spiritually disheveled, and how God's remedy is obedience and restoration. He traces through Paul's teaching that our afflictions are 'light and momentary' not because they lack severity but because the coming glory dwarfs them by comparison. He warns from 1 Corinthians 10 that Israel's failures — grumbling, idolatry, immorality — are prefigurative warnings for us, citing how the tribe of Simeon's sin reduced them to the smallest tribe with the smallest inheritance.

He connects this to James's teaching on perseverance through temptation and the danger of double-mindedness. He introduces the Hebrew word 'sharev' from James to explain the hot dry wind of the hamsin season and its link to the omer counting period. He concludes with Hebrews 12's cloud of faithful witnesses contrasted against the cloud of complainers, and Isaiah's promise that God keeps in perfect peace those whose minds are stayed on Him.

Hook / Wordplay

Head in the Cloud or Clouds in Your Head — the same word 'cloud' means either God's guiding presence (follow it) or mental fog from doubt and guilt (clear it).

2024-06-30

What Do You See & Say?

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When facing a daunting situation, consciously separate observable facts from the fearful spin I layer on top—ask whether I'm seeing through God's promise or through grasshopper eyes.
  • Before repeating someone else's interpretation of events, pause to consider whether I'm passing along encouragement or amplifying discouragement that could demoralize others.
  • Invest in daily prayer as ongoing relationship rather than crisis intervention—Mark 9 shows that capacity for hard spiritual moments is built through sustained communion, not last-minute effort.
One-Line Action
Before speaking about a challenge this week, pause and ask: am I reporting what God has promised, or echoing someone else's fear?
Memorable Quote
It means don't be intoxicated by the words and by the actions and the interpretations of what people say.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi teaches from Shlach L'kha, where twelve spies surveyed the Promised Land but ten returned with a fear-driven report that demoralized the entire nation. Only Joshua and Caleb saw the same giants yet trusted God's promise, declaring Israel well able to take the land. The people's inability to filter discouraging spin from God's actual word cost them forty years in the wilderness—one year for each day of the spies' journey.

The parasha concludes with the command to wear tzitzit as a physical reminder not to follow one's own eyes and heart astray. Drawing from Hebrews 3-4, the rabbi warns against hardened hearts and unbelief that prevent entering God's rest, stressing that hearing a promise without combining it with trust renders it powerless. He connects to John 21 where Peter, freshly commissioned by Yeshua, immediately got distracted wondering about John's future—spawning a false rumor among the brothers.

From 1 Peter 5 he urges sobriety and alertness, redefining sober as refusing to be intoxicated by others' negative interpretations. The sermon's throughline is that what we think we see directly shapes what we say, and unchecked negative speech can derail an entire community from God's plan.

Hook / Wordplay

The tzitzit fringes become a double metaphor: don't be a 'fringe element on the outskirts' of God's plan—instead, claim the 'fringe benefits' of walking in union with Him.

2024-07-09

Korakh: How True is Your Image of You?

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When jealousy targets someone else's position or gifts, you don't gain what they have—you lose the unique place God already gave you, just as Korach traded his honored Levitical role for destruction.
  • The difference between Saul and David wasn't severity of sin but response to conviction: Saul protected his public image while David pursued restored relationship with God—choose David's mirror.
  • Beware the Milgram-experiment dynamic in spiritual communities: group loyalty and social pressure can override personal moral reasoning, making even leaders of renown capable of catastrophic rebellion.
One-Line Action
This week, when tempted to compare yourself to others or manage your image, stop and ask whether you are looking into God's face or just your own reflection.
Memorable Quote
And the message of this is that when you're jealous of somebody else for what they have, you not only don't acquire what they have instead, but you also lose the special place that you have, there were probably few people, if any, that a higher position.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon explores the Torah portion of Korach (Numbers 16) through the lens of self-image, asking whether we see ourselves accurately or through dangerous distortion. Rabbi uses the metaphor of a cat seeing a lion in the mirror—or a lion seeing only a cat—to frame how inflated or diminished self-perception leads to spiritual destruction. He traces a cascading pattern of rebellion from Miriam and Aaron's jealousy, through the spies' grasshopper self-image, to Korach's uprising, emphasizing that these were leaders of renown and reputation, not lifelong rebels.

The sermon contrasts King Saul, who prioritized his public image over genuine repentance, with King David, who cared about restoring his relationship with God regardless of how scandal would look. Paul is held up as someone who counted all worldly credentials as refuse compared to knowing Messiah and embraced weakness as the venue for God's power. The rabbi references the Milgram experiment to illustrate how group-think can override moral reasoning even among ordinary people, connecting it to how 250 leaders of Israel followed Korach into rebellion.

Drawing from Romans 1 and Jude, he shows how dismissing God's authority creates an avalanche of moral and spiritual deterioration. The sermon closes with James 4's call to humble ourselves before God, who alone can lift us up and develop the true image of Messiah within us.

Hook / Wordplay

The cat-lion mirror: are you the cat inflating yourself into a lion, or the lion who only sees an alley cat? Either distortion blinds you to who God actually made you to be.

The Pain Between A Rock & A Hard Place

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Complaining feels productive but stifles God's Spirit — when feeling trapped, redirect energy from cataloging what's missing to acknowledging what God has already provided.
  • Even Moses cracked after 40 years of patience; when frustrated with difficult people, remember that others extend the same patience toward me.
  • The bronze serpent lost its meaning within three generations and became an idol — spiritual truths must be continually re-anchored in Scripture, not inherited passively.
One-Line Action
This week, when a situation feels like it's closing in, pause before complaining and instead name one specific provision God has already made.
Memorable Quote
There is something about complaining that makes people think that they're doing something productive. When in reality it is stifling the move of God's spirit.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon explores Torah portion Hukkat, focusing on Numbers 20-21 where Israel complains about water and food in the wilderness. Moses, after 40 years of enduring the people's complaints, finally cracks under pressure and strikes the rock instead of speaking to it as God commanded. Despite Moses' disobedience, God still provides water abundantly, showing His desire to bless His people even when leaders fail.

The bronze serpent episode illustrates how looking to God's provision brings healing from the consequences of sin, directly foreshadowing Yeshua being lifted up (John 3:14-16). The rabbi traces the rock imagery throughout Scripture: the cornerstone rejected by builders (Luke 20, Acts 4), the living stone on which believers are built (1 Peter 2), and the rock higher than ourselves (Psalm 61). He highlights how complaining feels productive but actually stifles God's Spirit, and how panic leads to irrational choices.

The generational degradation of the bronze serpent from healing symbol to idol illustrates the danger of passive spiritual inheritance. The message encourages believers to look to the Rock as foundation rather than focusing on the walls closing in around them.

Hook / Wordplay

"Rock" serves triple duty: the literal rock Moses struck, the idiom "between a rock and a hard place," and the Messianic cornerstone — weaving the wilderness narrative into a sustained metaphor about where we place our trust under pressure.

2024-07-21

Balak: Acting Out Of Fear

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Fear-driven decisions tend to produce the very outcomes we dread—Pharaoh feared Israel's alliance with enemies so he enslaved them, and that alignment with God destroyed Egypt instead.
  • Micah 6:8's three directives (act justly, love grace, walk in purity with God) serve as a practical antidote to fear-driven living and greed-driven compromise.
  • Everyone has a 'price' that tempts compromise—internalizing the depth of God's love (Romans 8:38-39) is what makes that price meaningless.
One-Line Action
When fear or greed starts driving a decision, pause and return to Micah 6:8—act justly, love grace, walk in purity with God.
Memorable Quote
If we could understand the power of God's love in our life, fear would not hold us so bound.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon explores Parashat Balak (Numbers 22-24) through the lens of how fear distorts perception and leads to self-destructive decisions. Moab's king Balak, gripped by fear of Israel's numbers and military victories, hired the prophet Balaam to curse Israel, but fear-driven plans rarely succeed. Balaam initially spoke God's words and delivered powerful blessings and messianic prophecies over Israel, yet was gradually corrupted by greed, eventually devising a backdoor strategy to lead Israel into sin with Moabite women.

The rabbi draws a striking parallel to Pharaoh, whose fear of Israel's growing population led him to enslave them, and the very catastrophe he feared came upon Egypt through Israel's God. New Testament texts from Jude, 2 Peter, and Revelation are cited to show how Balaam became an archetype of corruption through greed and self-deception. The rabbi contrasts destructive, tormenting fear with the reverential fear of God described in Proverbs 9 as the beginning of wisdom.

Micah 6:8's three requirements—act justly, love grace, walk in purity with God—are presented as the antidote to fear-driven living. Romans 8:31-39 is explored to show that nothing in all creation can separate believers from God's love, rendering fear powerless. The sermon closes with Joshua 1:9, emphasizing that God's abiding presence is the stabilizing factor against every challenge.

Hook / Wordplay

Those who acted out of fear were destroyed by the very things they feared—Pharaoh's enslavement of Israel brought about his own destruction, and Balaam's greed led to his own ruin.

2024-07-28

Peace in the Midst of Chaos

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Fear recycles the same hopeless narrative—Elijah repeated the identical complaint twice in the cave; recognize when you are ruminating on the same negative story and break the loop by recalling what God has already shown you.
  • Yeshua's peace is not the absence of trouble but his active presence within it (John 14:27); stop waiting for calm circumstances before choosing to walk in shalom.
  • Fear isolates you into thinking you are the only one left, just as Elijah believed—but God had 7,000 faithful; resist the lie that you fight alone.
One-Line Action
When fear starts to paralyze or redirect you, recall that God's peace is engineered to function inside chaos, not after it ends.
Memorable Quote
The title for the mess today is Peace in the midst of chaos.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The rabbi opens with the Torah portion Pinchas, where Phinehas receives God's covenant of shalom after his zealous act of stopping the plague that killed 24,000 Israelites. He highlights the paradox that an act of violence yielded a covenant of peace, then traces the devastating numerical impact on the tribe of Simeon—dropping from 59,300 to 22,200—as a direct consequence of Zimri's sin and the plague it invited. The sermon pivots to fear as its central theme, using Elijah's flight from Jezebel as a case study: the prophet went from witnessing God's greatest display of power to hiding in a cave, repeating the identical hopeless complaint twice.

God's response—revealing 7,000 who had not bowed to Baal—demonstrates that fear isolates us into believing we fight alone. The rabbi draws extensive parallels to modern fear-driven manipulation in politics, media, and culture, referencing Orwell's 1984 to illustrate how narratives are rewritten to maintain control. He examines Caiaphas's fear-based calculation that one man should die to save the nation, and how Judas's greed blinded him to the significance of what was unfolding.

The theological anchor lands on John 14:27, where Yeshua offers 'my peace, not as the world gives'—a peace designed to operate inside chaos, not after it subsides. He reinforces this with 1 John 4:18, that perfect love casts out fear, and Paul's example of pressing forward despite many opponents. The closing call is to fix our eyes on the prize like Olympians who refuse to be psyched out, walking in the covenant of peace Yeshua secured.

Hook / Wordplay

"Peace is not the absence of chaos—peace is the refuge in the midst of chaos." The sermon's signature reframe, displayed as a quote and threaded through every section.

2024-08-04

How to Move On

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • What feels like an ending is actually a God-ordained starting point for a new stage—reframe setbacks as launchpads rather than dead ends.
  • Past trials you survived were equipping you for future challenges you couldn't yet see; trust that the current difficulty is doing the same.
  • Stay in union with God to navigate life's stages rather than trying to decode every detail of the plan on your own—the plan unfolds as you move forward.
One-Line Action
Identify one life situation you've been treating as an ending and reframe it as God's starting point for your next stage.
Memorable Quote
If all God cared about was getting us to heaven, He would save us and kill us the next moment. But there's something about going through the stages of life, going through and moving from one phase to the next phase, moving forward to the next phase, moving on and gleaning from it all of the things that God is preparing in us for the next phase.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi teaches from the double portion Mattot-Mas'ei, briefly addressing vows from Numbers 30 and the principle of letting your yes be yes and your no be no. He then focuses on Numbers 33, highlighting the repeated phrase 'they moved on' as Moses recorded each stage of Israel's forty-year wilderness journey by God's command. He explains that each stage had a divinely appointed starting point, and what looks like an ending in our lives is often the beginning of a new phase God is orchestrating.

Using the Parent Trap movie illustration of a granddaughter telling her grandfather 'I'm making a memory,' he draws a parallel to how God makes formative memories in each stage of our lives, even ones we don't recognize at the time. He shares personal testimony about past trials that seemed unbearable but were actually equipping him for greater challenges ahead.

Drawing from Mark 8, 1 Peter 1, and Ephesians 1, he argues that God's plan unfolds progressively and that staying in union with Messiah is the key to navigating each stage. He closes by urging the congregation not to get stuck deciphering each stage but to keep moving forward in God, trusting that He builds character from glory to glory through the journey.

Hook / Wordplay

The phrase 'they moved on' repeats dozens of times in Numbers 33—each repetition becomes a sermon refrain showing that God's people are always meant to keep moving forward, stage by stage.

2024-08-11

D'varim: Taking Hold Of Words

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Words can instantly redirect even the strongest faith (Elijah crumbled at one sentence from Jezebel after defeating 450 prophets)—actively guard which voices I let shape my emotional state and decisions.
  • Every judgment passage in Scripture contains God's avenue of return; when I feel convicted or overwhelmed by failure, look for the restoration pathway rather than staying in despair.
  • Trials are not just endurance tests but equip me to encourage others—the comfort I receive from God through hardship becomes my credential and content for ministry to people facing similar struggles.
One-Line Action
This week, catch yourself when negative words from others begin to override what God has spoken, and consciously replace that narrative with a specific promise from His word.
Memorable Quote
Whenever you see judgment mentioned in the Bible, I say read a little bit further and you'll see God's avenue of return.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon opens the book of Devarim (Deuteronomy), meaning 'words,' framing Moses' final address as a covenantal recap before Israel enters the Promised Land. Rabbi draws a sustained parallel between God's command to 'take hold of the land' and our responsibility to take hold of God's word today. He illustrates the destructive power of negative words through Elijah fleeing after Jezebel's threat despite just witnessing God's fire, and Moses striking the rock when the people's complaints overwhelmed his composure.

From Isaiah 1, he teaches that God's judgment passages always contain an avenue of return—wash yourselves, stop doing evil, learn to do good—showing God's heart is restoration, not destruction. Joshua 1 and the Psalms reinforce that courage comes from God's presence and His commitment to hold our hand through every challenge. In John 15, Yeshua teaches that His words prune us to bear fruit, and staying united with Him is the condition for fruitfulness.

Paul's testimony in Philippians 3 models 'taking hold of that for which Messiah took hold of me,' counting worldly advantages as garbage compared to knowing Yeshua. The sermon closes with 2 Corinthians 1, reframing trials as preparation for ministry—God comforts us so we can comfort others with the same encouragement we received.

Hook / Wordplay

'Devarim' means 'words' and God says 'take hold of the land'—so taking hold of God's words is how we possess what He has promised, and the twist is that Messiah already took hold of us first.

2024-08-18

Va'etchanan: God's Binding Covenant of Love

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Reframe Torah commands as expressions of covenant love, not mere rules—Deuteronomy is a love letter, and reading it through that lens changes how obedience feels and functions.
  • When doubting God's faithfulness, anchor in Jeremiah 33: His covenant is tied to cosmic permanence—as certain as day following night and the foundations of the earth.
  • Obedience is the language of love in covenant relationship—John 14:15 and the Torah commands operate on the same principle, so keeping God's word is how we speak love back to Him.
One-Line Action
This week, read one chapter of Deuteronomy slowly as a love letter rather than a law code, and note where God's affection for His people comes through.
Memorable Quote
From Devariim, Deuteronomy, which happens to be in this week's portion, the cornerstone of our faith.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon explores Parashat Va'etchanan, framing God's covenant as an unbreakable bond of love. Rabbi begins with Moses being denied entry to the Promised Land, demonstrating that God's standards apply equally to all and that obedience matters regardless of stature. He highlights Deuteronomy 7's covenant language, arguing that Torah is fundamentally a love letter rather than a book of judgment, and likens Deuteronomy to a ketubah—a marriage document outlining both blessings and responsibilities.

He connects Yeshua's temptation in the wilderness to the Torah portions being read in synagogues during that very period, noting all three scriptures Yeshua quoted came from Deuteronomy. The marriage metaphor is extended through Ephesians 5, where Paul calls the Messiah-congregation relationship a profound mystery mirrored in marriage. From Jeremiah 31, the rabbi emphasizes God's everlasting love and the new covenant written on hearts, noting that even after Israel broke the original covenant, God found an avenue to bring them back.

He invokes Jeremiah 33's declaration that God's covenant is as permanent as day and night and the foundations of the earth. The sermon closes with the image of a pinky promise—God reaching down to redeem us into covenant—and the call to carry this legacy of covenantal love to future generations.

Hook / Wordplay

Deuteronomy as a ketubah: the Torah is not a rule book but a marriage document between God and His people, spelling out the blessings and responsibilities of a binding love covenant.

2024-08-25

Ekev — Little By Little

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When progress feels imperceptibly slow, look back to see how far God has already brought you — the distance behind is often far greater than it felt in the moment.
  • Endurance is not passive waiting but active forward motion through difficulty; treat patience as a verb, not just a posture.
  • Don't 'dig up the seed' by trying to accelerate God's process with your own effort — trust that what is planted in faithfulness will produce a harvest in its season.
One-Line Action
Identify one area where you've been impatiently 'digging up the seed' and consciously release it to God's timing this week.
Memorable Quote
the few that are chosen are those that recognize that the process is as much a part of the calling as the calling itself in fact without going through the process there is no calling
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon explores Deuteronomy 7:22 where God promises to expel the nations 'little by little,' using this as a framework for understanding how God works transformation in our lives gradually rather than all at once. Rabbi draws a parallel between Israel's incremental possession of the land and our own spiritual growth, noting that the process itself builds the character needed to sustain the blessing. He uses the mustard seed as a central image — the smallest of seeds that grows into a tree large enough for birds to nest in — to illustrate how God takes what seems insignificant and produces abundance.

Key New Testament passages reinforce that transformation happens 'from one degree of glory to the next' through patience and endurance. The rabbi warns against trying to accelerate God's process, comparing it to a farmer who digs up seeds to check on them and thereby destroys the harvest. He connects this to Romans 12:2's call to be transformed by the renewing of the mind rather than conforming to worldly standards.

Drawing from Isaiah 51, he reminds the congregation that God called Abraham as just one person, yet from that single seed came an entire nation and ultimately the Messiah. The sermon closes with Hebrews 10:35-39, urging the congregation not to throw away their courage but to keep trusting, because endurance produces character, character produces hope, and hope does not disappoint.

Hook / Wordplay

"I have a mustard seed and I'm not afraid to use it" — the smallest seed holds the greatest possibilities when yielded to God's timing rather than forced by human impatience.

2024-09-01

Re'eh — When Do We Arrive?

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • My true destination is not an outcome or milestone I am waiting for—it is daily intimacy with God; I should measure 'arrival' by the depth of that relationship rather than by external circumstances changing.
  • When difficulty tempts me to grab at quick fixes or alternative paths, I need to recognize that pattern as settling for what will not satisfy—the 'weak and miserable elemental spirits' of Galatians 4—instead of trusting God's longer route.
  • Sacrificial love is measured more by willingness to be inconvenienced for five minutes than by willingness to take a bullet; I should examine where I withhold small daily patience from people while imagining myself capable of grand gestures.
One-Line Action
Stop measuring spiritual progress by circumstances and start measuring it by daily closeness with God.
Memorable Quote
There are people who will take a bullet for you. That will happen.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Teaching from Torah portion Re'eh (Deuteronomy 11:26), Rabbi uses the metaphor of children asking 'are we there yet?' on a road trip to frame the spiritual life as a journey toward God's intended destination. God sets before us blessing and curse—blessing tied to obedience, curse to disobedience—and these outcomes follow our choices like gravity follows stepping off a building. He warns against being lured by alternative spiritual paths or settling for less when the journey feels long, noting that Deuteronomy 12:8 says 'you have not yet arrived at the rest and the inheritance.' Drawing from Isaiah 55, he urges seeking the Lord while He is available rather than spending effort on what doesn't satisfy.

From 1 John 3, he reframes the destination as intimate daily relationship with God, not a geographic or circumstantial arrival point. Using 1 Corinthians 13, he teaches that our current knowledge is partial—we see through a glass dimly—but when the perfect comes we will know fully.

He challenges the congregation that love must be active and inconvenient, not just heroic or verbal. He closes with Matthew 6, urging them to seek first the kingdom of God and trust Him with provision rather than being anxious about the timeline.

Hook / Wordplay

'Are we there yet?' / 'Only five more minutes' — the childhood car trip becomes the frame for our entire spiritual journey, where impatience with God's timeline mirrors children who cannot grasp the distance to the destination.

High
2024-09-08

Shoftim: When Only Real Justice Will Do

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When confronting someone's wrongdoing, consider whether I'm acting as a truthful witness with skin in the game or just an accuser—Yeshua's response to the woman's accusers shows the difference between righteous judgment and entrapment.
  • Resist the pull to 'bench myself' from my actual calling by spending all my energy on outrage against outside factions; the adversary's strategy is divide-and-conquer, and constant accusation emulates him, not God.
  • Practice the Isaiah 42 pattern: before trying to correct or condemn someone who is spiritually blind, lead with light and liberation—demonstrate what God did for me while I was still in darkness, rather than cataloging how lost they are.
One-Line Action
Stop accusing and start illuminating—bring good news instead of outrage, and let God's justice do the heavy lifting.
Memorable Quote
From Devarim, Deuteronomy 6, the cornerstone of our faith.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The Torah portion Shoftim commands Israel to appoint judges who pursue righteous judgment without favoritism or bribery—justice, only justice. The rabbi reframes the John 8 story of the woman caught in adultery through Torah context, showing Yeshua addressed the witnesses specifically, not making a general statement about sinlessness, and gave them room to save face by writing in the dirt and not making eye contact. He demonstrated both compassion for the accusers—letting them walk away—and tough love for the woman by telling her to sin no more, rather than pitting love against law.

Isaiah 53's description of Messiah's unjust arrest and execution connects to the justice theme: Messiah took upon himself the justice due to us. Drawing from Proverbs 6, the rabbi warns that false witnesses, bribery, and sowing strife destroy justice and divide communities, even splitting families over political positions. He identifies the adversary as the true disruptor behind social division—not any human faction—urging believers not to become accusers of the brethren themselves.

Citing 1 Corinthians 5, he distinguishes between accountability within the believing community and leaving outsiders to God's judgment, cautioning against spending all energy labeling those who disagree. The sermon closes with Isaiah 42's vision of opening blind eyes and freeing prisoners from inner darkness, calling believers to be vehicles of that justice through beautiful feet that bring good news rather than benching themselves through outrage.

Hook / Wordplay

The Torah's doubled emphasis—'Justice, only justice you must pursue'—becomes the sermon's lens: real justice isn't vigilantism or getting your way, but God's truth applied with both compassion and accountability.

2024-09-16

Properly Preparing For War

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When quarrels arise, check internal motives first (James 4)—the person opposing you may not be the real enemy; the adversary behind the scenes is stirring conflict between people who then mistake each other for the threat.
  • Apply the 'no vacancy' principle: actively fill every area of life with God's presence and Word so that strongholds and accusatory thought patterns cannot take root.
  • Reconciliation is not passive comfort but an active ministry God entrusts to us—even helping an enemy's burdened donkey (Exodus 23) demonstrates a different kind of warfare that can open doors no argument ever could.
One-Line Action
Fill every area of your life with God's presence so the adversary finds no vacancy to establish a stronghold.
Memorable Quote
If we allow him to occupy all of us, if we allow the Lord to come and occupy every place, the adversary has no vacancy. There's no room for him to move in and take over.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon opens with the Torah portion Ki Tetze (Deuteronomy 21:10), noting that even God's instructions for physical warfare include surprisingly compassionate standards, such as dignified treatment of captive women. Rabbi uses this as a springboard to explore four dimensions of warfare: actual, societal, marital, and spiritual, arguing that behind every human conflict an unseen adversary is stirring people against one another. Drawing from James 4, he identifies the root of quarrels as internal desires battling within us, not the people we perceive as enemies, and prescribes the remedy: submit to God, draw near to Him, and the adversary will flee.

He introduces a vivid principle—if God occupies every part of us, the adversary has 'no vacancy' to establish a stronghold. From 2 Corinthians 10 he explains that spiritual weapons demolish strongholds by taking every thought captive to Messiah, and from 2 Corinthians 5 he frames believers as ambassadors entrusted with a ministry of reconciliation. He connects Exodus 23's command to help even an enemy's burdened donkey to this reconciliation mandate, showing that Torah's compassion toward adversaries is itself a form of warfare.

The Ephesians 6 armor passage provides the practical equipment: truth, righteousness, peace, faith, deliverance, and the Word of God. He ties the message to the approaching High Holiday season, urging the congregation to humble themselves before God as in 2 Chronicles 7:14. He concludes that proper preparation for battle means uniting with Messiah, wielding His spiritual equipment, and becoming instruments of reconciliation rather than retaliation.

Hook / Wordplay

Ki Tetze means 'when you go out to war,' but proper preparation starts inside—fill every space with God and the adversary finds no vacancy.

2024-09-22

Ki Tavo: Out of Darkness, Into Light

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When overwhelmed by difficult circumstances, focus on deepening union with God rather than fighting the darkness directly — Ephesians 5:8 teaches that light is a byproduct of being united with Messiah, not a product of self-effort.
  • Hating a brother while claiming spiritual enlightenment is self-deception (1 John 2:9-11); genuine walking in the light requires loving relationships, not just correct theology.
  • Like Paul before King Agrippa, do not soften truth based on audience status — the commission to open eyes and turn people from darkness to light applies regardless of who is listening.
One-Line Action
This week, identify one relationship where I have withheld love or warmth and take a concrete step to bring light into it.
Memorable Quote
The light that we have is not our own kindling. It is not what we have fabricated to make light by ourselves.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon explores the Ki Tavo portion's contrast between covenant blessings and cursings, anchored in Deuteronomy 28:29's image of groping at noon like a blind person in the dark. Rabbi notes the literary structure where cursings bracket the blessings, emphasizing that darkness results from turning away from God's commands. Moving to the Haftarah in Isaiah 60, he argues that while we cannot control when darkness appears, God's glory rises over those who remain united with Him.

The story of Madame Guyon, a Catholic mystic who radiated God's presence even from a rat-infested prison, illustrates that external circumstances cannot extinguish divine light. Through Ephesians 5:8, the rabbi distinguishes between merely having light and actively living as children of light, stressing that our illumination is not self-generated but flows from union with Messiah. Paul's conversion in Acts 26 serves as a case study of someone who thought he was enlightened but was actually in spiritual darkness until confronted by divine light.

The sermon threads through Colossians 1, 1 John 2, and 1 Peter 2 to show that loving one's brother is inseparable from walking in light, and that hatred leaves a person blind regardless of doctrinal correctness. Closing with 2 Corinthians 4's image of treasure in clay jars, the rabbi calls the congregation to carry God's light into dark places rather than retreating from them.

Hook / Wordplay

You can't turn off darkness, but you can turn on the light — and God can make even the darkness shine like noonday.

2024-09-29

Entering into the Covenant

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Covenant with God is not a passive status — it demands active yielding, allowing God to transform me so that transformation becomes visible to others.
  • When people around me are struggling, engage rather than withdraw; God Himself was 'troubled in all their troubles,' modeling solidarity over self-protection.
  • Digest Scripture until it moves from head knowledge to heart knowledge — 'the word is in your mouth and in your heart' means letting it become an activated, living part of daily life.
One-Line Action
This week, identify one area where you hold a covenantal 'title' passively and take one concrete step to actively yield that area to God's transforming work.
Memorable Quote
From Devarim, Deuteronomy, the cornerstone of our faith.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon explores the double Torah portion Nitzavim-Vayelech (Deuteronomy 29-31), centering on what it means to truly enter into covenant with God rather than merely holding a covenantal title. Rabbi emphasizes that every person — leaders, women, children, foreigners, wood-choppers — stood together before God, and that this inclusive assembly models what God desires for community. He warns against allowing a 'root of bitterness' to spring up and contaminate many, drawing the connection from Deuteronomy 29:17 to Hebrews 12:15.

The emotional peak comes in Deuteronomy 30, where God promises that even if His people are scattered to the far end of the sky, He will go there and get them — an expression of relentless covenantal love. Drawing from Hebrews 8, the rabbi frames the new covenant not as a replacement but as an expansion, where God writes Torah on hearts and minds so it becomes a living, activated part of the believer. Isaiah 61-62 reinforces God's unbreakable commitment to Zion, with a timely reference to Netanyahu's UN address and the nations' hostility toward Jerusalem.

The sermon culminates in Acts 3, where Peter and John demonstrate covenantal boldness by healing a crippled man and preaching fearlessly, resulting in five thousand coming to faith. The rabbi closes by distinguishing between passively asking God into our lives and actively allowing Him to transform us — the true meaning of entering covenant.

Hook / Wordplay

Nitzavim means 'standing' — but God doesn't just want us standing before Him with a certificate; He wants us entering into covenant, letting Him write Torah on our hearts so the word becomes flesh in us again.

2024-10-06

Birthing In Our Old Age

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • The preparations and waiting seasons are not delays but foundational parts of the calling itself — without submitting to them, the calling cannot materialize
  • Even after receiving what God promised, hold it with open hands; making a blessing into an idol blocks the very relationship that produced it
  • God's resume requirement is not competence or charisma but a poor and humble spirit that trembles at His word — that posture is where His power shines through earthen vessels
One-Line Action
Use the Days of Awe to honestly identify one area where you have been trying to force God's promise in your own strength, and consciously surrender it back to Him.
Memorable Quote
Oftentimes the preparations along the way are as much a part of the calling as the calling without the preparations and submitting to those preparations, there is no calling.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The rabbi opens the Rosh Hashanah message with Genesis 21-22, framing Sarah birthing Isaac at 90 and Abraham at 100 as the paradigm for God fulfilling promises far later than we expect. He illustrates with two modern examples: the singing janitor who won America's Got Talent in his late 50s after decades of quiet faithfulness, and Colonel Sanders who launched KFC at 65 after losing his restaurant to eminent domain. He then turns to the Akedah, noting the traditional Jewish understanding that Isaac was as-if offered and resurrected, and draws out the principle that even our most precious God-given blessings must not become idols.

Moving through Jeremiah 31, he highlights God's everlasting love and the promise of a new covenant written on hearts. From Joel 2 he emphasizes that God will restore the years the locusts have eaten, and that old men dreaming dreams means late-life vision is still viable.

Isaiah 66 asks who can contain God, and answers that He favors the one with a poor and humble spirit who trembles at His word. The rabbi closes by urging the congregation to use the ten Days of Awe for honest self-assessment, trusting that what God brings to birth He will deliver.

Hook / Wordplay

"Birthing in our old age" — the literal miracle of Sarah at 90 becomes the metaphor for every promise that arrives decades after we expected it, proving God's delivery room has no age limit.

2024-10-13

Getting Away to Draw Near

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Distinguish between fleeing God when things are hard (Jonah to Tarshish) and deliberately withdrawing from noise to meet Him—same physical motion, opposite spiritual direction.
  • Isaiah 58 redefines fasting: if my fast doesn't produce justice, generosity, and compassion for others, I've missed the point God actually cares about.
  • Messianic Yom Kippur fasting is standing in the gap with the Jewish community—not doubting redemption through Messiah but advancing it by reaching out to those still seeking atonement.
One-Line Action
Identify one distraction you're using to avoid drawing near to God, remove it this week, and replace that time with quiet prayer.
Memorable Quote
You can run from your circumstances, but your circumstances will follow you. You can run from responsibility, but your lack of responsibility will pay its toll.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Delivered on Yom Kippur, the sermon explores the paradox captured in its title: getting away to draw near. Rabbi opens with the Leviticus 16 Azazel ceremony and the permanent regulation of Yom Kippur, noting the Talmudic detail that the scarlet thread stopped turning white after Yeshua's sacrifice. He pivots to Isaiah 58's rebuke of superficial fasting, where God demands justice for the oppressed and care for the hungry rather than mere self-denial.

The central illustration is Jonah, who tried to get away from God by fleeing to Tarshish rather than proclaiming judgment to Nineveh. Rabbi contrasts Jonah's flight with Yeshua's practice of withdrawing to lonely places to pray—getting away from distractions to draw near to the Father. James 4 ties the threads together: stop quarreling, submit to God, draw near and He will draw near to you.

He addresses why Messianic believers fast on Yom Kippur, explaining it is not from uncertainty about atonement but to stand in the gap with the Jewish people and move forward in the fullness of Messiah's redemption. Romans 3 underscores that righteousness comes through Yeshua's faithfulness, not personal merit, and God put Yeshua forward as the kapparah—the same Hebrew root as Yom Kippur.

Hook / Wordplay

"Getting away to draw near"—Jonah got away FROM God; Yeshua got away WITH God. The same act of withdrawal leads to opposite destinations depending on whether you're running from the Lord or running to Him.

2024-10-20

Be A Sukkah that Shouts from the Rooftop!

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When facing opposition or dark circumstances, focus on 'turning on the light' rather than cataloging the darkness—be an active source of God's presence rather than a passive critic of the world's problems.
  • Embrace spiritual frailty as a feature, not a flaw: like the sukkah on the rooftop, our weakness set on God's foundation becomes the very platform from which His message is proclaimed.
  • Trust that God equips in the moment of need, as He did with Peter at Shavuot—preparation matters, but the Spirit provides the words when the appointed time arrives.
One-Line Action
This week, when you encounter darkness or opposition, consciously choose to 'turn on the light' by sharing one encouraging truth from God's word with someone who needs it.
Memorable Quote
You can't turn off darkness, but you can turn on the light and darkness disappears.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon, delivered during Sukkot, centers on the metaphor of believers as frail sukkahs that nonetheless proclaim God's message boldly from the rooftops. Rabbi opens by tracing Sukkot's place among the moedim, emphasizing it as the only festival specifically associated with rejoicing, preceded by the reflective season of Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur. He presents a textual case for Messiah's birth during Sukkot, anchored in John 1's language of the Word 'tabernacling' among us and the timeline of Yohanan the Immerser's ministry.

Drawing from Matthew 10:16-27, he connects Yeshua's instruction to 'proclaim from the housetops' with the image of a sukkah perched on a rooftop—a frail dwelling set on an unshakable foundation. He stresses that believers need not fear opposition because God provides the words and power at the appointed time, citing Peter's spontaneous sermon at Shavuot as proof. Zechariah 14's prophecy that all nations will eventually come to Jerusalem to celebrate Sukkot demonstrates God's redemptive purpose even through judgment.

The rabbi weaves in Revelation 21's vision of God's Shekhinah dwelling with mankind as the ultimate fulfillment of the tabernacling theme. He shares his personal testimony of coming to faith during the Sukkot season and experiencing 'rivers of living water' before ever reading John 7:38. The overarching message is that God deliberately chooses fragile vessels—clay jars, temporary booths—to house His presence and proclaim His light to a dark world.

Hook / Wordplay

The sukkah—a frail booth of branches and palm leaves—becomes the metaphor for believers: temporary, fragile vessels perched on God's unshakable foundation, called not to huddle inside but to shout His message from the rooftop.

2024-10-27

Beresheet: Being Reset For A New Beginning

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Grace is not a parking spot—receiving salvation is the starting line, not the finish; actively walk in the transformation grace provides rather than treating it as a one-time event.
  • The adversary operates as an 'influencer' whose tools are lying, deceiving, and accusing—recognizing those patterns in the culture and in my own impulses is the first step to choosing the right influencer.
  • Past failures are not meant to traumatize but to serve as markers of where God met me; learn from them and press forward toward the high calling rather than letting them anchor me in guilt.
One-Line Action
Identify one area where you have received grace but done nothing with it, and take a concrete step of obedience this week.
Memorable Quote
How do you frustrate grace? You try to do it yourself.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Marking the annual Torah cycle restart with Parashat Beresheet, the rabbi explores Genesis 1:1-2 as a divine 'reset'—God re-forming a world that had become void and desolate, drawing on Isaiah 45:18 to argue God never intended chaos. He introduces a 'two influencers' framework: God shaped humanity in His image, but the adversary corrupted that design like a virus in a computer, producing the lying, accusing, deceiving tendencies visible in human nature. He connects John 1's 'In the beginning was the Word' back to Genesis 1 to show God's unbroken desire to dwell among His people from creation through incarnation.

Citing 2 Corinthians 5:17, he presents Messiah as the mechanism of personal reset—making believers new creations with a ministry of reconciliation. The Hebrew word for violence in Genesis 6, 'Hamas,' becomes a striking bridge to modern events post-October 7, with movements worldwide now self-identifying with that ancient violence.

He warns against receiving grace passively, arguing from Galatians 2:21 that frustrating grace means either doing nothing with it or trying to accomplish God's work in your own strength. The sermon closes by urging the congregation to press forward through past failures, learning from them rather than being held captive by them, and to let God's reset produce active, outward-facing transformation.

Hook / Wordplay

God's 'reset button' in Genesis 1:2—what He did for a void cosmos (let there be light), He offers to do for every individual life through Messiah as new creation.

2024-11-03

A World Drowning in the Overwhelming

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When overwhelmed by emotion — anger, grief, loss — my capacity to reason and receive truth shrinks physiologically; recognize that state and anchor to Messiah before responding or deciding
  • Good intentions are not resolutions: examine whether my commitments are truly resolute or just well-meaning plans I'll abandon under pressure
  • Don't adopt the world's combative methods to fight corruption — conquer evil with good and let light displace darkness rather than matching aggression with aggression
One-Line Action
When you feel overwhelmed, pause and reconnect with God as your anchor before responding or acting.
Memorable Quote
God doesn't always attack in His discipline our weak points. Oftentimes He attacks what we think are our strong points.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi explores Parashat Noach not through the flood itself but through the spiritual state preceding it: a world already drowning in overwhelm and corruption before the waters ever rose. He notes that the Hebrew word for the violence filling the earth is Hamas, and that the text emphasizes all living beings had corrupted their ways — not one specific sin but a total corrosion of moral clarity. He draws on superhero narratives and Greek mythology to illustrate how even well-intentioned people are susceptible to corruption when power or emotion overwhelms them, and connects this to neuroscience showing the hippocampus shrinks during anger, cutting off rational thought.

He traces the pattern through the Tower of Babel, where human capability untethered from God produced destructive pride. Turning to Matthew 24, he highlights that people in Noah's day were simply living normal lives, oblivious to coming judgment — not necessarily doing spectacularly evil things but lacking spiritual awareness.

Through Romans 8, Romans 12, 1 John 5, and 1 Corinthians 10:13, he builds the case that Messiah Yeshua is the anchor that prevents believers from being swept away, and that God always provides a way of escape from temptation. The application is not to fight corruption with the world's methods but to let God's love cast out fear and His light dispel darkness.

Hook / Wordplay

The world was already drowning — in overwhelm, corruption, and moral blindness — long before the literal floodwaters rose. The flood was the consequence; the real catastrophe was the spiritual drowning that preceded it.

2024-11-10

Where Are We Going?

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When facing major life uncertainty, resist demanding all details upfront—Avram moved forward at 75 with only a promise, not a roadmap, and God removed obstacles in His own timing.
  • Identify what 'wealth' or self-made security you cling to that may block deeper trust; the Rich Young Ruler's obstacle was not sin but attachment to what he had built for himself.
  • Submit your natural skills and credentials to God's direction rather than relying on them independently—Paul's abilities became far more productive when surrendered to God than when deployed on his own terms.
One-Line Action
Release your grip on needing to know the full destination and trust God as both the way and the navigator.
Memorable Quote
In some ways it doesn't matter where we're going, as long as we're going with him, because he will bring us to the goal, even if we don't understand fully what that goal is.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon explores Parashat Lekh L'kha through the question every person faces: where are we going and how do we get there? Rabbi traces Avram's journey from age 75, called to leave everything familiar without knowing the destination, through decades of famine, family conflict, battles, and childlessness. He notes that Avram's only real security was God's promise, and that at the end of his life all he physically owned was a burial plot—yet God's covenant endured through generations.

Drawing from Isaiah 40, the sermon emphasizes that God never grows weary and renews strength for the exhausted and powerless. Yeshua's declaration 'I am the way, the truth, and the life' in John 14 is presented as the direct answer to both Thomas's confusion and our own life uncertainty. The Rich Young Ruler in Matthew 19 illustrates how personal securities—not just obvious sins—can block deeper trust in God's path.

Paul's testimony in Philippians 3 models counting worldly credentials as loss compared to knowing Messiah, pressing toward the goal without claiming to have arrived. The sermon concludes that knowing the destination matters far less than knowing the One who leads us there, urging the congregation to let God be their navigator rather than racing ahead alone.

Hook / Wordplay

'Where Are We Going?' echoes both Avram's uncharted departure and Thomas's confusion—until Yeshua answers: 'I am the way.' The destination is less important than the One who leads.

2024-11-24

The Secret Power of Gratitude

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Before any family or social gathering, decide in advance to be an encourager—prepare a posture of gratitude rather than reacting to provocations in the moment.
  • Practice showing up for people in difficulty even with nothing to say; presence and a hand to hold can minister more powerfully than words.
  • When feeling despondent over what is lacking, redirect attention to what is present—gratitude crowds out negativity by reframing focus.
One-Line Action
This week, approach each gathering with a pre-decided posture of encouragement and express specific gratitude to at least one person who wouldn't expect it.
Memorable Quote
We often say that people will be willing to take a bullet for you, but they won't be inconvenienced.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi acknowledges the Torah portion Hayyei Sarah but pivots to a timely pre-Thanksgiving message on the 'secret power' of gratitude. He contrasts gratitude with the criticism and division running rampant—noting families refusing to gather over election disagreements—and argues the gift we have is one another. He briefly connects to the parasha through Abraham's hospitality to strangers, Sodom's lack of gratitude, and the servant's undistracted faithfulness in finding a wife for Isaac.

Working through Psalm 100, Psalm 118, and Philippians 4, he highlights Paul's 'secret' of contentment whether in abundance or need. He illustrates with the story of Nightbird (Jane Marczewski) from America's Got Talent, who while facing terminal cancer said you can't wait until life isn't hard anymore to be happy—which he reframes as 'to be grateful.' He draws parallels between Sukkot and Thanksgiving including outdoor feasts, hospitality to outsiders, and the cornucopia resembling a shofar. From Colossians 3 and 1 Thessalonians 5, he urges the congregation to clothe themselves with compassion, forgive freely, and give thanks in everything.

He emphasizes that visiting someone in difficulty—even in silence—carries real spiritual power. The practical charge: go into Thanksgiving prepared to encourage, embrace, and love rather than gloat, attack, or despair.

Hook / Wordplay

Gratitude is called a 'secret power' because people don't recognize what it does—it transforms the giver, not just the recipient, making negativity hard to sustain.

2024-12-01

Tol'dot Thanksgiving Leftovers

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Examine what I 'serve' people through my words—am I offering moldy leftovers (criticism, bitterness) or fresh abundance from what God has given me?
  • Don't cut people off over disagreements; very few people truly know us, so treat every relationship as iron sharpening iron rather than squandering it.
  • Let Thanksgiving gratitude produce leftovers that don't spoil—carry encouragement, generosity, and reconciliation forward well beyond one holiday meal.
One-Line Action
This week, deliberately give someone an encouraging word or act of generosity—serve them your best leftovers, not your scraps.
Memorable Quote
The thankfulness should carry through more than just the evening together. It should carry forward.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Continuing the Thanksgiving theme from the prior week, the rabbi reframes the holiday around the question 'What leftovers do you serve?' He opens with the Tol'dot portion, noting that Isaac and Esau's family strife might have kept them from each other's table, yet God used even that dysfunction in His larger plan. He turns to Malachi where the Levites brought lame and sick animals as offerings—essentially giving God their worst leftovers—and contrasts that with Yeshua feeding 5,000 from five loaves and two fish, producing twelve baskets of blessed leftovers. He parallels this with the widow's single flask of oil in 2 Kings 4 that filled every container in the house.

Drawing from James 3, he warns that the tongue sends both fresh and bitter water from the same opening, and from Matthew 15 that what defiles a person is not food but what comes out of the heart. He extends the metaphor: our real leftovers are the words, encouragement, and spiritual abundance we carry forward beyond one holiday meal—leftovers that don't spoil but keep expanding. He closes by connecting Messiah's resurrection on the third day as the ultimate expansion of what seemed finished, urging the congregation to distribute God's abundance generously to others throughout the year.

Hook / Wordplay

'What leftovers do you serve?'—pivoting from Thanksgiving turkey leftovers to the spiritual leftovers we give others: are they moldy and lame, or abundant and life-giving?

2024-12-08

Vayetze: The Leap From Impossible to Possible

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Focus on knowing God more deeply rather than trying to manufacture faith separately; trust grows organically from intimacy with Him, as the Roy story illustrates.
  • When facing an impossible situation, review God's track record of prior deliverances—Jacob had Bethel's promise to hold onto through every Laban scheme—and let that history anchor present confidence.
  • Fear is what causes sinking, not the impossibility itself; like Peter, recognize Yeshua's voice first, step out, and keep eyes fixed on Him rather than the wind.
One-Line Action
This week, identify one situation that feels impossible and deliberately recall a past instance where God came through, letting that memory fuel present trust.
Memorable Quote
From Devarim due to Rani Mi 6, the cornerstone of our faith.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The rabbi traces Jacob's departure from Beer Sheva toward Aram, framing his entire journey as a chain of impossible circumstances that God turned into possibilities. He rehabilitates Jacob's reputation, arguing that Jacob was consistently taken advantage of—by Laban's wage changes, the wedding-night switch of Leah for Rachel, and the hostility of Laban's sons—rather than being the conniver tradition paints him as. God was the one who directed Jacob's livestock strategy and protected his wealth despite Laban's manipulations.

The sermon connects this pattern to Hosea's call for Israel to return from guilt, then pivots to Nathanael's skepticism ('Can anything good come from Nazareth?') as another impossible-to-possible moment. Peter walking on water illustrates that fear—not the impossibility itself—causes sinking, and that recognizing Yeshua's voice is what empowers stepping out.

The rabbi brings in Mary's impossible calling, the rich young ruler's attachment to wealth as a self-imposed impossibility, and Paul's willingness to count his impressive résumé as loss. He closes with a personal story from Bible school: his teacher Roy prayed for more faith, and God answered, 'When you know me better, you'll trust me more'—reframing the entire sermon's thesis as relationship-driven trust rather than willpower-driven belief.

Hook / Wordplay

Jacob's life is a spiritual obstacle course: each time one impossible situation resolves, a new one appears—Laban behind him, Esau ahead—but God uses the pattern to build cumulative trust, turning serial impossibilities into a track record of faithfulness.
