2024-12-15

Staying Until Now!

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When waiting feels unbearable, recognize it as God's active preparation season—He is 'working in us to will and to do of His good pleasure' even when nothing visible is happening.
  • Resist the Reuben pattern: forcing a premature outcome to establish yourself doesn't accelerate God's plan, it disqualifies you from the very thing you were reaching for.
  • Be honest with God about fear and anxiety the way Jacob was—transparent prayer before God is the antidote to ruminating over worst-case scenarios for years.
One-Line Action
Identify where you are trying to force your own 'now' and deliberately release it to God's timing this week.
Memorable Quote
Sometimes when we try to advance now early, we end up nullifying all the benefits that are set in order to be a part of who we are.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon explores Jacob's statement 'I have stayed until now' (Genesis 32:5) as a lens for understanding God's appointed timing. After 21 years with Laban, Jacob finally uses his 'return ticket' but fears Esau based on their last encounter—illustrating how our most recent reference point with someone shapes our expectations. The rabbi emphasizes that Jacob led his family courageously, passing ahead of them to face Esau, and was met with an unexpected embrace rather than violence.

Rachel's theft of household idols and Reuben's later power grab with Bilhah are presented as cautionary examples of trying to force one's own 'now' before God's appointed time—both ending in loss. Drawing from 1 Corinthians 5, the sermon warns that a little leaven of compromise or premature action can corrupt the whole batch. The rabbi shares his personal calling story: sensing ministry at seven years in the Lord but not entering full-time service until roughly twenty years later, after a miscommunication cost him an earlier opportunity.

Through Hebrews 11:1, 2 Corinthians 6:2, Mark 4:39-40, and Yeshua's repeated use of 'now' in John 17, the sermon builds the case that God orchestrates appointed 'now moments' we cannot manufacture ourselves. The central challenge is to remain faithful during the unseen preparation period, trusting that God is working behind the scenes to bring about His purposes at the right time.

Hook / Wordplay

Jacob's 'I have stayed until now' becomes a meditation on divine timing—'now' is God's appointed moment, not ours to manufacture, and every premature grab at it nullifies the blessing.

2024-12-22

Vayeshev: From Pit to Palace

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Don't let jealous or critical voices kill a God-given vision; the mundane and even painful details of life are preparation God uses to cultivate what He wants to accomplish through us.
  • Resist settling into difficult seasons—don't 'decorate the pit' by making hardship comfortable; recognize trials as temporary transit, not permanent residence, and keep pressing forward.
  • Root of bitterness warps perception of reality: Joseph's brothers misread his grace even years after reconciliation because they projected their own motives onto him—guard against letting unresolved bitterness contaminate how you see others' intentions.
One-Line Action
Identify one 'pit' you've been settling into and take a concrete step forward this week instead of making it comfortable.
Memorable Quote
Don't start decorating your pit. Plan to move from there, plan to move forward, plan to walk in union with him and see God bring about a transition.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon explores Torah portion Vayeshev (Genesis 37:1) alongside Christmas-season parallels between Joseph and Yeshua, framed as 'from pit to palace, from manger to king.' Rabbi notes that the history of Jacob immediately pivots to Joseph's story, illustrating how our legacy lives through what we pass forward. Joseph's journey through pit, Potiphar, proposition, prison, predictions, and palace demonstrates that God uses every difficult stage as preparation, not final destination. Drawing from Amos 2, the sermon warns against letting others compromise our calling, just as Nazarites were given wine and prophets were told not to prophesy.

A striking counterfactual argues that had the cupbearer remembered Joseph immediately, he would have returned home and his family would have perished in the famine—God's delay served a greater redemptive purpose. Hebrews 12 and 5 anchor the Yeshua parallel, showing Messiah endured suffering for the joy set before him and learned obedience through what he suffered.

Through James 1, 1 Peter, and 2 Peter, the rabbi builds the case that trials refine faith like fire tests gold, and that God has already provided everything needed for life and godliness. The sermon closes with John 16's assurance that we are never truly alone and a memorable exhortation not to decorate our pit but to keep pressing toward God's purpose.

Hook / Wordplay

Pit to palace / manger to king—alliterative parallel linking Joseph's arc to Yeshua's, with the 'pit stops are pathways to destiny' wordplay and the closing image of 'don't decorate your pit.'

2024-12-29

Mikketz: Daring to Dream

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • God equips us through mundane hardships we don't recognize as training — Joseph learned large-scale governance by managing a household and a prison, skills he never would have sought on his own.
  • When visions finally come true they look nothing like we imagined — Joseph expected to lead his family, not the known world — so stop trying to pre-interpret God's plan and focus on faithfulness in the current season.
  • When overwhelmed like being caught in an undercurrent, the counter-intuitive move is to stop fighting and trust God's process to carry you through to the surface; resistance exhausts, surrender restores.
One-Line Action
Identify the 'mundane hardship' you are currently resenting and reframe it as God's hidden training ground for something you cannot yet fathom.
Memorable Quote
The secret to getting out of an undercurrent like that is not to fight against the undercurrent. It's to rest and relax and let the undercurrent carry you through to the surface once again.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The rabbi opens the Torah portion Mikketz at Genesis 41:1, highlighting the two-year gap between Joseph's accurate dream interpretation for the cupbearer and Pharaoh's summoning him — a painful delay when Joseph expected swift deliverance. He traces how God used those years and the preceding hardships (Potiphar's house, false accusation, prison leadership) to equip Joseph with skills he would need to govern Egypt, though Joseph had no idea he was being trained. Joseph named his sons Manasseh ('causing to forget' his family's dysfunction) and Ephraim ('fruitful in the land of my misfortune'), marking a transformed identity.

The rabbi then parallels this with the Maccabees, who emerged from caves of despair when incensed by the desecration of the temple altar, their boldness igniting courage across a despondent nation. He explains that Hanukkah connects to Sukkot — the festival they were forbidden to celebrate — and that the Haftarah portions themselves originated from Maccabean-era resistance to Torah-reading bans.

Drawing on Psalm 46, Isaiah 40, Romans 8, and 1 John 4, he urges the congregation to dare to dream according to God's purpose rather than their own, since fulfilled visions always look different than imagined. The central call is to stop fighting God's process like an undercurrent and instead trust Him to bring us to the surface, becoming sparks that rekindle hope in others.

Hook / Wordplay

Joseph literally interpreted dreams while imprisoned, yet the deeper dare was holding onto God's dream for his life through 20 years of misfortune — the dreamer had to learn to let God interpret the dream for him.

Identity and Calling

2025-01-05

Vayigash: Integrity & Communication — Keys to Success

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Communication isn't complete until the other person understands—check for comprehension, not just delivery.
  • When circumstances look bleak, remember that God may be working something in the background that neither you nor the other parties can yet see, just as Joseph and his brothers couldn't.
  • Doing good is the best answer to critics: 'It is God's will that your doing good should silence the ignorant talk of foolish people' (1 Peter 2:15).
One-Line Action
This week, in one difficult conversation, slow down to confirm the other person actually understood what you meant—don't just say it and move on.
Memorable Quote
If they don't understand what you said, you didn't communicate anything of value. You didn't communicate if they did not receive and understand what you were saying.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi teaches from Parashat Vayigash, focusing on how Judah's transformed heart and honest communication moved Joseph to reveal his identity to his brothers. He defines integrity through its etymology—from 'integer,' meaning a whole number—framing it as completeness, transparency, and honesty. He stresses that true communication requires the listener to actually understand, not merely that the speaker spoke.

The 22-year gap between Joseph's enslavement and this reunion illustrates how people change in ways we cannot see, and how God works in the background to preserve life. Judah's willingness to become a slave in Benjamin's place—a radical self-sacrifice from the very brother who once instigated Joseph's sale—broke Joseph open emotionally. Drawing from Joshua 1, the rabbi connects success to immersion in God's Word and awareness of His presence.

He weaves in New Covenant texts from Colossians, Romans, and 1 John to show that walking in union with Messiah, renewing our minds, and loving one another are the practical outworkings of integrity. From 1 Peter he teaches that doing good silences the ignorant talk of foolish people, reframing success as the best answer to critics. He closes with Proverbs 22:1—a good name is more desirable than great riches—urging the congregation not to sell themselves short of all God wants them to experience.

Hook / Wordplay

Integrity comes from the same root as 'integer'—a whole number. Walking in integrity means being a complete, undivided person who communicates from a place of wholeness and transparency.

Prayer and God's Presence

2025-01-12

Vayechi — When God's Good Touches Human Evil

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When I assume the worst about someone's intentions, check whether I'm projecting my own old-nature revenge-thinking onto them the way Joseph's brothers did—they fabricated a message from Jacob because they couldn't imagine genuine forgiveness.
  • Evaluate whether my actions are self-indulgent (old nature) or outward-dispensing (Spirit): love, patience, and kindness are things I give to others, not feelings I hoard for myself.
  • In difficult seasons, deliberately look for where God's finger is touching my life rather than ruminating on the problems—Jacob blessed his grandsons by recalling 'the angel who rescued me from all harm,' not by cataloging his sufferings.
One-Line Action
When negative assumptions about others' motives arise, pause and ask whether you are filtering through your old nature or through the mind of Messiah.
Memorable Quote
And it says, Yakuof lived in the land of Egypt 17 years.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon covers Parashat Vayechi, noting a striking symmetry: Joseph was 17 when sold into slavery, and Jacob lived exactly 17 years in Egypt before his death. Jacob blesses his grandsons Ephraim and Manasseh, crosses his hands to favor the younger, and delivers prophetic blessings over each son, including the messianic scepter prophecy through Judah. The heart of the sermon is Genesis 50:20—after Jacob's funeral, the brothers assume Joseph will now take revenge, projecting their own old-nature thinking onto him.

Joseph weeps not from conviction but because he had already settled the matter 17 years earlier; the brothers spent those years torturing themselves with guilt they could not release. The rabbi draws in Isaiah 54 to show God's pattern of temporary anger and everlasting grace, then connects Matthew 21's parable of the tenants to illustrate how evil distorts perception of inheritance.

Galatians 5 provides the climactic framework: old-nature works are self-indulgent while the fruit of the Spirit is dispensed outward to others. The call is to let God's word transform our thinking from the inside out so that His goodness can touch and change the places where human evil has taken hold.

Hook / Wordplay

Joseph's brothers spent the first 17 years torturing Joseph; they spent the last 17 years of Jacob's life torturing themselves—because God made a way for them and they still couldn't take hold of it.

2025-01-19

Shemot: From Known to Unknown to I Am Known

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Getting to know God is a gradual, non-linear process—Moses went from reluctant stammerer to great deliverer over time, so be patient with your own faith development and trust God is bringing you along step by step.
  • God's promise 'I am with you' is the ultimate credential when facing daunting tasks; the key isn't having all the answers upfront but trusting that His presence supplies everything needed.
  • Identity and purpose are discovered through relationship, not self-effort—Psalm 139 and Jeremiah 1 show God knew us before we were born, so being known by God is the foundation from which we come to know ourselves and Him.
One-Line Action
This week, when facing a task that feels beyond your ability, pause and recall that 'I am with you' is God's primary credential—and act from that assurance rather than waiting for certainty.
Memorable Quote
God with us is a majority. God with us is the one who makes all of these things possible.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon explores Parashat Shemot through the thematic lens of 'From Known to Unknown to I Am Known,' tracing a progression of knowing and being known. Joseph was honored and known in Egypt, but a new king arose who didn't know him, and Israel's freedoms were slowly stripped until they lived in a slave mentality. Moses went from hidden infant to palace dweller to fugitive to encountering God at the burning bush, where God's repeated promise 'I am with you' served as the ultimate sign and credential.

The rabbi emphasizes that Moses lacked confidence and called himself a terrible speaker, yet God equipped him step by step, and as Moses got to know God better, his faith grew. Jeremiah 1 and Psalm 139 demonstrate that God's knowledge of us precedes our existence—He knew us before we were formed in the womb. In Matthew 16, Peter confesses Yeshua as Messiah but then rebukes Him, illustrating the non-linear journey of truly knowing God.

Galatians 4:8-9 and 1 Corinthians 8 drive home that being known by God matters more than our own knowledge. Acts 3 shows Peter's transformation from self-focused disciple to one who deflects glory entirely to Yeshua after healing the lame man. The sermon concludes with Hebrews 11's portrait of Moses persevering 'as one who sees the unseen' and Isaiah 40's promise that those who hope in Hashem will renew their strength, arguing that knowing God provides all the empowerment we need.

Hook / Wordplay

'From Known to Unknown to I Am Known'—the word 'known' threads through the entire narrative while 'I Am Known' doubles as personal identity and echo of God's name 'I AM,' so that knowing the Great I AM is what makes us truly known.

2025-01-26

V'era: Negotiated Victory Over Discouragement

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When feeling inadequate for something God has placed before you, remember Moses protested he couldn't speak — yet he, not Aaron, ended up making every demand to Pharaoh; God equipped him in the doing, not before it.
  • Don't negotiate with God for a lesser calling by asking 'what's the least I have to do to get by' — that mindset forfeits the impossible things God specializes in doing through willing vessels.
  • Train yourself to reframe hardships as Paul did: 'light momentary afflictions' compared to the eternal weight of glory — the reframe doesn't deny the pain but shifts focus from the temporary to the eternal.
One-Line Action
When discouragement hits, speak God's promises aloud rather than retreating into silence — Moses found his voice by using it, not by waiting until he felt ready.
Memorable Quote
God wants to negotiate with us a victory that we can't even see when we're in that dark place. And God wants to bring us to the place of light, to the place of illumination, to the place of understanding that God's purpose and plan is far bigger than what our inadequacies could do.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The rabbi frames the first seven plagues of Egypt as a multi-level negotiation — between Moses and Pharaoh, Moses and God, and the people and Moses — showing how God moved a deeply discouraged man toward boldness. Moses twice protested he was a poor speaker and the people refused to listen, yet as each plague unfolded, Moses' courage visibly grew until he was the one making direct demands of Pharaoh. A key exegetical insight is that Egypt's magicians could only replicate problems (more blood, more frogs) but never solve them, highlighting that matching a demonstration of power is not the same as providing a solution.

Pharaoh's own negotiations shifted from flat refusal to conditional concessions to a confession of sin, yet he hardened again each time relief came. The rabbi draws a parallel to Joshua's insecurity before entering the Promised Land, noting that God's command 'be strong, be bold' implies Joshua was feeling the opposite.

Moving into the New Testament, he connects the pattern to Yeshua's teaching that trouble will come but He has conquered the world, Paul's reframing of afflictions as light and momentary compared to eternal glory, and the declaration in Romans 8 that nothing can separate believers from God's love. He closes with Isaiah 54's promise that no weapon formed against God's servants will prevail, urging the congregation that discouragement may be visited but is never meant to be where we live.

Hook / Wordplay

Pharaoh claimed 'my Nile is mine, I made it for myself' — but he was in denial, not in the Nile. The plagues were God's counter-negotiation, and Egypt's magicians could only replicate problems, never solve them.

2025-02-02

When We Ask, 'What Is This?'

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When facing confusing circumstances, consciously redirect the question from peers to God in prayer—He promises to give wisdom generously without holding back (James 1:5).
  • Don't walk away from hard teachings like the talmidim in John 6; stay and press in like Peter, trusting that God's answer will come even when it isn't immediately clear.
  • Be ready to answer when someone asks 'what is this?'—have the story of God's deliverance prepared to pass on to the next generation, not as rote recitation but with conviction.
One-Line Action
This week, bring one specific confusing circumstance directly to God in prayer instead of polling friends for opinions, and wait in trust for His answer.
Memorable Quote
No matter how many people you ask about circumstances in your life, there's only one who will give you the right answer and that is going to God with it.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon draws its title from Exodus 13:14, where a son asks 'Mah zot—what is this?' about Passover, and extends that question into every confusing circumstance of life. Rabbi traces the last three plagues in Parashat Bo, highlighting Pharaoh's escalating concessions—first only men may go, then children too, but never the livestock—showing how pride and hardheartedness kept him from full submission even as he repeatedly acknowledged God's power. Jeremiah 33:3 frames God's invitation to reveal 'great and hidden things of which you are unaware,' reframing our confusion as an opening for divine revelation.

In John 16, Yeshua's disciples asked each other 'what is this little while?' instead of asking Him directly, illustrating the futility of seeking answers from equally confused peers. John 6 provides a stark contrast: many talmidim walked away when the teaching was hard, but Peter declared 'Lord, to whom will we go? You have the words of eternal life.' James 1:5-8 anchors the practical application—ask God who gives wisdom generously, but ask in faith without doubting, or be like a wave tossed by wind.

The sermon connects Exodus 13:14 to Acts 2:37, where Jewish men at Shavuot asked 'Brothers, what should we do?'—the same sons-asking-fathers pattern fulfilled in the temple. A humorous analogy about asking three New Yorkers for directions drove home that only God gives reliable guidance. The overarching call is to bring every 'what is this?' question to God rather than leaning on human opinion, trusting that His promises all find their 'yes' in connection with Messiah.

Hook / Wordplay

'Mah zot'—the Passover question a child asks at the seder table—becomes the question every believer should bring to God when life's circumstances make no sense.

2025-02-09

Will We Ever Be Battle-Free?

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When fear makes a battle look insurmountable, rehearse past victories God has already delivered—like David encouraging himself in the Lord—to restore perspective before reacting.
  • Never assume a previous strategy applies to a new challenge; David inquired of the Lord each time, and the second battle required a completely different approach (waiting for the sound in the mulberry trees).
  • Pursue love rather than pursuing enemies or vindication—1 Corinthians 14:1 follows the love chapter because love accomplishes what anger and retaliation never can.
One-Line Action
This week, when a new battle or frustration rises, pause to name one past victory God brought you through before deciding how to respond.
Memorable Quote
The fear of the unknown is what makes the battle look more intense than it really is. It's a stepping stone to seeing God bring victory and equip us for the next challenge that comes along.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The rabbi opens from the Torah portion B'Shallach, noting that even after Pharaoh released Israel, God routed them away from the direct path because seeing war might cause them to retreat to Egypt—raising the question of whether believers can ever escape battles. He traces Israel's pattern of miraculous deliverance followed immediately by complaint and fear, showing that each victory is not a final destination but a stepping stone God uses to equip us for the next challenge. He draws on David's example of encouraging himself in the Lord when his own men turned against him, and David's discipline of inquiring of God before every battle rather than assuming a previous strategy would work again.

From Judges, the story of Deborah, Barak, and Yael illustrates that God raises unexpected people—including a woman with a tent peg—to accomplish decisive victories when conventional leaders hesitate. In John 6, the rabbi shows how talmidim who couldn't endure a hard teaching effectively deserted the battle, while Peter's response—'Lord, to whom would we go?'—models faithfulness amid confusion. Paul's teaching in 2 Corinthians 10 and Ephesians 6 reframes the battle as spiritual, fought with truth, faith, and the word of God rather than worldly weapons.

The sermon resolves the title question with Revelation 21:3-4—yes, we will be battle-free in eternity when God wipes away every tear—but until then, God is in the battle with us and has already secured the victory. He closes with 1 Corinthians 13-14, urging believers to pursue love as the greatest weapon and the enduring reality beyond partial knowledge.

Hook / Wordplay

The title asks 'Will we ever be battle-free?' and the sermon lands on a paradox: we are already battle-free because God in the battle with us has already won, yet the battles themselves are the stepping stones He uses to grow us toward eternity.

2025-02-16

Yitro: Creating Solutions Instead of Chaos

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When conflict stirs, ask 'why am I reacting this way?' before responding — the person triggering you may love you and mean no harm, and the real issue is your own emotional wiring.
  • Let God serve as the neutral mediator for your inner conflicts: come to Him honestly about what is blocking you rather than looking for someone to blame.
  • Maintain daily 'altar moments' of self-examination before God — a one-time decision is not enough; ongoing surrender is what keeps the dominoes from falling.
One-Line Action
Next time conflict rises in you, pause and ask 'Lord, what is wrong with me?' before assigning blame — let God mediate the chaos inside first.
Memorable Quote
We need to look and say, why am I reacting the way I am? Especially if I think through the fact that the person who may be causing this problem loves me and would never want to hurt me.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon opens with Jethro observing Moses settling disputes from morning to evening and advising delegation — a practical conflict-resolution model that prevents burnout. Rabbi connects this to James 4, arguing that quarrels and chaos stem from desires and emotions battling inside us rather than from external circumstances alone. Using the domino effect as a central metaphor, he illustrates how unchecked inner conflict cascades through relationships and communities unless God's hand intervenes to stop the chain.

He traces this pattern through Jacob wrestling with God (really wrestling with himself), the Israelites making the golden calf despite knowing better, Isaiah's commission to a hard-hearted nation, and Stephen's stoning by a mob acting in unified chaos. A personal anecdote about nearly arguing with his wife over taking out the trash illustrates how small triggers reveal deeper emotional wiring — and how asking 'why am I reacting this way?' defuses conflict. He examines the rich young ruler and Paul's internal struggle in Romans 7 as examples of people whose inner conflicts blocked spiritual progress despite outward compliance.

The Holy Spirit is presented as a 'neutral third-party mediator' who helps us reach honest self-examination rather than blame-shifting. He concludes that God finds us in our chaos but refuses to leave us there, offering Messiah Yeshua as the ultimate solution — the one who carries everything on his shoulders.

Hook / Wordplay

The domino effect: everything falls unless God's finger stops the chain — creating solutions instead of chaos.

2025-02-23

Mishpatim: God's Rules For Proper Balance

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • God's rules function as guardrails protecting community health, not arbitrary restrictions — when I chafe against structure, I should ask whether I'm building or quietly eroding the foundation around me.
  • The Sinai-to-golden-calf and Jeremiah 34 pattern warns that following through on commitment matters far more than the initial declaration; I need to check where I've said 'whatever God says I'll do' and then quietly reversed course.
  • Before sharing information — even true information — apply the lashon hara test: am I speaking to protect others, or to elevate myself above the person I'm talking about?
One-Line Action
This week, before reacting to any frustrating situation, pause and ask whether your response follows God's guardrails or your own improvised rules.
Memorable Quote
Well okay you're a rebel great but the point is that rules give you structure they give you guardrails they give you boundaries to know where your strength or where there is an issue that's happening so that we can be aware of the need to correct and move forward and be productive in what it is that we are called to do.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The Torah portion Mishpatim lays out rulings that served as God's framework for community health, not arbitrary punishment. The slavery laws functioned as a welfare system to restore people to their feet, and 'eye for an eye' set limits on retaliation in an era when a stolen loaf could cost a hand. Rabbi warned against following the crowd to do evil, citing the Holocaust as a chilling example of normal people just following orders.

The Haftarah from Jeremiah 34 showed the pattern of Israel committing to free their slaves, then reversing course and re-enslaving them, bringing judgment upon themselves. A recurring theme emerged: throughout history the oppressed become the oppressor, but Israel was commanded to remember their own bondage precisely to break that cycle. Yeshua expanded the Torah's principles by going to the heart — love your enemies, carry the pack two miles, don't just love those who love you.

The sermon connected Paul's bold stand before the Sanhedrin to the same tension between God's rulings and man-made regulations. Hebrews 9–10 was brought in to show that Messiah entered the true heavenly holy place, not a man-made copy, and that we must hold fast to our acknowledged hope without wavering while spurring one another toward love and good deeds.

Hook / Wordplay

God's rules are not the establishment to rebel against — they are the guardrails that keep the balanced stones from toppling, and even a small wave of self-will can throw everything off.

2025-03-02

T'rumah: Better Than The Original

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When life's challenges feel like a chisel, recognize God is sculpting a masterpiece—the discomfort is purposeful refinement stripping away dross, not pointless suffering.
  • Resist the 'desktop publishing mindset' in spiritual life: settling for self-made 'good enough' spirituality forfeits the richness of the Master's touch.
  • The goal is not imitation of God but union with God—Ephesians 2:10 says we are created in union with Messiah, meaning we become part of the original rather than remaining a copy.
One-Line Action
This week, identify one area where you are settling for a self-made 'good enough' copy and consciously yield it to God's refining hand.
Memorable Quote
We become one with that experience of his presence and so we're better than the original because we're now a part of the original because he is made this door of opportunity open to us as we yield to him.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The Torah portion Terumah (Exodus 25) records God instructing Moses to build a tabernacle according to the heavenly pattern—a copy of the original in the heavenlies. Rabbi uses the sculptor's chisel as a metaphor: God sees a masterpiece inside each person and chips away the excess through life's challenges. He traces the copy-to-original theme through 1 Kings, where Solomon and Hiram's alliance rapidly built the temple because of David's prior preparation—a picture of how God's sequential work in our lives builds toward a purpose.

Hebrews 8–10 shows Messiah entering the true holy place, not the man-made copy, and establishing a new covenant written on hearts rather than stone. Drawing from his graphic design career, the rabbi illustrates how desktop publishing tempted people to accept 'good enough' self-made work instead of the master's professional touch—a parallel to spiritual self-sufficiency. Romans 1 warns that exchanging God's glory for self-made images leads to darkened thinking and moral decline.

The theological climax lands on Ephesians 2:10: we are God's masterpiece, created in union with Messiah Yeshua for good actions already prepared for us. Through Messiah's sacrifice we move from poor facsimiles to participants in the original itself—better than the heavenly copy because we become one with the Master.

Hook / Wordplay

The tabernacle was a copy of heaven, but through Messiah we don't get a better copy—we become part of the original itself, making us 'better than the original.'

2025-03-09

The Source of Dignity & Splendor

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • True humility is not self-deprecation or humiliation but the natural response to encountering God's greatness—and then marveling that He still wants to be near us.
  • If my sense of dignity depends on how people perceive me I will always be unstable; anchoring it in God's commitment to me creates an unshakable foundation.
  • Philippians 4:13 is not a blank check for material desires but a declaration that whether in abundance or need, union with Messiah supplies the inner dignity to endure any circumstance.
One-Line Action
Stop sourcing your sense of dignity from how others see you and begin drawing it daily from time spent in God's presence.
Memorable Quote
What humbles us more than anything else is seeing the greatness of God.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon explores Parashat Tetzaveh and the priestly garments God commanded for Aaron and his sons, emphasizing that these garments were designed to express God's dignity and splendor rather than elevating the priests themselves. Rabbi draws a critical distinction between humility and humiliation, arguing that true humility comes not from being beaten down but from encountering the overwhelming greatness of God and realizing He still desires nearness with us. He traces this theme through Job's humbling before God's direct questioning, Hannah's declaration that her dignity was restored by Hashem, and the Proverbs 31 woman clothed in strength and dignity.

Moving into the New Testament, he connects the priestly garments to believers being clothed with Messiah in Galatians and seated in heavenly places in Ephesians, establishing that our dignity flows from union with Yeshua. He uses Yeshua's teaching about lilies and birds in Matthew 6 to show that God's splendor outstrips even Solomon's glory.

The sermon culminates with the story of Madame Guyon, a wealthy woman imprisoned for her faith, who wrote that her prison walls shone with God's glory, illustrating how authentic dignity transforms any atmosphere. The closing exhortation distinguishes between being regal and being pompous, calling listeners to yield to God as the true source of all dignity and splendor.

Hook / Wordplay

"It doesn't come in a package, it's not like Splenda… it's not our own Splenda, it's God's Splendor being made manifest through us because He loves us."

2025-03-16

Singled Out For Service

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • God orchestrates timing in ways we cannot manufacture — Haman's arrival coinciding with the king reading about Mordechai's loyalty shows that trusting God's behind-the-scenes work matters more than trying to control outcomes.
  • Consuming hatred blinds and self-destructs: Haman had everything — wealth, power, royal favor — yet his obsession with one man's refusal to bow unraveled it all, a warning against letting grievance define us.
  • Like Esther, we may occupy our current position 'for such a time as this' — the calling to stand up carries real cost but God backs those He singles out with resources beyond what we can see.
One-Line Action
Identify the specific stand God is asking you to take right now and step into it, trusting His orchestration behind the scenes.
Memorable Quote
And I should mention too that this is King Asheras before he found out that the Queen Esther was Jewish.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon threads the Ki Tissa Torah portion with the Purim story under the theme 'Singled Out for Service.' Beginning with Exodus 31 where God singles out Bezalel and fills him with wisdom for crafting the Tabernacle, the rabbi establishes that God sets specific people apart for specific tasks. He briefly traces Elijah's dramatic victory on Mount Carmel followed by his collapse into depression, showing how quickly one can revert after experiencing God's power. The bulk of the message walks through the Book of Esther, detailing Haman's elevation, his obsessive hatred of Mordechai, and the escalation to a decree of genocide against the Jews.

The rabbi coins 'MDS — Mordechai Derangement Syndrome' to describe how consuming hatred blinds a person to reality and leads to self-destruction. He highlights God's meticulous orchestration of timing — Haman arriving to request Mordechai's execution at the exact moment the king discovers Mordechai saved his life.

Drawing modern parallels to campus anti-Semitism and political double standards, he argues the same ancient spirit persists today. He then connects to Hebrews 11's cloud of witnesses who were singled out and stood firm regardless of outcome, and closes with Colossians on the secret of Messiah united with believers and 1 Peter 2 on being living stones set apart as priests.

Hook / Wordplay

MDS — Mordechai Derangement Syndrome: when your entire focus is hatred of another person, you lose sight of reality and destroy yourself.

2025-03-23

Cleansed From Uncleanness

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Examine areas of spiritual 'scent deafness'—sins or attitudes I've grown so accustomed to that I no longer notice them—and bring them honestly before God rather than assuming I'm clean enough.
  • Treat the weeks leading into Passover as an intentional season of inner purification, not just physical chametz removal, by spending daily time in Scripture and letting God's Word identify what needs pruning.
  • Stop trying to change others first; focus on letting God do the heart transplant in me—replacing stony, resistant areas with a soft heart that can bear fruit and then help set others free.
One-Line Action
Identify one area of spiritual 'scent deafness' this week and bring it to God for cleansing before Passover preparations begin.
Memorable Quote
Some captives don't know they're captive. They've gotten so used to all of the bondage that they think it's normal.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

On Shabbat Parah, the rabbi taught on being cleansed from uncleanness, connecting the Torah portion Vayakhel with the special reading from Numbers 19 about the red heifer and Ezekiel 36's promise of spiritual renewal. He contrasted last week's golden calf (idolatry) with this week's red heifer (purification), noting that the Hebrew word 'parah' means both cow and to be fruitful—linking purification directly to bearing fruit. Using ongoing renovations at both the synagogue and his own home as a sustained metaphor, he illustrated how dust and sin accumulate unnoticed, comparing spiritual blindness to 'scent deafness' where we grow accustomed to impurity.

He walked through Ezekiel 36:25-28 on God's promise to sprinkle clean water, give a new heart of flesh, and put His spirit within us. He connected this to John 15 where Yeshua says 'you are clean through the word I have spoken' and to 1 John 1's assurance that walking in light brings purification through Messiah's blood. He clarified that Peter's vision in Acts 10 was about accepting non-Jewish people into God's family rather than changing dietary laws.

Throughout, he used vivid everyday illustrations—dialysis hand-washing protocols, restaurant mint jars, COVID-era cleaning, and the two-second rule for dropped food—to show how we take shortcuts with spiritual cleanliness. He urged the congregation to honestly acknowledge uncleanness and let God's Word and Spirit do the deep cleansing that produces genuine fruitfulness ahead of Passover.

Hook / Wordplay

The Hebrew word 'parah' means both cow (the red heifer of purification) and to be fruitful—so the very instrument of cleansing points to the fruitfulness God wants to produce in us.

2025-03-30

Pekudei: The Glory When Things Seem Cloudy

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Do a spiritual inventory like the Tabernacle accounting—take stock of what God has placed in you, then submit it to Him rather than trying to figure out what to do with it on your own.
  • When walking through a foggy, unclear season, the remedy is not more self-effort but entering God's presence through His Word and Spirit, which brings clarity of purpose.
  • Stop feeding exaggerated ideas of self-importance through endless consumption of trivial content; let God's glory, not personal accomplishment, be the motivating factor in daily life.
One-Line Action
This week, take inventory of what God has placed in you and surrender the plan for it back to Him.
Memorable Quote
And what's beautiful about it is whatever is clouding our thoughts, whatever is compromising our walk, when we enter into that glory cloud of God and the manifest presence of his glory, we have clear revision to be able to see what it is that God is calling us to do.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The Torah portion Pekudei details the accounting of every material used to build the Tabernacle—gold, silver, fabrics—earning its nickname as the accountants' portion. Once everything was assembled exactly as God commanded, the cloud of His glory filled the Tabernacle so powerfully that Moses himself could not enter. The same manifestation occurred when Solomon completed the Temple, yet Solomon humbly acknowledged that even the heavens of heavens cannot contain God, so how much less a house built by human hands.

God chose to dwell among His people then, and He still chooses to dwell in each of us as living tabernacles—temporary vessels carrying His eternal presence. Paul teaches in 2 Corinthians that the glory of the Spirit far surpasses the fading glory of the written text, and in Messiah the veil over our hearts is removed so we can behold God's glory face to face. We are earthen vessels, clay jars, so that the overwhelming power is evidently from God and not from us.

The sermon's central wordplay contrasts two kinds of cloud: the fog of confusion in our lives versus the Shekinah glory cloud of God's manifest presence, which actually brings clarity. Romans 12 calls us away from self-exalting conformity to the world and toward transformation by renewed minds, finding our purpose not in personal glory but in union with Messiah. When we enter God's glory cloud, the fog lifts and we gain clear vision to step forward into what He is calling us to do.

Hook / Wordplay

Two kinds of cloud: the fog of confusion that obscures our path versus the Shekinah glory cloud that filled the Tabernacle. Entering God's cloud is what clears the cloudiness away.

2025-04-06

Vayikra: Called Guilty, But Forgiven

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • You cannot receive forgiveness you have not made room for — acknowledging guilt honestly, even when uncomfortable, is the prerequisite to experiencing God's cleansing rather than staying locked in self-justification.
  • Defense mechanisms that explain away wrongdoing ('everyone does it,' 'it was their fault') function like the Pharisees in John 9 — they preserve the appearance of seeing while guaranteeing blindness remains.
  • Forgiving others is not optional generosity but structural necessity: an inability to forgive others reveals an inability to absorb forgiveness, keeping guilt's weight firmly in place.
One-Line Action
This week, identify one area of unacknowledged guilt, own it honestly before God, and consciously release the weight by receiving His forgiveness.
Memorable Quote
Bahuadadu nai ame vora, a road carna ame vora, levla ame by hand.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon opens Parashat Vayikra by noting that God called Moses from the Tent of Meeting, establishing that God desires nearness, not distance. Rabbi Jan highlights the repeated refrain in Leviticus 4-5 that even inadvertent sin renders a person guilty, emphasizing that this is not meant to crush but to catalyze repentance and forgiveness. He argues that acknowledging guilt is the essential first step toward experiencing God's redemptive hand, since you cannot ask for forgiveness if you believe you have done nothing wrong.

Drawing from Isaiah 43-44, he illustrates how unaddressed guilt creates a spiritual bottleneck that blocks praise and forward movement, comparing it to a clogged funnel. The John 9 narrative of the man born blind serves as an extended illustration of how defensive postures and institutional self-protection prevent people from seeing the truth right in front of them. From 1 John 1, he reinforces that claiming sinlessness is self-deception, while honest confession opens the door to purification.

The sermon culminates in Romans 8:1, declaring no condemnation for those in union with Messiah Yeshua, presenting this as the ultimate resolution of the guilt cycle. Throughout, he uses vivid analogies — guilt as physical weight, the man still carrying his burden while riding in a wagon — to make abstract spiritual concepts tangible. As Passover approaches, he frames the entire message as a call to move from slave mentality to spiritual liberation through honest ownership and divine forgiveness.

Hook / Wordplay

"Vayikra" means "He called" — and what God calls us is both guilty and forgiven, making the calling itself the bridge from conviction to liberation.

2025-04-14

Tzav: The Hametz Must Go!

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Just as hametz is physically removed from the house before Passover, identify specific areas of compromise or distraction in your life and actively clear them out rather than letting small sins accumulate into something that 'leavens the whole lump.'
  • Check your motivations, not just your output: even abundant ministry work is 'tinkling brass' (1 Cor 13) if it isn't rooted in genuine love and relationship with God—activity without devotion misses the point the same way the temple merchants did.
  • Keep the fire burning continually by rehearsing God's faithfulness each year at Passover; repetition is not redundancy but the mechanism by which deliverance becomes embedded in the heart.
One-Line Action
This Passover season, name one specific piece of spiritual hametz in your life and remove it before the week begins.
Memorable Quote
There are elements that we need to address in our life so that the flow of God's life can happen without delaying, without hindrance. We can move forward and experience the abundance that God wants to bring.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The Torah portion Tzav commands that fire be kept burning on the altar continually, which Rabbi Cbring connects to the annual rehearsal of Passover as a way of embedding God's faithfulness into our hearts. Transitioning to the Haftorah from Malachi 3, he explains that God promised to send a messenger who would purify the Levitical priests like a refiner's fire, because they had fallen into compromise and were setting a bad example for the people. In Matthew 21, Yeshua enters the temple before Passover and drives out the merchants, fulfilling this Malachi prophecy by purifying the priesthood and declaring the temple a house of prayer rather than a den of robbers.

The leaders questioned His authority but notably never called Him crazy—they recognized something legitimate in His actions. Rabbi Cring ties Yohanan the Baptist to the Elijah figure promised in Malachi, a humble forerunner who immersed people in repentance. He connects the Nicodemus dialogue about being born again to the Jewish concept of mikvah as spiritual rebirth.

The central metaphor is that just as we physically remove hametz before Passover, we must remove the leaven of sin from our lives so the flow of God's life can move through us without hindrance. Even good ministry becomes hollow noise if not motivated by genuine love and relationship with God.

Hook / Wordplay

The hametz must go—just as you scrub leaven from every corner of the house before Passover, scrub the spiritual leaven of sin and compromise from your heart so God's life can flow unhindered.

2025-04-20

Why Jewish Resurrection Always Follows Pesach

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Don't take past deliverances for granted—keep asking God for new waves of understanding and fresh encounters with His resurrection power, as Moses did even after witnessing the Exodus.
  • Recognize that disappointments and 'dry bones' seasons are part of God's preparation for a reset you will deeply appreciate; without the devastation, the deliverance wouldn't register.
  • Resurrection life is not only a future hope—walk in newness of life now by yielding to God's Spirit rather than drifting back to comfortable but lower patterns of thinking.
One-Line Action
Identify one area of your life that feels 'dry and very dry' this week and bring it specifically before God, asking Him for resurrection life and a fresh start in that place.
Memorable Quote
Resurrection is not just in the future when we die, we'll be raised to a newness of life. God wants to raise us to a newness of life now.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The rabbi frames the Shabbat after Pesach around God's recurring pattern of resurrection following deliverance, opening with the Amida's declaration that God 'revives the dead.' From Exodus 33-34 he draws the image of God giving Moses replacement tablets after the first were broken—a reset and new beginning even after failure with the golden calf. Ezekiel 37's dry bones vision provides the central metaphor: Israel felt completely cut off, yet God promised to open their graves and restore them, fulfilled in the rebirth of the state of Israel in 1948 out of the ashes of the Holocaust. He connects Yeshua's raising of Eleazar in John 11—dead four days, deliberately right before Passover—as a foreshadowing of His own coming resurrection.

The rabbi offers a distinctive reading of Gethsemane: Yeshua's plea to 'let this cup pass' was not about avoiding the cross but about surviving a physical crisis in the garden so He could reach the sacrifice, since He had already rebuked Peter for suggesting He wouldn't die. Isaiah 11's messianic branch from Jesse, read during the eighth day of Passover, and Romans 6's call to 'walk in newness of life' reinforce that resurrection is woven through the entire liturgical season.

The overarching argument is that resurrection always follows Pesach because God's pattern is never deliverance alone—He always brings new life and a reset on the other side. God wants believers to experience resurrection power now, not merely as a future hope, and to share that life with others.

Hook / Wordplay

The first fruits sheaf offering uses the Hebrew 'resheet' (beginning)—the same root as 'Beresheet' (In the beginning): God is constantly taking beginnings and making new beginnings.

2025-04-27

Shemini—Can We Live Again?

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • The things we go through are not our destination; they are what we pass through on the way to where God is bringing transformation — hold that distinction when hardship feels permanent.
  • Yeshua endured the cross 'for the joy set before Him,' meaning His motive was the reconciliation of hearts, not suffering for its own sake — let that reframe how I understand sacrifice and service.
  • Faith requires action, not just words: Bonhoeffer, Corrie ten Boom, and the unnamed woman who switched places all put their lives behind their convictions — ask where my faith needs to move from sentiment to costly action.
One-Line Action
When facing hostility or discouragement, remind yourself that what you are passing through is not your destination — God is working restoration on the other side.
Memorable Quote
The things we go through is not the destination in our life. They're what we pass through on the way to where God is bringing transformation and gold in our lives.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon connects the Torah portion Shemini with Yom HaShoah, opening with the inauguration of the tabernacle where God's fire consumed the offerings, only for Nadab and Abihu to immediately compromise by offering unauthorized fire and being consumed themselves. The rabbi draws a parallel to 2 Samuel where Uzzah died touching the Ark carried improperly, reinforcing that God's holiness demands obedience especially at inaugurating moments. He pivots to the Holocaust, arguing that the hatred behind it was ultimately directed at God Himself through His people, and traces a line from Luther's dehumanizing rhetoric to Hitler's ideology.

He highlights Bonhoeffer's martyrdom, Corrie ten Boom's family, and a moving testimony of a non-Jewish woman in a concentration camp who switched places with a Jewish woman and went to the gas chamber in her stead. From John 14 and 16 he draws Yeshua's promise of peace unlike the world's peace and the assurance that He has overcome the world. He closes with Hebrews 12, reframing the passion of Messiah around motive — it was for the joy set before Him — and urges the congregation not to grow despondent because what we pass through is not our destination but the path to God's restoration.

Hook / Wordplay

"Can we live again?" — Ezekiel's question over dry bones becomes the question over six million, answered by Israel's rebirth and God's promise of restoration through every devastation.

2025-05-04

Rebirth of A Nation

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Adversity and pressure are not signs of abandonment but labor pains preceding what God is delivering—lean into them rather than retreating.
  • God consistently uses outcasts and unlikely vessels (lepers in 2 Kings 7, Israel among the nations) to bring provision and blessing—never discount what He can do through those on the margins.
  • Spiritual renewal is not a one-time event but a daily practice: 'born again, again'—each morning rise and take hold of what God has made available.
One-Line Action
Press through present difficulties as spiritual labor pains, trusting that God is birthing something in your life you could never produce on your own.
Memorable Quote
The rebirth of a nation is a good thing. But the empowerment by the Spirit of God, by the Rua Hakkotech, is even better because then he breathes life into them as a nation.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi connects the double Torah portion Tazria-Metzora—dealing with conception, birth, and skin afflictions—to the rebirth of Israel as a nation, timed with Yom HaZikaron and Yom Ha'atzmaut. He anchors the theme in Isaiah 66:7 asking whether a nation can be born in a day and traces God's promise of restoration through Jeremiah 31's new covenant and Yeshua's prophecy in Luke 21 about Jerusalem being trampled until the age of the Gentiles runs its course. The 2 Kings 7 Haftarah illustrates how four lepers—outcasts—became agents of provision for a starving city, showing God uses unlikely vessels to birth hope.

He contrasts God's rebirth of Israel with the 1915 racist film Birth of a Nation, arguing that when man births something it reflects human character, but when God births something it reflects divine purpose. Romans 11 reinforces that Israel's restoration means life from the dead and warns Gentile believers against replacement theology or boasting over the natural branches. Drawing on 2 Corinthians 4, he frames present-day afflictions as labor pains producing an eternal weight of glory.

The counting of the Omer toward Shavuot parallels the anticipation of a coming spiritual harvest—a Ruach revolution. He closes with the personal image of coaching his wife through labor, noting that birth pangs are forgotten the moment you hold the child, urging the congregation to press through difficulty toward what God is delivering.

Hook / Wordplay

Tazria means 'she conceived'—the Torah portion about birth becomes the launchpad for exploring the rebirth of Israel as a nation and the spiritual rebirth God wants to birth in every believer through His Ruach.

Acharei Mot: How To Serve After the Death

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Reframe failure as 'postponed success' — like a seed underground, setbacks are part of the growth cycle, not the end of it.
  • Resist the urge to 'dig up the seed' by anxiously checking spiritual progress; trust the hidden process God is working beneath the surface.
  • Transformation comes from union with Messiah, not self-effort — yield availability rather than relying on personal ability.
One-Line Action
When facing disappointment, stay planted in union with Messiah and trust the hidden growth process rather than trying to force or measure results.
Memorable Quote
There is something about the way that God is able to take what seems to be done, finished, kaput, and the stimulated once again to bear fruit and to bear abundance.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon takes its framework from the Torah portion Acharei Mot, which opens with God speaking to Moses after the death of Aaron's two sons, and asks the central question: how do we serve God after experiencing tragedy, failure, or deep disappointment? The rabbi develops a sustained seed-and-harvest metaphor, drawing on John 12:24's grain of wheat that must die to produce a harvest and Job 14:7's hope for a tree cut down that can sprout again. He walks through the Yom Kippur atonement ritual of the two goats and connects Leviticus 17:11's 'the life is in the blood' to Messiah's ultimate sacrifice.

The Amos haftarah reinforces the theme with God's promise to raise up David's fallen booth and bring such agricultural abundance that the plowman overtakes the reaper. Turning to practical application, the rabbi contextualizes 'love your neighbor as yourself' within Leviticus 19's fuller framework of honest rebuke, refusing slander, and not standing idly by. He emphasizes that spiritual growth, like a planted seed, must not be dug up prematurely to check progress but trusted to develop beneath the surface.

Drawing from Philippians 2, he presents Messiah's self-emptying as the model for how believers esteem others above themselves. The sermon closes with Galatians 6's exhortation not to grow weary, affirming that those who sow into the Spirit will reap everlasting life and see God rebuild what seemed ruined.

Hook / Wordplay

The Torah portion's name 'Acharei Mot' (After the Death) becomes a lens for all forms of death — literal loss, failure, disappointment — reframed through the agricultural principle that a seed must die before it can produce a harvest.

2025-05-18

Emor: From Profane to Profound

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Audit not just explicit speech but the 'filler' attitudes (complaining, jealousy, selfish ambition) that James calls worldly wisdom — these are the subtle profanities that erode spiritual discernment before we notice.
  • Don't restrict God to 'certain rooms' of life; the rabbi's image of inviting God in but limiting His access is a convicting check on areas I keep cordoned off from transformation.
  • When Yeshua says 'you have heard it said…but I say,' he is modeling profound engagement with Scripture — I should read Torah not for the minimum requirement but for the heart-level demand underneath.
One-Line Action
Identify one area of life where I've been restricting God's access and consciously yield it to Him this week.
Memorable Quote
Praise be his name, whose glory is kingdom, is forever a man.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon explores parashat Emor through the repeated Torah command not to profane God's holy name, defining profanity broadly as any irreverence, compromise, or diminishment of God's standards in speech and lifestyle. The rabbi contrasts this with the 'profound' — deep, penetrating insight and transformation that comes only through God's Spirit. Using Ezekiel's sons of Zadok as a model, he emphasizes that spiritual leaders must not only teach the difference between holy and common but demonstrate it in their own lives.

He connects Yeshua's Sermon on the Mount teachings ('you have heard it said…but I say') as examples of moving beyond surface-level obedience to heart-level transformation. Drawing from 1 Corinthians 2, he argues that true spiritual depth comes not from human eloquence but from the Spirit who probes the profoundest depths of God. He warns from Galatians 4 against sliding back into enslavement to weak elemental spirits after having been set free.

James 3–4 provides the closing framework: wisdom from above is pure, peaceable, merciful, and without hypocrisy, while jealousy and selfish ambition produce disharmony. The congregation is called to yield every area of life — not restricting God to certain rooms — so He can transform the profane into the profound and make them shine like stars in a twisted generation.

Hook / Wordplay

'Profane' and 'profound' — two words that sound alike but sit at opposite ends of the spiritual spectrum; the sermon traces God's power to move us from one to the other.

2025-05-25

Whose Rules Do You Follow?

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • God's regulations function as guardrails that keep you from going off a cliff, not as instruments of control—contrast them with man-made mandates that often lack infrastructure or understanding and serve mainly to give people power over others.
  • Even after the most severe covenant consequences in Leviticus 26, God says 'I will not reject them'—no matter how far you've strayed, the door of return remains open because God's covenant is stronger than your failure.
  • Love is the only force that transforms people walking in darkness; you cannot argue or rationalize someone out of blindness, but when you replace fear-driven reactions with genuine love, even the hardest hearts can melt, as illustrated by Muslim converts who had never encountered love until believers showed it to them.
One-Line Action
Replace consternation and fear with active expressions of God's love toward those around you, trusting His regulations as protective boundaries rather than burdens.
Memorable Quote
If we are walking around in consternation and fear, guess what we're not exuding to the world around us? The love that will change and transform even the most decrepit of people.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The double Torah portion Behar-Bechukotai addresses God's regulations for land sabbaths, Jubilee restoration, and the covenant blessings and curses. The rabbi reframes biblical 'slavery' as a family-based welfare system with guaranteed restoration through Jubilee, countering a common skeptic objection with historical context. Using the film Alita: Battle Angel, he poses the central question: whose rules do you follow?

God's regulations serve as protective boundaries that bring blessing—five chasing a hundred, a hundred chasing ten thousand—while man-made rules often give people power to lord over others. Leviticus 26 reveals an escalating pattern of consequences for disobedience, yet remarkably concludes with God's promise: 'I will not reject them.' The rabbi connects this to the killing of two young people in Washington, illustrating how those walking in darkness applaud violence without capacity to see clearly.

Drawing from John 14-15, he emphasizes that keeping God's commands flows from love, not compulsion, and Romans 1 describes the descent into moral blindness when people exchange truth for falsehood. He closes with a striking illustration: Muslim converts who testified that nobody in their upbringing ever mentioned love, and when they experienced it from believers, their hearts melted—showing that authentic love, not argument, transforms even the darkest hearts.

Hook / Wordplay

Whose rules do you live by? Some say 'my own rules, I'm a rebel'—but having no rules is a misnomer, because you're always living by somebody's rules.

High
2025-06-04

Bamidbar / Pre-Shavuot Shabbat Service

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • The Acts 2 outpouring happened in the Temple courts, not the upper room—Luke 24:53 says the disciples were in the Temple daily, and the Ezekiel 1 Haftarah with its whirlwind and fire was being read at the moment those phenomena appeared, creating a live scriptural parallel.
  • Shavuot frames the covenant as a ketubah (marriage certificate), with Hosea 2's triple betrothal—in righteousness, compassion, and faithfulness—functioning as God's wedding vows, which reframes obedience as intimacy rather than obligation.
  • The ten days of unified prayer between the Ascension and Shavuot were the preparation that positioned the disciples for the outpouring—being in one accord was the active ingredient, not passive waiting.
One-Line Action
Invest in sustained, unified prayer and worship as active preparation for God's power rather than sitting as a passive audience waiting for a manifestation.
Memorable Quote
Love is the one commodity that God gives us that we can give it all away and never run out because it's a non-stop flow of his love towards us and then as a conduit to share that with everybody around us.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The rabbi briefly touches the Bamidbar census opening before pivoting to the Shavuot readings that dominate the service. He traces God's restoration impulse through Hosea 2, where God woos unfaithful Israel in the wilderness and pledges betrothal in righteousness, framing the Torah itself as a ketubah given at Sinai. He walks through Exodus 19's theophany—thunder, lightning, fire, and the bat kol—then shows how the people abandoned that covenant within 40 days at the golden calf.

He builds his central argument that the Acts 2 outpouring occurred in the Temple courts, not an upper room, citing Luke 24:53's statement that the disciples spent all their time in the Temple praising God. He then overlays the Shavuot Haftarah from Ezekiel 1—whirlwind from the north, raging fire, living creatures with burning coals—with the Acts 2 phenomena of rushing wind and cloven tongues of fire, arguing the congregation was reading those very words when the manifestation occurred.

He connects Daniel 9's seventy weeks to Messiah being cut off in the midst of the seventieth week and the subsequent destruction of Jerusalem. He concludes that Shavuot calls believers to active preparation through unified prayer and worship, to listen for God's voice, and to bear fruit by sharing the message of restoration with others.

Hook / Wordplay

The Torah as ketubah—Shavuot is God's wedding day with Israel, where the covenant at Sinai served as marriage vows and Hosea 2's betrothal language as the renewal of those vows.

2025-06-05

The Story of Ruth During Shavuot

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Selfless service without expectation of reward can open doors you never knew existed—Ruth's devotion to Naomi, not strategic positioning, led to her place in David's line.
  • When circumstances feel like a dead end, resist the lie that this is your final destination; it is a passage on the way to God's provision and glory.
  • God orchestrates 'coincidences'—Ruth 'happened to be' in Boaz's field—so stay faithful in small obedience even when you cannot see the larger design.
One-Line Action
Serve someone in your life selflessly this week without calculating what you might get back, trusting God to work in the unseen.
Memorable Quote
It was Ruth's love for Naomi that opened the door that she couldn't possibly have known would be available to her.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

On the 50th day of the Omer count—Shavuot—the rabbi teaches from the Book of Ruth, traditionally read during this holiday. He opens with the Acts 2 Pentecost backdrop, noting how the Spirit's outpouring reversed the Tower of Babel by letting each person hear God's praises in their own language. He then walks through Ruth's story: a Moabite woman who chose to stay with her depressed mother-in-law Naomi out of pure love, with no knowledge of any benefit awaiting her.

Ruth's selfless gleaning in the fields 'happened' to place her in the portion belonging to Boaz, a kinsman redeemer. The rabbi emphasizes that Ruth's devotion—not calculation—opened the door to her becoming the great-grandmother of King David and an ancestor of Messiah. He ties this to Romans 8, arguing that present sufferings are not comparable to the coming glory, and that the Spirit helps us in weakness just as providence guided Ruth.

The broader Shavuot theme emerges: God's covenant message extends to all nations, as foreshadowed by Ruth the Moabite entering Israel's lineage. He also explains the Shavuot dairy tradition, connecting it to the Torah being 'sweet to the taste' and the newness of the dietary laws at Sinai. He closes by urging the congregation to trust that current hardships are passages, not final destinations, on the way to God's abundance.

Hook / Wordplay

Babel scattered languages in rebellion; Pentecost restored them in worship—and Ruth, a foreign woman, walked right into the lineage of the King.

2025-06-08

Naso: Compromised vs Covenantal Vows

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Compromise is incremental: Samson didn't break covenant in one leap but through a series of boundary-blurring choices—watch for the small drifts in your own walk before they become catastrophic.
  • You often don't understand the value of what God has freely given until it's gone; don't wait until everything is stripped away to recommit to covenant faithfulness.
  • Don't neglect or sell short the gifts and calling God has placed in you by settling for short-term compromises—throw yourself fully into the work and destiny God has set before you.
One-Line Action
Identify one area where you have been slowly blurring a boundary with God and take a concrete step this week to recommit to covenant faithfulness before the drift deepens.
Memorable Quote
I believe he more completely understood the scope of the covenant when all the benefits of the covenant had disappeared.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon draws from Parashat Naso in Numbers, surveying the census of Levites, the law of the Sotah, the Nazarite vow, and the Aaronic blessing before zeroing in on Samson as the central case study. Rabbi Rosenberg highlights that Samson was a Nazarite from birth rather than by personal choice, which meant he never fully grasped the covenant's weight until its benefits were stripped away. He traces Samson's slow drift toward compromise with Delilah, showing how incremental boundary-blurring led to catastrophic loss of strength, sight, and freedom.

Yeshua's rebuke of hypocritical Pharisees in Matthew 23 is brought in to illustrate how polished exteriors mask interior rot. The sermon then moves through David's sin and repentance via Nathan's parable, Peter's denial and threefold restoration, and Ananias and Sapphira's fatal deception to show that compromise takes many forms but God's invitation to return remains constant. Paul's participation in a Nazarite vow in Acts 21 demonstrates faithful Torah observance against false rumors.

Timothy is exhorted not to neglect his gift or compromise short-term what God intends long-term. The message closes with the Aaronic blessing reframed not as incantation but as God's genuine intent to restore and shine His face on those who return to covenant faithfulness.

Hook / Wordplay

"Winning by a Hair"—Samson's literal hair was the outward sign of his Nazarite covenant, and his story shows that spiritual victory hinges on the thin margin between drifting into compromise and holding fast to covenantal faithfulness.

2025-06-15

B'Ha'Alotkha — Follow The Cloud

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When negativity or complaint surrounds you, recognize it as the 'mixed multitude' influence; consciously redirect attention to what God has delivered you from and where He is leading, rather than absorbing the crowd's anxiety.
  • If you feel accused or dejected, remember Zechariah's Joshua: God removes filthy garments and guilt—don't internalize the identity the accuser projects onto you, because accusation is the adversary's signature, not God's.
  • Difficult circumstances are transit points, not destinations; maintain forward-looking trust that God is completing a work and that present afflictions are producing an eternal weight of glory far beyond their momentary sting.
One-Line Action
This week, when I catch myself echoing a complaint I absorbed from others, pause and ask whether I am following the cloud or the crowd.
Memorable Quote
If you're in a difficult circumstance, that's not your destination. That's what you're passing through on the way to where God is bringing out a goal that he has in mind for us.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The Torah portion B'Ha'Alotkha opens with the menorah casting its light forward, which the rabbi frames as a picture of God illuminating the path ahead rather than behind. The cloud over the tabernacle guided Israel's every move—when it lifted they marched, when it rested they stayed—modeling total dependence on God's leading. Yet the mixed multitude that left Egypt grew greedy for easier comforts, and their complaints infected Israel until even Moses felt crushed by the burden. This crowd-following spirit produced jealousy in Miriam and Aaron and cost Israel sight of what God had delivered them from and where He was taking them.

From Zechariah the rabbi drew the scene of Joshua the high priest standing in dung-covered garments while Satan accused him, showing how God removes guilt and restores dignity despite the accuser's projections. Zechariah 4:6's declaration 'not by force, not by power, but by my spirit' reinforced that transformation is God's work alone. Hebrews 3 warned against hardened hearts that drift from trust, urging daily mutual encouragement to hold firm until the goal is reached.

Second Corinthians 4 reframed afflictions as light and momentary against the weight of eternal glory, calling believers to be clay vessels through whom God's overwhelming power shines. The rabbi applied these threads to modern crowd dynamics—protest culture, accusation cycles, and ideologies of terror—contrasting them with God's love that turns enemies into brothers. He closed by urging the congregation to follow the cloud, shine light forward, and let the crowd follow them into life rather than destruction.

Hook / Wordplay

Follow the cloud, not the crowd—and don't follow the crowd, let the crowd follow you.

2025-06-22

The Sad Ripple Effect of Fake News

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When circumstances change, God hasn't changed—look to Him to navigate rather than surrendering to fear, because the very enemies Israel feared were terrified of them the whole time.
  • Treasure God's word through memorization and meditation (Psalm 119:11) as the active defense against deceptive voices competing for your attention.
  • Stay locked into your own calling—Peter's 'What about him?' to Yeshua about John is a warning that comparison is one of the fastest routes to derailment.
One-Line Action
Memorize and meditate on Scripture daily so that when fearful or deceptive voices arise, God's word—not spin—is your compass.
Memorable Quote
If circumstances change, God hasn't changed. If circumstances change, we look to God to show us how to navigate through those troubled waters.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The rabbi teaches from the Torah portion Sh'lach Lekha (Numbers 13) about the twelve spies sent to survey the Promised Land, framing the ten spies' negative report as one of the earliest recorded instances of fake news. The facts about the land were accurate—it did flow with milk and honey—but the spin the spies added about fierce inhabitants caused national panic, rebellion against God, and a desire to return to Egyptian slavery. Caleb urged trust in God's promise, but the fearful majority prevailed, and that generation was barred from the land.

Drawing from the haftarah in Joshua 2, the rabbi reveals the dramatic irony: Rahab told the later spies that Jericho's inhabitants had been terrified of Israel for all forty years, proving the original fear was baseless. He connects this to the Jewish tradition that the rebellion occurred on Tisha B'Av, the saddest day on the Jewish calendar, showing how one episode of faithlessness rippled through centuries. Using John 21's exchange between Yeshua and Peter about John, the rabbi warns against getting sidetracked by comparing ourselves to others instead of following our own calling.

He grounds the antidote in Psalm 119:9-11—treasuring God's word in our hearts through memorization and meditation—as the defense against deceptive voices. The sermon closes with a call to let the ripple effect of following God be one of restoration and cleansing rather than fear and rebellion.

Hook / Wordplay

The spies' report was history's first fake news: the facts were real but the spin was fatal—and the ripple effect lasted forty years and beyond.

2025-06-29

What Do You Boast About in a Selfie-Centered World?

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When under attack or criticized, follow Moses' model: intercede for the attacker rather than becoming defensive—this breaks the cycle of self-protection and invites God's power into the conflict.
  • Share honestly where you have been delivered from rather than projecting polished righteousness; transparency about past weakness is more effective witness than claiming spiritual superiority.
  • Periodically ask God to 're-center' you on Messiah the way you hit re-center on a GPS—check whether your energy is going toward protecting your image or toward God's purposes.
One-Line Action
This week, catch yourself in a moment of self-promotion or defensiveness, pause, and consciously redirect that energy toward interceding for or serving the other person instead.
Memorable Quote
If you focus your energy on trying to have what somebody else has, you know what happens? You lose what is yours.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon opens with the Torah portion Korach, where Korah and 250 prominent Israelite leaders rebel against Moses and Aaron, claiming everyone is equally holy and accusing Moses of being a dictator. The rabbi highlights that these were not nobodies but high-credibility leaders whose self-importance drove them to tear down God's appointed authority. Moses' response was not defensiveness but intercession—falling on his face to pray for the very people attacking him.

God's dramatic judgment—the earth swallowing the rebels—demonstrated that the rebellion was ultimately against Him, not Moses. The rabbi then pivots to Paul's 'fool's speech' in 2 Corinthians 11-12, where Paul models the antidote to selfie-centered boasting: boasting in weakness so that Messiah's power can rest upon him. Philippians 2 reinforces the point with Yeshua's own self-emptying as the ultimate example of humility.

The sermon warns through 2 Timothy 3 that end-times culture will be defined by self-love, arrogance, and retaining religion's form while denying its power. A personal story about sugar addiction illustrates how self-confidence can quickly become a trap. The closing call is to let God 're-center' us on Messiah rather than on self.

Hook / Wordplay

'Selfie-centered world' reframes modern selfie culture as a spiritual diagnosis—self-centeredness dressed up as normal life—and the sermon's resolution is moving from selfie-centered to Messiah-centered, capped by a GPS 're-center' metaphor.
