2025-07-06

When Rocks Cry Out

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Complaining rewires perception: the Israelites forgot slavery and glamorized Egypt, a reminder that when I rehearse grievances I lose sight of what God has actually provided and sustained me through.
  • Detours are preparation, not punishment—Yiftach's exile built the leadership skills Israel later needed; when my path redirects, treat it as training for the next phase rather than evidence of abandonment.
  • Forgiveness is not absolution for the offender but liberation for myself; holding onto it keeps me captive to someone who may not even be present in my life anymore.
One-Line Action
When complaint or frustration rises, pause and cry out to God instead of spiraling—look up like Israel looked at the bronze serpent rather than down at the grievance.
Memorable Quote
It is not letting somebody else off the hook. It's letting us off the hook because we're bound by it even if they're no longer communicating with us.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon explores Parashat Hukkat (Numbers 19–22:1), centering on the episode where Moses, frustrated by Israel's relentless complaints, strikes the rock instead of speaking to it—forfeiting his entry into the Promised Land. Rabbi emphasizes that complaining distorts reality: the Israelites romanticized Egyptian slavery and despised the manna that had sustained them for forty years. He connects the bronze serpent to self-examination, arguing that looking up at it forced the people to see how they had been emulating the adversary's lies rather than following God.

A sidebar on 1 Corinthians 15:29 explains 'baptism for the dead' as the Torah purification rite for those who prepared bodies for burial in anticipation of resurrection, not proxy salvation. The sermon then moves to Luke 19, linking Zacchaeus's immediate repentance and the triumphal entry where Yeshua declares the stones will cry out if the disciples are silenced, then weeps over Jerusalem.

The Judges haftarah figure Yiftach illustrates how exile and detours can build leadership capacity. Throughout, the rabbi returns to the image of rocks and tears—water from the struck rock, Yeshua's tears over Jerusalem, and the call for believers to cry out to God before the stones must do it for them.

Hook / Wordplay

'When Rocks Cry Out and Tears Are Shed'—Moses struck rock and water flowed; Yeshua said if people stay silent the stones will shout; the question is whether we will cry out to God or let the rocks do it for us.

2025-07-13

When Men Act Like Donkeys

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When fear drives decision-making, I default to my own solutions instead of turning to God first — pause and pray before reacting to threatening circumstances.
  • Balaam technically obeyed the letter of God's word but found a loophole to undermine its spirit; examine where I might be keeping the surface rules while compromising the deeper intent.
  • The donkey saw what the prophet couldn't because ambition blinded him — stay alert to the quiet warnings from unlikely sources that my own agenda may be causing me to ignore.
One-Line Action
When fear or ambition clouds your vision, stop and ask whether you are acting out of blind stubbornness or surrendered determination — and let God direct your path.
Memorable Quote
When men act like donkeys, we have a choice of how we act in arrogance or in humility. Out of emotional stubbornness or out of a focused determination to follow through on what God says to do.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

King Balak of Moab, gripped by dread after seeing what God did for Israel against the Amorites, hired the prophet Balaam to curse the people — a reaction born of fear rather than faith. God explicitly told Balaam not to curse Israel because they were blessed, yet Balaam's integrity fractured under the weight of financial temptation, and he kept seeking permission to go. On the journey his donkey saw the angel of the Lord blocking the path while Balaam himself was blind to it — the donkey had more spiritual discernment than the prophet.

Three pop-culture donkeys illustrate the spectrum: Eeyore the pessimist, Shrek's Donkey the optimist, and Pinocchio's boys who became donkeys through indulgence and became beasts of burden. Though Balaam could not directly curse Israel, he found a loophole by counseling Balak to seduce Israel through Moabite women, leading to idolatry at Baal Peor and a plague that killed 24,000. Phinehas took a decisive stand to stop the plague, embodying focused determination rather than reckless stubbornness.

The New Covenant writers in Jude, 2 Peter, and Revelation all warn against following Balaam's path of compromise for profit. The sermon closes by contrasting Balaam's arrogant donkey ride with Yeshua's humble entry into Jerusalem on a donkey, framing the central choice: will we act like donkeys in blind stubbornness or in humble, focused obedience? Proverbs 3 anchors the call — trust in the Lord, don't lean on your own understanding, and He will direct your path.

Hook / Wordplay

"When Men Act Like Donkeys" plays on the dual nature of the donkey image: Balaam's donkey saw the angel he couldn't, and Yeshua entered Jerusalem humbly on a donkey — stubbornness can be destructive blindness or focused, God-directed determination.

2025-07-20

Pinchas: Thinking Through What To Do

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When facing crisis, follow David's model at Ziklag: first strengthen yourself in the Lord, then formally consult God before taking action — emotion-driven reactions lead to poor outcomes even when intentions are sincere.
  • Compromise and instant gratification carry quantifiable costs — Simeon's tribe lost two-thirds of its population and inheritance allotment because of choices made right before entering the Promised Land; the timing of failure matters.
  • Embed God's word deeply enough that right action becomes instinctive like Pinchas, rather than relying on bold emotional declarations like Peter's which collapsed under pressure.
One-Line Action
Before reacting to the next crisis or distraction, pause to consult God and let Him direct the response rather than following emotional impulse.
Memorable Quote
The existential threat is losing the grip on what God is doing in our life and allowing ourselves to be distracted from that.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon opens with Pinchas's decisive act of driving a spear through Zimri and the Midianite woman Cozbi, stopping the plague that killed 24,000 Israelites, and contrasts his zealous action with the passive weeping of others who mourned without addressing the root cause. Rabbi highlights the devastating census data showing the tribe of Simeon dropped from the third largest tribe (59,300) to the smallest (22,200), illustrating the concrete cost of compromise through instant gratification. He then draws a parallel to Elijah, who after the mighty victory over the prophets of Baal collapsed into depression and flight at a single threat from Jezebel, failing to think through his response and instead reacting from fear.

The counter-example is David at Ziklag, who upon finding his city burned and families taken captive, strengthened himself in the Lord, consulted God through the priestly vest, and was told to pursue and recover everything. David's obedience led to a providential encounter with an abandoned Egyptian slave who guided them directly to the raiders. Turning to the New Covenant, the rabbi examines how Caiaphas thought he was being practical by sacrificing one man to save the nation, yet his faulty reasoning led to the very destruction he tried to prevent.

The disciples similarly made bold emotional declarations of loyalty without understanding what was actually coming, and scattered when tested. The rabbi warns against modern distractions like phone screens and social media that produce a spirit of dullness, echoing Isaiah's words about eyes that do not see and ears that do not hear. The central charge is to embed God's word deeply enough that right responses become instinctive, and when facing the unknown, to pause, consult the Lord, and let Him reveal the path forward rather than reacting from emotion or faulty reasoning.

Hook / Wordplay

"Spear me the details" — Pinchas's single decisive thrust ended a plague that passive weeping could not, framing the sermon's call to act from embedded conviction rather than emotional impulse.

2025-07-27

The Stage Is Set For Victory

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When a trial feels like a permanent blot, reframe it as a transitional stage—God is moving you toward a destination you cannot yet see.
  • Trust is not built by declaration but by surviving challenged faith and seeing God prove faithful; review your own track record of His faithfulness to fuel courage for the next stage.
  • Don't negotiate a comfortable settlement short of God's counsel—like Gad and Reuben choosing land east of the Jordan, partial obedience can isolate you from the fullness God intends.
One-Line Action
Identify which transitional stage you are in right now and choose to keep following God into it rather than clinging to the last plateau.
Memorable Quote
At the time we think the transitional stage is a permanent blot on our life. But it's what we're going through on our way to where God's destination is bringing us.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The double Torah portion Matot-Masei covers tribes and journeys, framing a teaching on life as God-ordained transitional stages toward victory. The rabbi begins with how vows anchor spiritual momentum—breaking a vow can break the cycle of progress, sending us back to the start. He recounts Moses' final military commission against the Midianites and Balaam, showing God settling accounts before advancing Israel to the next stage.

The recorded stages of Israel's 40-year wilderness journey become a lens for seeing how every season has a starting point, a process, and a conclusion that launches the next phase. Drawing from 1 Peter 1, he teaches that trials test trust like fire purifies gold—each stage is not an end in itself but preparation for what follows. Mark 8:34-38 reinforces the call to deny self-centeredness so we can follow God into uncomfortable new phases without clinging to the last plateau.

He traces the same pattern in Yeshua's own life—the flight to Egypt, the Temple dialogue at age 12—to show God moving people through stages they did not plan. From Jeremiah 1, God equips the reluctant prophet stage by stage for what lies ahead. The sermon's core exhortation is that what feels like a permanent blot is actually a transitional passage on the way to God's destination of victory.

Hook / Wordplay

"Masei" means stages or journeys—the rabbi reframes every difficult life stage as God literally setting the stage for victory, turning the Torah portion title into the sermon's thesis.

2025-08-03

Devarim: Words Beyond Dire Straits

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When facing a crisis, do what David did — stop, encourage yourself in the Lord, then specifically inquire of Him for wisdom on how to proceed rather than reacting out of emotion or despair.
  • God's pruning can feel indistinguishable from being cut off, but the test is whether you remain connected to the vine; stay united with Him and His words, and fruitfulness will follow the pain.
  • Once you have asked God for wisdom, stop re-asking and start anticipating — He gives generously and without reproach, so shift from petition mode to expectation mode.
One-Line Action
The next time you hit a dire strait this week, pause before reacting and deliberately inquire of the Lord for specific wisdom on how to proceed.
Memorable Quote
From Devarim, Deuteronomy 6, actually next week's portion, the cornerstone of our faith.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi opens with Deuteronomy 1, noting that an 11-day journey took Israel 40 years because they tried to speed up what God had paced for them. He connects the Torah portion Devarim to Tisha B'Av, the saddest day on the Jewish calendar, and explains the three-week mourning period called Bein HaMetzarim — between the straits. He frames Deuteronomy as the Gospel according to Moses, calling it the love book of the Torah because of its persistent emphasis on God's love for His people.

Drawing from Isaiah 1, he walks through God's escalating rebuke of Israel only to arrive at the stunning reversal: come, let us reason together — though your sins are like scarlet, they will be white as snow. From John 15 he teaches that pruning feels identical to being cut off, but its purpose is greater fruitfulness. From James 1 he explains that trials produce perseverance leading to wholeness, and that when we lack wisdom we must ask God who gives generously without reproach.

He shares personal stories — a mother who lost her son to a drunk driver and a young congregant with cancer — to illustrate how dire straits change our perspective and how double-mindedness robs us of peace. He closes by pointing to Zechariah's promise that fast days of mourning will become celebrations, and urges the congregation to let God's words carry them beyond whatever dire straits they face.

Hook / Wordplay

Devarim means words; Bein HaMetzarim means between the straits. The sermon's frame: it is God's words (devarim) that carry us beyond (bein) our dire straits (ha-metzarim).

2025-08-10

Va'Etchanan: Wisdom & Understanding

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Wisdom is not accumulating scripture knowledge but knowing what to do with what I know; pursue applied obedience, not just memorization.
  • When feeling overlooked by God, resist the instinct to complain—complaints clog the pipeline of what God wants to pour out; press in with trust instead.
  • Demonstrate the value of faith to those watching not by lecturing but by visibly investing time and effort into what I claim matters most, since people register commitment through action, not words.
One-Line Action
This week, identify one area where I know what God says but haven't acted on it, and take a concrete step of obedience.
Memorable Quote
When there is a complaint, it's like clogging up a pipeline that keeps all the things that God wants to pour out upon us. They get backed up because our complaints clog everything.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi teaches from parashat Va'Etchanan, opening with Moses pleading to cross the Jordan and God's refusal—underscoring that even Moses was subject to obedience and no one receives favoritism. He draws the key theme from Deuteronomy 4:6: the nations will recognize Israel as wise and understanding only when they observe God's commands being lived out, not merely recited. He distinguishes knowledge from wisdom, defining wisdom as knowing what to do with what you know.

Using the Shema and V'ahavta, he emphasizes that teaching children diligently means demonstrating faith's value through visible investment, not rote instruction—illustrating with a personal story about his son doubting he would finish an album after twelve years of talk. He moves to Isaiah 40, highlighting that complaints clog the pipeline of blessing and that God's unfathomable understanding is renewed in those who wait on Him.

He connects Yeshua's wilderness temptations to the Torah portions being read in synagogues during those same forty days, showing Yeshua drew His answers from the living word of that season. He closes with John 14, where Yeshua promises that the Father will make His home in those who keep His word, and urges the congregation to rush toward their destiny by spending time with God and letting His word pulse within them.

Hook / Wordplay

Knowing what to do with what you know is wisdom—Solomon was the wisest king yet also the most foolish, because knowledge without applied obedience is vanity.

2025-08-17

Ekev: Avoiding the Traps of Life

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Traps never announce themselves; prosperity and comfort can ensnare just as effectively as adversity—watch for the moment self-reliance replaces gratitude.
  • Depth in Scripture is the frontline defense: Yeshua defeated temptation not by negotiating but by reflexively citing the Word, so saturate yourself the same way.
  • Avoid spending life in fearful trap-scanning; instead focus positively on knowing God and letting His Word become alive in you—discernment follows naturally from intimacy.
One-Line Action
Saturate yourself in God's Word daily so that traps are exposed by the light of Scripture before they can close around you.
Memorable Quote
It's a little bit of Yeshua just enough to form a vaccine that makes us unable and resilient to allowing him to have access into our hearts.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi teaches from Parashat Ekev that God warned Israel not to serve the gods of conquered nations because doing so would become a trap. He identifies an escalating taxonomy of traps: idolatry, ingratitude after prosperity, pride in self-sufficiency, fear, and spiritual complacency. He draws a striking analogy to vaccines—just as a small dose of a virus can create immunity, a superficial exposure to Yeshua can inoculate a person against truly letting Him into their heart.

He shows how Yeshua faced the adversary's traps in the wilderness by quoting from this very Torah portion, and how the adversary even weaponized Psalm 91 as a counter-trap. He anchors the defense in Psalm 119:110—the wicked set traps, yet the psalmist did not stray from God's precepts—arguing that saturation in God's Word is the primary safeguard.

He stresses that the goal is not fearful vigilance but a positive, abundant pursuit of God's purposes, allowing the mind of Messiah to sharpen discernment at every step. He closes with Isaiah 55, urging listeners to seek the Lord while He may be found and to trust that God's higher ways will guide them beyond their own understanding.

Hook / Wordplay

A vaccine uses a small dose of the virus to build immunity—similarly, a little bit of Yeshua can inoculate you against truly receiving Him. That counterfeit exposure is itself a trap.

Primary Theme

2025-08-24

Seeing Storm-Ravaged Cities Restored

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When circumstances feel overwhelming, resist the impulse to fix things in your own strength (as David refused to strike Saul); yield the situation to God and trust His timing rather than forcing a resolution.
  • Practice discernment by testing every voice and claim against Torah's foundation—if an interpretation or leader's message contradicts God's Word, step back rather than follow charisma (1 John 4:1).
  • Focus accountability energy inward on your own community and spiritual walk (1 Corinthians 5:12) rather than exhausting yourself judging the world outside; God handles that jurisdiction.
One-Line Action
When life feels storm-ravaged, resist the knee-jerk reaction and turn back to the Lord and His Word as your foundation for restoration.
Memorable Quote
And when that stone was rolled away and he was risen, there was exposed to us in a way, a departure stone.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The Torah portion Re'eh (Deuteronomy 11:26) presents Israel with a fundamental choice: blessing for obedience or curse for disobedience. Drawing on the Haftarah from Isaiah 54, the rabbi frames the sermon around God's promise to restore even 'storm-ravaged cities'—places and lives devastated by forces beyond human control. He connects this to the week's hurricane off the New Jersey coast and to historical catastrophes like the Texas floods and 9/11's Tunnel to Towers foundation, noting how devastation often becomes a catalyst for unexpected service and restoration.

The alternate Haftarah from 1 Samuel 20 illustrates human 'storms' through Saul's rage against David, where jealousy and hatred wreak havoc like a literal tempest. The rabbi emphasizes that David refused to take matters into his own hands, leaving justice to God—a model for believers facing personal storms. He warns against false prophets and charismatic figures offering easy solutions, citing 1 John 4:1 on testing every spirit and Matthew 24 on signs preceding Jerusalem's destruction.

Romans 11 reinforces that God's plan includes restoring Israel and that Gentile believers should remain faithful rather than boast. The sermon closes with a striking typological connection: the Even HaEzel (departure stone) where David and Jonathan parted prefigures the rolled-away tomb stone, both representing a departure—one from a life under threat, the other from sin and death into new life.

Hook / Wordplay

The Even HaEzel ('departure stone') where David and Jonathan parted becomes a type of the rolled-away tomb stone—both are 'departure stones,' one from friendship under threat, the other from sin and death into newness of life.

2025-08-31

Shoftim: Eye For An Eye

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Before reacting to a situation or person, ask whether I'm judging by surface appearance or investigating deeper — Yeshua's model in John 8 was to slow down, avoid snap judgment, and apply the actual standard rather than the emotional one.
  • 'Eye for an eye' sets a ceiling, not a floor — proportional justice is a mercy that limits punishment, so when I feel wronged I should seek measured resolution rather than escalating retaliation.
  • Prioritize genuine face-to-face connection over digital interaction; the rabbi's point about nursing mothers making eye contact versus parents on phones is a concrete reminder that real presence communicates what texts and screens cannot.
One-Line Action
This week, before reacting to a conflict or forming an opinion about someone, pause and ask: am I judging by surface appearance, or looking deeper the way God weighs the heart?
Memorable Quote
he didn't do I for I but he had an eye on us and said we are the apple of his eye it's not a matter of how do we see it but how does he see it and he sees it as an opportunity to bring redemption
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The rabbi teaches from Parashat Shoftim on the appointment of judges and the command to pursue justice without favoritism or bribes. He anchors the sermon in John 7:24—stop judging by surface appearance and judge the right way—then builds an extended case study from John 8, arguing that Yeshua's response to the woman caught in adultery was not love overriding Torah but Torah applied correctly: without two or three witnesses willing to cast the first stone, there was no valid case. He proposes Yeshua bent down to write in the dirt specifically to avoid eye contact, allowing the accusers to save face and walk away.

He then reframes 'eye for an eye' not as a mandate for revenge but as a legal ceiling on punishment in an era of unlimited retribution, directly engaging the Gandhi and MLK quotes that call the principle barbaric. He connects this to Les Misérables' Inspector Javert, whose obsession with rigid justice blinded him to genuine transformation.

The sermon pivots to Isaiah 53, showing that Messiah himself did not demand proportional retaliation but laid down his life as a suffering servant with his eye on humanity's redemption. He closes by urging the congregation to look God in the eye, connect face-to-face with one another rather than through screens, and let God weigh the heart rather than judging from outward appearance as the season of Elul calls for reflection and return.

Hook / Wordplay

"Eye for an eye — how do you see it?" The double meaning of 'eye' threads the entire sermon: the legal principle of proportional justice and the act of seeing and perceiving rightly, culminating in the shift from retribution to being the apple of God's eye.

2025-09-07

Ki Tetze — God's Call For Cancel Culture

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Remember what God delivered you from without reliving the trauma—treat the memory as evidence of His faithfulness, not as a chain that keeps you bound.
  • Build the progressive spiritual culture of 2 Peter 1 in your own life (faith → goodness → knowledge → self-control → perseverance → godliness → love) as an intentional antidote to destructive cultural forces.
  • Speak into culture with boldness that comes from walking with Messiah—offer a 'new infusion of life' rather than condemnation, modeling the pattern rather than just critiquing the problem.
One-Line Action
This week, when a painful memory resurfaces, consciously shift your focus from the trauma itself to the specific way God brought you through it.
Memorable Quote
And God's calling for cancel culture is not to cancel us out, but to cancel out those things that so easily take hold of us, being transformed by the renewing of our mind, then we will prove what is that good and acceptable and perfect will of God, to move forward and to speak boldly.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon centers on Deuteronomy 25:17-19, the command to remember what Amalek did to Israel's vulnerable stragglers after the Exodus and to blot out Amalek's memory. Rabbi reframes this as God's original 'cancel culture against cancel culture'—Amalek tried to cancel Israel, so God commands Israel to cancel Amalek's legacy. He traces the spirit of Amalek through history: King Saul's incomplete obedience allowed a survivor whose descendant Haman later attempted genocide against the Jews in Esther, and the same spirit manifests today in Hamas, anti-Semitism, and totalitarian youth indoctrination.

Pivoting to Isaiah 54, he shows God's promise to enlarge and restore His people, declaring that shame and trauma will be forgotten and replaced by an unbreakable covenant of peace. He then turns the lens inward through Psalm 51, arguing that beyond blotting out external enemies, we need God to blot out the enemy within—our own sin and guilt. Drawing on Jeremiah 29:11, 2 Peter 1's progressive growth ladder, and Galatians 3's warning against returning to the old nature, he builds the case that God's culture replaces deterioration with progressive revelation.

Closing with Philippians 3:13-14, the rabbi urges forgetting what is behind—not ignoring history but refusing to relive its trauma—and pressing toward God's upward calling. The ultimate call is to speak life into culture rather than condemn it, writing a new story of freedom rather than erasing the old one.

Hook / Wordplay

"God's Call for Cancel Culture"—the long version is "God's Call for Cancel Culture against Cancel Culture." Amalek brought the original cancel culture against Israel; God commands us to cancel it right back.

2025-09-14

Ki Tavo: From Wanderers to Great Wonders

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Ask people genuine questions about what they believe rather than debating them — many hold positions they have never deeply examined, and caring inquiry creates inroads to share the message of life.
  • Do not prejudge how someone will respond to the gospel; the rabbi's own friends assumed he would never come to faith while something was powerfully stirring inside him that they could not see.
  • Treat this season of Teshuvah as a personal returning point — step forward boldly with God's message rather than retreating, because light does not negotiate with darkness, it simply displaces it.
One-Line Action
This week, find one person who holds different beliefs and ask a genuine, caring question about what they believe instead of debating them.
Memorable Quote
I believe we live far below what God's called us to be because we settle for so little. And yet God has called us to experience so much and so much more.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The Torah portion Ki Tavo opens with the declaration that Abraham was a wandering Aramean whom God transformed into the father of a great nation, establishing the sermon's theme of moving from wanderers to great wonders. The rabbi devotes significant time to the recent assassination of Charlie Kirk, praising his compassionate approach of inviting opponents to the front of the line and giving them a microphone for genuine dialogue. He highlights Kirk's widow Erica, who spoke powerfully just 48 hours after her husband's death, declaring that the killers had no idea what movement they had unleashed.

Connecting Kirk's organization name 'Turning Point' to the Hebrew season of Teshuvah, the rabbi reframes it as a 'returning point' where God calls His people to step forward boldly. Drawing from Isaiah 60, he emphasizes that even though thick darkness covers the earth, God's glory rises upon His people and commands them to arise and shine. From Luke 21, he teaches that persecution and chaos will prove an opportunity to bear witness, and that Yeshua Himself will provide eloquence no adversary can refute.

He urges the congregation not to retreat but to carry God's light into darkness like noonday in the night, noting that you cannot remove darkness but when you turn on the light it disappears. The rabbi shares his own conversion story to illustrate that we cannot know what is stirring inside someone, so we must never prejudge responses to the gospel. He closes with 1 Peter 2:9-12, calling believers to live such visibly good lives that even those who speak against them will ultimately give glory to God.

Hook / Wordplay

From wanderers to great wonders — Abraham began as a nomad and became the father of nations; God's pattern is to take aimless wandering and transform it into purposeful, world-changing witness.

2025-09-21

Nitzavim: A Return to Our Hidden Purpose

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • God's word is not far away or inaccessible—it is in my heart and mouth, meaning I already have what I need to obey; the barrier is willingness, not capability (Deut 30:14).
  • Guard against bitterness taking root: it doesn't just harm me privately but 'contaminates many,' so I need to address grievances before they metastasize (Heb 12:15).
  • Pay attention to HOW I hear, not just what I hear—a skewed filter can distort even true words into something misleading (Luke 8:18).
One-Line Action
This week, identify one area where spiritual drift has hidden God's purpose from you and take one concrete step back toward it.
Memorable Quote
I can't think of a more powerful expression of love than to say, I will go there and get you.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Drawing from Parashat Nitzavim, the rabbi teaches that God's covenant was made not only with those standing at Sinai but with all future generations. Deuteronomy 29:28 distinguishes hidden things that belong to God from revealed things that belong to us—and our task is to act faithfully on what has been revealed. Deuteronomy 30 shows God's extraordinary promise to gather His people even from the farthest places, which the rabbi calls the most powerful expression of love in Scripture.

The word of God is not distant or inaccessible; it is in our mouths and hearts, and we can do it. God sets before us life and death, blessing and curse, and commands us to choose life. From Hebrews 12, the rabbi warns against letting a root of bitterness spring up, which contaminates not just the individual but those around them.

Ephesians 5 diagnoses what the rabbi terms 'spiritual narcolepsy'—drifting asleep while darkness encroaches—and calls believers to wake up and live as children of light. First Peter 3 points to the 'hidden man of the heart,' the inner spiritual core where God forms His dwelling. The season of teshuvah is God's annual invitation to return to a purpose He has always intended for us—hidden not because He withholds it, but because our distractions have obscured it.

Hook / Wordplay

The 'hidden purpose' carries a double meaning: Deuteronomy 29:28 says hidden things belong to God, yet our own life-purpose becomes hidden from us through drift and distraction—so teshuvah is the act of uncovering what God always intended to reveal.

2025-09-28

Vayelech: Returning During the Season of Return

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • The Ten Days of Awe are not spiritual insurance—being 'good for 10 days' is meaningless without genuine heart openness; use this season for honest self-examination rather than performative piety.
  • God is actively watching for our return like the prodigal's father—don't assume failure disqualifies you; the very act of turning back triggers God running to meet you.
  • Resist the enemy by drawing near to God (James 4:7-8) rather than merely confronting opposition head-on; spiritual boldness comes from proximity to God, not self-generated courage.
One-Line Action
Identify one area where self-sufficiency has replaced dependence on God this year and bring it honestly before Him before Yom Kippur.
Memorable Quote
Imagine if you were at a wedding and you were sharing your vows and your vow was to say to the person that you're marrying. And if you go astray and you're carried off and so many different things and you suffer all these things, come back and I will receive you and we'll move on from there.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi teaches on Parashat Vayelech during Shabbat Shuva, connecting Moses' final charge to Israel—be strong, be bold, don't be afraid—with the season's call to return to God. He draws from Hosea 14's 'take with you words and return' to argue that teshuvah is not ritual performance but genuine heart transformation. He warns that treating the Ten Days of Awe like a spiritual insurance policy misses God's intent for flayed-open honesty before Him.

Using the Prodigal Son parable from Luke 15, he illustrates how the father was watching the whole time, waiting to run out and meet his returning son—a picture of God's preemptive love. He addresses the older brother's self-righteousness as a cautionary parallel for those quick to dismiss others' failures. From Romans 5-6, he emphasizes that Messiah died while we were still rebels, not after we cleaned ourselves up.

He reframes Deuteronomy 30's covenant promise as a wedding vow—God telling Israel in advance that even after they stray, He will receive them back. He calls the congregation to seek God while He may be found (Isaiah 55), to draw near so He draws near (James 4), and to boldly proclaim the message of life without fear. He closes urging return without reservation so God can restore what the locusts have eaten and pour out blessing not room enough to contain.

Hook / Wordplay

The rabbi revisits his own Bar Mitzvah Haftarah portion (Hosea 14, Shabbat Shuva) from 60 years ago, telling his mother afterward: 'I just did my Haftarah portion—but this time I knew what I was talking about.'

2025-10-05

Ha'Azinu: The Hope & Power of a Great Rock Song

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When I catch myself feeling self-sufficient—'I accomplished this'—treat it as a warning signal that I'm drifting from dependence on God, the pattern Moses explicitly warned about in the song.
  • Engage with Scripture not just as words to hear but as a foundation to build on: Yeshua's parable in Luke 6 makes clear that hearing without acting is a house without bedrock.
  • Sing and share God's truth with others as Ephesians 5 instructs—spiritual songs are meant to edify one another, not just be private devotion; the 'rock song' gains its power when it goes outward.
One-Line Action
Audit what you are actually building your confidence on this week—your own track record or the Rock—and consciously re-anchor one decision to God's foundation.
Memorable Quote
God wants us to understand that whatever we think we're building in life can be blown away if there's no foundation to it. And God doesn't want us to find out that way.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi explores Parashat Ha'Azinu (Deuteronomy 32), Moses' final song to Israel before they enter the Promised Land. The song opens praising God as 'the Rock' whose work is perfect, then pivots starkly to warn that Israel will grow complacent, abandon God, and face exile. Rabbi highlights the striking nature of this covenant: God gives Israel a song rehearsing their future unfaithfulness before they have even crossed the Jordan—like wedding vows that anticipate infidelity yet promise restoration.

The word 'rock' appears seven times in the chapter, each instance underscoring God as the immovable foundation of Israel's identity and salvation. Drawing from the Haftarah in 2 Samuel 22, he shows David echoing Moses' themes generations later, declaring 'Hashem is my rock, my fortress and deliverer' from lived experience of failure and repentance. Isaiah 51 extends the metaphor, calling Israel to 'consider the rock from which you were cut,' connecting foundational identity back to Abraham and Sarah.

New Covenant passages in Ephesians 5 and Luke 6 reinforce that hearing God's word without acting on it is building a house without foundation. The sermon's central argument is that the real 'rock song' is not about human achievement but about God providing Himself as the unshakable foundation on which everything meaningful is built.

Hook / Wordplay

Moses wrote a literal song (shirah) in Deuteronomy 32 that names God as 'the Rock' seven times—making it, quite literally, the greatest rock song ever written, no shirtless rock star required.

2025-10-12

Sukkot: When Broken & Humble Is Exalted By God

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When facing painful or confusing circumstances, actively look for what God may be birthing rather than being paralyzed by the pain—birth pangs are temporary but what emerges lasts.
  • Resist the temptation to build spiritual identity on religious performance or comparison with others; God honors the broken and contrite heart, not the self-righteous scorecard.
  • Make yourself available as a vessel rather than trying to produce results—God brings the increase; we water and sow, and His light shines through our cracks.
One-Line Action
This week, spend quiet time in your own 'sukkah moment'—acknowledge your frailty before God and ask Him to fill your brokenness with His treasure and purpose.
Memorable Quote
He takes us like frail, flimsy, temporary shelters and puts His treasure in us.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi teaches on the Sukkot festival, framing it through the image of a fragile sukkah perched on the solid rock of Jerusalem's wall—a picture of how God exalts the broken and humble. He surveys the biblical foundations in Leviticus 23, then moves to Zechariah 14's prophecy that nations who attacked Jerusalem will one day come up to celebrate Sukkot, drawing parallels to the October 7th attacks and the anticipated hostage release happening during this very Sukkot season. Psalm 51 anchors the theme: God does not despise a broken and contrite heart, while self-righteous performance holds no weight with Him.

He reframes 1 Thessalonians 5:3 ('peace, then sudden destruction') not as a curse on hoping for peace, but as birth pangs that produce joy, connecting it to Yeshua's teaching in John 16 about a woman forgetting her suffering when the child is born. Stephen's martyrdom birthing Paul's ministry serves as a key illustration of God producing something powerful from tragedy. The sermon returns to John 7-8, where Yeshua declared 'I am the light of the world' and 'if anyone is thirsty, come to me' during the Sukkot water and light ceremonies.

He challenges the congregation not to measure themselves against others or build on self-righteousness, but to humble themselves as available vessels for God's purposes. The closing call is to recognize that we are frail, temporary dwellings in whom God has placed His eternal treasure, and to trust Him to accomplish what we never could on our own.

Hook / Wordplay

The sukkah on Jerusalem's wall: a fragile temporary shelter built on an unshakeable rock—just as God takes our brokenness and sets it on His eternal foundation.

2025-10-19

Beresheet: From the First Day…

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Every new step is a new beginning that requires 'in the beginning, God'—fresh starts only work when God is the starting point, not just willpower or resolutions.
  • The two-influence framework from the Garden applies daily: am I reflecting God's image or defaulting to the adversary's pattern of blame-shifting and scapegoating?
  • God's commitment to restoration is so extreme He says He will go to the ends of the sky to retrieve us—stop assuming distance from Him is permanent.
One-Line Action
Identify one area where you have been deflecting blame or hiding from God and bring it honestly before Him this week.
Memorable Quote
And there's something unique about this time-called life, this time that we have to be able to allow God to perfect and to develop us in our walk and to see what it is that God is doing.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

As the congregation returns to Beresheet after completing Deuteronomy, the rabbi frames the sermon around God being the constant from the first day of creation to the last days. He explores the Hebrew of Genesis 1:1-2 alongside Isaiah 45 to suggest that the earth may have undergone a judgment and recreation, with humanity as God's central focus. He highlights the two competing influences in the Garden—God's image and the serpent's—and how Adam and Eve's deflection of blame mirrors the accuser's character, a pattern we still replicate.

Drawing a parallel to politicians who promise action 'from day one' but fail to deliver, he contrasts human inconsistency with God's unwavering commitment. He connects Yeshua's Sukkot declaration 'I am the light of the world' back to Genesis 'let there be light,' showing thematic continuity across Scripture. Through Isaiah 42-43, he extends God's redemptive plan beyond Israel to all nations, citing the 70 Sukkot offerings as foreshadowing universal restoration.

He ties Joshua 1's charge to be strong and bold to the same message God gave from the Garden onward: don't be afraid, I am with you. The sermon closes with the affirmation that we live in the shortest part of eternity and must yield to God's transforming work rather than exchanging His glory for lesser things.

Hook / Wordplay

Politicians always say 'from day one' but their day one never delivers—God's 'from day one' is the only one that holds from Genesis to Revelation.

2025-10-26

Noah: Finding Calm in the Midst of A Storm

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When life feels out of control, shift focus from the storm to God's sovereignty — like Yeshua asleep in the stern, He is 'on board' even when He seems silent, and the peace He offers is not circumstantial but rooted in His presence.
  • Resist building personal 'towers of Babel' — relying on technology, institutions, or self-sufficiency for security — and instead run to 'the strong tower' of God's name (Proverbs 18:10) as the only reliable refuge.
  • Stay spiritually vigilant rather than lulled by routine normalcy; the warning of Matthew 24 is not about dramatic sin but about being so absorbed in everyday life that you miss what God is doing and saying.
One-Line Action
Next time a crisis hits, consciously pause and say 'quiet, be still' over your own anxious thoughts before trying to fix the situation, trusting that God is already on board.
Memorable Quote
Peace, when everything is peaceful, is not as dramatic as when everything is falling apart, and you have peace in the midst of us.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi opens with the Torah portion of Noach, noting how quickly humanity descended into violence (Hamas) after creation, requiring God to narrow the human race to eight people through the flood. He emphasizes that Noah tried diplomacy for 120 years while building the ark, but people mocked him because they had never seen rain. The sermon's central thesis is that true calm is not found in the eye of a storm (which is temporary) but in God's abiding presence, illustrated by Yeshua sleeping peacefully during the storm in Mark 4 while seasoned fishermen panicked.

Drawing from Psalm 107, he traces the recurring cycle of following God, straying, crying out, and being delivered. He connects the Tower of Babel's confusion of languages with the restoration of communication at Shavuot in Acts 2, showing God's pattern of reversing curses. The rabbi warns against the 'days of Noah' complacency described in Matthew 24, where people were caught up in everyday life and failed to see judgment coming.

He pivots to contemporary parallels including AI, climate anxiety, and institutional corruption as modern forms of the same human hubris that built the Tower of Babel. He closes with John 14's promise of the Holy Spirit as the ultimate source of peace — God-breathed intelligence that brings lasting shalom surpassing anything human systems can offer.

Hook / Wordplay

The calmest spot in a hurricane is the eye of the storm, but that calm is temporary — the real calm is not a weather pattern but a Person, the One who said to the waves 'quiet, be still' and meant it permanently.

Lekh L'kha: Remove Yourself To Become Great

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Identify your 'Haran' — the comfortable halfway point where you stopped short of full obedience — and take the next concrete step forward rather than staying where it's familiar.
  • Release the bottleneck of perfectionism or control; what you grip too tightly may be blocking not just that one thing but an entire flood of creativity and blessing behind it.
  • Check whether concern for personal performance or reputation is overshadowing genuine service — practice 'He must increase, I must decrease' in real situations, not just as a spiritual slogan.
One-Line Action
Name the one comfort zone or perfectionism that is bottlenecking your growth and take one step to release it to God this week.
Memorable Quote
If you're occupied in somebody else's story, you're not developing your own storyline with God.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Based on the Torah portion Lekh L'kha (Genesis 12:1–17:27), the sermon explores the dual meaning of God's command to Abram: leave your country physically, but also get your country out of you internally. Rabbi traces Abraham's staged journey from Ur through Haran to Canaan, noting he remained stuck in Haran — comfortable but incomplete — until his father died and the last familial bottleneck broke. Only after separating from Lot did God reveal the full scope of the promised land, and only after refusing the King of Sodom's spoils did God open new revelation about his descendants.

The rabbi shares a personal story of being stuck for 12 years with unfinished music due to a 'false sense of perfectionism,' and how releasing control to God unlocked not just that album but a flood of creativity — 13 albums total. He connects this to John the Baptist's declaration 'He must increase, I must decrease' as the model for true spiritual greatness.

A vulnerable story about performing at a Glenn Beck event illustrates how self-preoccupation with performance can overshadow genuine service. The sermon closes with Philippians 3 and 1 Peter 5, urging believers to humble themselves, cast anxiety on God, and press forward — becoming great by becoming nothing in His all.

Hook / Wordplay

"Get yourself out of your country" becomes "get your country out of you" — Lekh L'kha is not just a physical departure but an internal removal of self-reliance, comfort, and pride to make room for God's greatness.

2025-11-09

Vayera: Faith Often Appears When You Least Expect It!

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Don't let circumstances speak louder than God's promises — Sarah laughed at the impossible, but the question 'Is anything too hard for Hashem?' reframes every situation where the odds seem zero.
  • Prolonged exposure to ungodly environments subtly shifts moral boundaries — Lot's willingness to offer his daughters and his children's later actions show how corruption erodes judgment even in someone called righteous.
  • God's faithfulness covers even our failures of faith — Abraham lied about Sarah within the very year the promise was due, yet God still protected the promise and delivered Isaac on schedule.
One-Line Action
When a situation looks impossible this week, consciously ask yourself 'Is anything too hard for Hashem?' before letting the circumstances dictate your response.
Memorable Quote
Even when we prove ourselves to be unfaithful, he shows himself faithful to bring an avenue of return for us to rescue us from all of those things that would seek to destroy us.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon covers the Torah portion Vayera, focusing on how faith and God's faithfulness surface in moments we do not anticipate. Abraham was simply relaxing at his tent when God appeared with two angels, and Abraham responded with extraordinary hospitality. God promised Sarah would bear a son despite her being past childbearing age, posing the rhetorical challenge: is anything too hard for Hashem?

The rabbi traces Abraham's intercession for Sodom, offering an original calculation that Abraham stopped at ten because he was counting Lot's extended family. The destruction of Sodom and the moral compromise of Lot's family illustrate how prolonged exposure to corruption erodes even a righteous person's judgment. Abraham then repeated his earlier failure by passing Sarah off as his sister to Abimelech, jeopardizing the promise within the very year it was to be fulfilled, yet God protected Sarah and the promise anyway.

The binding of Isaac demonstrates Abraham's ultimate trust, believing God could raise his son from the dead. A parallel is drawn to Elisha and the Shunammite woman in 2 Kings, where a promised child died and was raised, showing faith appearing where even the prophet least expected it. The sermon concludes that while we cannot predict how God will fulfill His promises, His faithfulness is the one thing we can always expect, and it is that faithfulness which builds our own faith.

Hook / Wordplay

Vayera means 'he appeared' — and the sermon plays on this double meaning: just as God appeared to Abraham without warning, faith itself appears when we least expect it, and so does God's faithfulness.

Chayei Sarah: Follow Without Looking Back!

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When tempted to second-guess past decisions, redirect that energy forward; the only productive backward glance is to appreciate how far God has already brought you.
  • Eleazar refused comfort and delay even when offered generous hospitality—don't let 'just a few more days' of ease derail momentum on a God-given assignment.
  • Stop auditing other people's callings (John 21:22); 'what is it to you?' means my job is to follow my own commission, not compare it to someone else's.
One-Line Action
Identify one area where you are looking backward or sideways instead of forward, and consciously redirect your focus toward the next step God is asking you to take.
Memorable Quote
People will be willing to take a bullet for you, but they won't be willing to be inconvenienced.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi teaches from Torah portion Chayei Sarah, opening with Abraham purchasing the cave of Machpelah for Sarah and noting how Abraham never owned the promised land beyond that plot—yet God's promise remained. He pivots to Genesis 24 and Eleazar, Abraham's servant, as the sermon's central character study: a man so selflessly focused on his master's mission that he refused food before stating his purpose, declined Laban's invitation to linger, and seamlessly transferred loyalty from Abraham to Isaac. The rabbi unpacks Eleazar's name (God is help) and a wordplay on Damascus (to ladle out and give drink) to illuminate his servant nature.

He connects this to Yeshua's words in Luke 9 about the hand on the plow—if you keep looking backward the furrow will never be straight and the harvest will fail. Philippians 3 reinforces the theme: Paul counted all prior advantage as garbage compared to knowing Messiah and pressed forward toward the upward calling.

From John 10 he draws the sheep-and-shepherd relationship where following requires recognizing the shepherd's voice, and from John 12 the grain-of-wheat principle that dying to self produces harvest. He closes with John 21:22 where Yeshua redirects Peter's curiosity about John with a blunt corrective: what is it to you—you follow me.

Hook / Wordplay

A man walks forward while looking backward—heading straight into an open manhole. The only time you should ever look back is to see how far you've already come.

Tol'dot: Building A Family History

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When overwhelmed by confusing circumstances, follow Rebekah's model: inquire of the Lord rather than reacting emotionally or trying to engineer your own solution.
  • Don't dig up the seed — God's most transformative work happens invisibly below the surface, and impatience or attempts to control the process can prevent the growth He intends.
  • Use family gatherings as opportunities for connection and learning rather than winning arguments; the goal is to win family members into deeper relationship, not to win debates.
One-Line Action
When facing painful or confusing seasons, resist the urge to take control — inquire of the Lord and trust that He is building something beneath the surface you cannot yet see.
Memorable Quote
We should never think that what we're going through is the final destination. But it's what we go through in life to where God brings out the gold in us.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon explores the Torah portion Toldot (Genesis 25:19ff), tracing the history of Isaac's family from Rebekah's barrenness through the birth of Jacob and Esau. The rabbi highlights Rebekah's response to the tumult in her womb — she inquired of the Lord rather than reacting from emotion — as a model for handling overwhelming circumstances. He examines Esau's short-sighted trade of his birthright for stew, illustrating how we often overvalue the immediate and shiny while undervaluing God's deeper treasures. The sermon traces Isaac repeating his father Abraham's mistake of calling his wife his sister, showing how generational patterns persist yet God works through them.

A central metaphor compares God's work to a tree's invisible root system — the most important growth happens below the surface where we cannot see. The rabbi shares personal testimony of devastating seasons that later proved to be God's training exercises, preparing him for greater challenges ahead. He connects the Torah portion to Malachi's call for the priests to return to their calling and to Jeremiah 29's promise of hope and future through wholehearted seeking.

The sermon culminates in the New Testament vision of believers as one body and God's children, building a spiritual family history that carries the message of Messiah forward through generations. A closing exhortation frames upcoming Thanksgiving gatherings as opportunities for connection rather than contention. Throughout, the rabbi emphasizes that God's hidden work beneath the surface of our lives is what ultimately produces the visible fruit.

Hook / Wordplay

"Toldot" means history — and whatever the history of your family is, God wants to develop "His story" in our lives, building a family history generation after generation.

2025-11-30

Vayetze: Thanksgiving Leftovers

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Carry thankfulness past the holiday—when gratitude starts fading into routine complaint or blame, consciously recall what God has done and redirect toward encouragement.
  • Trust God's timeline even when it far exceeds expectations; Jacob expected months and got 21 years, yet God fulfilled every promise He made at Bethel.
  • Actively share spiritual 'leftovers' with others—blessings God gives are meant to be distributed, not hoarded; look for spontaneous moments to feed someone physically or spiritually.
One-Line Action
This week, take one specific blessing from your Thanksgiving table conversation and turn it into an act of encouragement toward someone outside your household.
Memorable Quote
Nothing God gives us goes to waste. It goes to establishing us, strengthening us, making us equipped to move forward and to share the things that we have with others.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon connects the Torah portion Vayetze—Jacob's departure from home and God's promise at Bethel—to the Thanksgiving season through the metaphor of leftovers. Jacob received God's assurance 'I am with you' as he left everything familiar, and despite 21 years of hardship under Laban's manipulation, he maintained a thankful spirit anchored in that promise. The rabbi draws a parallel to Thanksgiving: we express gratitude around the table, but the real question is whether we carry those 'leftovers' of thankfulness into the days that follow or revert to blaming and complaining.

He references Yeshua feeding the 5,000, where 12 baskets of leftovers remained, and the widow's oil in Kings as examples of God multiplying small, leftover things into abundance. A sobering detour through Hosea shows what happens when satisfaction breeds pride and people forget God.

From 2 Corinthians 2:14 he frames believers as the 'aroma of Messiah'—well-preserved leftovers bringing life to others. The sermon closes with a call to expand gratitude rather than let it dissipate, sharing spiritual blessings with others just as God's doggy bag protocol intends.

Hook / Wordplay

Thanksgiving Leftovers / God's Doggy Bag Protocol—spiritual blessings are the leftovers we take home and share, not for Rover but for the people around us.

2025-12-07

Vayishlach: Be Rescued By Wrestling Through

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When facing an uncertain situation, do what Jacob did: honestly voice your fear to God, then immediately pivot to reciting His specific promises back to Him — this shifts focus from catastrophe to covenant.
  • Resist the urge to 'ruminate on the rake' — when someone's behavior confuses or hurts you, ask 'What's wrong?' before constructing narratives; unchecked assumptions poison relationships and give the adversary access.
  • Recognize that God doesn't zap you with courage or patience but gives opportunities to practice them — so when a frightening new situation arises, treat it as the training ground, not the punishment.
One-Line Action
When fear of the unknown paralyzes you, remind God (and yourself) of His specific promises and refuse to let go until you receive the blessing.
Memorable Quote
We make the mistake of thinking that the circumstances are more real than God's commitment to us. But God's commitment to us is real and he wants to carry us through.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon explores Jacob's intense fear as he returns to face Esau after 21 years, knowing Esau's last words were a death threat and now approaches with 400 men. Rabbi shows how Jacob responded not by fleeing but by crying out to God, honestly expressing his fear, then pivoting to remind God of His covenant promises to multiply his descendants and bring him safely home. The physical wrestling with the angel mirrors Jacob's internal wrestling with fear and uncertainty — God's method of rescue was not to remove the trial but to walk him through it.

A vivid class reunion illustration showed how we freeze people in their worst moment, assuming Esau must still be furious, when in reality 21 years had transformed him into a brother who ran to embrace Jacob with tears. The rabbi connected this to Ephesians 6, teaching that our strength in spiritual battles comes from union with God rather than our own power, and that the adversary's chief tactic is getting us to ruminate on worst-case circumstances until we believe them more than God's promises. A rake-and-mall anecdote dramatized the danger of rumination — a man spirals into resentment over an imagined slight, only to learn his friend had just lost his mother.

The sermon warned against the paralysis that fear produces and called believers to press forward like Jacob, refusing to let go until blessed. It closed with Joshua 1:9's charge to be strong and bold because God is present wherever we go, and urged believers not to receive God's grace and then do nothing with it.

Hook / Wordplay

God's rescue method is not removal from the struggle but wrestling through it — Jacob was literally 'rescued by wrestling through,' and so are we when fear tempts us to hide.

Vayeshev: Keys To Overcoming the Unknown

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When facing uncertainty, consciously recall past trials God brought you through rather than fixating on the current darkness—those memories are reference points that combat paralysis.
  • Don't give 'testimony for the adversary': when sharing struggles, lead with what God is doing rather than amplifying the opposition, because focus determines framing.
  • Like the Maccabees who sparked courage in people hiding in caves, look for opportunities to bring light into someone else's dark season instead of retreating into your own fear.
One-Line Action
This week when an unknown situation triggers fear, pause and name one past trial God carried you through, then step forward trusting the same God is present now.
Memorable Quote
We don't need to give testimony for the adversary. We need to give place for the Lord to illuminate our hearts and to show us God is light.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon connects the Torah portion Vayeshev (Genesis 37) with the Hanukkah story under the theme of overcoming the unknown through trust in God. Rabbi Rosenberg notes that Jacob's history is told through Joseph's story, illustrating how God works across generations in ways no one can foresee. He traces Joseph's trials—sibling hatred, slavery, false accusation, imprisonment—as a pattern of God preparing someone through darkness for a purpose beyond comprehension.

He parallels this with the Maccabees, who sparked courage in a people hiding in caves and led them from impossible despair to military victory. The rabbi argues that fear of the unknown causes spiritual paralysis, but God uses those very seasons to build tenacity and perseverance. He draws on David's unlikely rise, Yeshua enduring the cross 'for the joy set before him,' and Paul's radical transformation as further examples.

The progressive lighting of the Hanukkah menorah becomes a metaphor for God's incremental illumination in our lives. The central message is that Yeshua is both the door and the key—the one who unlocks what we cannot see and carries us through what we cannot understand.

Hook / Wordplay

Yeshua is both the door and the key—he doesn't just show us the way through the unknown, he is the instrument that unlocks it.

Mikketz: Out of Darkness, Light Shines

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Dark seasons may be building credibility for something far bigger than I can imagine—Joseph thought he'd lead his family but ended up leading the known world; stop evaluating God's plan by current circumstances.
  • Don't 'flick your own Bic' trying to manufacture light through self-effort—yield to God's illumination and let him open doors I could never see on my own.
  • Small acts of light carry outsized power: a smile, a touch, stepping in to help—I may be the one to break someone free from what holds them bound.
One-Line Action
This week, intentionally step into one dark or discouraging situation around me and bring a concrete act of kindness as a carrier of God's light.
Memorable Quote
You can't turn off the darkness. There's no switch that allows you to turn off the darkness.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi teaches from Parashat Mikketz (Genesis 41) where Joseph, forgotten in prison for two full years, is suddenly elevated to rule Egypt when Pharaoh's dream defies all interpreters. He emphasizes that God's delayed timing produced something far greater than Joseph's own plan—not just family leadership but governance of the known world. He parallels Joseph's story with the Maccabees, who sparked a rebellion from caves of despair and routed a vastly superior enemy.

Drawing on Zechariah's menorah vision, he highlights the principle of 'not by might nor by power but by my spirit.' He weaves Isaiah's prophecies of light breaking through darkness with the John 10 Hanukkah passage where Yeshua declares his sheep secure in his hands. The sermon connects Joseph's rejection by his brothers to Yeshua's rejection by his own people, both becoming the 'chief cornerstone.' He names the children Manasseh (forgetting the past) and Ephraim (fruitfulness) as evidence that Joseph refused to let darkness dampen his spirit. The rabbi calls the congregation to be carriers of God's light rather than generators of their own, meeting the needs of the suffering and letting God's illumination transform darkness into noonday.

Hook / Wordplay

You can't turn off the darkness—there's no switch for that. But when you turn on the light, darkness disappears. The more light there is, the more darkness has to flee.

Homecoming & God's Family Reunions

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Judah's self-sacrifice broke Joseph because it defied 22 years of negative expectations — don't let 'muscle memory' of someone's worst moment define your belief in their capacity for change.
  • Rumination is cyclical replaying of negative voices that pull us from God's presence; actively set aside those loops rather than letting them downgrade the promises God has made.
  • Being 'caught up' with the Lord is a daily, present-tense practice of union with God — don't defer the homecoming to some future event when His presence is available now.
One-Line Action
Replace cyclical rumination with daily return to God's presence, choosing present union with Him over the negative voices that keep you distant.
Memorable Quote
Even though he had nothing left, he had his father. And the one who had everything didn't fully understand the relationship that his father wanted to have with his sons.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon explores Torah portion Vayigash through the lens of homecoming, centering on Judah's unexpected self-sacrifice before Joseph — offering himself as a slave so Benjamin could return to their father. Rabbi highlights how this act broke Joseph emotionally because it contradicted his muscle memory of Judah as the brother who sold him into slavery 22 years earlier. He draws extensive parallels between Joseph and Yeshua: both rejected by their own, both bringing redemption to those who wronged them.

The theme extends to Jacob's fearful return to Esau, where anticipated violence became an embrace, and to the prodigal son whose father saw him from a long way off. Rabbi reframes the Shavuot event in Acts as another homecoming — the disciples empowered in God's presence at the Temple during the festival, not hiding in an upper room. He addresses Christmas-season misconceptions, correcting narratives about Jesus as Palestinian and arguing for a Sukkot-era birth that explains the lodging situation naturally.

Through Hosea 11, he shows God's heart recoiling at the idea of giving up on Israel despite their rebellion. The sermon culminates with a reinterpretation of John 14's 'where I am, there you may be also' as an invitation to present union with God rather than merely a future heavenly destination. He closes with his homiletics teacher's insight that being 'caught up' to meet the Lord is for those already caught up with Him now.

Hook / Wordplay

"Those who are going to be caught up to meet the Lord when he returns are those who are caught up with him right now" — rapture language reframed as present-tense devotion.

Season / Tag
Torah Portion Vayigash (Genesis 44:18–47:27)

2026-01-04

Vayechi: Living Through Transitions

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When facing an uncomfortable life change, resist the urge to 'cut the cocoon'—recognize that the struggle itself is building the capacity needed for the next phase, and avoid enabling shortcuts that bypass the growth.
  • Audit where you may be 'locked in a prison of your own making' like Joseph's brothers—holding onto fear or assumptions about others' intentions that 17 years of evidence should have already disproven.
  • Practice Romans 12:2 concretely: identify one area where worldly standards or media-driven priorities have shaped your thinking more than Scripture, and deliberately renew your mind on that point.
One-Line Action
Identify one current life transition you are resisting or complaining about, and reframe it as God's building block for your next phase.
Memorable Quote
When we get caught up in the details of what's going on and we don't learn from the transitional phases that are there, we end up locked in a prison of our own making.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon explores the Torah portion Vayechi (Genesis 47:28–50:26), focusing on Jacob's final 17 years in Egypt and the prophetic blessings he bestowed on his sons and grandsons. Rabbi traces how Jacob's entire life—conflict with Esau, deception by Laban, the loss and restoration of Joseph—was a chain of transitions that refined him into a man of prophetic clarity. Jacob's deliberate blessing of Ephraim over Manasseh and his messianic prophecy over Judah (Genesis 49:10) demonstrated purpose forged through a lifetime of struggle.

The sermon examines how Joseph's brothers, even after 17 years of provision, still feared his vengeance—illustrating how failure to internalize life's transitions creates self-imposed prisons of fear. Drawing from Romans 12:1-2, the rabbi urges believers to be transformed by the renewing of their minds rather than conforming to worldly standards and manufactured priorities. Personal anecdotes—a near-death experience at age three, his daughter Tova's unexpected tenacity, a childhood bike accident—illustrate how God uses painful transitions as building blocks for future purpose.

The butterfly-cocoon metaphor reinforces that struggle is essential to spiritual growth, just as the cocoon struggle strengthens wings for flight. Supporting texts from 1 Peter 2, Ephesians 5, Acts 26, and 2 Corinthians 4 develop the overarching theme of God calling His people from darkness into light through successive transitions. The sermon concludes that each transition is not a final destination but the next phase in God's unfolding plan, and believers should cooperate with His spirit through every one.

Hook / Wordplay

'Hello, I'm in transition'—the rabbi uses a visual sign and the cloud-and-lights display to frame life itself as walking into the unknown, where God is forming us for what comes next.

2026-01-11

Shemot: Who Am I? Where Do I Fit In?

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Reframe the identity question: stop asking 'Who am I?' and start asking 'Lord, who are You and how can I walk in that?'—identity settles when God's presence fills the puzzle piece, not when I analyze myself.
  • The preparations are as much a part of the calling as the calling itself; the scars, detours, and waiting seasons are God's raw-material work, not wasted time.
  • Both self-depreciation ('I have no place') and self-sufficiency ('I don't need you') break the body—watch for each tendency and counter it with the 1 Corinthians 12 lens.
One-Line Action
This week, when insecurity or comparison surfaces, consciously redirect the question from 'Who am I?' to 'Lord, who are You in this moment?' and let that reframe the situation.
Memorable Quote
It's not who you are right now, but it's who I see inside of you. The potential that is there.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon opens the book of Shemot (Exodus) by tracing how the Israelites lost their identity, shifting from honored guests in Egypt to an enslaved people whose self-concept shrank to 'I am nothing—I am a slave.' Against that backdrop the rabbi examines Moses at the burning bush asking 'Who am I that I should go before Pharaoh?'—a man with no confidence whom God already saw as the deliverer. He draws a parallel to Jeremiah, who protested 'I'm just a child,' and to Peter, who in one breath confessed Yeshua as Messiah and in the next was rebuked as Satan for thinking from a human perspective. The rich young ruler's story illustrates how identity built on wealth rather than God's call keeps us from following.

Using Psalm 139 the rabbi shows that God knew us before the womb and is shaping us in secret through every scar and circumstance. First Corinthians 12's body metaphor then reframes the identity question: each believer is a puzzle piece with a unique fit, and both self-depreciation ('I'm not needed') and pride ('I don't need you') distort the design. The central turn is that instead of asking 'Who am I?' we should ask 'Lord, who are You, and how can I appropriate who You are in my life?' Our identity resolves not through self-analysis but through yielding to the great I Am who is working in us.

Hook / Wordplay

Moses asks 'Who am I?' and God answers 'I Am who I Am'—the human identity crisis is met head-on by the divine identity declaration, suggesting we are 'extensions of the I Am.'

Primary Theme

2026-01-18

Vaera: Liberation From Despair

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When stuck in discouragement, don't try to will yourself happy — redirect to Scripture meditation (Joshua 1:8) as God's actual mechanism for renewing the mind, since 'how we think is how we are.'
  • Receive God's comfort through trials not as a destination but as equipment — 'He comforts us to be able to comfort others' — actively look for someone to encourage this week.
  • Identify one specific 'ball and chain' — a fear, slave-mentality pattern, or burden — and consciously release it rather than carrying it alongside God's provision, recognizing affliction is 'what we're passing through, not our new residence.'
One-Line Action
Name your ball-and-chain this week, set it down, and spend that freed energy meditating on one passage and encouraging one person.
Memorable Quote
He doesn't comfort us to make us comfortable, but he comforts us to be able to comfort others.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

Rabbi teaches from Parashat Vaera, where God tells Moses 'I appeared' to the patriarchs and now promises deliverance — yet the Israelites refuse to listen because their slavery is too cruel and Moses keeps falling back into self-doubt ('poor speaker that I am'). He uses the image of a ball and chain labeled 'discouragement' and the story of a man riding a wagon but still holding his heavy bundle to illustrate how we carry burdens God has already offered to bear. He connects Philippians 4's 'think on these things' to Joshua 1's charge to be strong and bold — not through self-effort but through meditating on Torah day and night.

From John 16 he draws the principle that grief turns to joy and that Yeshua has overcome the world on our behalf. He highlights 2 Corinthians 1 where Paul admits he 'despaired even of living through it,' showing that even great leaders face crushing discouragement, and that God's comfort equips us to comfort others rather than making us comfortable. He closes with Romans 8:31-39, declaring that nothing — trouble, hardship, danger, or any element of despair — can separate us from God's love in Messiah, and that true liberation comes through walking in union with Him, not through self-help or positive thinking alone.

Hook / Wordplay

The ball-and-chain paired with the wagon story: a man given a free ride still clutches his heavy bundle because 'you were kind enough to give me a ride — I don't need you to carry my burden as well,' mirroring how we hold onto discouragement even inside God's provision.

2026-01-25

Bo: When Darkness Helps People See More Clearly

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • When going through a disorienting or 'dark' season, treat it as a mining operation — ask what hidden wisdom or growth God is refining in the difficulty rather than just enduring it.
  • Guard against the subtle drift of calling darkness 'light' — when material security or comfortable numbness replaces genuine spiritual vitality, let the discomfort be the wake-up call to realign.
  • Like the blind man whose testimony was simply 'I was blind, now I see,' let personal experience of God's transformation be the anchor of witness rather than getting drawn into theological debate.
One-Line Action
This week, identify one area of life that feels disorienting or dark and deliberately bring it before God as a place where hidden treasure may be waiting to surface.
Memorable Quote
You can't turn off darkness. But if you turn on the light, the darkness disappears.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon explores the paradox that darkness can actually sharpen spiritual sight, anchored in the ninth plague of Exodus where Egypt experienced darkness so thick it could be felt while Israel had light in their homes. Rabbi Cahn traces Moses' rising confidence through the plagues, noting how he moved from insecurity to boldly declaring 'not a hoof will be left behind' in his negotiation with Pharaoh. He connects the Exodus darkness to Yeshua's teaching in Matthew 6 that when the 'light in you is darkness, how great is that darkness,' warning against mistaking material accumulation for genuine illumination.

The sermon pivots to Paul's three-day blindness in Acts 9, paralleling the three days of Egyptian darkness, showing how forced sightlessness became the catalyst for Paul's radical spiritual transformation. The man born blind in John 9 provides a third case study, whose simple testimony 'I was blind, now I see' cut through all theological debate. A striking reading of Job 28 reframes darkness entirely: miners deliberately enter dark places not to suffer but to extract hidden wealth, sapphires, and gold that surface dwellers never see.

Rabbi Cahn synthesizes these threads into the principle that darkness serves as a reference point, a wake-up call that drives us to seek God's light rather than settle for counterfeit comfort. He concludes that believers carry the light of life into dark places not to be contaminated by them but to set captives free, reflecting Messiah's light as a lamp to our feet and a light to our path.

Hook / Wordplay

Darkness so thick you could feel it — yet that same darkness became the catalyst that made people finally see. The plague that shut every eye in Egypt opened the door to freedom.
