2026-02-01

B'Shallach: When the Enemy Looks Too Big, Remember… God is Bigger!

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Recognize the 'slave mentality' reflex: when hardship hits, the pull to retreat to familiar suffering (even if oppressive) is a pattern to name and resist rather than obey.
  • Boldness is grown, not granted instantly—God develops it by bringing challenges that require courage, so lean into difficulty as training rather than treating it as punishment.
  • The adversary needs an opening you provide; resist by drawing near to God rather than fixating on the enemy's apparent size, because proximity to God is itself the act of spiritual resistance.
One-Line Action
When circumstances loom large, resist the reflex to retreat and instead draw near to God, letting His actual size reset your perspective.
Memorable Quote
When we diminish our place and diminish God's capacity to work in us in such great ways, we start to look at an enemy bigger than he really is.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon follows Torah portion B'Shallach, where Israel panics at the sight of Pharaoh's pursuing army despite having just witnessed God's miraculous deliverance from Egypt. Rabbi explores how fear distorts perception, making enemies appear larger than they are, and how Israel's instinct was to retreat to the familiar misery of slavery rather than trust the God who had already proven Himself. He draws a parallel to David and Goliath, noting how David's own brother projected his fear onto David by calling him conceited, while David's boldness came from genuine trust in God's track record.

The sermon threads through Deborah and Barak, where military timidity required a woman judge to step forward, and into the New Covenant with Peter and John's Spirit-empowered boldness before the authorities. The Ephesians 6 armor passage is unpacked as God's provision against the adversary's primarily deceptive—not physical—tactics.

A key insight is that boldness cannot be instantly downloaded; God develops it by providing challenges that require courage. The sermon concludes that the adversary's power depends entirely on our surrender, and that drawing near to God is itself the act of resistance that causes the enemy to flee.

Hook / Wordplay

Pharaoh as Wile E. Coyote—always thinking 'I got him this time' only to have it blow up in his face—plus 'MDS: Moses Deranged Syndrome' as a tongue-in-cheek diagnosis of hatred-fueled obsession.

Yitro A Son Is Given — Our Future Starts With A Birth

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Identify what is blurring your spiritual vision—wealth, comfort, ambition, or old habits—and yield it so God can birth His purpose in you, just as the rich young ruler was challenged to release what held him back.
  • Stop living below your spiritual inheritance: as joint-heirs with Messiah (Romans 8), lean into the authority and resources God has already made available rather than defaulting to fear or self-reliance.
  • Like Isaiah who volunteered before knowing the assignment, practice saying yes to God's promptings even when the full picture is unclear—trust that being in His presence is reason enough to step forward.
One-Line Action
Identify one area where comfort or ambition is blurring your calling and surrender it this week so God can birth something greater through you.
Memorable Quote
I think many of us live far below what God says is our portion and our calling.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon opens with the Torah portion Yitro (Exodus 18–20), highlighting Jethro's amazement at God's deliverance and his counsel for Moses to appoint capable leaders—framing leadership as being 'given to serve' the people, not to rule over them. Rabbi pivots to the Haftarah's messianic prophecies in Isaiah 7:14 and 9:5-6, showing how 'unto us a son is given' operates on multiple levels: Messiah as the promised child, leaders appointed to shepherd Israel, and believers becoming children of God. He draws a parallel between Isaiah's volunteer response ('Here am I, send me') and our own calling to step forward in faith without knowing the full picture.

Through the rich young ruler in Luke 18, he illustrates how personal wealth or ambition can blind us to God's deeper purpose for our lives. Connecting to Romans 8 and 1 Peter 2, he argues that through Messiah we receive not a spirit of slavery but of sonship—becoming heirs and joint-heirs with authority to extend God's kingdom.

He emphasizes that most believers live far below their spiritual inheritance because they are drawn back into old patterns of fear and self-reliance. The sermon closes with the conviction that our future began with Messiah's birth, was secured by his death and resurrection, and unfolds as we remain in union with him.

Hook / Wordplay

'A son is given' operates on three levels—Messiah prophesied in Isaiah, leaders appointed to serve Israel under Moses, and believers born into God's family—unifying governance, prophecy, and identity in one phrase.

Mishpatim: The Regulations of Love

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • God's regulations are expressions of love and protection, not constraints on freedom—recognizing his boundaries as gifts reframes obedience from burden to gratitude.
  • When you fall, God's heart is always restoration; he calls unfaithful Israel 'virgin of Israel,' showing his commitment to renewal no matter how far you've strayed.
  • Loving enemies is the proving ground of authentic faith—extending grace to those who don't deserve it is exactly how God's love reached us while we were still rebels against him.
One-Line Action
This week, extend deliberate grace to someone difficult, recognizing that God's love reached you while you were still his enemy.
Memorable Quote
Even judgment is a great expression of God's love because he doesn't allow them to be torn away from him.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The sermon explores Parashat Mishpatim (Exodus 21:1–24:18), reframing God's rules and regulations not as stifling restrictions but as boundary markers of love that keep us from going off a cliff. The rabbi explains how biblical slavery laws functioned as a protective welfare system with built-in freedom provisions after six years, contrasting this with the brutality of slavery elsewhere. He connects the command to honor parents with learning to honor God, and shows how communal regulations protected the vulnerable and maintained justice.

Drawing from Jeremiah 34, he illustrates how Israel freed their slaves in obedience but then re-enslaved them, bringing God's judgment—demonstrating that violating God's loving boundaries has real consequences. From Jeremiah 31 and 33, he presents God's everlasting love that restores Israel even after unfaithfulness, calling them 'virgin of Israel' despite their violations.

He references Yeshua's teaching in Matthew 5 to love enemies and Romans 13's declaration that love fulfills Torah. He creatively uses a Fourier transform analogy to illustrate that God's love contains many complex ingredients, concluding that Messiah at the center is the only algorithm needed to experience the fullness of that love.

Hook / Wordplay

God's regulations are not restrictions that stifle—they are the recipe of love, boundary markers that keep us from going off a cliff. Finding Messiah at the center is the only algorithm we need to decompose and experience every ingredient of that love.

2026-02-22

Where Do You Store Your Story?

Watch on YouTube
Takeaways
  • Don't bank only on old testimonies—actively ask 'what is God about to do?' and position yourself to receive the fresh testimony He is building in your life right now.
  • Encourage others in their specific gifts rather than comparing or competing; even the person 'making loops' is essential to the tabernacle coming together and reaching critical mass.
  • Reread 1 Corinthians 13 in the Amplified version—'takes no account of the evil done to it, pays no attention to a suffered wrong'—and honestly assess the gap between that standard and your current practice.
One-Line Action
This week, identify one person in your community whose 'loop-making' gift you've overlooked and specifically encourage them in it.
Memorable Quote
Better than walking with us is Messiah walking in us as we take his nature. So our testimony is an extension of him, of his nature, of his life.
Open detailssummary, scripture, themes
Sermon Summary

The Torah portion Terumah (Exodus 25) describes God calling for wholehearted contributions to build the tabernacle, which the rabbi reframes as not a GoFundMe but a God-fund-me—God resourcing His people to participate in His purposes. He emphasizes that God told Moses to put the testimony into the ark, drawing a parallel to the fresh testimony God is building in our lives now, not just what He did before. Every person had a role in constructing the tabernacle, from master craftsman Bezalel down to those making simple loops, and no contribution was insignificant.

Through 1 Corinthians 12 he explores both sides of dysfunction in the body: insecurity that dismisses one's own gift and pride that dismisses others' gifts. Matthew 6:19-34 redirects treasure-storing from material to spiritual, with the wordplay that 'my story is his story'—without Messiah our story is bland and meaningless. A personal turning point is shared: reading 1 Corinthians 13 in the Amplified version and realizing that love 'takes no account of the evil done to it' exposed how far he still had to grow.

First Peter 2 presents believers as living stones being built into a spiritual house—the true fulfillment of what the tabernacle foreshadowed. Philippians 2 calls believers to humility after Messiah's own example, regarding others as better than ourselves because collectively we accomplish what none could alone. The sermon closes with the image of reaching 'critical mass' when the body unites—like the tabernacle's completion bringing God's manifest presence—releasing power that vanquishes the enemy and transforms lives.

Hook / Wordplay

Not a GoFundMe—a God-fund-me. And my story is his story: without Messiah, our story becomes bland and meaningless.
